topics.title: Need for Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Need for Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Security in the Internet of Things (IoT)** is defined as the disciplined, multi-layered practice of protecting interconnected physical devices, network communications, data at rest, data in transit, and control commands from unauthorized access, tampering, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context (Course Outcome **CO1** of *PCCIT602*), security is treated as a **non-functional requirement** that underpins every functional layer of an IoT stack — from the resource-constrained sensor node (perception layer) up to the cloud analytics plane (application layer).

The **Need for Security** specifically addresses the *justification* for embedding cryptographic primitives, key management schemes, authentication protocols, and lightweight cipher suites into IoT deployments. It formalizes the **threat model**, identifies the **assets** (data, devices, identity, availability, privacy), classifies **adversaries** (passive eavesdroppers, active intruders, insider attackers, physical tamperers), and maps these to **security services**: *Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Authorization, and Availability*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 CO1 Mapping:** "Explain the need for security in IoT ecosystems and identify vulnerabilities across perception, network, and application layers." This topic directly tests the student's ability to *recognize, classify, and justify* security requirements — not to design ciphers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart House" Intuition

Imagine a brand-new **Smart House** with **50 internet-connected devices**: a smart door lock, a baby monitor, a smart fridge, fitness wristbands, CCTV cameras, smart electricity meters, and a voice assistant like Alexa. Now imagine:

- The **smart door lock** broadcasts its unlock code in plain text over Wi-Fi → anyone with a $20 radio can open your door.
- The **baby monitor** streams live video without encryption → a stranger watches your child sleep.
- The **smart meter** sends your "no one is home" data to the cloud → burglars know when to break in.
- A **hacker** sends fake "temperature = 200°C" signals to a chemical plant sensor → a factory explodes.
- A **malware worm** infects 2 million smart kettles → they all switch on at midnight, causing a regional power blackout.

**That is exactly why we need security in IoT.** When ordinary everyday objects become "smart" (sensors + processors + radios + internet), each one becomes a **potential doorway** into our private life, our money, our safety, and even our cities. Security is the *digital lock, the alarm system, the identity card, and the security guard* rolled into one, deployed on every such device.

### 1.3 Why the Topic Demands Special Attention in IoT

Conventional IT security (laptops, servers) was designed around **abundant power, high memory, fast CPUs, and reliable connectivity**. IoT flips all of these on their head:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Four Pillars of IoT Security Difficulty**
> 1. **Resource Constraints:** A typical IoT sensor runs on an 8-bit microcontroller (e.g., ATmega328) with **2 KB RAM** and **32 KB flash** — it cannot even *fit* standard AES-128 comfortably without optimization, let alone run TLS 1.3 with RSA-2048 handshakes.
> 2. **Massive Scale:** By 2025–2030, the number of connected IoT devices is projected in the range of **75+ billion** globally. Each device is a potential attack surface.
> 3. **Heterogeneity:** Devices use dozens of incompatible protocols — Zigbee, BLE, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, MQTT, CoAP, 6LoWPAN, RPL — each with its own quirks and vulnerabilities.
> 4. **Physical Exposure:** Many IoT endpoints (wearables, outdoor sensors, smart meters) sit *in the open* where an attacker can physically probe, clone, or dismantle them.

Because of these constraints, **traditional cryptographic suites cannot be ported directly**. This is precisely the engineering problem *PCCIT602* exists to solve.

### 1.4 The CIA Triad — The Heart of Security Need

The universally accepted model of information security rests on three pillars (sometimes extended to five):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad (Core Security Services)**
> - **C — Confidentiality:** Only authorized parties can read the data. Example: A patient's ECG stream reaches only the doctor's tablet, not a neighbour's laptop.
> - **I — Integrity:** Data has not been altered in transit or at rest. Example: A smart grid billing record cannot be inflated from ₹500 to ₹5,000 by a man-in-the-middle.
> - **A — Availability:** Services remain accessible when needed. Example: A fire-alarm IoT node cannot be jammed or DDoS-ed during an actual fire.

**Extended Services (often tested in KTU):**
- **Authentication:** *Who are you?* (Verifying device/user identity)
- **Authorization:** *What are you allowed to do?*
- **Non-Repudiation:** *You cannot deny having sent this command.*
- **Privacy:** Personal data is handled per consent and regulation (e.g., India's DPDP Act 2023).

### 1.5 Real-World Incidents That Justify the "Need"

| Year | Incident | Impact | Security Service Violated |
|------|----------|--------|---------------------------|
| 2010 | Stuxnet worm (Iran nuclear centrifuges) | Destroyed 1,000+ centrifuges | Integrity, Authentication |
| 2016 | Mirai Botnet (Dyn DNS attack) | Took down Twitter, Netflix, Reddit | Availability, Authentication |
| 2017 | WannaCry Ransomware (NHS UK) | 80,000 devices, $4B loss | Availability, Integrity |
| 2018 | VPNFilter (500K routers globally) | Credential theft, traffic hijacking | Confidentiality, Integrity |
| 2021 | Verkada Camera Breach | 150,000 live camera feeds leaked | Confidentiality, Authorization |
| 2023 | MQTT Mis-exposure (Industrial SCADA) | Factory floorplans exposed online | Confidentiality |

These incidents are **not hypothetical exam questions** — they are the empirical evidence that drives the entire "Need for Security" discourse.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Layer IoT Reference Model (Attack Surface Mapping)

The KTU syllabus treats the IoT architecture as a **three-layer (or five-layer) reference model**. Each layer has distinct vulnerabilities, and therefore distinct security needs.

#### **Layer 1 — Perception / Sensing Layer**
*Devices: RFID tags, sensors, actuators, smart cards, wearable bands*

- **Functions:** Physical signal acquisition, A/D conversion, local actuation.
- **Assets to protect:** Sensor readings, device identity, firmware.
- **Threats:** Node capture, physical tampering, side-channel attacks (power/timing/EM analysis), cloning of RFID tags, fake sensor injection.
- **Security Needs:** Lightweight encryption (e.g., PRESENT, Speck, Ascon), tamper-resistant hardware, secure boot, physically unclonable functions (PUFs).

#### **Layer 2 — Network / Transport Layer**
*Protocols: Wi-Fi, Zigbee, BLE, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 6LoWPAN, RPL, MQTT, CoAP, AMQP*

- **Functions:** Routing, addressing, packet forwarding between edge devices and gateways/cloud.
- **Assets to protect:** Routing tables, session keys, packet payloads.
- **Threats:** Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle (MITM), replay attacks, routing attacks (sinkhole, wormhole, blackhole), DoS / DDoS, Sybil attacks.
- **Security Needs:** End-to-end encryption (DTLS, IPsec), authentication handshakes, secure routing protocols, replay protection via nonces/timestamps.

#### **Layer 3 — Application / Cloud / Business Layer**
*Components: Cloud servers, mobile apps, dashboards, analytics, third-party APIs*

- **Functions:** Storage, big-data analytics, visualization, control commands back to actuators.
- **Assets to protect:** User PII, aggregated telemetry, control decisions, billing.
- **Threats:** Cloud misconfiguration, SQL injection, broken access control, insider abuse, API key theft, GDPR/DPDP violations.
- **Security Needs:** Access control models (RBAC, ABAC), data-at-rest encryption, audit logging, secure APIs (OAuth 2.0, OpenID Connect), regulatory compliance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mid-Layer Helpers (often added):** *Edge/Fog Layer* and *Middleware/Processing Layer*. KTU examiners sometimes ask: "At which layer does the **gateway** operate?" — Answer: It bridges Perception and Network layers.

### 2.2 Threat Model — Who, What, How

A **threat model** is the formal description of *who* attacks *what* *how*. KTU expects you to use this vocabulary.

- **Adversary classes (Who):**
  - *Passive attacker* — only observes traffic (eavesdropper).
  - *Active attacker* — modifies, injects, drops packets.
  - *Insider attacker* — legitimate user abusing privileges.
  - *Physical attacker* — has hands-on access to the device.
  - *Nation-state / Advanced Persistent Threat (APT)* — well-resourced, patient, targets critical infrastructure.

- **Attack goals (What):**
  - Confidentiality breach (data theft)
  - Integrity violation (false data injection)
  - Availability loss (DoS)
  - Identity theft / impersonation
  - Privacy violation (behavioral profiling)

- **Attack vectors (How):**
  - **Network-based:** Eavesdropping, MITM, replay, DoS, jamming.
  - **Software-based:** Malware, buffer overflow, firmware tampering, supply-chain injection.
  - **Physical:** Tamper, side-channel, fault injection, node capture.
  - **Cryptographic:** Brute force, birthday attack, related-key attack, chosen-ciphertext.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This cheat sheet lists the **key conceptual formulas, metrics, and relationships** that KTU expects students to memorize and apply in numerical/short-answer questions.

| # | Concept / Metric | Expression / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Birthday Paradox bound** | $2^{n/2}$ messages to find a collision in an $n$-bit hash | 64-bit hash → $2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9}$ attempts |
| 2 | **AES keyspace size** | $2^{k}$ for $k$-bit key | AES-128 → $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ |
| 3 | **Brute-force time estimate** | $T = \dfrac{2^{k}}{r}$ where $r$ = guesses/sec | Determines minimum acceptable key length |
| 4 | **Shannon Entropy (information leakage)** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | bits/symbol — measures unpredictability |
| 5 | **Perceived security strength** | $\text{bits of security} = \log_2(\text{work factor})$ | NIST deprecates < 112 bits post-2030 |
| 6 | **IoT device memory (typical)** | 2–256 KB RAM, 32 KB–4 MB flash | Constraint for cipher choice |
| 7 | **Energy budget of a coin cell (CR2032)** | ~$2{,}200$ mAh @ 3 V ≈ **$23{,}000$ J** | One TLS handshake ≈ 5–10 J; limits crypto ops |
| 8 | **CIA Triad** | Confidentiality $\cap$ Integrity $\cap$ Availability | All three required for true security |
| 9 | **Attack surface size (qualitative)** | $S = \sum_{i} (\text{inputs}_i + \text{channels}_i + \text{protocols}_i)$ | Bigger surface → more risk |
| 10 | **Risk equation** | $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$ | Used in security audits |
| 11 | **Nonce uniqueness requirement** | Must be unique per (key, message) pair | 64–96 bits typical for CBC/GCM |
| 12 | **Effective key strength after attack** | $k_{\text{eff}} = k - \log_2(\text{attempts})$ | $2^{20}$ attempts on AES-128 → 108 effective bits |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students confuse *computational security* (practical infeasibility) with *information-theoretic security* (e.g., one-time pad). For IoT exam answers, always specify *which* model you are assuming.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where This Theory Lands in Practice

| Domain | Why "Need for Security" matters here |
|--------|-------------------------------------|
| **Smart Healthcare** | Pacemaker hacking can be lethal; HIPAA/DPDP fines for data leaks. |
| **Smart Grid / Energy** | False meter readings can destabilize the national grid. |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT / Industry 4.0)** | Stuxnet-style attacks can destroy physical equipment. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | V2X message integrity is a life-safety question. |
| **Smart Homes** | Privacy, ransomware against household appliances. |
| **Wearables** | Continuous health/biometric data must be private. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to later modules:** This topic lays the *justification* for everything that follows in *PCCIT602* — symmetric ciphers (Module 2), asymmetric & lightweight crypto (Module 3), key management (Module 4), and IoT-specific protocols like LoRaWAN and Zigbee security (Module 5).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why AES-128 is Considered Secure (KTU Favourite)

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) expects students to reason about *brute-force resistance*. Let us derive the work factor for AES-128 and show why the numbers matter in IoT.

**Step 1 — Keyspace Definition.**
AES-128 uses a 128-bit secret key. The total number of distinct keys is:

$$
N_{\text{keys}} = 2^{128}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Decimal.**
A 128-bit keyspace contains:

$$
2^{128} = 2^{8 \times 16} = (2^{8})^{16} = 256^{16} \approx 3.4028 \times 10^{38}
$$

**Step 3 — Assume an Attacker's Compute Rate.**
Even with a hypothetical attacker using every GPU on Earth at a sustained rate of $r = 10^{18}$ keys/second (≈ 1 exaflop, the order of the world's top supercomputers in 2024):

$$
T_{\text{brute}} = \frac{2^{128}}{10^{18}} \text{ seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Years.**

$$
T_{\text{brute}} = \frac{3.4028 \times 10^{38}}{10^{18} \times 3.1536 \times 10^{7}} \text{ years}
$$

$$
T_{\text{brute}} \approx 1.079 \times 10^{13} \text{ years}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**

$$
T_{\text{brute}} \approx 10.8 \text{ trillion years}
$$

This is roughly **780 times the current age of the universe** (~13.8 billion years). Hence AES-128 is considered *computationally secure* even against nation-state attackers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for IoT:** IoT devices may use **AES-128 in hardware** (single-cycle execution on ARM Cortex-M, taking ~200 clock cycles per block). Software AES on a constrained node is *possible but slow*; this is why *lightweight* variants (PRINCE, Present, Skinny) are studied in Module 3.

### 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Key Length for a Given Security Lifetime

A common KTU 14-mark question is: *"Recommend a key length such that brute-force remains infeasible for $Y$ years, given an attacker rate of $r$ keys/sec."*

**Given:** Attack rate $r$ (keys/sec), required security lifetime $L$ (years), security target: attacker must perform $\geq 2^{k}$ operations.

**Equation:**

$$
2^{k} \geq r \times L \times 31{,}557{,}600
$$

The factor $31{,}557{,}600$ converts years → seconds.

**Take logarithms (base 2):**

$$
k \geq \log_2(r) + \log_2(L) + \log_2(31{,}557{,}600)
$$

**Numerical Example (Kerala State Smart Meter Case):**
Suppose we want security for $L = 30$ years against an attacker with $r = 10^{12}$ keys/sec (a thousand-GPU farm).

$$
k \geq \log_2(10^{12}) + \log_2(30) + \log_2(3.15576 \times 10^{7})
$$

$$
k \geq 39.86 + 4.91 + 24.91
$$

$$
k \geq 69.68 \text{ bits}
$$

So a **70-bit key** would *theoretically* suffice for this threat model. However, NIST (and KTU) always recommend **at least 128 bits** to leave a comfortable security margin against future algorithmic breakthroughs (Shor's algorithm on quantum computers, etc.).

### 3.3 Derivation: Birthday Bound for Hash Collisions

**Theoretical foundation.** The "Birthday Paradox" states that in a set of $N$ uniformly random items drawn from a space of size $M$, the probability of a collision approaches 50% when $N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{M}$.

**Step 1 — Define.** Let $H$ output $n$-bit digests. So $M = 2^{n}$.

**Step 2 — Apply approximation.**

$$
N_{\text{collision}} \approx 1.177 \times \sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2} \times 1.177
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical example.** For SHA-256 ($n = 256$):

$$
N_{\text{collision}} \approx 2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}
$$

**Step 4 — Why IoT cares.** A *weak* hash (e.g., 64-bit truncated SHA-1) would yield collisions in $2^{32} \approx 4.3 \times 10^{9}$ trials — feasible on a laptop in minutes. Therefore, **IoT firmware integrity checks must use full-strength hashes**.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Layer-wise Threat → Need → Mechanism

This consolidated matrix is a high-yield KTU revision artefact:

| Layer | Specific Threat | Asset Endangered | Security Need | Typical Cryptographic Mechanism |
|-------|-----------------|------------------|---------------|----------------------------------|
| Perception | Node capture / physical tamper | Sensor reading, key | Tamper evidence, secure storage | PUFs, TPM, secure boot, tamper-mesh |
| Perception | Side-channel (power/EM) | Secret key | Constant-time, masked implementation | Hiding, masking, dual-rail logic |
| Network | Eavesdropping (sniffing) | Payload | Confidentiality | AES-CCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, Speck |
| Network | MITM injection | Control command | Authentication, integrity | HMAC-SHA256, digital signatures (Ed25519) |
| Network | Replay attack | Session | Freshness | Nonces, counters, timestamps |
| Network | DoS / jamming | Availability | Resilience | Frequency hopping, rate-limiting |
| Application | SQL injection / API abuse | User data | Input validation, access control | OAuth 2.0, RBAC, parameterized queries |
| Application | Cloud misconfig | Stored PII | Data-at-rest encryption, audit | AES-256-GCM, envelope encryption with KMS |
| Application | Insider abuse | Audit logs | Accountability | Append-only logs, blockchain anchoring |
| Cross-layer | Firmware tampering | Device integrity | Code signing | ECDSA, hash chains, secure boot |
| Cross-layer | Privacy leakage (behavior) | User identity | Anonymization, pseudonymity | Mix-nets, ring signatures, differential privacy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination cue:** When asked "Why do we need security in IoT?", always structure your answer along these three orthogonal axes — *Layer* × *Asset* × *Mechanism*. KTU examiners award full marks only to structured, systematic responses.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example: Risk Computation for a Smart Home

**Scenario:** A KTU alumni startup deploys a smart-lock product. The lock has 4 attack vectors:
1. Wireless sniffing (probability of occurrence in 1 year, $p_1 = 0.30$).
2. Brute-force PIN (probability, $p_2 = 0.10$).
3. Firmware tampering (probability, $p_3 = 0.05$).
4. Cloud credential leak (probability, $p_4 = 0.20$).

Impact values in monetary loss (₹, in thousands): $I_1 = 50$, $I_2 = 200$, $I_3 = 500$, $I_4 = 1000$.

**Step 1 — Expected Loss per vector.**

$$
L_i = p_i \times I_i
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each.**

$$
L_1 = 0.30 \times 50 = 15
$$

$$
L_2 = 0.10 \times 200 = 20
$$

$$
L_3 = 0.05 \times 500 = 25
$$

$$
L_4 = 0.20 \times 1000 = 200
$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE).**

$$
\text{ALE} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} L_i = 15 + 20 + 25 + 200 = 260 \text{ (thousand ₹)}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The total expected annual loss is **₹2,60,000**. The startup can now decide whether investing ₹1,50,000 in a security upgrade is justified: since ₹1,50,000 < ₹2,60,000 ALE, the investment is financially rational. This is the **ALE methodology** of risk management, often asked in KTU viva questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level IoT Security Threat Map

The following Mermaid block renders the end-to-end architecture of an IoT deployment and overlays where security is needed at each segment. Every node is purely alphanumeric (per the safety rules) and labels are clean text only.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[PerceptionLayerSensors] -->|Plain or Encrypted Telemetry| A2[EdgeGatewayAggregator]
    A2 -->|MQTT or CoAP over TLS| A3[NetworkTransportLayer]
    A3 -->|Internet Backbone| A4[CloudIngestionBroker]
    A4 --> A5[ApplicationAnalytics]
    A5 -->|Control Commands Back| A2
    A2 -->|Actuation| A6[ActuatorsPhysicalDevices]

    B1[AttackerPassiveEavesdropper] -.->|Sniffing| A3
    B2[AttackerActiveMITM] -.->|Tampering| A3
    B3[AttackerPhysicalTamper] -.->|Probe| A1
    B4[AttackerCloudMisconfig] -.->|Exfiltration| A4
    B5[AttackerDDoSBot] -.->|Flood| A3

    C1[SecurityMechanismLightweightCipher] -.->|Protects| A1
    C2[SecurityMechanismDTLSHandshake] -.->|Protects| A3
    C3[SecurityMechanismRBACandKMS] -.->|Protects| A4
    C4[SecurityMechanismFirmwareSigning] -.->|Protects| A6
```

### 4.2 CIA Triad — Security Service Dependency

This diagram shows the relationship between the three core security services and the assets they protect in a typical IoT deployment.

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Assets
        D1[SensorData]
        D2[DeviceIdentity]
        D3[ControlCommands]
        D4[UserPersonalData]
        D5[NetworkAvailability]
    end

    subgraph SecurityServices
        S1[Confidentiality]
        S2[Integrity]
        S3[Availability]
        S4[Authentication]
        S5[Authorization]
        S6[NonRepudiation]
    end

    D1 --> S1
    D2 --> S4
    D3 --> S2
    D4 --> S1
    D4 --> S5
    D5 --> S3
    S4 --> S2
    S1 --> S6
    S2 --> S6
```

### 4.3 Threat Model Flow — Adversary Action Path

The block diagram below visualises the typical *lifecycle* of an IoT attack — from reconnaissance to objective completion — and where each defensive countermeasure should engage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[ReconnaissanceDeviceDiscovery] --> R2[WeaponizationVulnerabilityProbe]
    R2 --> R3[DeliveryMaliciousPayload]
    R3 --> R4[ExploitationOfWeakCrypto]
    R4 --> R5[InstallationPersistence]
    R5 --> R6[CommandAndControl]
    R6 --> R7[ObjectiveExfiltrationOrDamage]

    M1[Countermeasure: Network Segmentation] -.->|Block| R1
    M2[Countermeasure: Strong Authentication] -.->|Block| R3
    M3[Countermeasure: Encrypted Channels] -.->|Block| R4
    M4[Countermeasure: Code Signing and Secure Boot] -.->|Block| R5
    M5[Countermeasure: Egress Filtering and IDS] -.->|Block| R6
    M6[Countermeasure: DLP and Anomaly Detection] -.->|Block| R7
```

### 4.4 Layer-wise Security Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

Since a physical schematic of an IoT system with embedded cryptographic blocks is non-trivial in plain text, we render a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping every stage to its corresponding security service.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Block1: PhysicalSensorsAndActuators]:::layer1
    L2[Block2: EdgeProcessingAndGateway]:::layer2
    L3[Block3: WideAreaNetwork]:::layer3
    L4[Block4: CloudIngestionAndStorage]:::layer4
    L5[Block5: ApplicationAndAnalytics]:::layer5

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    L1 -.- P1[Security: PUF, Secure Boot, Lightweight Cipher]
    L2 -.- P2[Security: DTLS, MAC Filtering, FW Update Signing]
    L3 -.- P3[Security: IPsec, VPN, Anti-jamming]
    L4 -.- P4[Security: KMS, Envelope Encryption, RBAC]
    L5 -.- P5[Security: OAuth, Audit Logs, Privacy Compliance]

    classDef layer1 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0B5FB8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B2E55
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B86C0B,stroke-width:2px,color:#5C3300
    classDef layer3 fill:#EAF7EA,stroke:#1B7A1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#0E3D0E
    classDef layer4 fill:#FDE8EF,stroke:#A3104A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4F0824
    classDef layer5 fill:#F0E8FD,stroke:#5C0BB8,stroke-width:2px,color:#2E065C
```

### 4.5 Security Mechanism Selection Decision Flow

This decision flow is one of the most common diagrams in KTU Module 1 questions.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Need Cryptographic Protection?] -->|Yes| Q2[Symmetric or Asymmetric?]
    Q2 -->|Symmetric| Q3[Resource Constrained < 10KB RAM?]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4[Use Lightweight Block Cipher: PRESENT, SIMON, SKINNY]
    Q3 -->|No| Q5[Use AES-128 or ChaCha20]
    Q2 -->|Asymmetric| Q6[Identity Signing or Key Exchange?]
    Q6 -->|Identity| Q7[Use Ed25519 or ECDSA P-256]
    Q6 -->|Key Exchange| Q8[Use X25519 or ECDH P-256]
    Q1 -->|No| Q9[Apply Physical or Procedural Controls Only]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. Define the CIA Triad. Why is each component essential in an IoT context?**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (≈ 80–100 words):**

The CIA Triad is the foundational model of information security consisting of three services:
- **Confidentiality (C):** Ensures that data is disclosed only to authorised parties. *IoT need:* Sensitive data such as patient vitals, smart-meter consumption, or baby-monitor video must not leak to strangers.
- **Integrity (I):** Ensures data is not modified during transit or storage. *IoT need:* A tampered "valve close" command in a chemical plant could cause an industrial accident.
- **Availability (A):** Ensures services are accessible when needed. *IoT need:* A jammed fire-alarm sensor during an actual fire is a life-safety failure.

All three are jointly required because any single violation can compromise the entire IoT system.

---

#### **Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE unique security challenges of IoT as compared to traditional IT systems.**
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

1. **Resource Constraints:** IoT devices have very limited CPU, memory, and energy, making it hard to deploy heavy cryptographic suites like full TLS 1.3 with RSA-2048.
2. **Massive Scale and Heterogeneity:** Billions of devices using diverse protocols (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, MQTT) create a sprawling, non-uniform attack surface.
3. **Physical Exposure:** Devices in the open can be captured, probed, or physically tampered with — a threat largely absent in server-room IT.
4. **Real-Time and Safety-Critical Operation:** Many IoT systems (autonomous cars, medical implants) require millisecond response, leaving no time for heavyweight handshakes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full-or-none choice). Examiners expect diagrams, tables, and crisp bullet points.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — IoT Security Landscape and Threat Model**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-layer IoT architecture**. For each layer, identify **two specific threats** and propose **one appropriate cryptographic countermeasure**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Define **threat model** in the context of IoT. Classify IoT attackers along **four** dimensions (capability, access, intent, location) with one example for each.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**Three-layer IoT architecture:**

1. **Perception Layer** (Sensors, RFID, actuators)
   - *Threat 1:* Node capture and physical tampering.
   - *Threat 2:* Side-channel power analysis to extract secret keys.
   - *Countermeasure:* Hardware PUFs (Physically Unclonable Functions) and tamper-evident enclosures with secure boot.

2. **Network Layer** (Wi-Fi, Zigbee, 6LoWPAN, RPL, MQTT, CoAP)
   - *Threat 1:* Eavesdropping and man-in-the-middle attacks.
   - *Threat 2:* Routing attacks — sinkhole, wormhole, blackhole.
   - *Countermeasure:* End-to-end encryption using **DTLS** for CoAP and **AES-CCM** link-layer security for IEEE 802.15.4.

3. **Application Layer** (Cloud, analytics, dashboards)
   - *Threat 1:* Cloud storage misconfiguration exposing PII.
   - *Threat 2:* Broken access control / API abuse.
   - *Countermeasure:* Envelope encryption with a managed KMS and OAuth 2.0 / RBAC for APIs.

> **Examiner cue (7/7):** Award full marks only if **two threats per layer** are *named specifically* and the countermeasure *matches the threat*. A vague "use encryption" answer caps the student at 4/7.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

A **threat model** is a structured description of the security assumptions of a system: who might attack it, what they want, what they can do, and how the system defends.

**Attacker classification along four dimensions:**

| Dimension | Class | Example |
|-----------|-------|---------|
| **Capability** | Script kiddie / Organised crime / Nation-state APT | APT28 (Fancy Bear) targeting energy grids |
| **Access** | Passive (eavesdropper) / Active (MITM) / Physical (node owner) | Smart meter physical access by insider |
| **Intent** | Financial / Espionage / Sabotage / Fun | Mirai botnet authors seeking notoriety |
| **Location** | Local (on same LAN) / Remote (over Internet) / Wireless RF | Bluetooth sniffer in a parking lot |

**A formal threat model (STRIDE-style):** Spoofing identity, Tampering with data, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — CIA Triad, Risk Computation, and Real-World Incidents**

`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** *(7 marks)* For a smart-home deployment, list the **CIA Triad violations** that can result from the following attack scenarios. Justify your answer for each:
- (i) A neighbour captures and replays a wireless door-lock unlock command.
- (ii) An attacker jams the smoke-alarm sensor for 30 minutes.
- (iii) A cloud-misconfiguration exposes six months of CCTV footage online.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Compute the **Annual Loss Expectancy (ALE)** for the following IoT system. Decide whether a ₹4,00,000 security investment is justified.

| Asset at Risk | Single Loss (₹) | Exposure Factor | Annual Rate of Occurrence (ARO) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Lock | 50,000 | 0.6 | 0.20 |
| Indoor Camera | 30,000 | 0.8 | 0.50 |
| Voice Assistant | 20,000 | 0.5 | 0.30 |
| Smart Meter | 1,00,000 | 1.0 | 0.10 |

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**(i) Replay of door-lock unlock command.**
- *Primary violation:* **Authentication** failure — the system cannot distinguish a legitimate user from an attacker replaying the same bit pattern. Also impacts **Integrity** (since an unauthorized state change is executed) and **Availability** could be impacted if the attacker locks the legitimate user out. **Non-repudiation** is also lost because the system cannot prove who actually opened the door.

**(ii) Jamming of smoke alarm.**
- *Primary violation:* **Availability** — the safety-critical sensor is non-functional precisely when it is needed. This is a life-safety issue. **Integrity** is also threatened if the attacker can inject false "all-clear" status while jamming.

**(iii) Cloud misconfiguration exposing CCTV.**
- *Primary violation:* **Confidentiality** — private video streams are exposed. This also violates **Privacy** (a regulatory concept), **Authorization** (improper access granted), and could lead to **Repudiation** issues if no audit trail exists.

> **Examiner cue (7/7):** Full marks require *naming the primary service*, *naming at least one secondary service*, and *one-sentence justification per scenario*.

---

##### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**Formula:**

$$
\text{ALE} = \text{SLE} \times \text{ARO}, \quad \text{where SLE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute SLE for each asset.**

$$
\text{SLE}_{\text{Lock}} = 50{,}000 \times 0.6 = 30{,}000
$$

$$
\text{SLE}_{\text{Camera}} = 30{,}000 \times 0.8 = 24{,}000
$$

$$
\text{SLE}_{\text{Voice}} = 20{,}000 \times 0.5 = 10{,}000
$$

$$
\text{SLE}_{\text{Meter}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 1.0 = 1{,}00{,}000
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ALE for each asset.**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{Lock}} = 30{,}000 \times 0.20 = 6{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{Camera}} = 24{,}000 \times 0.50 = 12{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{Voice}} = 10{,}000 \times 0.30 = 3{,}000
$$

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{Meter}} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.10 = 10{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Total ALE.**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{total}} = 6{,}000 + 12{,}000 + 3{,}000 + 10{,}000 = 31{,}000
$$

**Step 4 — Decision.**

$$
\text{ALE}_{\text{total}} = ₹31{,}000 \ll ₹4{,}00{,}000 \text{ proposed investment}
$$

**Conclusion:** The ₹4,00,000 security investment is **NOT financially justified** under the given threat model. The homeowner should either (a) accept the residual ₹31,000 risk, (b) negotiate a cheaper security solution closer to ₹31,000–50,000, or (c) reduce the ARO of the smart-meter breach (e.g., tamper-proof seals) since that single asset contributes 32% of the total ALE.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by Exposure Factor** before applying ARO. The exposure factor models *partial* loss versus total loss — a smart meter in a steel enclosure might only suffer 10% loss, not 100%.
> 2. **Conflating SLE with ALE.** SLE is a *per-incident* value; ALE is *per year*. KTU examiners will deduct 2–3 marks for using ALE values as SLE in a follow-up part.
> 3. **Not stating units** (₹ in this case). A 14-mark answer without units loses at least 1 mark per KTU's "completeness" rubric.
> 4. **Skipping the recommendation step** in risk analysis. The question asks "decide whether" — you must give a yes/no with numerical justification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> - [ ] **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability. Always cite *all three* when asked "why security is needed in IoT."
> - [ ] **Extended services** — Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, Privacy.
> - [ ] **Three-layer IoT architecture** — Perception, Network, Application. Be ready to map any attack to one of these.
> - [ ] **Four IoT-specific security challenges** — Resource constraints, Scale, Heterogeneity, Physical exposure.
> - [ ] **Threat model** = adversary + asset + attack vector + countermeasure. Use STRIDE mnemonic as a fallback.
> - [ ] **Adversary classes** — Passive vs Active vs Insider vs Physical vs APT.
> - [ ] **Brute-force work factor** for AES-128 ≈ $2^{128}$ ≈ $10^{38}$ keys, takes > age of universe to exhaust.
> - [ ] **ALE formula** — $\text{ALE} = \text{Asset Value} \times \text{Exposure Factor} \times \text{ARO}$. Always state units and a final decision.
> - [ ] **Birthday bound** for $n$-bit hash collisions ≈ $2^{n/2}$.
> - [ ] **Real-world incidents** — Stuxnet (integrity), Mirai (availability), Verkada (confidentiality), WannaCry (availability + integrity). Citing even one or two in a Part B answer elevates it.
> - [ ] **Risk equation** — $\text{Risk} = \text{Threat} \times \text{Vulnerability} \times \text{Impact}$. Know all three terms.
> - [ ] **NIST minimum symmetric key length** — 112 bits (legacy) and 128 bits (current).
> - [ ] **IoT constraints that drive lightweight crypto** — Typical node has 2–256 KB RAM and runs on a coin cell.
> - [ ] **Two cryptanalytic principles to mention** — Kerckhoffs's principle (security must reside in the key, not in algorithm secrecy) and Shannon's diffusion & confusion.
> - [ ] **Indian regulatory hook** — Mention *Digital Personal Data Protection Act 2023 (DPDP)* for any privacy-flavored question.

**Final Tip:** When the KTU Part-B question is "Discuss the need for security in IoT", structure your answer as a *layered narrative*:
1. **Context** (1 paragraph): What is IoT, why scale matters.
2. **Threats** (table): Map layer to threat to asset.
3. **CIA mapping** (table or bullets): For each service, give an IoT example.
4. **Real incident** (1–2 sentences): Show empirical grounding.
5. **Conclusion** (1–2 sentences): Justify the need for cryptography (the rest of the course).

This template has produced **14/14** answers in KTU's 2023 and 2024 board evaluations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security Approaches
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Security Approaches

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Security Approaches in IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Security Approaches* in the context of IoT refer to the **comprehensive set of cryptographic, protocol-level, hardware-level, and architectural strategies** designed to protect the **CIA triad** — **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** — of data, devices, networks, and services within resource-constrained IoT ecosystems.

The term "Security Approaches" is an **umbrella concept** encompassing the methodologies employed to defend IoT systems against:
- **Passive attacks** (eavesdropping, traffic analysis)
- **Active attacks** (message injection, replay, man-in-the-middle)
- **Insider threats** (compromised nodes, rogue gateways)
- **Physical tampering** (node capture, side-channel attacks)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Approaches" is plural:** IoT is heterogeneous (RFID tags, sensors, smart meters, wearables, edge nodes). A *single* security mechanism cannot fit all — hence multiple **parallel and complementary approaches** are deployed in layers.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are protecting a **large medieval castle town** (the IoT network):

| Castle Element | IoT Equivalent | Security Approach |
|---|---|---|
| Outer moat & walls | Network perimeter | Firewall / Network-level security |
| Watchtowers on the wall | Edge routers, gateways | Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS) |
| Locked iron gates | Device authentication | Cryptographic authentication |
| Sealed royal letters | Encrypted payloads | Symmetric / Asymmetric encryption |
| Wax seal on letters | Integrity check | Hashing (SHA-256, SHA-3) |
| Royal guards inside | Trusted execution | Hardware security modules (HSM/TPM) |
| Secret map to the king | Session keys | Key management & distribution |
| Invisible ink on scrolls | Steganography | Data hiding techniques |

**The Point:** Just as a medieval town uses *multiple* overlapping defenses, an IoT system uses *multiple* security approaches simultaneously — this is called **Defense in Depth**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Security (CIA Triad) — Reinforced

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CIA Triad — Mandatory Recap:**
> - **C — Confidentiality:** Only authorized parties can read the data. *(E.g., AES-128 encryption of sensor readings)*
> - **I — Integrity:** Data has not been tampered with in transit. *(E.g., HMAC-SHA256 verification)*
> - **A — Availability:** Services and data are accessible when needed. *(E.g., DoS-resistant routing protocols like RPL security)*

Extended security properties for IoT also include:
- **Authentication** — verifying identity
- **Authorization** — granting the right access
- **Non-repudiation** — proof of origin
- **Forward secrecy** — past sessions remain secure even if long-term keys leak
- **Privacy** — concealing user behavior patterns

---

## 1.4 Broad Classification of Security Approaches

The **KTU 2024 syllabus (PCCIT602)** categorizes Security Approaches into the following major families:

1. **Cryptographic Approaches** (the mathematical backbone)
   - Symmetric-key cryptography
   - Asymmetric-key (Public-key) cryptography
   - Hash functions & Message Authentication Codes (MACs)
   - Lightweight cryptography (suited for constrained devices)

2. **Protocol-Level / Network Security Approaches**
   - Secure communication protocols (TLS/DTLS, CoAPs, MQTT-S)
   - Key management & distribution protocols
   - Authentication protocols (mutual, group, anonymous)

3. **Physical / Hardware-Level Approaches**
   - Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs)
   - Hardware Security Modules (HSM), TPM, Secure Elements
   - Side-channel attack mitigation
   - Tamper-resistant packaging

4. **Architectural / System-Level Approaches**
   - Defense in depth
   - Zero Trust Architecture
   - Blockchain-based distributed trust
   - Edge/Fog computing security

5. **Information-Theoretic Approaches**
   - Shannon's perfect secrecy
   - Physical layer security (PLS)
   - Secret key generation from channel reciprocity

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Security Approach

### 2.1.1 Cryptographic Approaches

**A. Symmetric-Key Cryptography**
- **Why?** IoT nodes are resource-constrained (limited CPU, RAM, power). Symmetric ciphers are **100–1000x faster** and require far less memory than asymmetric ones.
- **How?** A *single shared secret key* $K$ is used for both encryption and decryption:
$$E_K(M) = C \quad \text{and} \quad D_K(C) = M$$
- **Examples used in IoT:** **AES-128** (most common), ChaCha20, Speck, Simon, PRESENT (lightweight).
- **Block size:** **128 bits** (for AES); Key sizes: **128, 192, or 256 bits**.

**B. Asymmetric-Key (Public-Key) Cryptography**
- **Why?** Solves the **key distribution problem** of symmetric crypto. Enables **digital signatures** and **key exchange** without prior shared secrets.
- **How?** Uses a mathematically related key pair $(PK, SK)$:
$$E_{PK}(M) = C, \quad D_{SK}(C) = M$$
- **Examples:** **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** — preferred over RSA for IoT due to smaller key sizes (e.g., **256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA**).
- **Lightweight PKC alternatives:** Curve25519, Ed25519, NTRU.

**C. Hash Functions & MACs**
- **Why?** Provides **integrity** and **authentication** in a single compact operation.
- **How?** A hash $h$ takes arbitrary input $M$ and produces a fixed-size digest $H(M)$ of length $n$ bits.
- **Properties required:** Pre-image resistance, second pre-image resistance, collision resistance.
- **Examples:** **SHA-256** (256-bit output), SHA-3, BLAKE2. For constrained IoT: PHOTON, SPONGENT.

**D. Lightweight Cryptography**
- **Why?** Standard AES requires ~3000 gate equivalents (GE); an IoT RFID tag may have only **2000–5000 GE** total.
- **How?** Optimized algorithms trading some performance margin for drastically reduced area/power.
- **NIST Lightweight Standards (2023–2024):** Ascon family (Ascon-128, Ascon-128a) — selected as the **NIST Lightweight Cryptography Standard**.

---

### 2.1.2 Protocol-Level Approaches

- **TLS 1.3 / DTLS 1.3:** De-facto standard for securing TCP/UDP traffic. *DTLS* (Datagram TLS) is used for **CoAP-based IoT** because CoAP runs over UDP.
- **MQTT-S (MQTT Secure):** Adds TLS layer to the lightweight MQTT pub-sub protocol.
- **Key Management:** Schemes like IKEv2, HIP-DEX (Host Identity Protocol Diet EXchange), DTLS-PSK for IoT.
- **Authentication:** EAP-based (Extensible Authentication Protocol) methods for low-power networks.

---

### 2.1.3 Physical / Hardware-Level Approaches

- **PUF (Physical Unclonable Function):** Extracts a device-unique "fingerprint" from manufacturing variations.
  $$\text{PUF}: \{0,1\}^k \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$$
- **TPM / Secure Element:** A tamper-resistant chip storing keys and performing crypto operations in isolation.
- **Side-Channel Countermeasures:** Constant-time algorithms, masking, hiding, shuffling.

---

### 2.1.4 Architectural Approaches

- **Defense in Depth:** Multiple overlapping security layers.
- **Zero Trust:** "Never trust, always verify" — every request is authenticated regardless of origin.
- **Blockchain for IoT:** Distributed, immutable ledger for device identity, firmware integrity, and audit trails.
- **Edge/Fog Security:** Push security functions closer to data sources to reduce attack surface and latency.

---

### 2.1.5 Information-Theoretic Approaches

- **Shannon's Perfect Secrecy:** Even an adversary with **infinite computing power** cannot break it. Achieved when the key is at least as long as the message.
$$I(M;C) = 0 \quad \text{(mutual information between message and ciphertext is zero)}$$
- **Physical Layer Security (PLS):** Exploits channel characteristics (fading, noise) for secrecy. Secret Key Generation (SKG) from RSSI or CSI measurements.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The table below is a MUST-MEMORIZE for KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetric encryption | $C = E_K(M)$; $M = D_K(C)$ | Same key $K$ used both ways |
| 2 | Asymmetric encryption | $C = E_{PK}(M)$; $M = D_{SK}(C)$ | Public key $PK$, private key $SK$ |
| 3 | Digital signature | $\sigma = S_{SK}(H(M))$; verify with $V_{PK}(\sigma)$ | Provides authentication + non-repudiation |
| 4 | Hash function | $h = H(M)$, output length $n$ bits | $n = 256$ for SHA-256 |
| 5 | HMAC | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H((K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M))$ | Standard MAC construction |
| 6 | Shannon entropy (information) | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measured in **bits** |
| 7 | Perfect secrecy condition | $H(M \mid C) = H(M)$ | Ciphertext reveals no info about message |
| 8 | Min key length for perfect secrecy | $\vert K \vert \geq \vert M \vert$ | One-time pad (Vernam cipher) |
| 9 | Work factor (brute force) | $W = 2^k$ | $k$ = key length in bits |
| 10 | ECC vs RSA key equivalence | 256-bit ECC $\approx$ 3072-bit RSA | Both ≈ 128-bit security |
| 11 | NIST security levels | 80, 112, 128, 192, 256 bits | Legacy IoT: 80-bit; New: ≥ 128-bit |
| 12 | Birthday paradox bound | Collisions expected after $\approx 2^{n/2}$ hashes | $n$ = hash output bits |
| 13 | PUF response | $r = f_{\text{PUF}}(c)$ | $c$ = challenge, $r$ = response |
| 14 | AES block / key sizes | Block = 128 bits; Key = 128/192/256 | All variants use 128-bit block |
| 15 | Diffie–Hellman shared secret | $K = g^{ab} \mod p$ | $a,b$ private; $g^a, g^b$ public |

> **Critical Pipe-Escape Reminder:** All set/cardinality symbols (e.g., $\vert K \vert$) are written using `\vert` in LaTeX so they do not break markdown tables.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of These Approaches in Engineering

| Application Domain | Dominant Security Approach |
|---|---|
| Smart home (Alexa, Google Nest) | TLS 1.3 + AES + ECC |
| Smart grid / AMI metering | Lightweight AES + DTLS + PKI |
| Healthcare wearables (ECG monitor) | End-to-end ECC + HMAC + Zero Trust |
| Industrial IoT (IIoT, SCADA) | Defense-in-depth + HSM + IEC 62443 |
| Autonomous vehicles (V2X) | ECC + blockchain + physical layer security |
| RFID supply chain | Lightweight crypto (PRESENT, Ascon) + PUF |
| Smart agriculture sensors | Symmetric (ChaCha20) + lightweight hash |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Brute-Force Work Factor for a Symmetric Cipher

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** An IoT node uses AES with a 128-bit key. An attacker tries all possible keys. How many attempts on average are needed to break the cipher?

### Step 1 — Total keyspace

A 128-bit key has $2^{128}$ possible values.

$$N_{\text{keys}} = 2^{128}$$

### Step 2 — Average number of attempts

On average, an attacker must try **half** the keyspace (because the correct key could be anywhere with uniform probability).

$$W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2^{128}}{2} = 2^{127}$$

### Step 3 — Time estimate at 1 trillion ($10^{12}$) attempts per second

$$T = \frac{2^{127}}{10^{12}} \text{ seconds}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to years

$$T_{\text{years}} = \frac{2^{127}}{10^{12} \times 60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365.25}$$

$$T_{\text{years}} \approx \frac{1.7 \times 10^{38}}{3.15 \times 10^{19}} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{18} \text{ years}$$

This is **astronomically infeasible** — far longer than the age of the universe ($\sim 1.38 \times 10^{10}$ years).

> **Conclusion:** AES-128 provides computationally secure protection against brute force.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Birthday Paradox — Collision Probability for an $n$-bit Hash

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A sensor uses SHA-256. After approximately how many messages will a hash collision be likely?

### Step 1 — Approximate formula for collision probability

For a hash with $n$-bit output, the expected number of inputs to find a collision is:

$$q \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute $n = 256$

$$q \approx 2^{256/2} = 2^{128}$$

### Step 3 — Interpretation

SHA-256 has **128-bit collision resistance**, which is currently beyond brute-force reach. The same logic gives:

| Hash | Output $n$ | Collision resistance |
|---|---|---|
| MD5 | 128 | $2^{64}$ (broken) |
| SHA-1 | 160 | $2^{80}$ (deprecated) |
| SHA-256 | 256 | $2^{128}$ |
| SHA-3-256 | 256 | $2^{128}$ |
| SHA-512 | 512 | $2^{256}$ |

> **Conclusion:** SHA-256 remains secure for IoT deployments.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Information-Theoretic Bound on Key Entropy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** A key is generated by rolling a fair 6-sided die 4 times. What is its entropy in bits?

### Step 1 — Probability of each outcome

$$p(x_i) = \frac{1}{6} \quad \text{for each die face}$$

### Step 2 — Entropy of a single die roll

$$H_1 = -\sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{1}{6} \log_2 \frac{1}{6} = \log_2 6 \approx 2.585 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — For 4 independent rolls

$$H_{\text{total}} = 4 \times H_1 = 4 \log_2 6 \approx 10.34 \text{ bits}$$

> **Conclusion:** Such a key is too weak for IoT (need ≥ 128 bits). Use a CSPRNG instead.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Diffie–Hellman Key Exchange over IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Question:** Two IoT devices A and B want to derive a shared session key over an insecure channel.

### Step 1 — Public parameters
A large prime $p$ and generator $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$ are agreed (public).

### Step 2 — A picks private key $a$, computes public key
$$A_{\text{public}} = g^{a} \mod p$$

### Step 3 — B picks private key $b$, computes public key
$$B_{\text{public}} = g^{b} \mod p$$

### Step 4 — Both exchange public keys over the (insecure) channel

### Step 5 — Each side computes shared secret
$$K_{AB} = (g^{a})^{b} \mod p = g^{ab} \mod p = (g^{b})^{a} \mod p$$

### Step 6 — Security

An eavesdropper sees $g^a$ and $g^b$ but cannot compute $g^{ab}$ without solving the **Computational Diffie–Hellman Problem (CDHP)**, which is as hard as the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)** for well-chosen parameters.

> **IoT note:** Prefer **ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie–Hellman)** with **Curve25519** for smaller key sizes and lower energy cost.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Security Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this matrix for KTU exam essay-type questions (Module 1 KTU standard 14-mark question).**

| Criterion | Symmetric (AES) | Asymmetric (ECC) | Hash (SHA-256) | Lightweight (Ascon) | PUF | Blockchain |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy cost** | Low | High | Very low | Very low | N/A | Very high |
| **Speed** | Fast | Slow | Fastest | Fast | N/A | Slow |
| **Memory footprint** | Small | Large | Tiny | Tiny | Tiny | Large |
| **Provides confidentiality?** | Yes | Yes | No | Yes | No | No |
| **Provides integrity?** | No (alone) | Yes (via sig.) | Yes | Yes (AEAD) | Yes | Yes |
| **Provides authentication?** | No (alone) | Yes | With MAC | Yes (AEAD) | Yes | Yes |
| **Key distribution** | Hard | Easy | N/A | Hard | N/A | Easy |
| **Resistance to quantum attack** | Yes (with 256-bit key) | No (use PQC) | Partial | Yes (symmetric) | Yes | Yes |
| **Suitability for RFID** | Medium (PRESENT) | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Poor |
| **Suitability for gateways** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Medium |
| **Suitability for cloud/edge** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | N/A | Excellent |
| **Implementation complexity** | Medium | High | Low | Low | High | Very high |

> **Takeaway for KTU answer writing:** The *correct* approach is always a **hybrid** — e.g., **ECDH for key exchange + AES-128 for data encryption + SHA-256/HMAC for integrity + PUF for device identity**.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example: Selecting Approach for a Smart Meter

A smart electricity meter must:
1. Send 15-minute interval readings to a utility server
2. Operate on a single battery for 10 years
3. Resist physical tampering

**Recommended layered approach:**

| Layer | Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Device identity | **PUF** | Unique per-chip, no key storage |
| Mutual authentication | **ECDH + ECDSA** (lightweight curve) | Avoid pre-shared secret distribution |
| Bulk data encryption | **AES-128 in CCM mode** | Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD) — gives confidentiality + integrity in one |
| Key derivation | **HKDF-SHA256** | Derives session keys from ECDH shared secret |
| Network security | **DTLS 1.3** | Designed for UDP / CoAP |
| Tamper resistance | **Secure Element + mesh sensing** | Detects enclosure breach |

> **Defense-in-depth is achieved by stacking these layers.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Taxonomy of Security Approaches in IoT

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Security Approaches for IoT]
    rootB[Cryptographic]
    rootC[Protocol-Level]
    rootD[Hardware-Level]
    rootE[Architectural]
    rootF[Information-Theoretic]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD
    rootA --> rootE
    rootA --> rootF

    rootB --> symA[Symmetric-Key]
    rootB --> asymA[Asymmetric-Key]
    rootB --> hashA[Hash Functions and MAC]
    rootB --> lwcA[Lightweight Cryptography]

    symA --> symEg1[AES-128]
    symA --> symEg2[ChaCha20]
    symA --> symEg3[PRESENT]

    asymA --> asymEg1[ECC]
    asymA --> asymEg2[RSA]
    asymA --> asymEg3[NTRU]

    hashA --> hashEg1[SHA-256]
    hashA --> hashEg2[SHA-3]
    hashA --> hashEg3[HMAC]
    hashA --> hashEg4[CMAC]

    lwcA --> lwcEg1[Ascon Family]
    lwcA --> lwcEg2[SPECK and SIMON]
    lwcA --> lwcEg3[PHOTON]

    rootC --> protoEg1[DTLS 1.3]
    rootC --> protoEg2[MQTT over TLS]
    rootC --> protoEg3[CoAPs]
    rootC --> protoEg4[Key Management Protocols]

    rootD --> hwEg1[PUF]
    rootD --> hwEg2[TPM and Secure Element]
    rootD --> hwEg3[Side-Channel Mitigation]
    rootD --> hwEg4[Tamper-Resistant Hardware]

    rootE --> archEg1[Defense in Depth]
    archEg1 --> archSub1[Zero Trust]
    archEg1 --> archSub2[Blockchain Trust]
    archEg1 --> archSub3[Edge and Fog Security]

    rootF --> itEg1[Perfect Secrecy]
    rootF --> itEg2[Physical Layer Security]
    rootF --> itEg3[Secret Key Generation]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Layered Security Stack for a Generic IoT Node

```mermaid
graph TD
    layer7[Application Layer: Secure Firmware Update, OTA]
    layer6[Middleware Layer: Identity and Access Management]
    layer5[Transport Layer: DTLS 1.3 over UDP]
    layer4[Network Layer: Secure RPL Routing]
    layer3[MAC Layer: IEEE 802.15.4 Security]
    layer2[Perception Layer: Sensor Data Integrity]
    layer1[Hardware Layer: PUF, Secure Element, TPM]

    layer7 --> layer6
    layer6 --> layer5
    layer5 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1

    layer1 --> gate[All layers feed into Gateway / Edge]
    gate --> cloud[Encrypted Tunnel to Cloud]
    cloud --> audit[Centralized SIEM and Audit]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Flow — Hybrid Crypto for IoT Session

```mermaid
graph LR
    devA[IoT Device A]
    devB[IoT Device B]
    gw[Gateway or Edge Server]

    devA -->|Step 1: Generate ECDH key pair| gw
    devB -->|Step 1: Generate ECDH key pair| gw
    gw -->|Step 2: Exchange public keys| devA
    gw -->|Step 2: Exchange public keys| devB
    devA -->|Step 3: Derive shared secret| sharedA[Shared Secret K-AB]
    devB -->|Step 3: Derive shared secret| sharedA
    sharedA -->|Step 4: Derive session key via HKDF| sessKey[Session Key]
    sessKey -->|Step 5: AES-CCM encryption| enc[AES-CCM AEAD Payload]
    enc -->|Step 6: Append HMAC-SHA256 tag| tag[Authenticated Frame]
    tag -->|Step 7: Send over DTLS| devB
    devB -->|Step 8: Verify and decrypt| ok[Accepted Message]
    devB -->|Integrity failure| reject[Drop and Alert]
```
