<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
**Define the term "Security Approaches" in the context of IoT. List any four major categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Security approaches in IoT refer to the set of cryptographic, protocol-level, hardware-level, architectural, and information-theoretic strategies used to protect the **CIA triad** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability) of IoT data, devices, and services.

Four major categories:
1. **Cryptographic approaches** — AES, ECC, SHA-256, lightweight crypto
2. **Protocol-level approaches** — DTLS 1.3, CoAPs, MQTT-S
3. **Hardware-level approaches** — PUF, TPM, Secure Element
4. **Architectural approaches** — Defense in depth, Zero Trust, Blockchain

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
**What is the role of a Physical Unclonable Function (PUF) in IoT security? Mention one advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A PUF is a hardware security primitive that derives a device-unique secret from **uncontrollable manufacturing variations** in the chip's physical microstructure. It maps a **challenge** $c$ to a unique **response** $r = f_{\text{PUF}}(c)$.

**Role:** Provides **device identity** and **secret key generation** without storing keys in non-volatile memory.

**Advantage:** *Tamper-evident* — physical attacks alter the microstructure and change the PUF response, instantly invalidating the secret.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Model)]**
> **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the major categories of security approaches in IoT. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Compare symmetric and asymmetric cryptography with respect to **key size, speed, energy, and key distribution** in IoT. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The major categories of Security Approaches in IoT are:

1. **Cryptographic Approaches** — Mathematical primitives:
   - **Symmetric:** AES, ChaCha20
   - **Asymmetric:** ECC, RSA, NTRU
   - **Hashing:** SHA-256, SHA-3, HMAC
   - **Lightweight:** Ascon, PRESENT, SPECK

2. **Protocol-Level Approaches** — Securing communication channels:
   - DTLS 1.3 (for UDP / CoAP)
   - MQTT-S (for pub/sub)
   - IKEv2, HIP-DEX (for key exchange)

3. **Hardware / Physical Approaches** — Root-of-trust at silicon level:
   - PUF (Physical Unclonable Function)
   - TPM, Secure Element, HSM
   - Side-channel attack mitigation

4. **Architectural Approaches** — System-level defense:
   - **Defense in depth** (multiple layers)
   - **Zero Trust** (verify every request)
   - **Blockchain** (decentralized trust)
   - **Edge / Fog security**

5. **Information-Theoretic Approaches** — Mathematically unbreakable:
   - **Perfect secrecy** (one-time pad)
   - **Physical Layer Security (PLS)** — uses channel reciprocity
   - **Secret Key Generation (SKG)** from RSSI/CSI

*(Refer to Section 4.1 for the supporting taxonomy diagram.)* ✓

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Parameter | Symmetric (AES-128) | Asymmetric (ECC-256) |
|---|---|---|
| **Key size** | 128 bits | 256 bits (≈ 3072-bit RSA equivalent) |
| **Speed** | Very fast (≈ 100–1000x faster) | Slow (heavy modular arithmetic) |
| **Energy per op** | ~1 µJ/block | ~10 mJ/signature |
| **Key distribution** | Hard (must share secretly) | Easy (public key is public) |
| **Key count** | $O(n^2)$ for $n$ devices | $O(n)$ — each device has 1 pair |
| **Suitability for IoT** | Excellent for bulk data | Excellent for key exchange/auth |
| **Algorithms** | AES, ChaCha20, PRESENT | ECDH, ECDSA, Ed25519 |
| **Memory** | ~3000 GE (AES HW) | ~10,000+ GE (ECC HW) |

> **Conclusion:** Modern IoT systems use a **hybrid approach** — ECC for authentication and key exchange, AES for bulk data encryption, HMAC/SHA-256 for integrity.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **(a)** Explain the concept of **Defense in Depth** in IoT security with reference to a smart-home deployment. *(7 marks)*
> **(b)** Describe the role of **lightweight cryptography** in IoT. Discuss the **Ascon** family as the NIST lightweight standard. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Defense in Depth** is the principle of using **multiple overlapping security layers** so that failure of one layer does not compromise the entire system.

**Smart-Home Deployment — Layered Approach:**

| Layer | Component | Security Approach |
|---|---|---|
| 1. **Hardware (Perception)** | Smart bulb MCU | **PUF** for unique ID; secure boot |
| 2. **Device (Edge)** | Camera, door lock | **AES-CCM** for video stream encryption |
| 3. **Network** | Wi-Fi / Zigbee gateway | **DTLS 1.3**, **WPA3** |
| 4. **Application** | Mobile app → cloud | **TLS 1.3**, **OAuth 2.0** |
| 5. **Cloud / Backend** | Server farm | **HSM** for key storage, **RBAC** |
| 6. **Audit / Monitoring** | SIEM, anomaly detection | **ML-based IDS** |

**Why it works:**
- A camera with weak firmware (Layer 2) is still safe at the network (Layer 3) and app (Layer 4).
- An attacker must defeat **all** layers — exponentially raising attack cost.
- Aligns with **Zero Trust** philosophy: every layer authenticates independently.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Why Lightweight Cryptography?**

Standard cryptographic algorithms (AES, SHA-256) were designed for PCs/servers. Many IoT devices have:
- **Limited memory** (often < 32 KB RAM)
- **Low power** (battery-operated for years)
- **Small silicon area** (RFID tags may have **2000–5000 gate equivalents**)

Lightweight crypto offers **tuned trade-offs** — slightly less margin on security parameters, but **drastically reduced area, power, and latency**.

**The Ascon Family (NIST LWC Standard, Feb 2023):**

| Algorithm | Type | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Ascon-128 | AEAD | 128-bit key, 128-bit nonce, 128-bit tag |
| Ascon-128a | AEAD (faster) | 128-bit key, 128-bit nonce, 128-bit tag |
| Ascon-Hash | Hash | 256-bit digest |
| Ascon-Xof | XOF (extendable output) | Variable |

**Key properties:**
- Designed for **authenticated encryption with associated data (AEAD)** and **hashing**
- Resistant to **side-channel attacks**
- Compact hardware (≈ **3000 GE**)
- Low energy and short latency — ideal for **RFID, smart sensors, medical implants**

> **Conclusion:** Ascon is the de-facto **NIST Lightweight Cryptography Standard** for IoT post-2023.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU PCCIT602 Module 1:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Security Approaches" with a single algorithm** — It is an *umbrella term*. Examiners expect a *taxonomy*, not just a description of AES.
> 2. **Omitting the IoT-specific constraint** — Always mention *resource constraints* (energy, memory, area) when justifying an approach.
> 3. **Forgetting the CIA triad** — Frame every approach in terms of which pillar(s) of CIA it supports.
> 4. **No real-world example** — KTU examiners award extra marks for an *application scenario* (smart meter, healthcare, smart home).
> 5. **Confusing HMAC with digital signatures** — HMAC = symmetric integrity; Signature = asymmetric + non-repudiation.
> 6. **Skipping the diagram** — A well-labeled taxonomy diagram is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
> 7. **Writing obsolete crypto** — Avoid recommending MD5, SHA-1, DES, or RSA-1024. They are *broken* or *deprecated*.
> 8. **Missing quantum note (asked in recent papers)** — Mention **post-quantum cryptography (PQC)** and NIST PQC winners (Kyber, Dilithium) for the "future" angle.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision — go through this list 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **Security Approaches** = umbrella term for all methods to protect IoT CIA + authentication + privacy.
- ☐ Five families: **Cryptographic, Protocol, Hardware, Architectural, Information-Theoretic.**
- ☐ **CIA Triad** — Confidentiality, Integrity, Availability.
- ☐ **Symmetric** (AES-128) = fast, low energy, hard key distribution.
- ☐ **Asymmetric** (ECC-256) = slower, solves key distribution, supports digital signatures.
- ☐ **256-bit ECC ≈ 3072-bit RSA ≈ 128-bit security.**
- ☐ **SHA-256** → 256-bit digest, 128-bit collision resistance.
- ☐ **HMAC** = keyed hash for integrity + authentication.
- ☐ **Lightweight crypto:** Ascon family (NIST LWC 2023 standard) for constrained IoT.
- ☐ **PUF** = hardware fingerprint for device identity; no key storage needed.
- ☐ **TPM / Secure Element / HSM** = tamper-resistant crypto storage/processing.
- ☐ **DTLS 1.3** = standard for CoAP/UDP-based IoT.
- ☐ **TLS 1.3** = standard for HTTP/MQTT-based IoT.
- ☐ **Defense in Depth** = multiple overlapping security layers.
- ☐ **Zero Trust** = verify every request, no implicit trust.
- ☐ **Perfect Secrecy (Shannon):** $H(M \mid C) = H(M)$; key must be $\geq$ message length.
- ☐ **One-Time Pad (Vernam)** is the only provably perfectly secret cipher.
- ☐ **Physical Layer Security** = exploits channel characteristics (RSSI, CSI) for key generation.
- ☐ **Blockchain** = decentralized trust for IoT device identity and firmware audit.
- ☐ **Work factor (brute force) = $2^k$** for a $k$-bit key; average attempts = $2^{k-1}$.
- ☐ **NIST security levels:** 80, 112, 128, 192, 256 bits. **New IoT designs use ≥ 128-bit.**
- ☐ **Quantum threat:** ECC and RSA will be broken by Shor's algorithm — use **PQC (Kyber, Dilithium)**.
- ☐ **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — taxonomy / layered stack earns easy marks.
- ☐ **Always mention an IoT use-case** in your answer — smart home, smart meter, healthcare, IIoT.

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE!** 🎓

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles of Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principles of Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Information Security?

**Information Security** is the discipline of protecting information by mitigating risks. It involves preventing unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, inspection, recording, or destruction of data. In the context of **IoT (Internet of Things)**, security extends beyond traditional computing to encompass resource-constrained devices, wireless communication, and large-scale distributed ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Module 1 Highlight):**
> Information Security is the preservation of **Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability** of information, along with additional properties such as **Authentication, Authorization, Non-repudiation, Accountability, and Privacy** — collectively known as the *Security Goals* or *Security Services*.

---

## 1.2 The CIA Triad — The Three Pillars of Security

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model for information security. Every security policy, mechanism, and protocol in IoT cryptography is designed to protect one or more of these three pillars.

| Pillar | Technical Definition | IoT Implication |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Ensuring that data is accessible only to those authorized to view it. | Sensor data, health metrics, and device credentials must not leak to attackers. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring that data is not altered or tampered with during storage or transit. | Firmware updates and command packets must be verified before execution. |
| **Availability** | Ensuring that systems and data are accessible when needed by legitimate users. | Critical IoT systems (e.g., medical devices, smart grid) must remain operational under attack. |

### 🔐 Conceptual Analogy — The Bank Vault Model

Imagine a **bank vault** as your IoT system:
- **Confidentiality** → The vault's **thick steel door** with a combination lock. Only authorized personnel (the data owner) can open it and view the contents.
- **Integrity** → The **tamper-evident seal** on the vault door. If anyone breaks in or alters the contents, the seal is broken, and the breach is detectable.
- **Availability** → The **24/7 power supply** and backup generators that keep the vault operational even during power failures or attacks (like DDoS).

In IoT, the "vault" might be a smart home, a wearable health device, or an industrial sensor network — but the same three principles apply.

> [!NOTE]
> The **CIA Triad** is sometimes called the **AIC Triad** (Availability, Integrity, Confidentiality) or the **Parkerian Hexad** (when extended to six elements: C, I, A, plus Possession, Authenticity, and Utility).

---

## 1.3 Extended Security Services (Beyond CIA)

While CIA is the core, KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes additional security services critical for IoT:

### a) **Authentication**
The process of verifying the **identity** of a user, device, or entity. *Example:* A smart meter proving to the utility server that it is a legitimate device.

### b) **Authorization**
Determines **what an authenticated entity is allowed to do**. *Example:* A guest user in a smart home can view camera feeds but cannot unlock doors.

### c) **Non-Repudiation**
Ensures that a party **cannot deny** having performed an action. Achieved through digital signatures. *Example:* A user cannot deny sending a command to unlock a smart lock.

### d) **Accountability / Auditability**
The ability to **trace actions** back to the responsible entity. *Example:* Logging all access attempts in a healthcare IoT system.

### e) **Privacy**
The right of individuals to control **how their personal data is collected, used, and shared**. Particularly critical in IoT where sensors continuously collect data.

### f) **Trust**
The established confidence that an entity will **behave as expected**. In IoT, trust management is challenging due to heterogeneous devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always map any security question back to one or more of the CIA + Authentication + Non-Repudiation pillars. Examiners expect students to explicitly name which security service is being addressed.

---

## 1.4 Why Security is Harder in IoT

IoT introduces unique challenges that traditional IT security does not face:

- **Resource Constraints:** Devices have limited CPU, memory, and battery.
- **Scale:** Billions of devices create massive attack surfaces.
- **Heterogeneity:** Diverse hardware, OS, and communication protocols.
- **Physical Accessibility:** Sensors deployed in the field can be physically tampered with.
- **Mobility:** Devices move between networks, complicating trust.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Security Metrics (KTU Expectation):**
> - **Encryption Strength:** Measured in **bits** (e.g., AES-128 means 128-bit key).
> - **Attack Resistance:** Measured in **computational complexity** (e.g., $2^{128}$ operations to brute-force AES-128).
> - **Availability Uptime:** Measured as a **percentage** (e.g., 99.999% "five nines" availability).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Security Services

### 2.1.1 Confidentiality — Mechanisms & Operations

Confidentiality is enforced primarily through **encryption**. The process transforms plaintext $P$ into ciphertext $C$ using a key $K$ and an algorithm $E$:

$$C = E_K(P)$$

The reverse operation uses decryption $D$:

$$P = D_K(C) = D_K(E_K(P))$$

**Key Question for KTU:** *What happens if the key is compromised?* — The entire confidentiality guarantee collapses. This is why **key management** is as critical as the algorithm itself.

### 2.1.2 Integrity — Mechanisms & Operations

Integrity is typically enforced through:
1. **Message Authentication Codes (MACs):** Symmetric, faster, but requires shared key.
2. **Digital Signatures:** Asymmetric, provides non-repudiation, slower.
3. **Hash Functions:** One-way functions producing fixed-size digests.

**Why integrity matters in IoT:** A malicious actor could inject false sensor readings (e.g., fake temperature in a nuclear plant), causing physical harm.

### 2.1.3 Availability — Mechanisms & Operations

Availability is ensured through:
- **Redundancy:** Multiple servers, fail-over systems.
- **DDoS Mitigation:** Rate limiting, traffic filtering.
- **Fault Tolerance:** Graceful degradation when components fail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **IoT-Specific Availability Threat:** The **Mirai Botnet (2016)** infected ~600,000 IoT devices (cameras, DVRs) using default credentials and launched a massive DDoS attack, taking down major internet services. This is a textbook KTU example of an availability attack on IoT.

---

## 2.2 The OSI Security Architecture (X.800 Standard)

The **ITU-T X.800** standard defines security in three dimensions:

| Dimension | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Attack** | Any action that compromises security. | Eavesdropping, man-in-the-middle. |
| **Security Mechanism** | A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from attacks. | Encryption, digital signatures. |
| **Security Service** | A service that enhances the security of data processing and information transfer. | Confidentiality, integrity. |

**Relationship:** Services counteract attacks; mechanisms implement services.

---

## 2.3 Types of Security Attacks — KTU Classification

### A. Passive Attacks (Hard to detect, easy to prevent)
- **Release of Message Contents:** Attacker reads the data.
- **Traffic Analysis:** Attacker observes patterns even if content is encrypted (e.g., timing, frequency).

### B. Active Attacks (Easy to detect, hard to prevent)
- **Masquerade:** Pretending to be an authorized entity.
- **Replay:** Capturing and re-sending valid data.
- **Modification:** Altering message contents.
- **Denial of Service (DoS):** Disrupting service availability.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Encryption** | $C = E_K(P)$, $\quad P = D_K(C)$ | Symmetric: same key. Asymmetric: public/private key pair. |
| **Hash Function** | $h = H(M)$, where $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ | Output size $n$ in **bits**. |
| **Information Entropy** | $H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)$ | Measured in **bits/symbol**. Higher entropy = more uncertainty. |
| **Unicity Distance** | $U = \frac{H(K)}{D}$ | $H(K)$ = key entropy, $D$ = redundancy per character. |
| **Work Factor (Brute Force)** | $W = 2^k$ | $k$ = key length in bits. |
| **Risk Equation** | $R = T \times V \times I$ | $T$ = Threat, $V$ = Vulnerability, $I$ = Impact. |
| **Kerckhoffs's Principle** | Security must reside **only in the key**, not in algorithm secrecy. | Foundational principle of modern cryptography. |
| **Diffusion** | Each plaintext bit affects many ciphertext bits. | Avalanche effect. |
| **Confusion** | Complex relationship between key and ciphertext. | Defeats statistical analysis. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — KTU High-Yield Principle:**
> **Shannon's Confusion & Diffusion (1949):** Every secure cipher must provide *confusion* (obscure key-ciphertext relationship) and *diffusion* (spread plaintext influence across ciphertext). AES and DES both implement this.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Domain | Security Service Used | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Healthcare** | Confidentiality + Integrity | Patient data privacy; correct dosage commands. |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Authentication + Non-Repudiation | Prevent unauthorized control of machinery. |
| **Smart Grid** | Availability + Integrity | Continuous power delivery; accurate billing. |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Authentication + Integrity | Prevent spoofed V2X messages. |
| **Smart Home** | Privacy + Confidentiality | Protect user behavior patterns. |
---

## 2.6 Security Models — Formal Foundations

### 2.6.1 **Bell-LaPadula Model (Confidentiality-focused)**
- **No Read Up:** Subject cannot read objects at a higher sensitivity level.
- **No Write Down:** Subject cannot write to objects at a lower sensitivity level.
- Used in military/government systems.

### 2.6.2 **Biba Model (Integrity-focused)**
- **No Read Down:** Subject cannot read lower-integrity objects.
- **No Write Up:** Subject cannot write to higher-integrity objects.
- Inverse of Bell-LaPadula.

### 2.6.3 **Clark-Wilson Model (Commercial Integrity)**
- Uses **well-formed transactions** and **separation of duties**.
- Common in banking and commercial IoT applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "explain a security model," always mention *what it protects* (confidentiality vs. integrity) and give one real-world example.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shannon's Information-Theoretic Security

**Shannon's Theorem (1949):** For perfect secrecy, the ciphertext must provide **zero information** about the plaintext.

**Step 1: Define the probability conditions.**

For a cryptosystem to have **perfect secrecy**, the probability of plaintext $P$ given ciphertext $C$ must equal the probability of $P$ alone:

$$P(P \mid C) = P(P)$$

**Step 2: Express this in terms of joint probabilities.**

By the definition of conditional probability:

$$P(P \mid C) = \frac{P(P \cap C)}{P(C)}$$

**Step 3: Set up the condition for perfect secrecy.**

$$\frac{P(P \cap C)}{P(C)} = P(P)$$

Rearranging:

$$P(P \cap C) = P(P) \cdot P(C)$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result.**

The joint probability equals the product of marginals. This means $P$ and $C$ are **statistically independent**. Knowing $C$ gives no information about $P$.

**Step 5: Shannon's Lower Bound on Key Size.**

Shannon proved that for perfect secrecy:

$$H(K) \geq H(P)$$

**Meaning:** The key must have at least as much entropy as the plaintext. For a message of $n$ bits, the key must be **at least $n$ bits long**.

**Step 6: Apply to the One-Time Pad (OTP).**

The OTP achieves perfect secrecy because the key $K$ is:
- Truly random,
- As long as the message,
- Used only once.

$$C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$$

Since $K_i$ is uniformly random, $C_i$ is also uniformly random regardless of $P_i$, satisfying $P(P \mid C) = P(P)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** The OTP provides *information-theoretic* (unconditional) security but is impractical for IoT due to key distribution and storage constraints. IoT systems rely on *computational* security (e.g., AES, RSA), which assumes attackers have bounded computing power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Brute-Force Attack Complexity

**Step 1: Define the key space.**

A symmetric cipher with key length $k$ bits has $2^k$ possible keys.

$$\text{Key Space} = 2^k$$

**Step 2: Expected number of trials to find the correct key.**

On average, an attacker must try half the key space:

$$E[\text{trials}] = \frac{2^k}{2} = 2^{k-1}$$

**Step 3: Total work factor.**

$$W = 2^{k-1}$$

**Step 4: Apply to common cipher key lengths.**

| Cipher | Key Length $k$ | Work Factor $W$ |
|---|---|---|
| DES | 56 bits | $2^{55} \approx 3.6 \times 10^{16}$ |
| AES-128 | 128 bits | $2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38}$ |
| AES-256 | 256 bits | $2^{255} \approx 5.8 \times 10^{76}$ |

**Step 5: Practical implication for IoT.**

Even at $10^{18}$ operations per second (theoretical supercomputer), brute-forcing AES-128 takes:

$$\frac{2^{127}}{10^{18}} \approx 2.1 \times 10^{20} \text{ seconds} \approx 6.7 \times 10^{12} \text{ years}$$

This is why AES-128 is considered secure for IoT.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Unicity Distance

**Step 1: Define redundancy.**

Natural language (e.g., English) has redundancy because not all letter combinations are equally likely. The redundancy $D$ per character is:

$$D = \log_2 L - H_L$$

Where $L$ is the alphabet size and $H_L$ is the per-character entropy.

**Step 2: Apply Shannon's formula for unicity distance.**

The unicity distance $U$ is the minimum ciphertext length needed to uniquely determine the key:

$$U = \frac{H(K)}{D}$$

**Step 3: Example calculation.**

For DES with $H(K) = 56$ bits and English redundancy $D \approx 1.5$ bits/character:

$$U = \frac{56}{1.5} \approx 37.3 \text{ characters}$$

**Interpretation:** With about 37 characters of English ciphertext encrypted with DES, an attacker can theoretically determine the key.

---

## 3.4 Risk Assessment Matrix (Analytical Framework)

The fundamental risk equation in security engineering:

$$R = T \times V \times I$$

**Where:**
- $T$ = Threat probability (0 to 1)
- $V$ = Vulnerability score (0 to 1)
- $I$ = Impact severity (0 to 1)

**Step-by-step application to an IoT smart home:**

| Asset | Threat $T$ | Vulnerability $V$ | Impact $I$ | Risk $R$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Smart Lock | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.9 | $0.378$ |
| Temperature Sensor | 0.4 | 0.3 | 0.3 | $0.036$ |
| Security Camera | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.8 | $0.320$ |
| Smart Meter | 0.5 | 0.4 | 0.7 | $0.140$ |

**Decision Rule:** If $R > 0.3$, mitigation is required.

**Conclusion:** Smart locks and cameras need immediate security investment (e.g., strong authentication, encryption).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Proof: Why Hash Functions Must Be One-Way

**Step 1: Definition of one-way function.**

A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is one-way if:
1. For all $x \in X$, $f(x)$ is easy to compute.
2. For randomly chosen $y \in f(X)$, finding $x$ such that $f(x) = y$ is computationally infeasible.

**Step 2: Property demonstration for SHA-256.**

SHA-256 maps arbitrary input to a 256-bit output:

$$H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^{256}$$

For any message $M$:

$$h = \text{SHA-256}(M)$$

**Step 3: Pre-image resistance.**

Given $h$, the probability of finding $M$ such that $\text{SHA-256}(M) = h$ is:

$$P(\text{find } M) = \frac{1}{2^{256}} \approx 10^{-77}$$

This is astronomically small, making pre-image attacks infeasible.

**Step 4: Collision resistance.**

By the **birthday paradox**, the probability of finding two inputs $M_1 \neq M_2$ with the same hash is:

$$P(\text{collision}) \approx \frac{1}{\sqrt{2^{256}}} = \frac{1}{2^{128}} \approx 10^{-38}$$

Still computationally infeasible with current technology.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the CIA Triad. Explain each component with one IoT example.**

### **Model Answer:**

The **CIA Triad** is the foundational model of information security, consisting of three pillars:

**1. Confidentiality:** Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized parties.
- *IoT Example:* A wearable health device encrypts patient heart rate data so that only the doctor's app can decrypt and view it.

**2. Integrity:** Ensures that data remains accurate and unaltered during storage or transmission.
- *IoT Example:* A smart meter uses a hash function to verify that the electricity consumption reading sent to the utility has not been tampered with.

**3. Availability:** Ensures that systems and data are accessible when needed by legitimate users.
- *IoT Example:* A hospital's patient monitoring system uses redundant servers and DDoS protection to ensure that vital signs data is always available to medical staff.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between active and passive attacks with two examples each.**

### **Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Passive Attacks | Active Attacks |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Monitor/eavesdrop without modifying data. | Modify, fabricate, or disrupt data. |
| **Detection** | Difficult to detect. | Easier to detect. |
| **Prevention** | Easier to prevent (via encryption). | Difficult to prevent. |

**Passive Attack Examples:**
1. **Eavesdropping:** Attacker intercepts wireless communication between a smart lock and a smartphone.
2. **Traffic Analysis:** Attacker observes the timing and frequency of packets in a SCADA system to infer operational patterns.

**Active Attack Examples:**
1. **Replay Attack:** Attacker captures a legitimate "unlock" command and re-sends it later to bypass authentication.
2. **Denial of Service (DoS):** Attacker floods a smart home gateway with junk traffic, preventing legitimate commands from reaching devices.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the OSI Security Architecture (X.800) in detail. List and briefly describe the **five security services** defined by X.800. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the challenges of implementing security in IoT environments. Compare and contrast the Bell-LaPadula and Biba security models. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The **OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800)** provides a systematic framework for defining and addressing security in networks.

**Three Key Dimensions:**
1. **Security Attack:** Any action that compromises the security of information.
2. **Security Mechanism:** A process designed to detect, prevent, or recover from attacks.
3. **Security Service:** A service that enhances security of data processing and information transfer.

**Five Security Services (X.800):**

1. **Data Confidentiality:** Protects data from unauthorized disclosure. *Mechanism:* Encryption (AES, RSA).

2. **Data Integrity:** Ensures data is not altered. *Mechanism:* Hashing, MAC, digital signatures.

3. **Authentication:** Verifies the identity of an entity. *Mechanism:* Passwords, certificates, biometrics.

4. **Non-Repudiation:** Prevents denial of actions. *Mechanism:* Digital signatures.

5. **Access Control:** Restricts access to resources based on identity/role. *Mechanism:* ACLs, RBAC.

> **Conclusion:** The X.800 framework is the universal language for describing security requirements, mechanisms, and threats.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Challenges of IoT Security:**

1. **Resource Constraints:** Limited CPU, memory, battery make heavy cryptographic operations difficult.
2. **Scale:** Billions of devices create a massive attack surface.
3. **Heterogeneity:** Diverse protocols (Zigbee, BLE, LoRa, WiFi) complicate uniform security.
4. **Physical Accessibility:** Devices deployed in the field can be physically tampered with.
5. **Mobility:** Devices move between networks, complicating trust and key management.
6. **Update Challenges:** Firmware updates over-the-air (OTA) are difficult to secure.

**Comparison: Bell-LaPadula vs. Biba**

| Feature | Bell-LaPadula | Biba |
|---|---|---|
| **Focus** | Confidentiality | Integrity |
| **Primary Rule 1** | No Read Up (NRU) | No Read Down (NRD) |
| **Primary Rule 2** | No Write Down (NWD) | No Write Up (NWU) |
| **Goal** | Prevent unauthorized disclosure. | Prevent unauthorized modification. |
| **Levels** | Sensitivity (e.g., Unclassified, Secret, Top Secret). | Integrity (e.g., Untrusted, Trusted, Critical). |
| **Application** | Military, government. | Commercial, banking, industrial. |
| **Example Use** | Preventing a low-clearance user from reading Top Secret files. | Preventing an untrusted process from modifying critical system files. |

> **Conclusion:** Bell-LaPadula is for *secrecy*, Biba is for *trustworthiness*. They are often considered complementary.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **CIA Triad** and the extended security services (Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation). Give one IoT example for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Shannon's condition for **perfect secrecy**. Explain why the One-Time Pad achieves this and discuss its practical limitations in IoT. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram of Security Services:**

```
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │     SECURITY FOUNDATION     │
        │                             │
        │   ┌──────────┐  ┌─────────┐ │
        │   │CONFIDENT.│  │INTEGRITY│ │
        │   └──────────┘  └─────────┘ │
        │         ┌──────────┐        │
        │         │AVAILABIL.│        │
        │         └──────────┘        │
        └─────────────────────────────┘
                    ▲
        ┌─────────────────────────────┐
        │   EXTENDED SERVICES         │
        │  • Authentication           │
        │  • Authorization            │
        │  • Non-Repudiation          │
        │  • Accountability           │
        │  • Privacy                  │
        └─────────────────────────────┘
```

**Detailed Explanation with IoT Examples:**

1. **Confidentiality:** Encryption of sensor data before transmission.
   - *IoT Example:* A smart thermostat encrypts temperature data using AES-128 before sending it to the cloud.

2. **Integrity:** Hashing to detect tampering.
   - *IoT Example:* A firmware update is hashed with SHA-256, and the device verifies the hash before installation.

3. **Availability:** Redundancy and DoS protection.
   - *IoT Example:* A traffic management system uses multiple redundant servers to ensure 24/7 operation.

4. **Authentication:** Verifying device identity.
   - *IoT Example:* A smart meter uses a unique X.509 certificate to authenticate itself to the utility server.

5. **Authorization:** Defining access rights.
   - *IoT Example:* In a smart office, employees can control lights but only managers can adjust security settings.

6. **Non-Repudiation:** Digital signatures prevent denial of actions.
   - *IoT Example:* When a user unlocks a smart lock via app, the action is digitally signed and logged, preventing later denial.

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of Shannon's Perfect Secrecy:**

**Step 1:** Perfect secrecy requires that ciphertext $C$ reveals no information about plaintext $P$:

$$P(P \mid C) = P(P)$$

**Step 2:** Expanding using conditional probability:

$$\frac{P(P \cap C)}{P(C)} = P(P)$$

**Step 3:** Rearranging:

$$P(P \cap C) = P(P) \cdot P(C)$$

This means $P$ and $C$ are **statistically independent**.

**Step 4:** In information-theoretic terms, the mutual information is zero:

$$I(P; C) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Shannon's lower bound on key entropy:

$$H(K) \geq H(P)$$

The key must have at least as much entropy as the plaintext.

**Why OTP Achieves Perfect Secrecy:**

The One-Time Pad uses $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ where $K_i$ is:
- Truly random,
- As long as the message,
- Used only once.

Since $K_i$ is uniform, $C_i$ is also uniform regardless of $P_i$, satisfying $P(P \mid C) = P(P)$.

**Practical Limitations in IoT:**

1. **Key Distribution:** Securely distributing a key as long as every message is extremely difficult in large IoT networks.
2. **Key Storage:** Devices have limited memory; storing long keys is impractical.
3. **Key Reuse Risk:** Reusing a one-time pad completely breaks security.
4. **Synchronization:** Sender and receiver must stay perfectly synchronized.
5. **No Integrity Protection:** OTP only provides confidentiality, not integrity.

> **Conclusion:** While OTP is theoretically perfect, it is impractical for IoT. Real systems use computational security (AES, ChaCha20) as a trade-off.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Authentication with Authorization** — Authentication verifies *who you are*; Authorization defines *what you can do*. Examiners deduct 1-2 marks for mixing these up.
> 2. **Writing "CIA = Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication"** — This is WRONG. Authentication is an *additional* service, not part of the core CIA triad.
> 3. **Forgetting IoT Context** — Generic security answers get partial credit. Always mention IoT-specific implications (resource constraints, scale, heterogeneity).
> 4. **Skipping the Block Diagram** — For 7-mark questions, a diagram is **mandatory**. Without it, expect a 2-mark penalty.
> 5. **Not Naming the X.800 Standard** — When listing security services, always cite "X.800" or "OSI Security Architecture" for full marks.
> 6. **Confusing Bell-LaPadula Rules** — Students often write "No Read Down" for Bell-LaPadula. It is "No Read **Up**" (cannot read above your clearance).
> 7. **Missing Practical Examples** — Theoretical definitions alone are insufficient. Always pair with a real or IoT example.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Quick-Reference Checklist

- [ ] **CIA Triad** = **C**onfidentiality, **I**ntegrity, **A**vailability (the three pillars).
- [ ] **Extended Services** = Authentication, Authorization, Non-Repudiation, Accountability, Privacy, Trust.
- [ ] **X.800** defines 5 services: Data Confidentiality, Data Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control.
- [ ] **Passive attacks** = eavesdropping, traffic analysis (hard to detect, easy to prevent).
- [ ] **Active attacks** = masquerade, replay, modification, DoS (easy to detect, hard to prevent).
- [ ] **Shannon's Perfect Secrecy:** $P(P \mid C) = P(P)$ → mutual information $I(P;C) = 0$.
- [ ] **Key size bound:** $H(K) \geq H(P)$ for perfect secrecy.
- [ ] **OTP** achieves perfect secrecy but is impractical for IoT.
- [ ] **Confusion & Diffusion** (Shannon) are required for secure ciphers.
- [ ] **Bell-LaPadula** = Confidentiality model (No Read Up, No Write Down).
- [ ] **Biba** = Integrity model (No Read Down, No Write Up).
- [ ] **Kerckhoffs's Principle** = Security depends only on key secrecy, not algorithm secrecy.
- [ ] **Brute-force work factor** for $k$-bit key = $2^{k-1}$ operations on average.
- [ ] **Unicity distance** $U = H(K) / D$ (smaller is worse for security).
- [ ] **IoT Challenges** = resource limits, scale, heterogeneity, physical access, mobility, OTA updates.
- [ ] **Real-world attacks:** Mirai Botnet (2016, DDoS via default credentials), Stuxnet (2010, SCADA integrity).
- [ ] **Risk equation:** $R = T \times V \times I$ (Threat × Vulnerability × Impact).

### 📌 Must-Memorize Terms (High-Frequency in KTU Exams)

| Term | One-Line Definition |
|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Keeping data secret from unauthorized parties. |
| **Integrity** | Ensuring data is not tampered with. |
| **Availability** | Keeping systems accessible to authorized users. |
| **Authentication** | Proving you are who you claim to be. |
| **Authorization** | Granting permission to access a resource. |
| **Non-Repudiation** | Preventing denial of having performed an action. |
| **Passive Attack** | Eavesdropping without modification. |
| **Active Attack** | Modifying, fabricating, or disrupting data. |
| **Perfect Secrecy** | Ciphertext reveals zero information about plaintext. |
| **Kerckhoffs's Principle** | The algorithm can be public; only the key is secret. |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Attacks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Attacks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **PCCIT602 – Cryptography for IoT Applications**, an *attack* is any deliberate attempt by an adversary (eavesdropper, intruder, or malicious node) to compromise the **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **authenticity**, or **availability** of information exchanged between legitimate IoT entities. According to the KTU 2024 scheme classification, attacks on cryptographic systems are broadly partitioned into two top-level families: **Passive Attacks** and **Active Attacks**, with each family further subdivided into classical cryptographic attack models (COA, KPA, CPA, CCA) and protocol-level attacks (Replay, MITM, DoS, Side-Channel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 – Core Definition**
> A **passive attack** attempts to learn or make use of information from the system but does *not* affect system resources, whereas an **active attack** attempts to *alter* system resources, subvert communication, or impersonate legitimate parties. The adversary is conventionally denoted **Oscar** (Opponent) in KTU textbooks, while the legitimate sender and receiver are **Alice** and **Bob**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Postal System"**
> Imagine Alice sends a sealed letter to Bob through a postal network. Oscar (the adversary) may attempt to harm this communication in two distinct styles:
> - **Passive (Stealthy Librarian):** Oscar quietly intercepts the envelope, photocopies the contents, and reseals it. Alice and Bob never know. This is like **eavesdropping** or **traffic analysis**.
> - **Active (Tampering Thief):** Oscar opens the envelope, erases Alice's message, replaces it with a forged one, and even impersonates Alice to Bob. This is **modification** or **masquerade**.
> - In an **IoT setting**, the "letter" is a sensor reading (e.g., temperature), and the "postal network" is the wireless channel (e.g., LoRa, BLE, ZigBee) — inherently broadcast and easily tapped.

### 1.3 Standard Security Metrics Used to Quantify Attack Resistance

The following KTU-standard metrics are repeatedly tested in ESE questions:

- **Work Factor ($W$):** Estimated computational effort (in operations or bit-years) required to break a cryptosystem. A secure system requires $W \ge 2^{80}$ for legacy systems and $W \ge 2^{128}$ for modern IoT-grade systems.
- **Computational Security:** System is secure if the best-known attack requires $N$ operations where $N$ is computationally infeasible.
- **Unconditional (Information-Theoretic) Security:** System is secure even against an adversary with *unlimited* computational power (e.g., One-Time Pad).
- **Adversary Power Levels:** *Ciphertext-Only (COA) < Known-Plaintext (KPA) < Chosen-Plaintext (CPA) < Chosen-Ciphertext (CCA)*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight Box**
> For any cryptosystem, the security guarantee is *only* as strong as the *weakest* assumed adversary model. A system secure against COA may collapse under KPA. Always identify the threat model before evaluating security.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Top-Level Classification: Passive vs Active Attacks

The hierarchy of attack types in cryptography is systematically structured as follows:

#### 2.1.1 Passive Attacks (Stealth — No Modification)

Passive attacks are characterised by **observation only**. The adversary does not inject, modify, delete, or replay any traffic. They are harder to detect but can be countered through **encryption** and **traffic padding**.

- **Release of Message Contents (a.k.a. Disclosure of Contents):**
  - *Why:* The adversary learns the *plaintext* content of a confidential transmission (e.g., reads a patient's ECG data from a wearable IoT device).
  - *How countered:* Apply a strong symmetric (AES-128/256) or asymmetric (ECC, RSA) encryption algorithm.

- **Traffic Analysis:**
  - *Why:* Even when message contents are encrypted, the *pattern* of communication (frequency, duration, peer identities, time-of-day) leaks sensitive operational metadata (e.g., a military IoT network revealing troop movements by traffic bursts).
  - *How countered:* Traffic padding, dummy messages, link encryption, onion routing.

#### 2.1.2 Active Attacks (Aggressive — Modification/Injection)

Active attacks involve **tampering** or **impersonation**. The adversary alters system state, injects fabricated messages, or disrupts service. They are detectable (in principle) but far more damaging.

- **Masquerade:**
  - *Why:* Oscar pretends to be Alice and sends a forged message to Bob (e.g., a malicious node impersonating a legitimate smart meter to report false consumption).
  - *How countered:* Strong **authentication** mechanisms — HMAC, digital signatures, certificates.

- **Replay Attack:**
  - *Why:* Oscar captures a *valid* message from Alice and retransmits it later to achieve an unauthorized effect (e.g., re-sending a valid "unlock door" command captured yesterday).
  - *How countered:* **Nonces**, **timestamps**, **sequence numbers**, and **session keys**.

- **Modification of Messages (a.k.a. Tampering / Data Manipulation):**
  - *Why:* Oscar alters portions of a legitimate message in transit (e.g., changing a "drug dosage = 10 mg" instruction to "1000 mg" in a medical IoT network).
  - *How countered:* **Message Authentication Codes (MAC)**, **digital signatures**, and **hash-based integrity verification**.

- **Denial of Service (DoS / DDoS):**
  - *Why:* Oscar prevents or inhibits normal use of communication facilities (e.g., jamming the 2.4 GHz ISM band used by ZigBee/Thread IoT nodes).
  - *How countered:* Rate-limiting, redundant infrastructure, spread-spectrum (FHSS/DSSS), anomaly detection.

### 2.2 Cryptanalytic Attack Models (Adversary Capabilities)

These models describe *what the adversary already knows* about the plaintext/ciphertext pairings:

| Attack Model | Adversary Capability | Typical Defeats |
|---|---|---|
| **COA** (Ciphertext-Only) | Sees only ciphertext(s) | Caesar, Vigenère, weak classical ciphers |
| **KPA** (Known-Plaintext) | Knows several (plaintext, ciphertext) pairs | Single-DES, weak block ciphers |
| **CPA** (Chosen-Plaintext) | Can obtain ciphertexts of *chosen* plaintexts | ECB mode, RSA without OAEP |
| **CCA** (Chosen-Ciphertext) | Can obtain plaintexts of *chosen* ciphertexts (adaptive or non-adaptive) | Plain RSA, textbook ElGamal |
| **COA Brute-Force** | Exhaustively tries all keys of $2^k$ possibilities | Keys with $k < 80$ bits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Security Hierarchy (Strict Ordering):**
> If a system is provably secure under **CCA**, it is automatically secure under **CPA, KPA, and COA**. This monotonicity is a frequent KTU two-mark question.

### 2.3 Other KTU-Mandated Attack Types (Module 1)

- **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) Attack:** Oscar positions himself *between* Alice and Bob, relaying and potentially altering messages while each party believes they are talking directly to the other. Countered by **mutual authentication** and **public-key certificates (PKI)**.

- **Side-Channel Attack:** Exploits *physical* leakage — power consumption, electromagnetic emanations, timing, cache misses — rather than algorithmic weaknesses. Critical in IoT because resource-constrained devices leak side channels easily.

- **Birthday Attack:** Exploits the **Birthday Paradox** in hash functions. For a hash with $n$-bit output, collisions are found in approximately $\sqrt{2^n} = 2^{n/2}$ trials. Therefore, **160-bit SHA-1** provides only **80-bit** collision resistance.

- **Algebraic / Mathematical Attacks:** Exploit the underlying algebraic structure — e.g., **Wiener's attack** on low-exponent RSA, **Pohlig-Hellman** on weak-group discrete logs.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Quantity | Formula / Definition | Physical / Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Key space size | $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^k$ | Total number of possible keys for a $k$-bit key |
| Brute-force work factor | $W = 2^{k-1}$ (on average) | Expected trials before finding correct key |
| Birthday bound | $N \approx 1.177 \sqrt{2^n}$ | Trials to find hash collision (probability $\ge 0.5$) |
| Effective collision resistance | $n_{\text{eff}} = n/2$ bits | For an $n$-bit hash under birthday attack |
| Unconditional security condition | $H(K) \ge H(M)$ | Entropy of key must exceed entropy of message (Shannon) |
| Avalanche effect (DES) | $\ge 50\%$ bit change | Each plaintext bit should affect $\approx$ half the ciphertext bits |
| Information leakage (passive) | $I(M;C) \le \varepsilon$ | Mutual information between message and ciphertext (CPA-security) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** IoT protocol designers (Zigbee, BLE, LoRaWAN) use these formulas to size keys, choose hash functions (SHA-256 → 128-bit collision resistance), and select MACs (HMAC-SHA-256). For a 10-year battery IoT node, work factor must exceed $\mathbf{2^{80}}$ to remain safe.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Brute-Force Work Factor for a Symmetric Key

**Problem Setup:** A symmetric cipher uses a $k$-bit key uniformly drawn from $\mathcal{K}$. An adversary performs an *exhaustive key search* (COA brute-force). Determine the **average** and **worst-case** work factor.

**Step 1 — Define the sample space.**
The key space has cardinality

$$
\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case analysis.**
In the worst case, the correct key is the *last* one tested, so

$$
W_{\text{worst}} = 2^{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Average-case analysis.**
Assuming the correct key is equally likely at any position, the average number of trials is

$$
W_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1 + 2 + \dots + 2^{k}}{2^{k}} = \frac{2^{k}(2^{k}+1)/2}{2^{k}} = \frac{2^{k}+1}{2}
$$

For large $k$, $2^{k}+1 \approx 2^{k}$, hence

$$
W_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{2^{k}}{2} = 2^{k-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration for IoT-grade AES.**
For $k = 128$ (AES-128):

$$
W_{\text{avg}} \approx 2^{127} \approx 1.7 \times 10^{38} \text{ operations}
$$

At $10^{18}$ ops/sec (modern ASIC), this requires $\approx 5.4 \times 10^{12}$ years — *physically infeasible*.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Birthday Attack — Expected Trials to Find a Hash Collision

**Problem Setup:** A cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ produces an $n$-bit digest. How many randomly chosen inputs $q$ are required before the probability of a collision exceeds $0.5$?

**Step 1 — Probability of no collision after $q$ samples.**
The first input has no restriction. The second must differ from the first (prob $1 - 1/2^n$). The third must differ from both, etc. So

$$
P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} \left(1 - \frac{i}{2^{n}}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the approximation $1 - x \approx e^{-x}$ for small $x$.**

$$
P(\text{no collision}) \approx \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} e^{-i/2^{n}} = e^{-\frac{1}{2^{n}}\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} i} = e^{-\frac{q(q-1)}{2^{n+1}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Set $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5 \Rightarrow P(\text{no collision}) = 0.5$.**

$$
e^{-\frac{q(q-1)}{2^{n+1}}} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Take natural logarithms of both sides.**

$$
-\frac{q(q-1)}{2^{n+1}} = -\ln 2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic in $q$ (approximating $q(q-1) \approx q^2$ for large $q$).**

$$
q^2 \approx 2^{n+1} \ln 2 \approx 2^{n+1} (0.693) \approx 1.386 \cdot 2^{n}
$$

$$
q \approx \sqrt{1.386 \cdot 2^{n}} \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

**Step 6 — Final form (KTU exam-ready).**

$$
\boxed{\,q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Numerical check for SHA-256 ($n = 256$).**

$$
q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{128} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{38} \text{ trials}
$$

This is the reason NIST mandates SHA-256 (not SHA-1) for IoT firmware integrity.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Attack Type → Countermeasure Mapping

| Attack Type | Cryptographic Primitive | Non-Cryptographic Countermeasure |
|---|---|---|
| Release of contents | AES (symmetric), ECC/RSA (asymmetric) | Access control, VPN tunnels |
| Traffic analysis | Onion routing, link encryption | Dummy traffic, traffic shaping |
| Masquerade | Digital signatures, HMAC | Biometrics, CAPTCHAs |
| Replay | Nonces, timestamps, sequence numbers | Session timeout, one-time tokens |
| Modification | MAC, AEAD (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305) | Checksums, CRC |
| DoS / DDoS | Rate-limiting via cryptographic puzzles | Firewalls, redundant mesh routing |
| MITM | PKI certificates, Diffie-Hellman with auth | Out-of-band verification |
| Side-channel | Constant-time implementations, masking | Faraday cages, noise generators |
| Birthday | Use SHA-256 instead of MD5/SHA-1 | Truncate attacker-controlled inputs |
| Brute-force | Increase key size to $\ge 128$ bits | Account lockout, key rotation |

### 3.4 Worked Example: Replay Attack on an IoT Smart Lock

A smart-lock IoT node accepts a "unlock" command signed with a fixed private key $\text{sk}_A$. Oscar captures a valid signed command $(M, \sigma)$ at time $t_1$ and replays it at time $t_2$.

**Step 1:** Original message is $M = \text{"unlock door"}$, signed as $\sigma = \text{Sign}(\text{sk}_A, M)$.
**Step 2:** Oscar stores $(M, \sigma)$ on a sniffer.
**Step 3:** At $t_2$, Oscar retransmits $(M, \sigma)$ verbatim. Since $\sigma$ is valid for $M$, the lock opens — *replay succeeded*.
**Step 4 — Countermeasure:** Append a fresh nonce $N_t$ and timestamp $t$ to every command: $M' = (M, N_t, t)$. Sign $M'$ instead. The receiver rejects any $M'$ where $N_t$ was seen before or $t - t_{\text{now}} > \Delta_{\max}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Replay attacks are *the* most common vulnerability in IoT firmware bootloaders and OTA updates. Always use **monotonically increasing counters** in resource-constrained nodes (cheaper than nonces).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Cryptographic Attacks

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Attacks on Cryptosystems] --> B[Passive Attacks]
    A --> C[Active Attacks]
    A --> D[Cryptanalytic Models]
    A --> E[Protocol Level Attacks]

    B --> B1[Release of Message Contents]
    B --> B2[Traffic Analysis]

    C --> C1[Masquerade]
    C --> C2[Replay]
    C --> C3[Modification of Messages]
    C --> C4[Denial of Service DoS]

    D --> D1[Ciphertext Only Attack COA]
    D --> D2[Known Plaintext Attack KPA]
    D --> D3[Chosen Plaintext Attack CPA]
    D --> D4[Chosen Ciphertext Attack CCA]
    D --> D5[Brute Force Attack]

    E --> E1[Man In The Middle MITM]
    E --> E2[Side Channel Attack]
    E --> E3[Birthday Attack]
    E --> E4[Algebraic Attack]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Attack–Defense Block Architecture for an IoT Node

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[Sensor Data] --> P1[Plaintext Buffer]
    P1 --> F1[Encryption Module AES 128]
    F1 --> C1[Ciphertext Channel]
    C1 --> A1[Adversary Oscar]
    A1 --> A2[Passive Sniffer]
    A1 --> A3[Active Injector]
    A2 --> M1[Counter: Link Encryption]
    A3 --> M2[Counter: HMAC Signature]
    C1 --> D1[Decryption Module]
    D1 --> V1[Integrity Verification]
    V1 --> S2[Actuator Command]
    S2 --> N1[Nonce and Timestamp Check]
    N1 --> O1[Authenticated Output]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Replay Attack and Countermeasure Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Alice as Alice IoT Node
    participant Oscar as Adversary Oscar
    participant Bob as Bob Gateway

    Note over Alice, Bob: Phase 1 - Legitimate Communication
    Alice->>Bob: M1, Sign(skA, M1)
    Oscar-->>Oscar: Captures M1, Sign
    Note over Oscar, Bob: Phase 2 - Replay Attack
    Oscar->>Bob: M1, Sign(skA, M1)  [delayed replay]
    Bob-->>Bob: Signature valid, but timestamp stale
    Bob-->>Oscar: REJECT - replay detected
    Note over Alice, Bob: Phase 3 - Countermeasure Active
    Alice->>Bob: M2, NonceN, TimestampT, Sign
    Bob-->>Bob: Verify Nonce fresh and T within window
    Bob->>Alice: ACK - authenticated
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]**
*Differentiate between passive and active attacks with one example each. Which is harder to detect and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Passive attacks** aim to *observe* or *eavesdrop* on information in transit *without modifying* system resources. Example: traffic analysis of an IoT mesh network to infer device activity patterns.
- **Active attacks** *modify* data, inject false messages, or impersonate legitimate entities. Example: a man-in-the-middle attack where Oscar alters the temperature reading of a smart thermostat.
- **Detection difficulty:** Passive attacks are *harder to detect* because they leave no trace in the system logs — the traffic flow appears normal. Active attacks are more visible because they change the system state, but they are more *damaging*.

*(Mark split: 1 mark each for the two definitions + examples, 1 mark for detection reasoning.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List any four cryptanalytic attack models in order of increasing adversary power. State the corresponding security guarantee for each level.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

In order of *increasing* adversary power:

1. **COA (Ciphertext-Only Attack):** Adversary has only ciphertexts. Security guarantee → *COA-security* (e.g., AES in any mode).
2. **KPA (Known-Plaintext Attack):** Adversary knows some (plaintext, ciphertext) pairs. Security guarantee → *KPA-security*.
3. **CPA (Chosen-Plaintext Attack):** Adversary can query an encryption oracle with chosen plaintexts. Security guarantee → *CPA-security* (semantic security, IND-CPA).
4. **CCA (Chosen-Ciphertext Attack):** Adversary can also query a decryption oracle on chosen ciphertexts (except the target). Security guarantee → *CCA-security* (IND-CCA2, the strongest practical standard).

*(Mark split: 1.5 marks for naming the four models in order, 1.5 marks for stating the security guarantees.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> *Common mark-losing mistakes on this topic:*
> 1. Confusing **passive vs cryptanalytic** classifications — they are *orthogonal*, not alternatives.
> 2. Writing **COA, KPA, CPA, CCA** in the wrong order (most students reverse them).
> 3. Forgetting to mention **nonces and timestamps** while describing replay-attack countermeasures.
> 4. In birthday-attack numericals, students often forget the factor $1.177$ and use $2^{n/2}$ directly — partial credit only.
> 5. Failing to cite **AES-128** as the IoT-standard cipher against brute-force ($2^{127}$ work factor).

---

#### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *Option for students preferring descriptive answers*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** Classify the different types of attacks on encrypted messages with neat diagrams. Explain any two passive attacks and three active attacks in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **man-in-the-middle (MITM) attack** on a Diffie-Hellman key exchange. How does authenticated DH (using digital signatures) defeat this attack? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification of Attacks — (7 marks)**

- **1 mark:** Introduction — define attack in the cryptographic context. Reference Alice–Bob–Oscar model.
- **1 mark:** Top-level split into *Passive* and *Active* attacks with a labelled diagram (Mermaid/textual block diagram acceptable).
- **2 marks:** Two passive attacks explained:
  - *Release of Message Contents:* Adversary obtains the plaintext of confidential IoT telemetry (e.g., patient vitals). Countered by **symmetric encryption** (AES-128) and **asymmetric encryption** (ECC) for key exchange.
  - *Traffic Analysis:* Even encrypted traffic leaks metadata — frequency, packet length, source-destination patterns. Countered by **traffic padding** and **onion routing**.
- **3 marks:** Three active attacks explained:
  - *Masquerade:* Oscar pretends to be Alice, sending false sensor data to Bob. Countered by **HMAC-based authentication** and **digital signatures**.
  - *Replay:* Oscar captures a valid signed command and retransmits it later (e.g., a "dispense insulin" command). Countered by **nonces**, **timestamps**, and **session keys**.
  - *Modification:* Oscar tampers with a message in transit (e.g., changes a drone GPS waypoint). Countered by **MACs (AES-CMAC, HMAC-SHA-256)** and **AEAD modes (AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)**.

**(b) MITM on Diffie-Hellman — (7 marks)**

- **2 marks:** Brief recap of unauthenticated DH — Alice sends $g^a \bmod p$, Bob sends $g^b \bmod p$, both derive shared key $g^{ab} \bmod p$.
- **3 marks:** MITM attack sequence:
  1. Oscar intercepts $g^a$ from Alice and $g^b$ from Bob.
  2. Oscar sends $g^c$ to Alice (pretending to be Bob) and $g^d$ to Bob (pretending to be Alice).
  3. Alice computes $K_1 = (g^d)^a = g^{ad}$; Bob computes $K_2 = (g^c)^b = g^{bc}$; Oscar knows *both* shared keys.
  4. Oscar can now decrypt, modify, and re-encrypt all traffic in real time — *full compromise*.
- **2 marks:** Countermeasure — **Authenticated Diffie-Hellman** (e.g., **STS protocol**, **MQV**, or signing the DH parameters with **ECDSA**). The public keys of Alice and Bob are *certified* by a trusted **CA (Certificate Authority)**, so Oscar cannot substitute his own values without detection.

---

#### 📗 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative choice for analytical/numerical students*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model QP Alternate]**

**(a)** Explain the **Birthday Attack** on hash functions. A 128-bit hash function is used in an IoT firmware integrity check. Estimate the number of trials required to find a collision with probability $\ge 0.5$. Comment on whether this is acceptable for IoT deployment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A symmetric cipher uses a 64-bit key. Compute the **average and worst-case work factor** for an exhaustive key search. If a custom IoT ASIC can test $10^{12}$ keys per second, calculate the average time to break the cipher. Hence justify the KTU recommendation to use AES-128 instead. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Birthday Attack — (7 marks)**

- **2 marks:** Concept — Birthday paradox states that among $\sqrt{N}$ randomly chosen items from a set of size $N$, the probability of *collision* exceeds $0.5$.
- **1 mark:** Application to hashing — Adversary generates $q$ random messages and stores their digests; a match indicates a collision (different inputs, same digest).
- **2 marks:** Derivation (reproduce Section 3.2):
  - $P(\text{collision}) \ge 0.5$ when $q \ge 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}$
  - For $n = 128$: $q \ge 1.177 \cdot 2^{64} \approx 2.17 \times 10^{19}$ trials.
- **1 mark:** Engineering feasibility — At $10^{12}$ hashes/sec, this requires $\approx 2.17 \times 10^{7}$ seconds $\approx 251$ days on a single ASIC. Distributing across $10^4$ GPUs/ASICs reduces to $\approx 36$ minutes — *clearly unacceptable* for IoT firmware integrity.
- **1 mark:** Recommendation — Upgrade to **SHA-256** ($n = 256$) → $q \approx 2^{128}$, infeasible at any current technology level.

**(b) Brute-Force Work Factor — (7 marks)**

- **1 mark:** Key space $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert = 2^{64}$.
- **2 marks:** Worst-case work factor: $W_{\text{worst}} = 2^{64} \approx 1.84 \times 10^{19}$ trials.
- **2 marks:** Average work factor:
  $W_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{2^{64}}{2} = 2^{63} \approx 9.22 \times 10^{18}$ trials.
- **1 mark:** Time to break at $10^{12}$ keys/sec:
  $T_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{9.22 \times 10^{18}}{10^{12}} = 9.22 \times 10^{6} \text{ seconds} \approx 107 \text{ days}.$
- **1 mark:** Justification — 107 days is *practically feasible* for a well-funded adversary. KTU mandates **AES-128** because $2^{127}$ trials at $10^{12}$ keys/sec require $\approx 5.4 \times 10^{18}$ years — *physically infeasible*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Passive attacks** = observation only (release of contents, traffic analysis); countered by encryption and traffic padding.
- ✅ **Active attacks** = modification/injection (masquerade, replay, modification, DoS); countered by authentication, MACs, signatures.
- ✅ **Adversary power order (low → high):** COA < KPA < CPA < CCA. CCA-security implies all weaker securities.
- ✅ **Brute-force average work factor** for a $k$-bit key is $\mathbf{2^{k-1}}$; for AES-128, this is $\approx \mathbf{2^{127}}$ — infeasible.
- ✅ **Birthday bound** for an $n$-bit hash: $\mathbf{q \approx 1.177 \cdot 2^{n/2}}$; effective collision resistance is only $\mathbf{n/2}$ bits.
- ✅ **Replay attacks** are defeated by **nonces, timestamps, and sequence numbers**, *not* by encryption alone.
- ✅ **MITM attacks** on key exchange require **authenticated DH** (e.g., STS, ECDSA-signed DH, or certificate-based PKI).
- ✅ **Side-channel attacks** exploit physical leakage — a major IoT concern due to constrained hardware.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 mandate:** IoT security design should assume CPA/CCA adversaries, not merely COA.
- ✅ **Quick numerical recall:** AES-128 brute-force $\approx 5.4 \times 10^{12}$ years at $10^{18}$ ops/sec; SHA-1 collision $\approx 2^{80}$ trials.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: OSI Security Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** OSI Security Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OSI Security Architecture (ITU-T X.800 Standard)**
> The **OSI (Open Systems Interconnection) Security Architecture**, formally defined by the **ITU-T Recommendation X.800 (1991)**, is a systematic framework that provides a unified, vendor-neutral vocabulary and a structured taxonomy for organizing the discipline of computer and communications security. It classifies the entire security domain into three foundational pillars: **Security Attacks**, **Security Services**, and **Security Mechanisms**, and explicitly defines how mechanisms combine to provide services, which in turn counter attacks.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *PCCIT602 – Cryptography for IoT Applications*, this architecture serves as the **conceptual skeleton** upon which all subsequent cryptographic primitives (symmetric encryption, asymmetric encryption, hashing, digital signatures) are layered.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Bank Vault System**
> Imagine a bank vault protecting money and documents.
>
> - The **attack** is the *attempted robbery* (e.g., lock-picking, tunneling through the wall, or forging a withdrawal slip).
> - The **security mechanism** is the *physical tool* used to defend the vault (e.g., a 12-inch steel door, a biometric scanner, a CCTV camera, or a guard).
> - The **security service** is the *high-level guarantee* the bank promises its customer — such as "only the account holder can withdraw money" (**authentication**) or "your balance will not be altered" (**integrity**).
>
> The OSI Security Architecture is essentially the **bank's master security manual** that lists every possible robbery (attack), every defensive tool (mechanism), and every customer promise (service) — and binds them together so engineers can build secure systems systematically rather than reactively.

---

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of OSI Security Architecture

| Pillar | Definition | Example in IoT Context |
|---|---|---|
| **Security Attack** | Any action that attempts to compromise the security of information owned by an organization. | A passive eavesdropper on a LoRaWAN channel reading sensor data |
| **Security Mechanism** | A primitive (software/hardware) designed to detect, prevent, or recover from a security attack. | AES-128 encryption of MQTT payload |
| **Security Service** | A processing or communication service that enhances the security of the data processing systems and the information transfers. | End-to-end confidentiality of patient telemetry in a wearable ECG device |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship (KTU Board Focus):**
> $$\text{Security Service} = f(\text{Security Mechanisms})$$
> $$\text{Security Attack} \xrightarrow{\text{threatens}} \text{Information Security}$$

A single service may require **multiple mechanisms**, and a single mechanism may be used to provide **multiple services**.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Constants Used

- **ITU-T X.800** is the official standard reference document published by the **International Telecommunication Union – Telecommunication Standardization Sector** in **1991**.
- The OSI model itself has **7 layers**, but security in X.800 is addressed primarily at layers **1 (Physical)** through **7 (Application)**, with emphasis on layers **3 (Network)**, **4 (Transport)**, and **7 (Application)** for IoT deployments.
- **RFC 2828** (Internet Security Glossary, 2000) provides an updated, internet-aligned counterpart to X.800.

---

## 1.5 Why OSI Security Architecture Matters for IoT

> [!NOTE]
> In **IoT (Internet of Things)** environments, devices are constrained (low memory, low CPU, battery-powered), communication is often wireless (BLE, Zigbee, LoRa, NB-IoT), and physical access is trivially possible (the device sits on a streetlight or in a hospital ward). Without a **systematic architecture**, designers patch vulnerabilities one-by-one. The **OSI Security Architecture forces a top-down, defense-in-depth design approach**, which is mandated by the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework for secure IoT system design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar I — Security Attacks (X.800 Classification)

A **security attack** is defined by X.800 as *any action that compromises the security of information owned by an organization*. Attacks are classified into **four passive** and **four active** categories.

### 2.1.1 Passive Attacks

Passive attacks attempt to **learn or make use of information** from the system but do **not modify system resources**. Their hallmark is **interception**, and they are notoriously difficult to detect because no data is altered.

| Sub-Type | Technical Definition | Real-World IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Release of Message Contents** | An unauthorized party gains access to the plaintext (or readable form) of a transmitted message. | Sniffing Zigbee traffic between a smart bulb and its hub to learn the user's lighting schedule |
| **Traffic Analysis** | Even when messages are encrypted, the attacker observes the **pattern, frequency, length, and endpoints** of communication. | Observing that a patient's insulin pump transmits every 5 minutes, inferring a chronic-disease profile |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feasibility Rule:** Passive attacks are **feasible** when an attacker has a **passive radio receiver** within range. Encryption does not stop traffic analysis — it only stops release of message contents.

### 2.1.2 Active Attacks

Active attacks attempt to **alter system resources or affect their operation**. They involve **some modification of the data stream** or the **creation of a false stream**. They are easier to detect but harder to prevent.

| Sub-Type | Technical Definition | Real-World IoT Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Masquerade** | An entity pretends to be a different entity. | A rogue MQTT broker impersonating the AWS IoT Core to collect sensor data |
| **Replay** | An attacker captures a legitimate transmission and replays it later. | Replaying an old "unlock door" command sent by the homeowner's phone |
| **Modification of Messages** | Portions of a legitimate message are altered or delayed/reordered. | Changing the temperature value "38.5 °C" to "36.5 °C" before it reaches the ICU monitor |
| **Denial of Service (DoS)** | The attacker overwhelms the system, preventing legitimate use. | Jamming a 2.4 GHz Wi-Fi channel so all smart-home sensors go offline |

---

## 2.2 Pillar II — Security Mechanisms (X.800)

A **security mechanism** is a primitive designed to detect, prevent, or recover from an attack. X.800 defines the following:

| # | Mechanism | Purpose | IoT Implementation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Encipherment (Encryption)** | Hide data using mathematical transformation | AES-128-CCM in BLE |
| 2 | **Digital Signature** | Bind identity to a message | ECDSA (secp256r1) |
| 3 | **Access Control** | Enforce permission rules | RBAC in AWS IoT |
| 4 | **Data Integrity** | Detect alteration | SHA-256 + HMAC |
| 5 | **Authentication Exchange** | Verify identity | Challenge-response via TLS |
| 6 | **Traffic Padding** | Defeat traffic analysis | Constant-bit-rate frames |
| 7 | **Routing Control** | Use secure routes | VPN over LTE |
| 8 | **Notarization** | Trusted third-party attestation | Timestamping authority |
| 9 | **Physical Security** | Physical protection | Tamper-proof enclosures |

---

## 2.3 Pillar III — Security Services (X.800)

A **security service** is a service provided by a protocol layer of communicating open systems, which ensures adequate security of the systems or of data transfers. X.800 defines exactly **five major services** (plus availability).

| # | Service | X.800 Definition | KTU One-Line Mnemonic |
|---|---|---|---|
| **S1** | **Data Confidentiality** | Protection of data from unauthorized disclosure. | "**No Peeking**" |
| **S2** | **Data Integrity** | Assurance that data received is exactly as sent, with no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay. | "**No Tampering**" |
| **S3** | **Authentication** | Assurance that the communicating entity is the one claimed. | "**Who Are You?**" |
| **S4** | **Non-Repudiation** | Protection against false denial by either sender or receiver of having participated in the communication. | "**You Said It, No Take-Back**" |
| **S5** | **Access Control** | Prevention of unauthorized use of a resource. | "**Knock First**" |
| **S6** | **Availability (Data)** | Property of being accessible and usable on demand by an authorized entity. (Added later, per RFC 2828) | "**Always On**" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sub-categories of S1 (Confidentiality) — KTU favorite question:**
> - **Connection Confidentiality** — entire connection encrypted
> - **Connectionless Confidentiality** — each datagram encrypted independently
> - **Selective-Field Confidentiality** — only specific fields encrypted (e.g., credit-card field in HTTP form)
> - **Traffic-Flow Confidentiality** — hides source, destination, frequency (defeats traffic analysis)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Mapping: Service ← → Mechanism

This is the **most-asked table** in KTU ESE for Module 1. Memorize it.

| Security Service | Encipherment | Digital Sig. | Access Ctrl. | Data Integ. | Auth Exch. | Traffic Pad. | Routing Ctrl. | Notary |
|---|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| **Confidentiality** | ✅ | — | — | — | — | ✅ | ✅ | — |
| **Integrity** | — | ✅ | — | ✅ | — | — | — | — |
| **Authentication** | — | ✅ | — | — | ✅ | — | — | — |
| **Non-Repudiation** | — | ✅ | — | ✅ | — | — | — | ✅ |
| **Access Control** | — | — | ✅ | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Availability** | — | — | ✅ | — | — | — | ✅ | — |

> Mark **Y (Yes)** where a mechanism contributes to the service; **—** means not used for that service.

---

## 2.5 The Model for Network Security (Stallings)

William Stallings augments X.800 with a complementary model:

1. Design a **security transformation** (e.g., AES) that makes the message unintelligible to the attacker.
2. Design a **secret information** distribution method (e.g., key exchange via Diffie-Hellman).
3. Specify a **protocol** enabling the principals to use the transformation and secret information (e.g., TLS 1.3 handshake).

$$ \text{Secure Channel} = f(\text{Algorithm}, \text{Key}, \text{Protocol}) $$

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telemedicine IoT**: Combines **authentication (S3)** + **integrity (S2)** + **confidentiality (S1)** using **DTLS (Datagram TLS)** — a direct application of X.800 services at the transport layer.
- **Smart Grid**: Uses **non-repudiation (S4)** for billing data via **ECDSA digital signatures** as per IEC 62351.
- **Connected Vehicles**: Uses **availability (S6)** via **automotive-grade intrusion-detection systems** and **routing control (M7)** via CAN-bus message authentication (SecOC).

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Standard Reference | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Active Attack | X.800 §2.1 | Categorical |
| $P$ | Passive Attack | X.800 §2.1 | Categorical |
| $S$ | Security Service | X.800 §3.2 | Six total |
| $M$ | Security Mechanism | X.800 §3.3 | Ten total |
| $K$ | Cryptographic Key | NIST FIPS 140-3 | Bits |
| $H(\cdot)$ | Cryptographic Hash | NIST FIPS 180-4 | 256 bits for SHA-256 |
| $E_K(\cdot)$ | Encipherment under key $K$ | X.800 M1 | Function |
| $D_K(\cdot)$ | Decipherment under key $K$ | X.800 M1 | Function |
| $\sigma_M$ | Digital Signature on $M$ | X.800 M2 | Bit-string |
| $\mathrm{MAC}_K(M)$ | Message Authentication Code | NIST FIPS 198-1 | Bit-string |
| $\vert\vert$ | Concatenation | Notation | NOT to be confused with conditional probability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kerala KTU Board Notation Note:**
> When writing **$D_K(E_K(P)) = P$**, always parenthesize the inner encryption: $E_K(P)$ denotes *ciphertext*; $D_K(C)$ denotes *plaintext recovery*. The **inverse property** holds because $E$ and $D$ are mathematical inverses over a finite field.

---

## 2.8 Why "Why" and "How" — The KTU Approach

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does X.800 separate attacks, services, and mechanisms?**
> - **Separation of Concerns:** A vendor can implement *mechanisms* (AES, SHA-256) once, then compose them into *services* (TLS) without redesigning.
> - **Vendor Neutrality:** Customers can specify required *services* (e.g., "we need confidentiality and integrity") and let vendors propose the *mechanisms*.
> - **Threat Modeling:** Security analysts enumerate *attacks* first, then map each to the *service* that counters it, and finally to the *mechanism* that delivers the service.
