# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Derivations

## 3.1 Analytical Setup: The OSI Security Triad

We model the interaction between Alice (sender), Bob (receiver), and Trudy (attacker) on an open channel. The OSI Security Architecture can be formalized as a **3-tuple**:

$$
\mathcal{A} \;=\; (\mathcal{U},\; \mathcal{S},\; \mathcal{M})
$$

where:
- $\mathcal{U}$ = the **Universe of Attacks** (set of all possible adversary actions)
- $\mathcal{S}$ = the **Set of Security Services** (defensive guarantees)
- $\mathcal{M}$ = the **Set of Security Mechanisms** (defensive primitives)

The **counter-relation** is defined as:

$$
\mathcal{R} \;=\; \{(u, s) \in \mathcal{U} \times \mathcal{S} \;\mid\; s \text{ mitigates } u\}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Service Coverage Index (KTU Original Concept)

We introduce a quantitative **Service Coverage Index** $\rho \in [0, 1]$ that an examiner can ask for, measuring how completely a chosen mechanism set covers all required services.

### Step 1 — Define the Coverage Matrix

Let $C \in \{0, 1\}^{n \times m}$ be the binary coverage matrix where:
- $n$ = number of services
- $m$ = number of mechanisms
- $C_{ij} = 1$ if mechanism $j$ contributes to service $i$; else $0$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Service Coverage Vector

$$
S_i \;=\; \bigvee_{j=1}^{m} C_{ij}
$$

where $\vee$ is the **logical OR**. The result $S_i \in \{0, 1\}$ indicates whether service $i$ is covered by **at least one** mechanism.

### Step 3 — Compute the Service Coverage Index

$$
\rho \;=\; \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Example (Confidentiality + Integrity + Authentication)

Suppose we deploy **two mechanisms**: Encipherment (AES) and Digital Signature (ECDSA). The coverage matrix $C$ from §2.4 is:

$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 \\ % Confidentiality
0 & 1 \\ % Integrity
0 & 1 \\ % Authentication
\end{bmatrix}
$$

Compute service vector row-by-row:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_1 &= \max(C_{11}, C_{12}) = \max(1, 0) = 1 \quad \text{(Confidentiality covered by AES)} \\
S_2 &= \max(C_{21}, C_{22}) = \max(0, 1) = 1 \quad \text{(Integrity covered by ECDSA)} \\
S_3 &= \max(C_{31}, C_{32}) = \max(0, 1) = 1 \quad \text{(Authentication covered by ECDSA)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Now compute the index:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\rho &= \frac{1}{3}(S_1 + S_2 + S_3) \\
     &= \frac{1}{3}(1 + 1 + 1) \\
     &= \frac{3}{3} \\
     &= 1.00
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** $\rho = 1.00$ means **100 % coverage** of all three required services by the chosen mechanism set. This is the **target design metric** for any IoT security protocol.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Defeating Passive vs Active Attack Costs

The defender chooses mechanisms to **raise the attacker's cost**. Let:
- $C_{\text{passive}}(E, K)$ = cost to break encipherment $E$ under key $K$
- $C_{\text{active}}(M, T)$ = cost to forge mechanism $M$ within time $T$

For **passive attacks**, security depends on **computational hardness**:

$$
C_{\text{passive}}(E, K) \;\geq\; 2^{K_{\text{eff}}}
$$

where $K_{\text{eff}}$ is the **effective key length** in bits. For **AES-128**, $K_{\text{eff}} = 128$.

For **active attacks** (e.g., MAC forgery), security depends on **output length**:

$$
C_{\text{active}}(\text{MAC}, T) \;\geq\; 2^{L_{\text{tag}}} \Big/ T_{\text{queries}}
$$

where $L_{\text{tag}}$ is the MAC tag length in bits and $T_{\text{queries}}$ is the number of attempts allowed by the protocol.

### Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

> **Question:** Estimate the minimum operations required to brute-force a single DES block. ($K_{\text{eff}} = 56$ bits)

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{passive}}(\text{DES}) &= 2^{56} \\
&= 72{,}057{,}594{,}037{,}927{,}936 \text{ operations} \\
&\approx 7.21 \times 10^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

At $10^{9}$ operations/sec (commodity hardware):

$$
\text{Time} = \frac{7.21 \times 10^{16}}{10^{9}} = 7.21 \times 10^{7} \text{ s} \approx 2.28 \text{ years}
$$

> **Conclusion:** DES is **computationally infeasible to brute-force on a single CPU**, but trivially broken by purpose-built hardware ($2^{56}$ operations in hours). This motivated **AES with $K_{\text{eff}} \in \{128, 192, 256\}$**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Attack × Service × Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Mapping Table (Most-Asked in KTU 2024 ESE):**

| Attack | Primary Service Defeated | Primary Mechanism Used |
|---|---|---|
| Release of Message Contents | Confidentiality | Encipherment |
| Traffic Analysis | Traffic-Flow Confidentiality | Traffic Padding |
| Masquerade | Authentication | Authentication Exchange |
| Replay | Integrity (sequence) | Sequence Numbers + Encipherment |
| Modification of Messages | Data Integrity | Data Integrity + Encipherment |
| Denial of Service | Availability | Access Control + Routing Control |

> **Synthesis Formula:**
>
> $$\text{Defense in Depth} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i \cdot \sum_{j=1}^{m} M_j$$
>
> where each service $S_i$ is reinforced by **multiple** mechanisms $M_j$, ensuring that a single mechanism failure does not collapse the entire defense.

---

## 3.5 Reverse Engineering: From X.800 to TLS 1.3

We now map **every X.800 service and mechanism** to its concrete location inside the **TLS 1.3 handshake** (the de-facto IoT transport security protocol).

| X.800 Element | TLS 1.3 Implementation | KTU Mapping Insight |
|---|---|---|
| Authentication | X.509 certificate + ECDHE | M5: Authentication Exchange |
| Confidentiality | AES-256-GCM | M1: Encipherment |
| Integrity | HMAC-SHA384 inside GCM | M4: Data Integrity |
| Non-Repudiation | RSA / ECDSA signature | M2: Digital Signature |
| Access Control | Certificate-based authorization | M3: Access Control |

> **Synthesis:** TLS 1.3 is a **realization of X.800 services** using modern mechanisms. Any KTU answer that quotes X.800 must close the loop by identifying the **TLS/IoT-specific mechanism**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

> Define the **OSI Security Architecture**. List any **three security services** specified by ITU-T X.800.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The OSI Security Architecture, defined by ITU-T Recommendation X.800, is a systematic framework that provides a unified vocabulary for classifying security threats, services, and mechanisms in open systems.

**Three Security Services (2 Marks — 1 Mark for naming + 1 Mark for brief definition):**

1. **Data Confidentiality (S1):** Ensures that information is accessible only to authorized parties; protects against unauthorized disclosure.
2. **Data Integrity (S2):** Assures that data has not been altered, inserted, deleted, or replayed during transmission.
3. **Authentication (S3):** Confirms the identity of the communicating entity (peer-entity or data-origin authentication).

> *(Alternatively: Non-Repudiation, Access Control, or Availability are also valid.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Retest]**

> Differentiate between **passive** and **active** attacks with **two examples each**.

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Aspect | Passive Attack | Active Attack |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Learns/makes use of information; does NOT modify resources | Alters system resources or affects operation |
| **Detectability** | Very difficult to detect | Easier to detect, harder to prevent |
| **Examples (1 Mark each)** | (i) Release of message contents; (ii) Traffic analysis | (i) Masquerade; (ii) Replay / Modification / DoS |

**Examples (Passive — 1 Mark):**
- **Release of Message Contents:** Sniffing unencrypted BLE traffic.
- **Traffic Analysis:** Inferring sleeping patterns from smart-bulb transmission times.

**Examples (Active — 1 Mark):**
- **Masquerade:** A rogue device impersonating an IoT gateway.
- **Denial of Service:** Jamming the 2.4 GHz ISM band.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **four security services** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation) defined by ITU-T X.800, with **one appropriate mechanism** for each. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Describe the **active security attacks** defined by X.800 with **real-world IoT examples** for each. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**S1 — Data Confidentiality [1.5 Marks]:**
- Protects data from **unauthorized disclosure**.
- Sub-categories: connection confidentiality, connectionless confidentiality, selective-field confidentiality, traffic-flow confidentiality.
- **Mechanism:** **Encipherment (M1)** — e.g., AES-128-CCM on Zigbee payload.

**S2 — Data Integrity [1.5 Marks]:**
- Ensures received data is **exactly as sent** (no modification, insertion, deletion, or replay).
- Achieved via **data-origin authentication** + **sequence numbers**.
- **Mechanism:** **Data Integrity (M4)** — e.g., HMAC-SHA-256 over the payload.

**S3 — Authentication [1.5 Marks]:**
- **Peer-entity authentication:** Verifies identity of the other party in a connection.
- **Data-origin authentication:** Verifies the source of an individual message.
- **Mechanism:** **Authentication Exchange (M5)** — e.g., challenge-response via DTLS handshake with ECDSA certificates.

**S4 — Non-Repudiation [1.5 Marks]:**
- Prevents sender (or receiver) from **denying** having sent (or received) a message.
- Requires an **unforgeable third-party-verifiable proof**.
- **Mechanism:** **Digital Signature (M2)** — e.g., ECDSA signature using secp256r1 curve, verified by a CA.

**Closing synthesis (0.5 Mark):**
These four services collectively provide **Defense in Depth** for IoT systems.

---

#### Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

| Active Attack | Definition [Marks] | IoT Example [Marks] |
|---|---|---|
| **Masquerade [1.75]** | One entity pretends to be a different entity to gain unauthorized privileges. | A rogue MQTT broker impersonating AWS IoT Core, collecting sensor credentials. |
| **Replay [1.75]** | An attacker captures a valid transmission and replays it later to produce an unauthorized effect. | Replaying an old "unlock smart-lock" command to gain unauthorized entry. |
| **Modification of Messages [1.75]** | Portions of a legitimate message are altered, delayed, or reordered. | Tampering with insulin-pump dosage from "5 units" to "50 units", causing a fatal overdose. |
| **Denial of Service [1.75]** | Disrupts or prevents the intended use of the system; may target a specific host, link, or service. | Flooding the Wi-Fi channel with deauth frames, forcing IoT devices offline. |

**Concluding synthesis (–0 from marks):**
Active attacks are **mitigated by integrity, authentication, and access-control services**, as opposed to passive attacks which require **confidentiality** and **traffic padding**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Alternate]**

> **(a)** With a **neat diagram**, illustrate the relationship between **Security Attacks, Security Mechanisms, and Security Services** as per X.800. **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **Stallings Model for Network Security**. Discuss how it is applied to **secure an IoT health-monitoring system**. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
ATTACKS                SERVICES              MECHANISMS
--------               --------              -----------
Passive Attacks    ─────►Confidentiality   ◄──── Encipherment
(Msg Release,          Integrity           Data Integrity
Traffic Analysis)     Authentication      Authentication Exch.
                          Non-Repudiation     Digital Signature
Active Attacks     ─────►Access Control    ◄──── Access Control
(Masquerade,             Availability         Routing Control
 Replay,
 Modification,
 DoS)
```

*(A properly labelled Mermaid/box diagram earns full 3 marks; partial labelling earns proportional marks.)*

**Explanations (4 Marks — 1 each):**

- **Attacks (1 Mark):** Any action that compromises information security; classified into Passive and Active per X.800 §2.1.
- **Services (1 Mark):** High-level guarantees provided to users; defined as the 6 services (S1–S6) per X.800 §3.2.
- **Mechanisms (1 Mark):** Low-level primitives (encryption, signature, hashing) that implement services; per X.800 §3.3.
- **Relationship (1 Mark):** Services counter attacks; Mechanisms deliver services. A single service may require multiple mechanisms; a single mechanism may support multiple services (e.g., Digital Signature supports Integrity, Authentication, AND Non-Repudiation).

---

#### Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Stallings Model for Network Security (4 Marks):**

The Stallings model formalizes the design of a secure channel between two parties. It consists of four components:

1. **Alice (Sender)** wishes to send a message $M$ to **Bob (Receiver)** over an insecure channel.
2. **Trudy (Attacker)** may intercept, modify, replay, or fabricate messages.
3. A **trusted third party** (e.g., Key Distribution Center / Certification Authority) handles key distribution and notarization.
4. The information channel is secured via:
   - **Algorithm:** mathematical transformation (e.g., AES)
   - **Secret information:** the key $K$
   - **Protocol:** rules of interaction (e.g., TLS handshake)

**Secure-Channel Equation:**

$$
C = E_K(M), \quad M = D_K(C)
$$

where $E$ and $D$ are inverse functions under key $K$.

---

**Application to IoT Health-Monitoring System (3 Marks):**

| Component | Stallings Mapping | IoT Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Alice (Sender)** | Wearable ECG sensor on the patient | AD8232 + ESP32 |
| **Bob (Receiver)** | Hospital cloud server | AWS IoT Core |
| **Trudy** | Wireless eavesdropper / MITM | Commodity SDR + laptop |
| **Algorithm** | AES-256-GCM | Hardware-accelerated on ESP32 |
| **Secret Info** | 256-bit session key | Derived via ECDHE |
| **Protocol** | TLS 1.3 over MQTT | Mutual authentication with X.509 |
| **Trusted 3rd Party** | Certificate Authority (CA) | DigiCert / Let's Encrypt |

**Resulting services provided (1 Mark for listing):**
- **Confidentiality (S1)** — AES encryption
- **Integrity (S2)** — HMAC inside GCM
- **Authentication (S3)** — TLS handshake
- **Non-Repudiation (S4)** — ECDSA certificate signature

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in KTU ESE on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing attack and service** (e.g., writing "encryption is a security service"). Encryption is a *mechanism*; *confidentiality* is the service. **(−1 Mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting sub-categories** of confidentiality (connectionless vs. selective-field). This is a guaranteed sub-question. **(−1 Mark)**
> 3. **Listing only two active attacks** when the question asks for four. Active attacks are **exactly four**: Masquerade, Replay, Modification, DoS. **(−2 Marks)**
> 4. **Missing non-repudiation** in the service list. Many students stop at 4 services, but X.800 defines **5 + availability = 6**. **(−0.5 Marks)**
> 5. **No real-world IoT example** when the question explicitly asks for one. KTU 2024 scheme mandates "contextual engineering examples" for full marks. **(−1 to −2 Marks)**
> 6. **Diagram without arrows or labels.** A box-diagram with no arrows between attacks/services/mechanisms scores only **half** the diagram marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — OSI Security Architecture):**
>
> - **Source Standard:** ITU-T Recommendation **X.800** (1991).
> - **Three Pillars:** Attacks, Services, Mechanisms.
> - **Passive Attacks (2):** Release of Message Contents; Traffic Analysis.
> - **Active Attacks (4):** Masquerade, Replay, Modification of Messages, Denial of Service.
> - **Security Services (6):** Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-Repudiation, Access Control, Availability.
> - **Security Mechanisms (10):** Encipherment, Digital Signature, Access Control, Data Integrity, Authentication Exchange, Traffic Padding, Routing Control, Notarization, Physical Security (and the auxiliary one).
> - **Key Equation:** $M = D_K(E_K(M))$ — encipherment and decipherment are inverse functions under $K$.
> - **Service Coverage Index:** $\rho = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$; target $\rho = 1.00$ for full coverage.
> - **Mapping Mnemonic:** **"MEAD-IC PAS-RN"** → Mechanisms (M), Exchanges (E), Access (A), Data Integrity (D), Incipherment? no → actually use the table from §2.4.
> - **Defense in Depth:** $\text{Defense} = \sum S_i \cdot \sum M_j$ — every service backed by multiple mechanisms.
> - **IoT Relevance:** TLS 1.3 over MQTT = real-world realization of X.800 services.
> - **Brute-Force Key Lengths:** DES = 56-bit (broken); AES-128 = $2^{128}$ operations (~infeasible).
> - **KTU 2024 Outcome Mapping:** This topic primarily satisfies **CO1 (Understand cryptographic foundations)** and **CO2 (Apply security architecture to IoT)** of PCCIT602.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Key Cryptosystems vs. Symmetric Key Cryptosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Public Key Cryptosystems vs. Symmetric Key Cryptosystems ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Cryptosystem (Cryptographic System):** A tuple of five elements $(\mathcal{P}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$ where $\mathcal{P}$ is the plaintext space, $\mathcal{C}$ is the ciphertext space, $\mathcal{K}$ is the key space, $\mathcal{E}$ is the encryption algorithm set, and $\mathcal{D}$ is the decryption algorithm set. The choice of how keys are structured, distributed, and utilized defines the *cryptosystem type*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Symmetric Key Cryptosystem (SKC):** A cryptosystem in which the **same secret key** $K$ is used for both encryption $E_K(\cdot)$ and decryption $D_K(\cdot)$. Formally, $D_K(E_K(M)) = M$ for every plaintext $M \in \mathcal{P}$. Both parties must possess $K$ *before* any secure communication can occur. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC) / Asymmetric Cryptosystem:** A cryptosystem employing a mathematically linked key pair $(PK, SK)$, where $PK$ is freely distributed and $SK$ is kept private. Encryption uses $E_{PK}(\cdot)$ and decryption requires $D_{SK}(\cdot)$ such that $D_{SK}(E_{PK}(M)) = M$. The security relies on the computational infeasibility of deriving $SK$ from $PK$ (a **trapdoor one-way function**). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition #### 🔐 The "Locked Mailbox" Analogy for Symmetric Key Imagine two friends, **Alice** and **Bob**, sharing a single physical padlock and its key. To exchange secrets: 1. They must meet *in person* beforehand to copy the key (this is the **key distribution problem**). 2. Alice locks her message in a box using the shared key. 3. Bob uses his *same copy* of the key to unlock it. If a third person, **Eve**, steals one copy of the key, the entire system collapses. This perfectly captures the **single point of failure** in symmetric key cryptography. #### 📬 The "Public Mailbox Slot" Analogy for Public Key Now imagine Bob installs a special mailbox with two slots: - A **public slot** that anyone can drop letters into, but no one (not even the sender) can retrieve letters from. - A **private slot** (and a private key Bob alone possesses) that lets *only* Bob pull letters out. Anyone in the world can encrypt a message using Bob's public key, but **only Bob** can decrypt it. No prior secret meeting is required. This solves the key distribution problem but is computationally expensive — like sending a heavy armored truck for every letter. --- ### 1.3 Why This Matters for IoT (KTU Syllabus Context) > [!NOTE] > In **IoT (Internet of Things)**, billions of resource-constrained devices (sensors, RFID tags, smart wearables) communicate over untrusted networks. Selecting the right cryptosystem directly impacts **latency, battery life, memory footprint, and scalability**. The KTU PCCIT602 syllabus highlights this trade-off as foundational for designing lightweight security protocols (e.g., DTLS, CoAP security, LoRaWAN). **Key Insight (KTU Board Favourite):** Pure PKC is rarely used for bulk IoT data. Instead, a **hybrid approach** is employed — PKC exchanges a *symmetric session key*, and SKC encrypts the actual data stream. This balances security with the computational limits of IoT nodes. ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Symmetric Key Cryptosystem (SKC) The operational flow of SKC can be broken down into **five structured steps**: 1. **Key Generation:** A shared secret key $K$ is generated using a cryptographically secure random number generator (CSPRNG). Typical key sizes: **128 bits (AES), 192 bits, or 256 bits**. 2. **Key Distribution:** $K$ must be securely transmitted to both parties *over a trusted channel* (e.g., physical delivery, pre-shared during manufacturing). This is the **Achilles' heel** of SKC. 3. **Encryption:** Sender computes $C = E_K(M)$ using algorithms like **AES, 3DES, ChaCha20, or Blowfish**. 4. **Transmission:** Ciphertext $C$ travels over an insecure channel (Wi-Fi, LoRa, BLE). 5. **Decryption:** Receiver computes $M = D_K(C)$ using the same $K$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Block vs. Stream Ciphers (KTU 2024):** SKC divides into two families: > - **Block Ciphers:** Operate on fixed-size blocks (e.g., AES uses 128-bit blocks). Modes include **ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, GCM, CTR**. > - **Stream Ciphers:** Generate a pseudo-random keystream XORed with plaintext (e.g., **ChaCha20, RC4** — now deprecated). Better suited for low-latency IoT. --- ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC) The PKC flow is fundamentally different and follows a **Trapdoor One-Way Function** model: 1. **Key Pair Generation:** The receiver (Bob) runs an algorithm (e.g., RSA's keygen) to produce $(PK, SK)$. The function $f(x) = y$ is *easy* to compute, but inverting it (finding $x$ from $y$) without $SK$ must be *computationally infeasible*. 2. **Public Key Publication:** $PK$ is broadcast via a digital certificate (X.509) or a public directory. It is **publicly available** — no secrecy needed. 3. **Encryption:** Sender (Alice) computes $C = E_{PK}(M)$ using an algorithm like **RSA, ElGamal, or Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)**. 4. **Transmission:** $C$ is sent over the insecure channel. 5. **Decryption:** Receiver (Bob) computes $M = D_{SK}(C)$ — only possible because $SK$ acts as the **trapdoor**. > [!NOTE] > **Common PKC Families (KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus):** > | Family | Mathematical Hardness | Typical Key Size | IoT Suitability | > |---|---|---|---| > | **RSA** | Integer Factorization | 2048–4096 bits | Poor (heavy) | > | **Diffie-Hellman / ElGamal** | Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP) | 2048+ bits | Moderate | > | **ECC (ECDSA, ECIES)** | Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) | 256–384 bits | **Excellent for IoT** | > | **NTRU / Lattice-based** | Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) | ~512 bits | Emerging (post-quantum) | --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorize the following table. These are the *exact* parameters and formulas the KTU board examiner expects you to reproduce. | Property / Formula | Symmetric Key (SKC) | Public Key (PKC) | |---|---|---| | **Number of keys for $n$ users** | $\binom{n}{2} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ shared keys | $2n$ keys ($n$ public + $n$ private) | | **Key length for equivalent security** | 128 bits (AES) | 3072 bits (RSA) or 256 bits (ECC) | | **Encryption speed** | $\sim 10^3$ MB/s (fast) | $\sim 1$–$10$ MB/s (slow) | | **Mathematical primitive** | Substitution, permutation, XOR, S-boxes | Modular exponentiation, point multiplication | | **Key distribution** | **Difficult** (requires secure channel) | **Easy** (public key can be broadcast) | | **Confidentiality** | Yes | Yes | | **Authentication / Non-repudiation** | No (cannot prove who sent it) | Yes (digital signatures with $SK$) | | **Confidentiality $+$ Auth (sign-then-encrypt)** | Not natively | Yes | | **Typical algorithm examples** | AES, DES, 3DES, ChaCha20, Blowfish | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, Rabin, Paillier | | **Energy cost per op (IoT)** | **Low** (0.1 mJ/block) | **High** (50–200 mJ for 2048-bit RSA) | | **Key lifetime** | Short (per-session) | Long-term (years) | **Core Mathematical Formulas (PKC — RSA flavour):** $$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$ $$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} \equiv M^{ed} \pmod{n}$$ $$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$ where $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ is Euler's totient and $n = p \cdot q$ is the RSA modulus. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Why" of RSA:** Security rests on the fact that computing $C$ from $M$ is easy (modular exponentiation), but recovering $M$ from $C$ without knowing $d$ is equivalent to **factoring $n$ into its primes $p$ and $q$** — a problem believed (though not proven) to be intractable for classical computers. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Utility | Domain | SKC Application | PKC Application | |---|---|---| | **IoT (LoRaWAN)** | AES-128 frame payload encryption | ECC-based join procedure (network key exchange) | | **HTTPS / TLS** | AES-GCM for session data | RSA/ECC for handshake & certificate validation | | **Blockchain** | Hash functions (SHA-256) inside blocks | ECDSA digital signatures for transactions | | **Smart Cards / RFID** | 3DES or AES (lightweight) | RSA signature verification (asymmetric load) | | **Email (PGP / S/MIME)** | AES for body | RSA for session key wrapping | ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: RSA Encryption-Decryption Correctness The KTU board frequently asks: *"Prove that RSA decryption recovers the original message."* Here is the full, exhaustive derivation. **Given:** - Modulus $n = p \cdot q$ (product of two distinct large primes) - Public exponent $e$ and private exponent $d$ satisfying $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ - Plaintext $M$ where $0 \le M < n$ **Encryption:** $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ **Decryption:** $M' \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ **Goal:** Show that $M' = M$, i.e., $M^{ed} \equiv M \pmod{n}$. $$\begin{aligned} M' &\equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \\ &\equiv (M^{e})^{d} \pmod{n} \\ &\equiv M^{ed} \pmod{n} \end{aligned}$$ Since $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, we can write $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k \ge 0$. Substituting: $$M' \equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \equiv M^{1} \cdot \left(M^{\phi(n)}\right)^{k} \pmod{n}$$ **Applying Euler's Theorem:** For any $M$ coprime to $n$, $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$. Therefore: $$M' \equiv M \cdot (1)^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$ **Case Handling:** If $\gcd(M, n) \neq 1$ (i.e., $M$ is a multiple of $p$ or $q$), we can still show correctness using the **Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT)** separately modulo $p$ and modulo $q$, leveraging Fermat's Little Theorem: $M^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$ (when $p \nmid M$). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the coprimality condition $\gcd(M, n) = 1$. For the edge case, the CRT argument is what separates a 7-mark answer from a 14-mark one. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (RSA Toy Instance) This is a *classic KTU 14-mark model problem*. Let's solve it step-by-step. **Problem Setup:** - Choose $p = 5$, $q = 11$ - Compute $n = p \cdot q = 55$ - Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 4 \cdot 10 = 40$ - Choose $e = 7$ (must satisfy $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$, i.e., $\gcd(7, 40) = 1$ ✓) - Compute $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ **Step A — Find $d$ using Extended Euclidean Algorithm:** $$40 = 5 \cdot 7 + 5$$ $$7 = 1 \cdot 5 + 2$$ $$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$ $$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$ Back-substituting: $$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2$$ $$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot (7 - 1 \cdot 5) = 3 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 7$$ $$1 = 3 \cdot (40 - 5 \cdot 7) - 2 \cdot 7 = 3 \cdot 40 - 17 \cdot 7$$ So $1 \equiv -17 \cdot 7 \pmod{40}$, which means $d \equiv -17 \equiv 23 \pmod{40}$. **Verification:** $e \cdot d = 7 \cdot 23 = 161 = 4 \cdot 40 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{40}$ ✓ **Public Key:** $(e, n) = (7, 55)$ | **Private Key:** $(d, n) = (23, 55)$ **Step B — Encrypt the plaintext $M = 10$:** $$\begin{aligned} C &\equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} \\ &\equiv 10^{7} \pmod{55} \end{aligned}$$ Compute $10^{7} \pmod{55}$ via repeated squaring: $$10^{1} \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{2} \equiv 100 \equiv 100 - 1 \cdot 55 \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{4} \equiv (10^{2})^{2} \equiv 45^{2} = 2025 \pmod{55}$$ $$2025 / 55 = 36.81... \Rightarrow 2025 - 36 \cdot 55 = 2025 - 1980 = 45 \Rightarrow 10^{4} \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{7} = 10^{4} \cdot 10^{2} \cdot 10^{1} \equiv 45 \cdot 45 \cdot 10 \pmod{55}$$ $$45 \cdot 45 = 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55} \text{ (as computed above)}$$ $$45 \cdot 10 = 450 \pmod{55}$$ $$450 / 55 = 8.18... \Rightarrow 450 - 8 \cdot 55 = 450 - 440 = 10$$ So $C \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$. (Coincidence — $M=10$ and $C=10$ because $10^{7} \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$. This is fine for the demo.) **Step C — Decrypt:** $$\begin{aligned} M' &\equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} \\ &\equiv 10^{23} \pmod{55} \end{aligned}$$ Using repeated squaring with binary exponent $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = 10111_2$: $$10^{1} \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{2} \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{4} \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{8} \equiv 45^{2} \equiv 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$10^{16} \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ So $10^{23} = 10^{16} \cdot 10^{4} \cdot 10^{2} \cdot 10^{1} \equiv 45 \cdot 45 \cdot 45 \cdot 10 \pmod{55}$ $$45 \cdot 45 = 2025 \equiv 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$45 \cdot 45 = 45 \pmod{55}$$ $$45 \cdot 10 = 450 \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$$ Thus $M' \equiv 10 \pmod{55}$ ✓ — **Decryption successful.** --- ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Exam Ready) | Evaluation Criterion | Symmetric Key Cryptosystem | Public Key Cryptosystem | |---|---|---| | **Key Management Scalability (n users)** | $O(n^2)$ — quadratic blowup | $O(n)$ — linear | | **Computational Cost** | Low (linear in data size) | High ($\sim 1000\times$ slower per byte) | | **Memory Footprint (key storage)** | Small ($\sim 16$ bytes for AES) | Large (256+ bytes for ECC, 256+ for RSA) | | **Bandwidth Overhead** | Negligible (ciphertext $\approx$ plaintext) | High (RSA ciphertext is 256+ bytes regardless of $M$) | | **Confidentiality** | ✓ | ✓ | | **Integrity (via MAC)** | ✓ (HMAC) | ✓ (digital signatures) | | **Authentication** | Limited (shared secret) | Strong (non-repudiation via signatures) | | **Quantum Vulnerability** | Grover's algorithm gives $\sqrt{N}$ speedup (use AES-256) | Shor's algorithm breaks RSA/ECC in polynomial time | | **IoT Suitability** | **High** (energy-efficient) | **Low–Moderate** (offload to gateway/edge) | ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Symmetric Key Cryptosystem — Communication Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Alice
Plaintext M] --> B[Symmetric
Encryption E_K] K1[Shared Secret Key K] --> B B --> C[Ciphertext C
over insecure channel] C --> D[Symmetric
Decryption D_K] K2[Shared Secret Key K] --> D D --> E[Bob
Recovered M] EVE[Eve Intercepts C
but cannot decrypt
without K] -.-> C ``` ### 4.2 Public Key Cryptosystem — Communication Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Alice
Plaintext M] --> B[Public Key
Encryption E_PK] PK1[Bob's Public Key PK
broadcast freely] --> B B --> C[Ciphertext C] C --> D[Private Key
Decryption D_SK] SK1[Bob's Private Key SK
kept secret] --> D D --> E[Bob
Recovered M] EVE2[Eve has PK but cannot
invert without SK] -.-> C G[Key Pair Generation
PK, SK] --> PK1 G --> SK1 ``` ### 4.3 Hybrid Cryptosystem — IoT Practical Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[IoT Sensor Node] --> P1[Step 1: Generate
random session key Ks] P1 --> P2[Step 2: Encrypt Ks with
Gateway's Public Key PK_GW] P2 --> T1[Transmit E_PK_GW over
untrusted LoRa/Wi-Fi] T1 --> R1[Gateway decrypts
Ks using SK_GW] R1 --> R2[Step 3: Symmetric
channel established] R2 --> R3[Step 4: Bulk data
encrypted with AES Ks] R3 --> APP[Cloud Application
receives payload] ``` ### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SKC[SYMMETRIC KEY CRYPTOSYSTEM] SK1[Single Shared Key K] SK2[Fast Block and Stream Ciphers] SK3[Quadratic Key Growth O n squared] end subgraph PKC[PUBLIC KEY CRYPTOSYSTEM] PK1[Key Pair PK and SK] PK2[Trapdoor One Way Function] PK3[Linear Key Growth O n] end SKC --> HYBRID[Hybrid Model for IoT] PKC --> HYBRID HYBRID --> IOT[Secure Lightweight
IoT Communication] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams (KTU Exam Tip):** In the KTU 2024 board exam, drawing a clean block diagram showing the flow `Plaintext → Encryption (with key) → Ciphertext → Decryption (with key) → Plaintext` for *both* SKC and PKC side by side is worth **2–3 marks** by itself. Label every arrow clearly. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** > *Compare Symmetric and Asymmetric key cryptosystems based on (i) number of keys used, and (ii) key distribution mechanism.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(i) Number of keys:** SKC uses a *single shared secret key* $K$ for both encryption and decryption. For $n$ users, the total keys required are $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ pairs. PKC uses a *key pair* $(PK, SK)$ per user, totaling $2n$ keys. **(1.5 marks)** - **(ii) Key Distribution:** SKC requires a *secure out-of-band channel* (physical courier, pre-shared during manufacturing) to exchange $K$ — a major weakness. PKC allows $PK$ to be transmitted over an *insecure channel* because only $SK$ must remain secret. **(1.5 marks)** #### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** > *Define a trapdoor one-way function. Why is it central to public key cryptography?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **trapdoor one-way function** $f(\cdot)$ is a function that is *easy to compute* in the forward direction ($y = f(x)$) but *computationally infeasible to invert* without a secret piece of information (the **trapdoor**), yet *trivial to invert* with it. **(2 marks)** It is central to PKC because it allows public encryption (anyone can compute $E_{PK}$) while restricting decryption to the holder of the trapdoor (private key $SK$). RSA relies on the hardness of factoring $n = p \cdot q$ — knowing $p, q$ is the trapdoor that enables efficient decryption. **(1 mark)** --- ### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a Public Key Cryptosystem. List any **four** differences between Public Key and Symmetric Key Cryptosystems. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** In an RSA system, a user chooses $p = 7$ and $q = 13$. Compute $n$, $\phi(n)$, and select a suitable public exponent $e$. Find the corresponding private exponent $d$. Encrypt the message $M = 5$ and show that decryption recovers the original plaintext. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — PKC Architecture (7 marks):** A Public Key Cryptosystem operates as follows: 1. **Key Generation Center / User** generates a key pair $(PK, SK)$. 2. **Public Key Directory** stores $PK$ (e.g., X.509 certificate server). 3. **Sender (Alice)** fetches Bob's $PK$, computes $C = E_{PK}(M)$. 4. **Insecure Channel** transmits $C$. 5. **Receiver (Bob)** uses his secret $SK$ to compute $M = D_{SK}(C)$. **Block diagram (textual for answer sheet):** ``` [KeyGen (PK,SK)] → PK → [Public Directory] → [Alice] Alice → E_PK(M) = C → [Channel] → Bob → D_SK(C) = M ↑ SK ``` **Four Differences (4 marks — 1 each):** | # | Criterion | SKC | PKC | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Keys | One shared key | Key pair (public + private) | | 2 | Speed | Fast (AES) | Slow (RSA/ECC) | | 3 | Authentication | Not inherent | Provides digital signatures | | 4 | Key distribution | Hard (secure channel) | Easy (public broadcast) | **Part (b) — RSA Numerical (7 marks):** Given $p = 7$, $q = 13$: $$n = p \cdot q = 7 \cdot 13 = 91$$ $$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 6 \cdot 12 = 72$$ Choose $e = 5$ (verify $\gcd(5, 72) = 1$ ✓). Find $d$ such that $5d \equiv 1 \pmod{72}$. Using extended Euclidean: $$72 = 14 \cdot 5 + 2$$ $$5 = 2 \cdot 2 + 1$$ $$2 = 2 \cdot 1 + 0$$ Back-substitute: $$1 = 5 - 2 \cdot 2 = 5 - 2 \cdot (72 - 14 \cdot 5) = 29 \cdot 5 - 2 \cdot 72$$ So $d \equiv 29 \pmod{72}$. **Verify:** $5 \cdot 29 = 145 = 2 \cdot 72 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{72}$ ✓ **Encrypt $M = 5$:** $$C \equiv 5^{5} \pmod{91}$$ $5^{2} = 25$, $5^{4} = 625$. Then $625 \pmod{91}$: $91 \cdot 6 = 546$, so $625 - 546 = 79$. Thus $5^{4} \equiv 79 \pmod{91}$. $5^{5} = 5^{4} \cdot 5 = 79 \cdot 5 = 395$. Then $395 \pmod{91}$: $91 \cdot 4 = 364$, so $395 - 364 = 31$. $$C \equiv 31 \pmod{91}$$ **Decrypt $C = 31$:** $$M' \equiv 31^{29} \pmod{91}$$ Apply CRT — split mod 91 into mod 7 and mod 13: *Modulo 7:* $31 \equiv 3 \pmod{7}$. Need $3^{29} \pmod{7}$. By Fermat, $3^{6} \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$. $29 = 4 \cdot 6 + 5$, so $3^{29} \equiv 3^{5} \pmod{7}$. $3^{2} = 9 \equiv 2$, $3^{4} \equiv 4$, $3^{5} \equiv 12 \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$. So $M' \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$. *Modulo 13:* $31 \equiv 5 \pmod{13}$. Need $5^{29} \pmod{13}$. By Fermat, $5^{12} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. $29 = 2 \cdot 12 + 5$, so $5^{29} \equiv 5^{5} \pmod{13}$. $5^{2} = 25 \equiv 12 \equiv -1 \pmod{13}$. $5^{4} \equiv 1 \pmod{13}$. $5^{5} \equiv 5 \pmod{13}$. So $M' \equiv 5 \pmod{13}$. *CRT:* $M' \equiv 5 \pmod{7}$ and $M' \equiv 5 \pmod{13}$. By CRT, $M' \equiv 5 \pmod{91}$ ✓ **Decryption recovered the original plaintext $M = 5$.** (1 mark for verification) --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **(a)** Explain the operational steps of a Symmetric Key Cryptosystem. Discuss the **key distribution problem** and how Public Key Cryptography resolves it. **(7 marks)** > > **(b)** A symmetric system uses AES-128. The system has **50 IoT sensor nodes** that need to communicate securely with a central gateway. (i) How many unique symmetric keys are required if all nodes communicate directly with each other? (ii) Calculate the equivalent RSA key length (in bits) required to provide comparable security to AES-128. (iii) If the gateway has a public key $PK_{GW}$ and a node generates a random session key $K_s$, describe the hybrid protocol to securely transmit sensor data. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution **Part (a) — SKC Operations + Key Distribution (7 marks):** *Operational steps (4 marks):* 1. A trusted party or key agreement protocol generates a shared key $K$. 2. $K$ is securely distributed to both sender and receiver over an *out-of-band secure channel* (e.g., physical USB exchange, hardware security module, pre-provisioned at manufacture). 3. Sender computes $C = E_K(M)$ using AES/3DES/ChaCha20. 4. $C$ is transmitted over the insecure IoT channel (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa). 5. Receiver computes $M = D_K(C)$ using the same $K$. *Key Distribution Problem (2 marks):* For $n$ parties, $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ unique keys are required, and each must be transmitted secretly. Scaling is impossible in IoT with thousands of devices — and if *any* key is compromised, the entire conversation is exposed. *How PKC Resolves It (1 mark):* PKC eliminates the need for a shared secret. Each user publishes $PK$ openly; only $SK$ is secret. To establish a secure channel, the sender encrypts a *random session key* with the receiver's $PK$. The receiver decrypts using $SK$, and both parties now share a fresh $K_s$ for symmetric encryption. This is the foundation of **TLS handshakes** used in HTTPS and DTLS for CoAP in IoT. **Part (b) — IoT Scenario (7 marks):** **(i) Symmetric key count for 50 nodes** (1 mark): $$\text{Keys} = \binom{50}{2} = \frac{50 \cdot 49}{2} = 1225 \text{ unique shared keys}$$ **(ii) Equivalent RSA key length for AES-128** (2 marks): According to NIST cryptographic strength guidelines, AES-128 (128-bit security) is equivalent to: $$\text{RSA key length} = 3072 \text{ bits}$$ (For reference: AES-192 → 7680-bit RSA; AES-256 → 15360-bit RSA.) **(iii) Hybrid Protocol Description** (4 marks): ``` Step 1: Sensor generates random 128-bit session key Ks Step 2: Sensor encrypts Ks: C1 = E_PK_GW(Ks) Step 3: Sensor transmits C1 to gateway Step 4: Gateway decrypts: Ks = D_SK_GW(C1) Step 5: Both now share secret Ks (symmetric channel) Step 6: Sensor encrypts bulk data M: C2 = AES_Ks(M) Step 7: Gateway decrypts: M = AES_Ks^(-1)(C2) ``` This hybrid model uses **PKC's key-exchange advantage** with **SKC's speed advantage** — ideal for resource-constrained IoT devices. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing the modulus and totient** in RSA: Students often write $n = (p-1)(q-1)$ instead of $n = p \cdot q$. Remember: $n$ is the *modulus*; $\phi(n)$ is the totient. (**−1 to −2 marks**) > 2. **Skipping the verification step** in RSA: After computing $d$, *always* show $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. Examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for this. > 3. **Ignoring the gcd condition:** If chosen $e$ is not coprime to $\phi(n)$, the entire RSA fails. State $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ explicitly. (**−1 mark**) > 4. **Wrong combinatorial formula:** SKC needs $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ *pairs*, not $n(n-1)$. One pair = 2 endpoints. (**−1 mark**) > 5. **In Part B numerical problems**, students often forget to state units (bits) or forget to apply CRT for large moduli, leading to arithmetic errors. Use CRT or Fermat's Little Theorem when $n > 100$. > 6. **Confusing PKC with digital signatures:** PKC *can* provide confidentiality (encrypt with $PK$) and authentication (sign with $SK$), but a *signature alone* does not encrypt the message. --- ### 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **SKC** = *one shared secret key*; **PKC** = *key pair* $(PK, SK)$. - SKC is **fast but suffers from key distribution**; PKC is **slow but solves key distribution** and provides authentication. - For $n$ users: SKC needs $\frac{n(n-1)}{2}$ keys; PKC needs $2n$ keys. - RSA core formulas: $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$, $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$, with $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. - RSA security = **integer factorization hardness**; ECC security = **elliptic curve discrete log hardness**; ECC uses much shorter keys (~256 bits ≈ 3072-bit RSA). - **IoT reality:** Pure PKC is rarely used for bulk data. The standard pattern is **hybrid cryptography** (PKC for key exchange + SKC for data). - **Block vs. stream ciphers** matter in IoT: AES (block) and ChaCha20 (stream) are both relevant. - **AES-128 ≈ 3072-bit RSA** in security strength (NIST equivalence). - Always verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ in RSA key generation. - In RSA decryption, use **CRT (Chinese Remainder Theorem)** to simplify large modular exponentiations. - **Quantum threat:** Both RSA and ECC are broken by Shor's algorithm; symmetric ciphers survive (Grover gives only a quadratic speedup — double the key size). - **Trapdoor one-way function** is the *conceptual foundation* of all PKC. - KTU exam expects: definitions (2 marks) + diagrams (2–3 marks) + comparison table (3 marks) + numerical (7 marks in Part B).

topics.title: Encrypting Communication Channels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Encrypting Communication Channels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Encrypted Communication Channel (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **Encrypted Communication Channel** is a cryptographically protected logical pathway established between two or more communicating entities (e.g., IoT device ↔ gateway ↔ cloud server) over an untrusted physical network, where **confidentiality**, **integrity**, **authenticity**, and optionally **forward secrecy** of the transmitted data are guaranteed using a negotiated **cipher suite** consisting of a **key exchange algorithm**, **authentication mechanism**, **bulk encryption algorithm**, and **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCIT602) context, encryption of communication channels is a **prerequisite layer** above physical (PHY) and Medium Access Control (MAC) layers of IoT protocol stacks such as **MQTT**, **CoAP**, **HTTP**, and **LoRaWAN**. The channel is "logical" — it does not replace physical wiring, but it overlays cryptographic protection onto every bit transmitted.

### Constituent Pillars of a Secure Channel

| Pillar | Cryptographic Primitive | Typical IoT Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Symmetric encryption | **AES-128/256** (CCM, GCM, CBC) |
| **Integrity** | Hashing / MAC | **SHA-256, HMAC-SHA256, Poly1305** |
| **Authentication** | Digital Signature / PSK | **ECDSA, RSA-PSS, Pre-Shared Key** |
| **Key Exchange** | Asymmetric / KEM | **ECDH (Curve25519), DH, RSA-OAEP** |

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT-Specific Keyword — Cipher Suite:**
> A *cipher suite* is a named bundle (e.g., `TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256`) that specifies *exactly* which algorithms will be used. The communicating peers negotiate one common suite before any payload data is exchanged.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Armored Tunnel"

Imagine two friends (Alice and Bob) standing on opposite cliffs, wishing to pass secret letters back and forth across a public valley where eavesdroppers (Eve) roam freely.

- **Without encryption:** Alice throws paper airplanes across the valley — anyone can catch and read them.
- **With an encrypted channel:** A **magical, transparent armored pneumatic tube** is constructed *only between Alice and Bob*. The tube has two ends: **Alice's end has a lock only Bob can open, and Bob's end has a lock only Alice can open.** Every letter placed inside is automatically encrypted before it travels, and authenticated upon arrival.

In IoT terms:
- **Alice** = Sensor node / wearable device
- **Bob** = IoT cloud broker / gateway
- **Armored tube** = TLS / DTLS session
- **Magic locks** = Symmetric session keys (`K_AB`)
- **Construction of the tube** = TLS Handshake (key exchange + authentication)
- **Letters inside the tube** = Application payloads (temperature, telemetry, commands)

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition for Students:** Encryption of a channel is NOT the same as encrypting a *single message*. Channel encryption establishes a **persistent, stateful cryptographic context** (session keys, sequence numbers, cipher state) that protects **all future traffic** until the session is torn down.

## 1.3 Why is Channel Encryption Critical for IoT? (KTU 2024 Syllabus Emphasis)

IoT networks are uniquely vulnerable because:

1. **Constrained devices** often lack physical security (deployed in public spaces).
2. **Wireless mediums** (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee) are broadcast — trivially sniffable.
3. **Long field-life** (10–20 years for sensors) means static keys rot; forward secrecy becomes essential.
4. **Heterogeneous protocols** (MQTT/TCP, CoAP/UDP) demand different channel-security solutions (**TLS vs DTLS**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Module 1 Anchor Statement:**
> *Encrypting communication channels is the foundational countermeasure against passive eavesdropping, active man-in-the-middle (MITM), replay, and injection attacks in any IoT deployment. Without it, all higher-layer IoT application security is moot.*

## 1.4 Physical / Standard Constants & Metrics (Highlight Box)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardised Channel Encryption Protocols (per IETF / NIST):**
> - **TLS 1.3** — RFC 8446 (deprecates TLS 1.0/1.1; mandatory in KTU 2024 references)
> - **DTLS 1.3** — RFC 9147 (TLS equivalent for UDP — used by CoAP)
> - **IPsec** — RFC 4301 (network-layer channel protection)
> - **SSH** — RFC 4253 (administrative channel)
> - **MACsec** — IEEE 802.1AE (Layer-2 channel protection)
> - **Noise Framework** — modern IoT-friendly channel protocol

**Standardised elliptic curves used in IoT key exchange:**
- **Curve25519** (128-bit security, ~256-bit key)
- **secp256r1** (NIST P-256)
- **secp521r1** (high-assurance deployments)

**Symmetric cipher key sizes commonly used in IoT channels:** **AES-128** (recommended baseline) and **AES-256** (high-value assets).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Layered Architecture of an Encrypted Channel

A KTU-grade analysis separates the channel encryption stack into **four logical planes**:

### Plane 1 — Key Exchange Plane
- Purpose: Establish a **fresh shared secret** between two parties over an insecure medium.
- Algorithms: **Diffie-Hellman (DH)**, **ECDH (Curve25519)**, **RSA-OAEP key transport**.
- KTU 2024 emphasis: **ECDH with Curve25519** is preferred due to small key size + high speed on 8/32-bit MCUs.

### Plane 2 — Authentication Plane
- Purpose: Defeat **MITM** by proving the identity of the peer.
- Methods:
  - **Asymmetric certificates** (X.509 — heavy for IoT).
  - **Pre-Shared Keys (PSK)** — common in constrained IoT (LoRaWAN, BLE).
  - **Raw Public Keys (RPK)** — middle ground used in CoAP/DTLS.
  - **EAP-PSK, EAP-TLS** — used in enterprise IoT (Wi-Fi).

### Plane 3 — Bulk Encryption Plane
- Purpose: Encrypt every application byte with a **symmetric cipher** (much faster than asymmetric).
- Modes: **AES-GCM** (AEAD — provides both confidentiality + integrity in one pass), **AES-CCM**, **ChaCha20-Poly1305** (ARM Cortex-M optimized).

### Plane 4 — Session / State Management Plane
- Purpose: Track sequence numbers, perform re-keying, and tear down sessions safely.
- TLS 1.3 mandates **forward secrecy**: every session uses ephemeral keys, so compromise of long-term keys cannot decrypt past traffic.

## 2.2 Why a Hybrid (Asymmetric + Symmetric) Design?

> [!NOTE]
> **The Central Trade-off of Channel Encryption:**
> - **Asymmetric cryptography** is *slow* (RSA-2048 ≈ 1000× slower than AES-128) but solves **key agreement over an insecure channel**.
> - **Symmetric cryptography** is *fast* but requires a **pre-shared secret**.
>
> **Solution:** Use **asymmetric crypto ONLY to agree on a fresh symmetric key**, then use that symmetric key for the bulk of the session. This is called the **hybrid paradigm** and is the universal design of TLS, DTLS, SSH, IPsec, and Noise.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas constitute the complete mathematical toolkit for solving Module-1 numerical problems on encrypted channels.** Every expression is **mathematically verifiable** and aligned with KTU 2024 expectations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Domain & Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Diffie-Hellman shared secret | $K_{AB} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}$ | $p$ is a large prime ($\geq 2048$ bits for security) | Discrete-log hard |
| 2 | ECDH shared secret | $K_{AB} = d_A \cdot Q_B = d_B \cdot Q_A$ (point on elliptic curve) | Curve over finite field $\mathbb{F}_p$ | 256-bit EC key ≈ 3072-bit RSA |
| 3 | RSA-OAEP key transport | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ , $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | $n = p \cdot q$, $\vert n \vert \geq 2048$ bits | KTU 2024 prefers ECDH over RSA key transport |
| 4 | AES-128 block size | $B = 128$ bits = **16 bytes** | Fixed | Counter mode requires 128-bit nonce |
| 5 | GCM authentication tag | $T = \text{GHASH}_H(A \Vert C) \oplus E_K(J_0)$ | 128-bit tag typical | AEAD output |
| 6 | TLS 1.3 HKDF-Expand | $PRK = \text{HKDF-Extract}(salt, IKM)$ | Hash: SHA-256 or SHA-384 | Derives many sub-keys |
| 7 | Forward secrecy (PFS) | For session $i$: $K_i \not\leftarrow \{K_1,\ldots,K_{i-1}\}$ | Ephemeral keys per session | Achieved via (EC)DHE |
| 8 | Security bits vs key length | AES-128 ⇒ **128 bits** ; RSA-2048 ⇒ **~112 bits** | NIST SP 800-57 | EC-256 ⇒ ~128 bits |
| 9 | Cipher suite string | `TLS_ECDHE_ECDSA_WITH_AES_128_GCM_SHA256` | TLS naming convention | KE / Auth / Cipher / Hash |
| 10 | MAC size (HMAC-SHA256) | $L = 256$ bits = **32 bytes** | Truncated to 128 bits in TLS | GCM tag $\rightarrow$ 128 bits |
| 11 | HMAC definition | $\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big((K \oplus opad) \Vert H((K \oplus ipad) \Vert M)\Big)$ | Iterated hash | RFC 2104 |
| 12 | Entropy of key | $H(K) = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ bits | $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ = keyspace size | For AES-128: $H = 128$ bits |
| 13 | Birthday bound for collisions | $\approx \sqrt{2^{n}} = 2^{n/2}$ for $n$-bit MAC | Probability $0.63$ | Why MAC $\geq 128$ bits |
| 14 | Replay window | Sliding window of size $W \leq 2^{32}-1$ | TLS record sequence | DTLS uses sliding window |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall — Symbol `|n|` in tables:**
> The pipe symbol is *forbidden* inside Markdown table cells because it breaks column parsing. Always write $\vert n \vert$ or $\mid n \mid$ for absolute value / cardinality inside any table. Examiners deduct **1 mark** for malformed tables in KTU answer scripts when they request "expressions in tabular form".

## 2.4 Operational Flow of an Encrypted Channel (Textual Walkthrough)

### Phase A — Handshake (one-time per session)
1. **ClientHello** → client sends supported cipher suites + ephemeral ECDH public key.
2. **ServerHello** → server selects one suite, sends its ECDH public key + certificate.
3. **Key derivation** → both sides compute the **shared secret** $Z = d_c \cdot Q_s$.
4. **HKDF** → $Z$ is expanded into six sub-keys:
   - `$client_write_key$`, `$server_write_key$` (encryption)
   - `$client_write_IV$`, `$server_write_IV$` (nonces)
   - `$client_write_MAC_key$`, `$server_write_MAC_key$` (integrity — folded into AEAD in TLS 1.3)
5. **Finished** → both sides send a MAC over the entire handshake transcript (binds the keys to the identity).

### Phase B — Bulk Data Transfer (record layer)
- Each record is encrypted with **AES-GCM** using the appropriate directional key + IV.
- The GCM **authentication tag** (16 bytes) is appended, providing AEAD.

### Phase C — Session Resumption / Re-keying
- **Session ticket** (encrypted blob) or **PSK** allows fast reconnection without full handshake.
- Re-keying happens every $N$ bytes or $T$ seconds to limit key exposure.

### Phase D — Teardown
- `close_notify` alert is sent in the **encrypted channel itself** to prevent truncation attacks.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where This Matters

- **Smart meters (Advanced Metering Infrastructure — AMI):** DTLS protects meter-to-collector links.
- **Connected vehicles:** TLS 1.3 secures V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) telemetry.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC-UA uses its own channel encryption built on top of TLS.
- **Healthcare wearables:** End-to-end TLS prevents leakage of patient vitals over public Wi-Fi.
- **Smart home:** MQTT over TLS 8883 vs MQTT plaintext 1883 — KTU 2024 explicitly contrasts these.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange (Classical)

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose of Derivation:** To demonstrate *mathematically* how two parties can agree on a shared secret over an *eavesdropped* channel, and why the eavesdropper cannot compute it.

### Setup (Public Parameters)
Let $p$ be a large safe prime, and $g$ a generator of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Both $p$ and $g$ are **public** — known to all parties including Eve.

### Step 1 — Alice's Action
Alice secretly chooses a private exponent $a \in \{2, 3, \ldots, p-2\}$.

$$
A \equiv g^{a} \pmod{p}
$$

Alice sends $A$ over the public channel.

### Step 2 — Bob's Action
Bob secretly chooses a private exponent $b \in \{2, 3, \ldots, p-2\}$.

$$
B \equiv g^{b} \pmod{p}
$$

Bob sends $B$ over the public channel.

### Step 3 — Both Compute the Shared Secret
Alice receives $B$ and computes:

$$
K_{AB} \equiv B^{a} \equiv \left(g^{b}\right)^{a} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}
$$

Bob receives $A$ and computes:

$$
K_{AB} \equiv A^{b} \equiv \left(g^{a}\right)^{b} \equiv g^{ab} \pmod{p}
$$

Both arrive at the **same shared secret** $K_{AB}$.

### Step 4 — Why Eve Cannot Compute $K_{AB}$
Eve observes $(p, g, A, B)$. To recover $K_{AB}$ she would need either $a$ or $b$ — that is, she must solve the **Computational Diffie-Hellman Problem (CDHP)**, which is equivalent to the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**:

$$
\text{Given } g, A = g^{a} \pmod{p}, \text{ find } a.
$$

For well-chosen primes ($p \geq 2048$ bits) and generators, the best known algorithms (Number Field Sieve) require sub-exponential but practically infeasible time. Hence, channel secrecy holds.

### Step 5 — DH on Small Toy Prime (For Hand Calculation in KTU Exam)
Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$.

Alice picks $a = 6$:

$$
A = 5^{6} \pmod{23} = 15625 \pmod{23}
$$

Compute: $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$, $5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$, $5^6 \equiv 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \equiv 4 \cdot 2 = 8 \pmod{23}$.

So $A = 8$. Alice sends $8$.

Bob picks $b = 15$:

$$
B = 5^{15} \pmod{23}
$$

We compute: $5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1$.

$5^8 = (5^4)^2 = 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23}$

$5^{15} = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640 \pmod{23}$

$640 = 23 \cdot 27 + 19 \Rightarrow 640 \equiv 19 \pmod{23}$.

So $B = 19$. Bob sends $19$.

Shared secret:
Alice: $K_{AB} = 19^6 \pmod{23}$.

Compute: $19^2 = 361 = 23 \cdot 15 + 16 \equiv 16 \pmod{23}$.

$19^4 \equiv 16^2 = 256 = 23 \cdot 11 + 3 \equiv 3 \pmod{23}$.

$19^6 \equiv 19^4 \cdot 19^2 \equiv 3 \cdot 16 = 48 = 23 \cdot 2 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

Bob: $K_{AB} = 8^{15} \pmod{23}$.

$8^2 = 64 = 23 \cdot 2 + 18 \equiv 18 \pmod{23}$.

$8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 = 23 \cdot 14 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

$8^8 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$.

$8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8 = 1152$.

$1152 = 23 \cdot 50 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$.

Both obtain $K_{AB} = 2$. ✔ Verification successful.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — ECDH on a Toy Elliptic Curve

> [!NOTE]
> **Curve Used:** $E: y^2 = x^3 + 7 \pmod{17}$ (a small toy curve for illustration — KTU exam-friendly).

Generator point $G = (2, 5)$.

### Step 1 — Alice's Key Pair
Alice picks $d_A = 5$.

$Q_A = d_A \cdot G = 5G$.

Compute via double-and-add:

$2G = G + G$. With $G = (2,5)$ on $y^2 = x^3 + 7 \pmod{17}$:

Slope $\lambda = (3x_1^2 + a) / (2y_1) = (3 \cdot 4 + 0) / (2 \cdot 5) = 12 / 10 \pmod{17}$.

$12 \cdot 10^{-1} \pmod{17}$. $10 \cdot 12 = 120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1$, so $10^{-1} \equiv 12 \pmod{17}$.

$\lambda \equiv 12 \cdot 12 = 144 = 8 \cdot 17 + 8 \equiv 8 \pmod{17}$.

$x_3 = \lambda^2 - 2x_1 = 64 - 4 = 60 = 3 \cdot 17 + 9 \equiv 9 \pmod{17}$.

$y_3 = \lambda(x_1 - x_3) - y_1 = 8(2 - 9) - 5 = -56 - 5 = -61 = -61 + 4 \cdot 17 = 7 \pmod{17}$.

So $2G = (9, 7)$.

$3G = 2G + G = (9, 7) + (2, 5)$:

$\lambda = (y_2 - y_1) / (x_2 - x_1) = (5 - 7) / (2 - 9) = -2 / -7 = 2/7 \pmod{17}$.

$7^{-1} \pmod{17}$: $7 \cdot 5 = 35 \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$, so $7^{-1} \equiv 5$.

$\lambda = 2 \cdot 5 = 10 \pmod{17}$.

$x_3 = 100 - 11 = 89 = 5 \cdot 17 + 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$.

$y_3 = 10(9 - 4) - 7 = 50 - 7 = 43 = 2 \cdot 17 + 9 \equiv 9 \pmod{17}$.

So $3G = (4, 9)$.

$4G = 2 \cdot (2G) = 2 \cdot (9, 7)$:

$\lambda = (3 \cdot 81) / (2 \cdot 7) = 243 / 14 \pmod{17}$.

$243 = 14 \cdot 17 + 5 \equiv 5 \pmod{17}$.

$14^{-1} \pmod{17}$: $14 \cdot 11 = 154 = 9 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1$, so $14^{-1} \equiv 11$.

$\lambda = 5 \cdot 11 = 55 = 3 \cdot 17 + 4 \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$.

$x_3 = 16 - 18 = -2 \equiv 15 \pmod{17}$.

$y_3 = 4(9 - 15) - 7 = -24 - 7 = -31 \equiv 3 \pmod{17}$.

So $4G = (15, 3)$.

$5G = 4G + G = (15, 3) + (2, 5)$:

$\lambda = (5 - 3)/(2 - 15) = 2 / -13 \pmod{17}$.

$-13 \equiv 4 \pmod{17}$. $4^{-1} \pmod{17}$: $4 \cdot 13 = 52 = 3 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1$, so $4^{-1} \equiv 13$.

$\lambda = 2 \cdot 13 = 26 = 9 \pmod{17}$.

$x_3 = 81 - 17 = 64 = 3 \cdot 17 + 13 \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.

$y_3 = 9(15 - 13) - 3 = 18 - 3 = 15 \pmod{17}$.

So $Q_A = (13, 15)$.

Alice publishes $Q_A$.

### Step 2 — Bob's Key Pair
Bob picks $d_B = 7$. By analogous point multiplication, $Q_B = 7G$.

The student is encouraged to verify: $Q_B = 7G = 3G + 4G = (4,9) + (15,3)$.

$\lambda = (3-9)/(15-4) = -6/11 \pmod{17}$.

$-6 \equiv 11 \pmod{17}$. $11^{-1} \pmod{17}$: $11 \cdot 14 = 154 \equiv 1$, so $11^{-1} \equiv 14$.

$\lambda = 11 \cdot 14 = 154 = 9 \cdot 17 + 1 \equiv 1 \pmod{17}$.

$x_3 = 1 - 19 = -18 \equiv 16 \pmod{17}$.

$y_3 = 1(4 - 16) - 9 = -12 - 9 = -21 \equiv 13 \pmod{17}$.

So $Q_B = (16, 13)$.

### Step 3 — Shared Secret Computation
Alice computes $S = d_A \cdot Q_B = 5 \cdot (16, 13) = 5Q_B$.

Bob computes $S' = d_B \cdot Q_A = 7 \cdot (13, 15) = 7Q_A$.

By the **associativity of elliptic curve point multiplication**, $5 \cdot (7G) = (5 \cdot 7) G = 7 \cdot (5G)$, so $S = S' = 35G$.

The shared point's $x$-coordinate becomes the key material $K = x_S$.

This derivation confirms **mathematically** that ECDH provides identical shared secrets with vastly smaller key sizes than classical DH.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — HKDF Key Derivation (TLS 1.3 Pipeline)

> [!NOTE]
> **HKDF** (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function, RFC 5869) turns the raw DH shared secret $Z$ into multiple cryptographically independent sub-keys.

### Step 1 — Extract Phase

$$
PRK = \text{HKDF-Extract}(salt, IKM) = \text{HMAC}_{salt}(IKM)
$$

where $IKM = Z$ (the DH/ECDH shared secret), and $salt$ is a (possibly empty) random value.

### Step 2 — Expand Phase

For each desired output block $i \in \{1, 2, \ldots, L\}$:

$$
OKM_i = \text{HKDF-Expand}(PRK, info, L) = T_1 \Vert T_2 \Vert \ldots \Vert T_L
$$

where:

$$
T_1 = \text{HMAC}_{PRK}(info \Vert 0x01)
$$

$$
T_2 = \text{HMAC}_{PRK}(T_1 \Vert info \Vert 0x02)
$$

$$
T_i = \text{HMAC}_{PRK}(T_{i-1} \Vert info \Vert 0x0i)
$$

The TLS 1.3 `key_schedule` uses HKDF with `label = "tls13 " + purpose` to generate:
- `$client_handshake_traffic_secret$`
- `$server_handshake_traffic_secret$`
- `$client_application_traffic_secret$`
- `$server_application_traffic_secret$`
- `$resumption_secret$`

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of IoT Channel Encryption Protocols

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix below is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 Part-B 14-mark questions asking for "Compare and contrast TLS, DTLS, and IPsec in IoT".

| Property | **TLS 1.3** | **DTLS 1.3** | **IPsec (ESP)** | **SSH-2** | **Noise XX** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | IP (L3) | TCP | TCP/UDP |
| **Used by IoT proto** | MQTT, HTTP | CoAP, LWM2M | Any IP traffic | Admin shell | Lightweight IoT |
| **Handshake RTT** | 1-RTT (0-RTT option) | 1-RTT (cookie) | 2 phases (IKEv2: 4 msgs) | 2-RTT | 1-RTT |
| **Forward secrecy** | Mandatory (ECDHE) | Mandatory (ECDHE) | Optional (PFS with DHE) | Optional | Yes (XX pattern) |
| **Cipher default** | AES-128-GCM-SHA256 | AES-128-GCM-SHA256 | AES-GCM (ESP) | ChaCha20-Poly1305 | ChaCha20-Poly1305 |
| **Handshake bytes** | ~600–1500 B | ~700–1700 B | ~1200+ B | ~700 B | ~150 B (light!) |
| **IoT suitability** | Moderate (TCP overhead) | **High** (UDP fits CoAP) | High (transparent) | Low (heavy) | **Very High** |
| **Authentication** | X.509 / PSK | X.509 / PSK / RPK | X.509 / PSK | Host key | Static pubkey |
| **Replay protection** | Sequence number | Sliding window | Anti-replay window | Sequence number | Counter |
| **KTU 2024 emphasis** | Mandatory study | Mandatory study | Comparison only | Awareness | Emerging |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — IoT MQTT over TLS

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** An IoT temperature sensor publishes to an MQTT broker every 30 s over TLS 1.3. Each publish is 64 bytes. Compute:
> (a) Bytes added by TLS record layer per packet (assuming AES-GCM-128).
> (b) Effective throughput on a 128 kbps LoRaWAN-equivalent channel.
> (c) Number of TLS handshakes per day if session tickets are valid for 1 hour.

### Solution

**(a)** TLS 1.3 record overhead per record:
- 5-byte header (content type + version + length, with version field deprecated → effectively **1-byte content type** in TLS 1.3)
- 16-byte GCM authentication tag

Let $O_{TLS} = 1 + 16 = 17$ bytes.

Total packet on wire = $64 + 17 = 81$ bytes.

**(b)** Throughput = channel rate $\times$ fraction of useful data:
$\eta = \dfrac{64}{81} \approx 0.790 = 79.0\%$ payload efficiency.

Useful data rate = $128 \text{ kbps} \times 0.790 = 101.12 \text{ kbps}$.

**(c)** Handshakes per day = $\dfrac{24 \text{ hours}}{1 \text{ hour}} = 24$ handshakes per day, provided session resumption is honored. Without resumption: 2880 handshakes per day (every 30 s).

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** KTU 2024 frequently tests **overhead analysis**. Always compute `(payload_size + TLS_overhead) / channel_MTU` and contrast with plaintext MQTT (which has zero cryptographic overhead).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — High-Level IoT Channel Encryption Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    device1["IoT Sensor Node"]:::device
    device2["IoT Gateway / Edge"]:::device
    device3["Cloud Broker / Server"]:::device
    eve["Adversary Eve"]:::threat

    subgraph SECCHAN["Encrypted Channel - TLS 1.3 Session"]
        direction LR
        hs["Handshake Phase<br/>ECDHE Key Exchange + Authentication"]:::phase
        bd["Bulk Data Phase<br/>AES-128-GCM Record Encryption"]:::phase
        rk["Re-keying / Resumption"]:::phase
    end

    device1 -->|"ClientHello + ECDHE pub key"| hs
    hs -->|"ServerHello + ECDHE pub key + Cert"| device2
    device2 --> bd
    bd -->|"Application records (encrypted + tagged)"| device3
    bd -.->|"Resumption ticket"| rk
    rk -.-> bd

    eve -.->|"Intercepts plaintext only - sees ciphertext"| SECCHAN

    classDef device fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef phase fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef threat fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The green phases (`hs`, `bd`, `rk`) are the *only* components protected by the channel encryption. The red Eve node intercepts the channel but sees only ciphertext — she cannot read or forge records without the session key.

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — TLS 1.3 Handshake Message Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant C as Client (Sensor)
    participant S as Server (Broker)

    Note over C,S: Public: Curve, Cipher Suites, Random Nonces

    C->>S: ClientHello<br/>(supported_groups, cipher_suites, key_share=ECDHE_pub_c, random_c)
    S->>C: ServerHello<br/>(chosen_suite, key_share=ECDHE_pub_s, random_s)
    S->>C: EncryptedExtensions
    S->>C: Certificate (X.509 chain)
    S->>C: CertificateVerify (ECDSA sig)
    S->>C: Finished (MAC over transcript)

    Note over C,S: Both sides derive handshake_traffic_secret via HKDF

    C->>S: Certificate (if mutual auth)
    C->>S: CertificateVerify
    C->>S: Finished (MAC over transcript)

    Note over C,S: Switch to application_traffic_secret

    C->>S: Application Data (AES-GCM record)
    S->>C: Application Data (AES-GCM record)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note on TLS 1.3:** Unlike TLS 1.2, **all handshake messages after ServerHello are encrypted** under handshake keys. This is a major privacy and security improvement.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — DTLS Path for CoAP (UDP-Based IoT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    app["CoAP Application<br/>(Confirmable / Non-confirmable)"]:::app
    coap["CoAP Layer<br/>Request/Response with Token"]:::app
    dtls["DTLS 1.3 Record Layer<br/>AES-GCM over UDP datagram"]:::crypto
    udp["UDP Socket<br/>(port 5683 / 5684)"]:::net
    ip["IP / 6LoWPAN"]:::net

    server["CoAP Server / Cloud"]:::device

    app --> coap --> dtls --> udp --> ip
    ip --> server

    classDef app fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef crypto fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef net fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef device fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Channel Encryption

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SEND["Sender Side - Encryption Pipeline"]
        a1["Application Payload"] --> a2["Fragmentation<br/>(split into TLS records)"]:::stage
        a2 --> a3["Compression<br/>(optional, deprecated in TLS 1.3)"]:::stage
        a3 --> a4["AEAD Encrypt<br/>(AES-128-GCM)"]:::stage
        a4 --> a5["Add Record Header<br/+(content_type, length)"]:::stage
        a5 --> a6["Transmit on TCP/UDP"]:::stage
    end

    subgraph RECV["Receiver Side - Decryption Pipeline"]
        b1["Receive TCP/UDP Bytes"]:::stage
        b1 --> b2["Parse Record Header"]:::stage
        b2 --> b3["AEAD Verify & Decrypt<br/>(check GCM tag FIRST)"]:::stage
        b3 --> b4["Decompress<br/">(optional)"]:::stage
        b4 --> b5["Reassemble Application Payload"]:::stage
    end

    a6 -.->|"Network channel"| b1

    classDef stage fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#0c5460,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Critical Security Order:** In GCM decryption, **the authentication tag MUST be verified BEFORE any plaintext is released**. If tag verification fails, the entire record is dropped silently. Releasing unauthenticated plaintext opens the door to padding-oracle and other adaptive attacks.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — Forward Secrecy via Ephemeral Keys

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ltk["Long-Term Key<br/>(static ECDSA private key)"]:::ltk
    sessA["Session 1<br/>(ECDHE ephemeral: e1)"]:::session
    sessB["Session 2<br/>(ECDHE ephemeral: e2)"]:::session
    sessC["Session N<br/>(ECDHE ephemeral: eN)"]:::session

    past["Past Sessions<br/>(encrypted with e1, e2, ...)"]:::past
    future["Future Sessions"]:::future

    ltk -.->|"signs handshake only"| sessA
    ltk -.->|"signs handshake only"| sessB
    ltk -.->|"signs handshake only"| sessC

    sessA -->|"uses ephemeral key e1 only"| past
    sessB -->|"uses ephemeral key e2 only"| past
    sessC -->|"uses ephemeral key eN only"| future

    past -.->|"Compromise of long-term key<br/>CANNOT decrypt these"| past

    classDef ltk fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef session fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef past fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef future fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Secrecy Definition (KTU 2024):** Compromise of the long-term authentication key MUST NOT compromise the confidentiality of *past* sessions. Achieved only by using **ephemeral** (one-time) DH/ECDH keys per session.
