### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in DES questions:**
> 1. **Confusing the key size** — many students write "DES uses a 64-bit key" without specifying the **56 effective bits** (8 are parity).
> 2. **Forgetting the final swap** — the post-round-16 swap of `L₁₆` and `R₁₆` is **mandatory**; omitting it makes decryption produce garbage.
> 3. **Mixing up encryption and decryption key order** — encryption uses `K₁, K₂, …, K₁₆`; decryption uses `K₁₆, K₁₅, …, K₁`. The key schedule itself is **identical**.
> 4. **Stating "DES is unbreakable"** — it is **broken**. Brute force took 22 hours in 1999; modern hardware does it in minutes.
> 5. **Omitting the non-linearity of S-boxes** — S-boxes are the **only** non-linear component. Without them, DES would be trivially solvable by linear algebra.
> 6. **Wrong round number** — it is **16 rounds**, not 14, not 32.
> 7. **Confusing the P-box with a permutation of plaintext** — the P-box permutes the **output of the S-boxes**, not plaintext.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DES = Data Encryption Standard**, a **symmetric block cipher** (FIPS 46-3).
- Operates on **64-bit blocks** with a **56-bit effective key** (64-bit key with 8 parity bits).
- Uses a **16-round Feistel network** — the same algorithm encrypts and decrypts (with reversed subkey order).
- The **round function `f`** consists of four stages: **Expansion (E) → XOR with subkey → Substitution (S-boxes) → Permutation (P-box)**.
- **Eight S-boxes** (6×4) are the **sole non-linear component** and provide **confusion**.
- The **P-box** provides **diffusion**.
- The **key schedule** uses **PC-1 (56 bits)**, **left circular shifts** (1,1,2,2,…,2,1), and **PC-2 (48 bits)**.
- Total shift over 16 rounds = **28 bits**, so the schedule is **cyclic**.
- **Strengths:** standardized, well-analyzed, hardware-friendly.
- **Weaknesses:** 56-bit key is brute-forceable; 64-bit block is collision-prone; vulnerable to differential/linear cryptanalysis.
- **Replaced by AES (Rijndael)** in 2001 — AES uses 128-bit blocks and 128/192/256-bit keys.
- **Decryption key order:** reverse of encryption subkey order; **algorithm is the same**.
- The 3DES (Triple-DES) variant applies DES **three times** (EDE mode) to extend effective key to 112/168 bits.
- **Avalanche effect:** ~50% of ciphertext bits change when 1 bit of plaintext or key changes (achieved by round 5).
- DES is still seen in **legacy IoT firmware** and is the **conceptual ancestor** of all modern Feistel ciphers (Blowfish, CAST-128, Twofish, Camellia).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Triple DES
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Triple DES

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Triple DES (3DES / TDEA / Triple Data Encryption Algorithm)** is a symmetric-key block cipher that applies the original **Data Encryption Standard (DES)** cipher algorithm **three successive times** to each 64-bit data block. It was officially standardized by **NIST** under **FIPS 46-3 (1999)** and later proposed to be replaced by the **Advanced Encryption Standard (AES)** in 2001 due to performance and security limitations.

Formally, the encryption operation is defined as:

$$C = E_{K_3}\bigl(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P))\bigr)$$

Where $P$ is the plaintext block, $C$ is the ciphertext block, and $E_{K_i}, D_{K_i}$ denote DES encryption and decryption under key $K_i$ respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Triple DES belongs to the family of **symmetric-key block ciphers** and is taught as the natural evolutionary step from single DES to multi-round key application. It is a high-yield topic for ESE Module 1.

## Real-World Analogy — "The Three-Layer Vault"

Imagine you want to send a confidential document to a colleague through a courier:

1. **Layer 1 (First Lock):** You seal the document inside **Box A** and lock it with **Key 1**.
2. **Layer 2 (Second Lock):** That locked Box A is then placed inside **Box B** and locked with **Key 2** — but with a *special trick* — the box is *opened* and re-arranged with Key 2 (the *decrypt* in the middle), not double-locked.
3. **Layer 3 (Third Lock):** Box B is placed into **Box C** and locked with **Key 3**.

To open the document, your colleague must reverse the process in the opposite order, applying **Key 3**, then **Key 2**, then **Key 1**.

The middle "decrypt" step is **not for security** — it is a clever backward-compatibility trick that allows a 3DES system to talk to an old single-DES system when all three keys are identical. This is the famous **EDE (Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt)** sequence.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Size:** Triple DES operates on **64-bit** data blocks (same as DES).
> **Key Size:** Up to **168 bits** of effective key (with three independent 56-bit DES keys).

## Why Triple DES Exists — The Motivation

- Single DES uses only a **56-bit** key.
- By the late 1990s, **brute-force attacks** (like EFF's *Deep Crack*, 1998) cracked DES in under 24 hours.
- A natural fix: **apply DES multiple times** with different keys.

| Parameter | Single DES | Triple DES (3-key) |
|---|---|---|
| Block Size | 64 bits | 64 bits |
| Key Size | 56 bits | 168 bits (effective) |
| Rounds | 16 | 48 (3 × 16) |
| Status (NIST) | Withdrawn | Deprecated (since 2017) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The 3DES Operational Flow — EDE Structure

Triple DES follows an **Encrypt → Decrypt → Encrypt (EDE)** sequence. The middle "decrypt" operation is mathematically equivalent to encryption under the *inverse key schedule*, but is deliberately named "decrypt" to make the structure symmetrical with the decryption process.

### Encryption Equation

$$C = E_{K_3}\bigl(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P))\bigr)$$

### Decryption Equation (Reverse Cascade)

$$P = D_{K_1}\bigl(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C))\bigr)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the perfect mirror symmetry: decryption uses the *same three operations* in the *reverse order*, with keys applied in the *reverse sequence*.

## The Three Keying Options (High-Yield for KTU Exams)

Triple DES supports three standardized keying options defined by NIST. These are **frequently asked** in KTU ESE papers.

### Option 1 — Three Independent Keys (Strongest)
- Keys: $K_1 \neq K_2 \neq K_3$ (all three distinct)
- **Effective key length:** 168 bits
- **Total key material:** 192 bits (including parity bits)
- Most secure form; recommended for new deployments.

### Option 2 — Two Independent Keys
- Keys: $K_1 = K_3 \neq K_2$ (only $K_1$ and $K_2$ are independent)
- **Effective key length:** 112 bits
- **Total key material:** 128 bits
- Vulnerable to **meet-in-the-middle (MITM)** attack that reduces effective security.

### Option 3 — Three Identical Keys (Backward Compatibility)
- Keys: $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$
- **Effective key length:** 56 bits
- **Total key material:** 64 bits
- Reduces to **plain single DES** (since $E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P))) = E_K(P)$).
- Maintained only for legacy interoperability.

## KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| 3DES Encryption | $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$ | Defines the EDE transformation cascade |
| 3DES Decryption | $P = D_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C)))$ | Mirror inverse of the encryption cascade |
| Block Size | $64$ bits | Fixed input/output size |
| Key Size (Option 1) | $3 \times 56 = 168$ bits | Maximum security configuration |
| Key Size (Option 2) | $2 \times 56 = 112$ bits | Vulnerable to MITM ($2^{112}$ work) |
| Total DES Rounds | $3 \times 16 = 48$ | Computational cost is ~3× DES |
| Single DES Effective Key | $2^{56}$ | Brute-forced in days by 1998 hardware |
| 3DES Brute-Force (Option 1) | $2^{168}$ | Computationally infeasible |
| 3DES Brute-Force (Option 2 MITM) | $2^{112}$ | Still strong but halved by MITM |
| Sweet32 Attack Threshold | $2^{32}$ blocks | Birthday-bound attack on 64-bit blocks |

## Why Decrypt in the Middle? — The Backward-Compatibility Argument

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question:** "Why does 3DES use $E–D–E$ and not $E–E–E$?"

There are **two technical reasons** for using the EDE structure:

1. **Backward Compatibility with Single DES:**
   If we set $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, then EDE collapses to:
   $$C = E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P))) = E_K(P)$$
   The two inner operations cancel, leaving a plain single DES. This allows a 3DES system to communicate with an old single-DES system without code changes.

2. **Structural Symmetry for Decryption:**
   The decryption of an EDE-encrypted block uses exactly the *same* $E$ and $D$ primitives (with keys reversed), simplifying hardware/software implementation.

If $E–E–E$ were used, decryption would require the *inverse* DES function, which is not the same algorithm — making the cipher much more complex to implement.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

Triple DES is still used in:
- **Financial sector** (EMV chip card transactions, ATM PIN encryption).
- **Legacy TLS/SSL** handshakes (TLS 1.0 and 1.1).
- **Government and banking backbones** that have not migrated to AES.
- **IoT firmware update signing** (older embedded systems).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Showing that EDE Collapses to Single DES When $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$

**Goal:** Prove that when all three keys are identical, Triple DES is mathematically equivalent to single DES.

**Step 1 — Start with the 3DES encryption equation** for $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$:

$$C = E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P)))$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the innermost operation** $E_K(P)$:

Let $X = E_K(P)$. This is just standard single-DES encryption.

**Step 3 — Evaluate the middle operation** $D_K(X)$:

By the definition of DES decryption, $D_K(X) = P$ since $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$ for any valid DES round.

Therefore:

$$D_K(E_K(P)) = D_K(X) = P$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the outermost operation** $E_K(P)$:

$$E_K(D_K(E_K(P))) = E_K(P)$$

**Final Result:**

$$C = E_K(D_K(E_K(P))) = E_K(P) \quad \text{(single DES)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** When the three keys are equal, the inner encrypt and decrypt cancel, leaving a single DES at the outer layer.

---

## Derivation 2: Meet-in-the-Middle Attack on 2-Key 3DES (Option 2)

The Meet-in-the-Middle (MITM) attack reduces the effective security of 2-key 3DES from $2^{112}$ to roughly $2^{112}$ in time but only $2^{56}$ in **memory**. Here is the exhaustive reasoning.

**Step 1 — Attacker knows a plaintext-ciphertext pair** $(P, C)$ where:
$$C = E_{K_2}(D_{K_1}(E_{K_1}(P)))$$

**Step 2 — Attacker defines the middle value** $M = D_{K_1}(E_{K_1}(P)) = E_{K_2}^{-1}(C)$.

**Step 3 — Forward table:** Encrypt $P$ under all $2^{56}$ possible values of $K_1$ to produce a table of $M$ values.
- Time: $2^{56}$ operations
- Memory: $2^{56}$ entries

**Step 4 — Backward table:** Decrypt $C$ under all $2^{56}$ possible values of $K_2$ to produce candidate $M$ values.
- Time: $2^{56}$ operations

**Step 5 — Match entries** where the forward $M$ equals the backward $M$. Each match reveals a candidate key pair $(K_1, K_2)$.

**Step 6 — Verify** each candidate with a second known plaintext-ciphertext pair.

**Effective Work Factor:**

$$W_{\text{MITM}} = 2^{56} \text{ (encryptions)} + 2^{56} \text{ (decryptions)} + 2^{56} \text{ (storage)}$$

This is why Option 2 (2-key 3DES) provides only **112-bit** *nominal* security but is treated as **80-bit** *practical* security in modern cryptanalysis.

---

## Derivation 3: Why 3DES Is ~3× Slower than DES

Each 3DES operation executes **three independent DES computations**, each consisting of 16 Feistel rounds.

$$T_{3DES} = 3 \times T_{DES} = 3 \times (16 \text{ rounds}) = 48 \text{ rounds total}$$

| Operation | Rounds | Relative Time Cost |
|---|---|---|
| Single DES | 16 | $1\times$ |
| 3DES (EDE) | 48 | $3\times$ |

This performance penalty is the **primary motivation** for migrating to AES, which achieves stronger security with fewer rounds and a larger block size.

---

## Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Single DES | 3DES (Option 1) | AES-128 |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Size | 64 bits | 64 bits | 128 bits |
| Key Size | 56 bits | 168 bits | 128 bits |
| Rounds | 16 | 48 | 10 |
| Structure | Feistel | Feistel (cascaded) | SPN |
| Brute-Force Cost | $2^{56}$ | $2^{168}$ | $2^{128}$ |
| Known Weakness | Exhaustive key search | Sweet32 ($2^{32}$ blocks) | None practical |
| Software Speed (relative) | 1× | ~0.33× | ~5× to 10× |
| NIST Status | Withdrawn | Deprecated | Approved |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: 3DES EDE Encryption Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P["Plaintext Block P (64 bits)"] --> E1["DES Encrypt with K1"]
    E1 --> M1["Intermediate Block M1 (64 bits)"]
    M1 --> D2["DES Decrypt with K2"]
    D2 --> M2["Intermediate Block M2 (64 bits)"]
    M2 --> E3["DES Encrypt with K3"]
    E3 --> C["Ciphertext Block C (64 bits)"]

    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style E3 fill:#ffd966,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style M1 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

## Diagram 2: 3DES Decryption Mirror Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C["Ciphertext Block C (64 bits)"] --> D3["DES Decrypt with K3"]
    D3 --> N2["Intermediate Block N2 (64 bits)"]
    N2 --> E2["DES Encrypt with K2"]
    E2 --> N1["Intermediate Block N1 (64 bits)"]
    N1 --> D1["DES Decrypt with K1"]
    D1 --> Pout["Recovered Plaintext P (64 bits)"]

    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style E2 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style N2 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style N1 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style Pout fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
```

## Diagram 3: The Three Keying Options — Hierarchical View

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Triple DES Keying Options"] --> B["Option 1: Three Independent Keys"]
    A --> C["Option 2: Two Independent Keys"]
    A --> D["Option 3: Three Identical Keys"]

    B --> B1["K1 distinct K2 distinct K3"]
    B --> B2["Effective Security 168 bits"]
    B --> B3["Recommended for Strong Security"]

    C --> C1["K1 equal K3 distinct K2"]
    C --> C2["Effective Security 112 bits nominal"]
    C --> C3["Vulnerable to Meet in the Middle Attack"]

    D --> D1["K1 equal K2 equal K3"]
    D --> D2["Effective Security 56 bits"]
    D --> D3["Backward Compatible with Single DES"]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#70ad47,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#ffc000,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B1 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style B2 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style B3 fill:#e2efda,stroke:#375623,color:#000
    style C1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style C2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style C3 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
```

## Diagram 4: Internal DES Round (16 Rounds × 3 Cascades = 48 Total Rounds)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L0["Left Half L0 (32 bits)"] --> XOR1["XOR with Subkey K1"]
    F1["F Function Expansion Permutation S-Boxes P-Permutation"] --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> R1["Right Half R1 (32 bits)"]
    L1["Swap L1 equals R0"] --> R1

    style L0 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    style R1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#1f4e79,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    style XOR1 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#833c0c,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> Each DES block in the 3DES cascade internally executes **16 Feistel rounds** as shown above. The 3DES EDE process repeats this structure **three times**, totalling **48 Feistel rounds** for full encryption or decryption.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Triple DES. Why is the middle operation a decryption rather than a second encryption?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Triple DES is a symmetric-key block cipher that applies the DES algorithm three times in succession using the **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt (EDE)** structure: $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$. (1 Mark)

The middle operation is decryption (not encryption) for **two reasons**: (2 Marks)
- **Backward compatibility:** If $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, the inner encrypt and decrypt cancel, and the system reduces to plain single DES — useful for interoperation.
- **Implementation symmetry:** Decryption uses the same E and D primitives in reverse order, simplifying hardware/software design.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the three keying options of Triple DES and state the effective key size of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Option | Keying | Effective Key Size |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $K_1, K_2, K_3$ — all independent | 168 bits (1 Mark) |
| 2 | $K_1 = K_3 \neq K_2$ | 112 bits (1 Mark) |
| 3 | $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$ | 56 bits (1 Mark) |

---

## Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the architecture of Triple DES with a neat block diagram. Compare it with single DES in terms of key size, block size, number of rounds, and security. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Prove mathematically that when $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, Triple DES collapses to single DES. Why is the EDE structure preferred over EEE? (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

Triple DES (3DES) uses the **Encrypt–Decrypt–Encrypt (EDE)** structure:

$$C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$$

The plaintext block $P$ (64 bits) is first encrypted using DES with key $K_1$, then decrypted with key $K_2$, then encrypted with key $K_3$, producing ciphertext $C$. (3 Marks)

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | DES | 3DES |
|---|---|---|
| Block Size | 64 bits | 64 bits |
| Key Size | 56 bits | 168 bits (3-key option) |
| Rounds | 16 | 48 (3 × 16) |
| Security | $2^{56}$ brute-force | $2^{168}$ brute-force |
| Speed | 1× | ~0.33× (slower) |
| Status | Withdrawn | Deprecated |

**Diagram:** *(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 — Diagram 1)* (1 Mark for diagram)

**Conclusion:** 3DES provides 3× stronger security at the cost of 3× computational time. (3 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

**Proof that 3DES collapses to single DES when $K_1 = K_2 = K_3 = K$:**

Starting from the encryption equation:

$$C = E_{K}(D_{K}(E_{K}(P)))$$

(1 Mark)

Let $Y = E_K(P)$. By definition of DES decryption, $D_K(Y) = P$.

(2 Marks)

So the middle step becomes $D_K(E_K(P)) = P$, and the outer step gives:

$$C = E_K(P)$$

(2 Marks)

**Why EDE over EEE (2 reasons):**

1. **Backward compatibility:** With $K_1 = K_2 = K_3$, EDE reduces to single DES, allowing interoperation with legacy systems. (1 Mark)
2. **Symmetry of decryption:** The decryption routine uses the *same* E and D primitives (with reversed keys), which is impossible with EEE because $E^{-1} \neq E$ for DES. (1 Mark)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Describe the meet-in-the-middle attack on 2-key Triple DES. How does it affect the effective security? (7 Marks)

**(b)** Explain why NIST deprecated Triple DES in 2017. Discuss its relevance in modern IoT cryptography. (7 Marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

The **meet-in-the-middle (MITM) attack** exploits the two-stage structure of 2-key 3DES. (1 Mark)

**Procedure:** The attacker knows a plaintext-ciphertext pair $(P, C)$ where $C = E_{K_2}(D_{K_1}(E_{K_1}(P)))$. (1 Mark)

**Step 1:** Encrypt $P$ under all $2^{56}$ possible $K_1$ values, store the intermediate values $M = D_{K_1}(E_{K_1}(P))$ in a table. (1 Mark)

**Step 2:** Decrypt $C$ under all $2^{56}$ possible $K_2$ values, producing candidate $M' = E_{K_2}^{-1}(C)$ values. (1 Mark)

**Step 3:** Find matches between the forward table and the backward table — each match reveals a candidate $(K_1, K_2)$ pair. (1 Mark)

**Step 4:** Verify with a second known plaintext-ciphertext pair to eliminate false positives. (1 Mark)

**Impact on effective security:** Although the nominal key size is 112 bits, the MITM attack reduces the **practical work factor** to $2^{56}$ time and $2^{56}$ storage — making 2-key 3DES effectively only **80-bit secure** in modern terms. (1 Mark)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

**Reasons for NIST Deprecation (2017):**

1. **Sweet32 Birthday-Bound Attack:** 3DES uses a 64-bit block. After $2^{32}$ blocks of encrypted data under the same key, collisions become probable, allowing plaintext recovery. (2 Marks)
2. **Performance:** 3DES is ~3× slower than DES and ~10× slower than AES in software. (1 Mark)
3. **Block Size Obsolescence:** 64-bit blocks are too small for high-throughput modern applications (HTTPS, VPNs). (1 Mark)
4. **Key-Management Burden:** Three independent keys increase storage and rotation overhead. (1 Mark)

**Relevance in IoT Cryptography (2 Marks):**

Although 3DES is deprecated for new applications, it still appears in legacy IoT systems, EMV payment cards, and TLS 1.0/1.1 handshakes. For modern IoT deployments, NIST recommends **AES-128 or AES-256**, or lightweight ciphers like **ChaCha20** or **Ascon** for constrained devices. Migration from 3DES to AES is essential in any new IoT design.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Forgetting the EDE mnemonic:** Students often write $E–E–E$ instead of $E–D–E$. The "D" in the middle is a defining feature — never drop it. (–2 marks)
> - **Confusing effective key size with total key material:** Option 1 has 168 bits effective, 192 bits *with parity*. Examiners mark on effective key. (–1 mark)
> - **Missing the backward-compatibility argument** when explaining why decryption is in the middle. This is the most-clipped 3-mark sub-question in KTU papers.
> - **Omitting the diagram** in 7-mark "Explain the architecture" questions — diagrams carry 1–2 marks each.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Full Name:** Triple Data Encryption Algorithm (TDEA / 3DES).
- **Standard:** NIST FIPS 46-3 (1999); Deprecated in NIST SP 800-131A (2017).
- **Block Size:** **64 bits** (same as DES).
- **Keying Options:** 168-bit (3-key) | 112-bit (2-key) | 56-bit (1-key = DES).
- **Operation:** Encrypt → Decrypt → Encrypt (EDE).
- **Equation:** $C = E_{K_3}(D_{K_2}(E_{K_1}(P)))$.
- **Decryption:** $P = D_{K_1}(E_{K_2}(D_{K_3}(C)))$.
- **Rounds:** 3 × 16 = **48 Feistel rounds** total.
- **Why EDE?** Backward compatibility with single DES + implementation symmetry.
- **MITM Attack:** Affects 2-key 3DES; reduces effective security to ~$2^{80}$.
- **Sweet32 Attack:** Birthday-bound attack after $2^{32}$ blocks; root cause of deprecation.
- **Status Today:** Deprecated for new applications; replaced by **AES** in modern systems.
- **Still Seen In:** EMV chip cards, legacy TLS, financial backbones, older IoT firmware.
- **Migration Path:** 3DES → AES-128/256, or lightweight ciphers (Ascon, ChaCha20) for IoT.
- **Performance:** ~3× slower than DES; ~10× slower than AES in software.
- **Memorize for Exam:** The EDE equation, the three keying options, and the EDE-equals-DES proof.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: International Data Encryption Algorithm (IDEA)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** International Data Encryption Algorithm (IDEA)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **International Data Encryption Algorithm (IDEA)** is a symmetric-key block cipher designed by cryptographers **Xuejia Lai** and **James L. Massey** at ETH Zurich in **1991** (originally named PES — Proposed Encryption Standard, then IPES — Improved PES, and finally renamed IDEA in 1992). It operates on **64-bit plaintext blocks** using a **128-bit key** to produce **64-bit ciphertext blocks** through a Feistel-like structure of **8 full rounds** followed by a **half-round output transformation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (CO1 – Cryptographic Foundations):**
> IDEA is a textbook-grade example of a *strong, license-free* block cipher that was specifically engineered as a drop-in successor to the aging DES, and was patented in the United States and Europe (patent expired in 2012). It is the cryptographic engine of the early versions of PGP (Pretty Good Privacy).

| Property | Specification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Block size | **64 bits** |
| Key size | **128 bits** |
| Number of rounds | **8.5** (8 rounds + 1 output transformation) |
| Total subkeys | **52** of 16 bits each |
| Core operations | **XOR, Addition mod 2¹⁶, Multiplication mod (2¹⁶ + 1)** |
| Structure | **Lai–Massey / Mixed-Operation Feistel** |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Triple-Lock Vault"

Imagine a vault that protects a 64-character secret message (the **plaintext**). To crack it, an intruder must simultaneously satisfy **three mathematically incompatible conditions** in every round:

1. 🧮 **Multiplication lock (mod 65537):** A numeric "dial" that multiplies.
2. ➕ **Addition lock (mod 65536):** A "shift" cipher lever.
3. ⚡ **XOR lock (parity bit):** A binary inversion toggle.

Each round combines all three locks. Because **multiplication, addition, and XOR are three algebraically incompatible group operations**, no single shortcut attack works — the keys cannot be merged, and exhaustive search over a 128-bit keyspace is computationally infeasible (≈ **3.4 × 10³⁸** keys).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition for IoT Applications:** IDEA's 128-bit key provides a security margin historically adequate for constrained IoT nodes when implemented in lightweight C/assembly, although newer IoT ciphers (PRESENT, SIMON, Speck) have since become preferred for ultra-low-power devices.

### 1.3 Why IDEA Was Designed

The cipher was a direct response to the perceived weakness of DES (56-bit key, brute-forceable by the 1990s) and to the controversial secret-design S-boxes of DES. IDEA's design philosophy: *use only well-understood, publicly analyzable arithmetic operations* — three group operations on 16-bit blocks whose joint diffusion is provably strong.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Confounding Group Operations

IDEA's security rests on the fact that its three core operations, when applied to 16-bit sub-blocks, belong to **three different algebraic groups**, none of which is distributive or associative across the others. This blocks linear and differential cryptanalysis.

| Symbol | Operation | Mathematical Form | Group Property |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| ⊕ | Bitwise XOR | $A \oplus B$ | Abelian group on $\mathbb{F}_2^{16}$ |
| ⊞ | Addition modulo $2^{16}$ | $(A + B) \bmod 65536$ | Abelian group on $\mathbb{Z}_{2^{16}}$ |
| ⊙ | Multiplication modulo $2^{16}+1$ | $(A \cdot B) \bmod 65537$, with $0$ treated as $2^{16}$ | Abelian group on $\mathbb{Z}_{65537}^{\ast}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiplication mod $2^{16}+1$?** Because $2^{16}+1 = 65537$ is a **Fermat prime** ($F_4 = 2^{2^4} + 1$), every non-zero element has a unique multiplicative inverse mod 65537. The element 0 is mapped to $2^{16}$ to ensure the operation forms a complete group, making decryption trivially defined by inverse keys.

### 2.2 Round Function (One of 8 Rounds)

The 64-bit plaintext is split into four 16-bit sub-blocks: $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$. Each round $r$ consumes **six 16-bit subkeys** $K_1^{(r)}, \ldots, K_6^{(r)}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & Y_1 = X_1 \odot K_1^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & Y_2 = X_2 \;\boxplus\; K_2^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & Y_3 = X_3 \;\boxplus\; K_3^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & Y_4 = X_4 \odot K_4^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & T_1 = Y_1 \oplus Y_3 \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & T_2 = Y_2 \oplus Y_4 \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & T_1 = T_1 \odot K_5^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & T_2 = T_1 \;\boxplus\; T_2 \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & T_2 = T_2 \odot K_6^{(r)} \\
\text{Step 10:} \quad & T_1 = T_1 \;\boxplus\; T_2 \\
\text{Step 11:} \quad & Y_1' = Y_1 \oplus T_2 \\
\text{Step 12:} \quad & Y_3' = Y_3 \oplus T_2 \\
\text{Step 13:} \quad & Y_2' = Y_2 \oplus T_1 \\
\text{Step 14:} \quad & Y_4' = Y_4 \oplus T_1 \\
\text{Step 15:} \quad & \text{Swap } Y_2' \leftrightarrow Y_3' \quad \text{(NOT performed in the last round)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The output of each round $(Y_1', Y_2', Y_3', Y_4')$ feeds into the next round (with swap undone at start of next round).

### 2.3 The Multiplication-Addition (MA) Half-Function

Steps 5–10 above constitute the **MA structure**, the cryptographic heart of IDEA. Its purpose is to provide **complete diffusion**: changing a single input bit of $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$ affects all four output bits after two rounds.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Inputs: } & T_1,\; T_2,\; K_5,\; K_6 \\
\text{Output pair: } & (T_1 \boxplus T_2 \odot K_6,\;\; (T_1 \odot K_5) \boxplus (T_1 \boxplus T_2 \odot K_6))
\end{aligned}
$$

This is non-trivial to invert, which is essential for constructing decryption subkeys.

### 2.4 Output Transformation (Half-Round)

After the 8th round, the **swap of $Y_2$ and $Y_3$ is suppressed**, and a final transformation produces the ciphertext $(Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4)$ using subkeys $K_{49}, K_{50}, K_{51}, K_{52}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_1 &= Y_1 \odot K_{49} \\
Z_2 &= Y_3 \;\boxplus\; K_{50} \quad (\text{note: } Y_3, \text{ not } Y_2,\ \text{to undo the unswapped state}) \\
Z_3 &= Y_2 \;\boxplus\; K_{51} \\
Z_4 &= Y_4 \odot K_{52}
\end{aligned}
$$

Concatenating these gives the **64-bit ciphertext block**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Unit / Range | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block size | $n = 64$ | bits | Fixed; cannot be changed |
| Key size | $k = 128$ | bits | Yields $2^{128} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ keys |
| Number of subkeys | $N_K = 8 \times 6 + 4 = 52$ | 16-bit words | 6 per round × 8 + 4 for output |
| Subkey size | $w = 16$ | bits | All subkeys are 16 bits |
| Multiplication modulus | $p = 2^{16} + 1 = 65537$ | prime | Fermat prime $F_4$ |
| Addition modulus | $m = 2^{16} = 65536$ | integer | Standard 16-bit wrap-around |
| Encryption rounds | $R = 8$ | full rounds | Plus output half-round |
| Plaintext split | $P \to (X_1 \vert\vert X_2 \vert\vert X_3 \vert\vert X_4)$ | 16-bit each | Use $\vert\vert$ for concatenation |
| Key split (initial) | $K \to 8$ sub-blocks of 16 bits | – | Each 16-bit sub-block used as starting point |
| Key rotation between subkey groups | Cyclic shift by **25 bits** | bits | After every 8 subkeys generated |
| Output transformation | $(Z_1, Z_2, Z_3, Z_4)$ from $(Y_1, Y_3, Y_2, Y_4)$ | – | No swap before final ops |
| Decryption subkeys | Additive & multiplicative inverses of encryption subkeys | – | Reverse order for output transformation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For the KTU 2024 exam:** You are expected to **draw the round structure** with correct labelling of all six subkeys $K_1 \ldots K_6$ per round, identify the MA function, and explain decryption in terms of inverse subkeys. Marks are routinely lost when students swap the positions of $X_2$ and $X_3$ at the output transformation.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Email Security:** IDEA was the bulk cipher used in PGP versions up through PGP 2.6 (1999), securing millions of encrypted emails.
- **IoT Context (Module Relevance):** In constrained IoT devices, IDEA's *pure software* implementation efficiency (no S-boxes, no lookup tables) made it attractive before AES-NI hardware acceleration became standard. Modern ultra-low-power MCUs more commonly use PRESENT or ASCON, but IDEA remains a benchmark.
- **Legacy Financial Systems:** Some European banking TLS configurations historically used IDEA-based ciphersuites.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Single Round of IDEA with Trivial Subkeys

To make IDEA's data flow concrete, we will perform **one round** with **16-bit sub-blocks** using small integer values. This example uses real modular arithmetic — the operations are identical to the full cipher, only with smaller readable numbers.

**Initial plaintext** (each 16-bit word represented as an integer):

$$
X_1 = 0x0001, \quad X_2 = 0x0002, \quad X_3 = 0x0003, \quad X_4 = 0x0004
$$

**Round subkeys** (chosen small for illustration):

$$
K_1 = 0x0002, \quad K_2 = 0x0003, \quad K_3 = 0x0004, \quad K_4 = 0x0002, \quad K_5 = 0x0003, \quad K_6 = 0x0002
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reminder of operations used below:**
> - $\odot$: multiply mod $65537$ (with $0$ mapped to $65536$)
> - $\boxplus$: add mod $65536$
> - $\oplus$: bitwise XOR (on 16-bit values)

#### Step 1 — Multiplication of $X_1$ and $K_1$

$$
Y_1 = X_1 \odot K_1 = (1 \times 2) \bmod 65537 = 2
$$

No reduction is needed because the product is far smaller than 65537.

#### Step 2 — Addition of $X_2$ and $K_2$

$$
Y_2 = X_2 \;\boxplus\; K_2 = (2 + 3) \bmod 65536 = 5
$$

#### Step 3 — Addition of $X_3$ and $K_3$

$$
Y_3 = X_3 \;\boxplus\; K_3 = (3 + 4) \bmod 65536 = 7
$$

#### Step 4 — Multiplication of $X_4$ and $K_4$

$$
Y_4 = X_4 \odot K_4 = (4 \times 2) \bmod 65537 = 8
$$

#### Step 5 — XOR $Y_1$ with $Y_3$

$$
T_1^{\text{raw}} = Y_1 \oplus Y_3 = 0x0002 \oplus 0x0007 = 0x0005 = 5
$$

#### Step 6 — XOR $Y_2$ with $Y_4$

$$
T_2^{\text{raw}} = Y_2 \oplus Y_4 = 0x0005 \oplus 0x0008 = 0x000D = 13
$$

#### Step 7 — Multiply $T_1$ by $K_5$ (entry to MA function)

$$
T_1 = T_1^{\text{raw}} \odot K_5 = (5 \times 3) \bmod 65537 = 15
$$

#### Step 8 — Add $T_1$ to $T_2$

$$
T_2^{\text{new}} = T_1 \;\boxplus\; T_2^{\text{raw}} = (15 + 13) \bmod 65536 = 28
$$

#### Step 9 — Multiply $T_2$ by $K_6$

$$
T_2 = T_2^{\text{new}} \odot K_6 = (28 \times 2) \bmod 65537 = 56
$$

#### Step 10 — Add $T_1$ and $T_2$ (output of MA function)

$$
T_1 = T_1 \;\boxplus\; T_2 = (15 + 56) \bmod 65536 = 71
$$

#### Steps 11–14 — Final XOR mixing

$$
\begin{aligned}
Y_1' &= Y_1 \oplus T_2 = 2 \oplus 56 = 58 \\
Y_3' &= Y_3 \oplus T_2 = 7 \oplus 56 = 63 \\
Y_2' &= Y_2 \oplus T_1 = 5 \oplus 71 = 66 \\
Y_4' &= Y_4 \oplus T_1 = 8 \oplus 71 = 79
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $71 = 0x0047$ and $56 = 0x0038$, the XOR operations are non-trivial; small inputs produce scrambled outputs. This is exactly the **avalanche property** that IDEA relies upon.

#### Step 15 — Swap $Y_2'$ and $Y_3'$

$$
\text{Round output: } (Y_1',\; Y_3',\; Y_2',\; Y_4') = (58,\; 63,\; 66,\; 79)
$$

These four 16-bit values are the input to the **next round**.

### 3.2 Summary of Round Output (Analytical Matrix)

| Stage | Value | Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $Y_1$ | **2** | $1 \odot 2$ |
| $Y_2$ | **5** | $2 \boxplus 3$ |
| $Y_3$ | **7** | $3 \boxplus 4$ |
| $Y_4$ | **8** | $4 \odot 2$ |
| $T_1^{\text{raw}}$ | **5** | $2 \oplus 7$ |
| $T_2^{\text{raw}}$ | **13** | $5 \oplus 8$ |
| $T_1$ (after $\odot K_5$) | **15** | $5 \odot 3$ |
| $T_2^{\text{new}}$ | **28** | $15 \boxplus 13$ |
| $T_2$ (after $\odot K_6$) | **56** | $28 \odot 2$ |
| $T_1$ (final) | **71** | $15 \boxplus 56$ |
| $Y_1'$ | **58** | $2 \oplus 56$ |
| $Y_3'$ | **63** | $7 \oplus 56$ |
| $Y_2'$ | **66** | $5 \oplus 71$ |
| $Y_4'$ | **79** | $8 \oplus 71$ |
| Swapped output | (58, 63, 66, 79) | $Y_2' \leftrightarrow Y_3'$ |

### 3.3 Decryption Subkey Derivation (Key Schedule Inverse)

Decryption in IDEA is structurally identical to encryption; **only the subkeys change**. For each round $r$ (taking encryption subkeys $K_1^{(r)} \ldots K_6^{(r)}$), the corresponding decryption subkeys are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KD_1^{(r)} &= (K_1^{(r)})^{-1} \pmod{65537} \\
KD_2^{(r)} &= -K_2^{(r)} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_3^{(r)} &= -K_3^{(r)} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_4^{(r)} &= (K_4^{(r)})^{-1} \pmod{65537} \\
KD_5^{(r)} &= K_5^{(r)} \\
KD_6^{(r)} &= K_6^{(r)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For the **output transformation**, the order of subkeys is **reversed** and the same inverse rules apply:

$$
\begin{aligned}
KD_{49} &= (K_{52})^{-1} \pmod{65537} \\
KD_{50} &= -K_{51} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_{51} &= -K_{50} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_{52} &= (K_{49})^{-1} \pmod{65537}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The decryption subkeys for the MA function ($KD_5, KD_6$) are the **same** as the encryption subkeys. Only the four subkeys feeding the outer (XOR/ADD/MUL) layer are inverted. This is a non-obvious property that examiners frequently test.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Top-Level IDEA Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PT[64-bit Plaintext P] --> SPLIT[Split into X1 X2 X3 X4 of 16 bits each]
    SPLIT --> R1
    R1[/Round 1 with K1-1 to K1-6/] --> R2[/Round 2 with K2-1 to K2-6/]
    R2 --> R3[/Round 3 with K3-1 to K3-6/]
    R3 --> R4[/Round 4 with K4-1 to K4-6/]
    R4 --> R5[/Round 5 with K5-1 to K5-6/]
    R5 --> R6[/Round 6 with K6-1 to K6-6/]
    R6 --> R7[/Round 7 with K7-1 to K7-6/]
    R7 --> R8[/Round 8 with K8-1 to K8-6/]
    R8 --> OT[Output Transformation with K49 to K52, no swap]
    OT --> CT[64-bit Ciphertext C]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Single-Round Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    X1 --> MUL1
    K1i["K1"] --> MUL1["Multiply mod 65537"]
    MUL1 --> Y1
    X2 --> ADD1
    K2i["K2"] --> ADD1["Add mod 65536"]
    ADD1 --> Y2
    X3 --> ADD2
    K3i["K3"] --> ADD2["Add mod 65536"]
    ADD2 --> Y3
    X4 --> MUL2
    K4i["K4"] --> MUL2["Multiply mod 65537"]
    MUL2 --> Y4
    Y1 --> XORa["XOR"]
    Y3 --> XORa
    XORa --> MUL3["Multiply by K5 mod 65537"]
    MUL3 --> T1
    T1 --> ADD3["Add mod 65536"]
    Y2 --> XORb["XOR"]
    Y4 --> XORb
    XORb --> ADD3
    ADD3 --> MUL4["Multiply by K6 mod 65537"]
    MUL4 --> T2
    T2 --> ADD4["Add mod 65536"]
    T1 --> ADD4
    ADD4 --> T1f["Final T1"]
    Y1 --> XOR1["XOR with T2"]
    T2 --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> Y1o["Y1 out"]
    Y3 --> XOR2["XOR with T2"]
    T2 --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> Y3o["Y3 out"]
    Y2 --> XOR3["XOR with T1"]
    T1f --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> Y2o["Y2 out"]
    Y4 --> XOR4["XOR with T1"]
    T1f --> XOR4
    XOR4 --> Y4o["Y4 out"]
    Y2o --> SWAP[/"Swap Y2 and Y3, except final round"/]
    Y3o --> SWAP
    SWAP --> NEXT["Output to next round"]
```

### 4.3 Key Schedule Flow (Generation of 52 Subkeys)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    MK[128-bit Master Key] --> A[Divide into 8 sub-blocks of 16 bits, label Z1 to Z8]
    A --> KS1[Take Z1 to Z6 as Round 1 subkeys K1-1 to K1-6]
    A --> KS2[Take Z7 Z8 plus next 6 bits, cyclically shift by 25 bits]
    KS2 --> KS3[Take next 6 subkeys for Round 2]
    KS3 --> ROT["Cyclic left shift by 25 bits"]
    ROT --> MORE[Continue until 52 subkeys generated]
    MORE --> FINAL[48 subkeys for 8 rounds, plus 4 for output transformation]
```

### 4.4 Operational Logic Summary

| Phase | Operation Type | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Subkey generation | Bit-shift, slicing | Produce 52 distinct 16-bit subkeys |
| Each round (×8) | ⊙, ⊞, ⊕, MA mixing | Confusion + diffusion |
| Output transformation | ⊙, ⊞ only | Final whitening + asymmetric finish |
| Decryption | Same structure, inverse subkeys | Recover plaintext from ciphertext |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*List the three arithmetic operations used in IDEA, the modulus used for each, and briefly explain why three different operations were chosen instead of one.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The three operations used in IDEA on 16-bit sub-blocks are:

1. **Bitwise XOR ($\oplus$):** operates on the group $(\mathbb{F}_2^{16}, \oplus)$; no modulus, just bit-wise comparison.
2. **Addition modulo $2^{16}$ ($\boxplus$):** modulus $65536$; defines an Abelian group on $\mathbb{Z}_{65536}$.
3. **Multiplication modulo $2^{16}+1$ ($\odot$):** modulus $65537$ (a Fermat prime); defines an Abelian group on $\mathbb{Z}_{65537}^{\ast}$ with $0$ mapped to $65536$.

**Reason for three different operations (1 mark):** Addition and multiplication are not distributive over XOR (and vice versa). Mixing them in a single round prevents the cipher from being reduced to a simple linear or affine system, thwarting linear and differential cryptanalysis. The three operations belong to *incompatible* algebraic groups, which is the foundation of IDEA's security.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Why is the multiplication operation in IDEA performed modulo $65537$ and not modulo $65536$? What special role does the value $0$ play?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Why $65537$ (1 mark):** $2^{16}+1 = 65537$ is a **Fermat prime** ($F_4$). For a modulus to support a complete multiplicative group (i.e., every non-zero element to have a unique inverse), the modulus must be prime. $65536$ is highly composite ($2^{16}$) and would not give a group under multiplication, breaking decryption.

**Role of $0$ (1 mark):** In standard modular multiplication, $0$ has no multiplicative inverse. To maintain group structure, IDEA maps the input $0$ to the internal value $65536$ before multiplying, then maps the result back to $0$ if it equals $65536$. This makes the multiplication operation a *complete* group on all $65536$ possible 16-bit inputs, ensuring every ciphertext is uniquely decryptable.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The Fermat-prime modulus plus the $0 \leftrightarrow 65536$ mapping gives IDEA a closed, invertible multiplication operation — a design feature critical to the round function's invertibility.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the round structure of the IDEA cipher. Label all six subkeys used per round and the position of the MA (Multiplication-Addition) function. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the key schedule of IDEA. How are the 52 subkeys generated from the 128-bit master key? Mention the role of the cyclic bit-shift. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Round Structure (7 marks):**

The 64-bit plaintext block is split into four 16-bit sub-blocks $X_1, X_2, X_3, X_4$. Each of the **8 rounds** uses **six 16-bit subkeys** $K_1, K_2, K_3, K_4, K_5, K_6$.

*Outer layer (1.5 marks):*
- $X_1 \odot K_1$ — multiplication mod 65537
- $X_2 \boxplus K_2$ — addition mod 65536
- $X_3 \boxplus K_3$ — addition mod 65536
- $X_4 \odot K_4$ — multiplication mod 65537

*MA (Multiplication-Addition) core (3 marks):*
- $T_1 = (X_1 \odot K_1) \oplus (X_3 \boxplus K_3)$
- $T_2 = (X_2 \boxplus K_2) \oplus (X_4 \odot K_4)$
- $T_1 \leftarrow T_1 \odot K_5$
- $T_2 \leftarrow (T_1 \boxplus T_2) \odot K_6$
- $T_1 \leftarrow T_1 \boxplus T_2$

*Final XOR mixing (1.5 marks):*
- $Y_1' = Y_1 \oplus T_2$, $\;Y_3' = Y_3 \oplus T_2$
- $Y_2' = Y_2 \oplus T_1$, $\;Y_4' = Y_4 \oplus T_1$
- **Swap $Y_2' \leftrightarrow Y_3'$** (omitted in the 8th round)

*Diagram (1 mark):* (Refer to the **Single-Round Architecture** Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2.)

The MA structure provides the *confusion* mechanism; the outer XOR-add-multiply layer provides the *diffusion* and feeds back to the next round.

**Part (b) — Key Schedule (7 marks):**

The **128-bit master key** is divided into **8 sub-blocks of 16 bits each**, labelled $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_8$ (1 mark).

The first **6 subkeys** $K_1$ through $K_6$ of Round 1 are taken as $Z_1, Z_2, \ldots, Z_6$ (1 mark). The remaining bits $Z_7, Z_8$ are concatenated with the original key and the entire 128-bit register is **cyclically left-shifted by 25 bits** (1.5 marks).

This 25-bit rotation ensures:
1. Each new extraction of 16 bits differs significantly from the previous one (avalanche in key schedule).
2. The 25-bit shift is coprime to 16, ensuring that after exactly 7 rotations all original bits have reappeared in new positions (full period $7 \times 25 = 175 \equiv 1 \pmod{128}$? actually $7 \times 25 = 175$ and $175 - 128 = 47$, full coverage is achieved over multiple rotations).

After every 8 subkeys are generated, the 25-bit shift is applied (1.5 marks). The schedule yields:
- $8 \times 6 = 48$ subkeys for the 8 rounds,
- $4$ more subkeys $K_{49}, K_{50}, K_{51}, K_{52}$ for the **output transformation** (1 mark).

**Total = 52 subkeys**, each 16 bits, totaling $52 \times 16 = 832$ bits of keying material (1 mark).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Q-A Part (b):**
> A common mistake is to write that the shift is **25 bits to the right** (it is to the **left**), or to state that 8 sub-blocks are used per round. Students also often forget to specify that the shift is *cyclic on the 128-bit key register*, not on individual sub-blocks. Deduction: 1 mark each.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the decryption process in IDEA. How are the decryption subkeys derived from the encryption subkeys? Give the exact relationship for each of the six subkeys per round. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With an example, show the computation of one full round of IDEA on a 64-bit plaintext block when the first six round subkeys are $K_1 = 0002_{16}$, $K_2 = 0003_{16}$, $K_3 = 0004_{16}$, $K_4 = 0002_{16}$, $K_5 = 0003_{16}$, $K_6 = 0002_{16}$ and plaintext is $X_1 \vert\vert X_2 \vert\vert X_3 \vert\vert X_4 = 0001 \vert\vert 0002 \vert\vert 0003 \vert\vert 0004$. State the operations clearly. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Answer

**Part (a) — Decryption Subkey Derivation (7 marks):**

IDEA uses the *same algorithmic structure* for decryption as for encryption. Only the subkeys change. For each round $r$ (1 mark each, total 4 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
KD_1^{(r)} &= (K_1^{(r)})^{-1} \pmod{65537} \\
KD_2^{(r)} &= -K_2^{(r)} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_3^{(r)} &= -K_3^{(r)} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_4^{(r)} &= (K_4^{(r)})^{-1} \pmod{65537}
\end{aligned}
$$

The MA-function subkeys are **unchanged** (1 mark):
$$
KD_5^{(r)} = K_5^{(r)}, \qquad KD_6^{(r)} = K_6^{(r)}
$$

For the **output transformation**, the subkeys are taken in **reverse order** and inverted (2 marks):

$$
\begin{aligned}
KD_{49} &= (K_{52})^{-1} \pmod{65537} \\
KD_{50} &= -K_{51} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_{51} &= -K_{50} \pmod{65536} \\
KD_{52} &= (K_{49})^{-1} \pmod{65537}
\end{aligned}
$$

This way, the decryption algorithm — running on the ciphertext — recovers the original plaintext bit-exactly.

**Part (b) — Worked One-Round Example (7 marks):**

Plaintext input: $X_1 = 1, X_2 = 2, X_3 = 3, X_4 = 4$ (all in 16-bit representation, decimal values shown for clarity).

**Subkey values (hex/decimal):**
$K_1 = 2, K_2 = 3, K_3 = 4, K_4 = 2, K_5 = 3, K_6 = 2$ (1 mark for listing).

**Step 1:** $Y_1 = X_1 \odot K_1 = (1 \times 2) \bmod 65537 = 2$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 2:** $Y_2 = X_2 \boxplus K_2 = (2 + 3) \bmod 65536 = 5$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 3:** $Y_3 = X_3 \boxplus K_3 = (3 + 4) \bmod 65536 = 7$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 4:** $Y_4 = X_4 \odot K_4 = (4 \times 2) \bmod 65537 = 8$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 5:** $T_1 = Y_1 \oplus Y_3 = 2 \oplus 7 = 5$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 6:** $T_2 = Y_2 \oplus Y_4 = 5 \oplus 8 = 13$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 7:** $T_1 \leftarrow T_1 \odot K_5 = 5 \times 3 \bmod 65537 = 15$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 8:** $T_2 \leftarrow T_1 \boxplus T_2 = 15 + 13 = 28$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 9:** $T_2 \leftarrow T_2 \odot K_6 = 28 \times 2 \bmod 65537 = 56$ (0.5 mark)
**Step 10:** $T_1 \leftarrow T_1 \boxplus T_2 = 15 + 56 = 71$ (0.5 mark)
**Steps 11–14:**
$Y_1' = 2 \oplus 56 = 58$
$Y_3' = 7 \oplus 56 = 63$
$Y_2' = 5 \oplus 71 = 66$
$Y_4' = 8 \oplus 71 = 79$ (1 mark for full mixing)

**Step 15 — Swap:** Output to next round is $(58,\; 63,\; 66,\; 79)$ after swapping $Y_2'$ and $Y_3'$, so the four outgoing sub-blocks are $Y_1' = 58, Y_2' = 63, Y_3' = 66, Y_4' = 79$ (1 mark for stating output clearly).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Q-B Part (b):**
> Students frequently confuse the order of operations in Step 8 — they sometimes multiply $T_1$ by $K_5$ **after** adding $T_2$, which is incorrect. The correct order is: first multiply $T_1$ by $K_5$, **then** add the result to $T_2$, **then** multiply by $K_6$. Memorize the sequence **MUL → ADD → MUL** inside the MA function. Marks are deducted for any misordered step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **IDEA = 64-bit block + 128-bit key + 8 rounds + 1 output half-round** (often called "8.5 rounds").
- ✅ **Three group operations** = ⊕ (XOR), ⊞ (add mod 65536), ⊙ (multiply mod 65537). These are *algebraically incompatible* — the source of IDEA's strength.
- ✅ **$2^{16} + 1 = 65537$ is a Fermat prime** $F_4$. The input $0$ is internally represented as $65536$ to ensure every value has a multiplicative inverse.
- ✅ **Total subkeys = 52**, each 16 bits wide: 6 per round × 8 rounds + 4 for the output transformation.
- ✅ **Key schedule:** divide 128-bit key into eight 16-bit words; extract six subkeys; then cyclically shift the 128-bit register left by **25 bits** to generate the next batch.
- ✅ **MA (Multiplication-Addition) function** is the non-linear core of each round and the reason IDEA resists linear/differential cryptanalysis.
- ✅ **Swapping of $X_2$ and $X_3$** happens after every round *except* the 8th.
- ✅ **Output transformation uses $(Y_1, Y_3, Y_2, Y_4)$** — the un-swapped values — to maintain decryptability.
- ✅ **Decryption subkeys:** inverses mod 65537 for ⊙, additive inverses mod 65536 for ⊞; subkeys 5 and 6 of the MA structure are **unchanged**; for the output transformation, the encryption subkeys are **reversed in order** and then inverted.
- ✅ **Decryption uses the same algorithm** as encryption — only the subkeys differ.
- ✅ **Designed by Xuejia Lai & James L. Massey, 1991**, originally named PES, then IPES, then IDEA. Patent expired in 2012.
- ✅ **Used in PGP (versions ≤ 2.6)** for email encryption; not commonly used in modern IoT, replaced by PRESENT/ASCON/SIMON for ultra-constrained devices.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced Encryption Algorithm (AES)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advanced Encryption Algorithm (AES)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Advanced Encryption Algorithm (AES)**, formally known as the **Rijndael Algorithm** (pronounced "rain-dahl"), is a **symmetric-key, block cipher** standardized by the **National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)** in **FIPS Publication 197 (November 2001)**. AES operates on a fixed **block size of 128 bits** and supports variable key lengths of **128 bits, 192 bits, or 256 bits**, resulting in cipher variants known as **AES-128, AES-192, and AES-256** respectively. The algorithm performs a sequence of substitution and permutation operations across multiple rounds, transforming plaintext into ciphertext under the control of a secret key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Terminology Checklist:**
> - Always refer to the cipher as **AES (Rijndael)**, not just Rijndael.
> - The block size is **strictly 128 bits** — never 64 bits (that is DES).
> - Key sizes are the *only* variable parameter: **128, 192, or 256 bits**.

### 1.2 The Three AES Variants — Quick Reference

| Variant | Key Size (Nk words) | Block Size (Nb words) | Number of Rounds (Nr) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **AES-128** | **4** (128/32) | **4** (128/32) | **10** |
| **AES-192** | **6** (192/32) | **4** (128/32) | **12** |
| **AES-256** | **8** (256/32) | **4** (128/32) | **14** |

> **Convention:** $1 \text{ word} = 32 \text{ bits} = 8 \text{ bits} \times 4 \text{ bytes}$. Hence 128 bits = 4 words = 16 bytes.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Safe with 10 Combination Steps"

Imagine you place a 16-character document inside a **high-security safe**. The safe does not simply use *one* combination — it uses **10 sequential combination steps** (for AES-128). At each step:

- **Step 1 — Scramble the Letters (SubBytes):** Every character is swapped for a different character from a secret substitution table.
- **Step 2 — Rearrange the Rows (ShiftRows):** The characters are physically shuffled around inside the safe in a fixed pattern.
- **Step 3 — Mix the Columns (MixColumns):** A mathematical "blender" mixes 4 characters at a time, completely diffusing their values.
- **Step 4 — Apply a Secret Twist (AddRoundKey):** A unique fragment of the master key is XORed into the data.

After 10 such steps, the document inside looks like **pure random noise**, and the only way to reverse the process is to know the original master key. This is **AES in essence**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AES is critical for IoT (per KTU Module 1 context):** Although classical AES is computationally heavy for low-power IoT nodes, it forms the **cryptographic primitive foundation** for lightweight adaptations (e.g., AES-128 in hardware, PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK). Understanding AES is therefore the gateway to studying **Lightweight Cryptography (LWC)** for IoT.

### 1.4 AES State — The Central Data Structure

All operations in AES are performed on a **2D array of bytes called the State**. The State is a $4 \times 4$ matrix of bytes (16 bytes total = 128 bits), filled column-by-column from the input plaintext block.

For a plaintext block $P$ of 16 bytes ($p_0, p_1, \dots, p_{15}$), the initial State matrix is constructed as:

$$
\text{State} = \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,0} & s_{0,1} & s_{0,2} & s_{0,3} \\ s_{1,0} & s_{1,1} & s_{1,2} & s_{1,3} \\ s_{2,0} & s_{2,1} & s_{2,2} & s_{2,3} \\ s_{3,0} & s_{3,1} & s_{3,2} & s_{3,3} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} p_0 & p_4 & p_8 & p_{12} \\ p_1 & p_5 & p_9 & p_{13} \\ p_2 & p_6 & p_{10} & p_{14} \\ p_3 & p_7 & p_{11} & p_{15} \end{bmatrix}
$$

The State is the working canvas that gets transformed in every round.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Overall AES Encryption Pipeline

The AES encryption process consists of three algorithmic phases:

1. **Initial Round (Round 0):** AddRoundKey only.
2. **Main Rounds (Round 1 to $N_r - 1$):** SubBytes $\rightarrow$ ShiftRows $\rightarrow$ MixColumns $\rightarrow$ AddRoundKey.
3. **Final Round (Round $N_r$):** SubBytes $\rightarrow$ ShiftRows $\rightarrow$ AddRoundKey. *(No MixColumns in the final round.)*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Frequently Missed Fact:** The **final round omits MixColumns**. This is a deliberate design choice to make the cipher and its inverse symmetric in structure (the inverse cipher's last round also omits the corresponding InvMixColumns).

### 2.2 Detailed Operation Analysis

#### 2.2.1 SubBytes (Byte Substitution Layer)
- **What:** Each byte in the State is replaced independently by its image under a **fixed 8-bit S-Box** (a $16 \times 16$ lookup table).
- **Why:** Provides **confusion** — the relationship between key and ciphertext is obscured.
- **How (Mathematical construction):** The S-Box is built by taking the multiplicative inverse of each byte in the **Galois Field GF($2^8$)** with the irreducible polynomial $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$, followed by an **affine transformation** over $\text{GF}(2)$.

The affine transformation is defined as:

$$
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
$$

where $c = \{0x63, 0x63, 0x63, 0x63, 0x63, 0x63, 0x63, 0x63\}$ is the constant byte (01100011 in binary).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The S-Box entry for the byte $0x00$ is $0x63$, and the entry for $0x63$ itself is $0x7C$. These two values are board-exam favorites.

#### 2.2.2 ShiftRows (Row-wise Permutation)
- **What:** The 4 rows of the State are cyclically shifted to the left by $0, 1, 2, 3$ positions respectively.
- **Why:** Provides **inter-column diffusion** — bytes from different columns get mixed into the same column.
- **How:**
  - Row 0: No shift.
  - Row 1: Left shift by 1 byte.
  - Row 2: Left shift by 2 bytes.
  - Row 3: Left shift by 3 bytes.

#### 2.2.3 MixColumns (Column Mixing Layer)
- **What:** Each column of the State is treated as a 4-byte polynomial over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ and multiplied modulo $x^4 + 1$ by the **fixed polynomial** $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$.
- **Why:** Provides **intra-column diffusion** and ensures each output byte depends on all 4 input bytes of that column.
- **How (Matrix form):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,c} \\ s'_{1,c} \\ s'_{2,c} \\ s'_{3,c} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} s_{0,c} \\ s_{1,c} \\ s_{2,c} \\ s_{3,c} \end{bmatrix}
$$

for each column $c \in \{0,1,2,3\}$.

#### 2.2.4 AddRoundKey (Key Mixing Layer)
- **What:** The 128-bit State is XORed with the 128-bit **Round Key** derived from the key schedule.
- **Why:** Incorporates the secret key into the cipher.

$$
\text{State}_{\text{new}} = \text{State}_{\text{old}} \oplus \text{RoundKey}
$$

### 2.3 AES Key Expansion (Key Schedule)

The key expansion generates a **linear array of $4 \times (N_r + 1)$ words** ($44$ for AES-128, $52$ for AES-192, $60$ for AES-256), denoted $W[0] \dots W[4N_r+3]$.

**Algorithm for AES-128 ($N_k = 4$):**
- For $i = 0, 1, 2, 3$: $W[i] = \text{(Key bytes } 4i \text{ to } 4i+3)$.
- For $i \ge 4$:
  - $temp = W[i-1]$
  - **If $i \bmod N_k = 0$:** $temp = \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(temp)) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/N_k]$.
  - **Else:** $temp = W[i-1]$ unchanged.
  - $W[i] = W[i-N_k] \oplus temp$

Where:
- **RotWord:** Cyclic left rotation of a 4-byte word by 1 byte.
- **SubWord:** Apply the AES S-Box to each of the 4 bytes.
- **Rcon (Round Constant):** $\text{Rcon}[j] = (RC[j], 0, 0, 0)$ where $RC[1] = 0x01$ and $RC[j] = 2 \cdot RC[j-1]$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$.

| Round $j$ | $RC[j]$ (hex) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | **01** |
| 2 | **02** |
| 3 | **04** |
| 4 | **08** |
| 5 | **10** |
| 6 | **20** |
| 7 | **40** |
| 8 | **80** |
| 9 | **1B** |
| 10 | **36** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Constant | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Block size | $N_b = 4$ words $= 128$ bits | Fixed for all AES variants |
| AES-128 rounds | $N_r = 10$ | When key size = 128 bits |
| AES-192 rounds | $N_r = 12$ | When key size = 192 bits |
| AES-256 rounds | $N_r = 14$ | When key size = 256 bits |
| Total key words | $4 \times (N_r + 1)$ | Size of expanded key |
| AES irreducible polynomial | $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ | Defines $\text{GF}(2^8)$ |
| MixColumns polynomial | $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ | Column mixing |
| InvMixColumns polynomial | $a^{-1}(x) = \{0B\}x^3 + \{0D\}x^2 + \{09\}x + \{0E\}$ | Inverse column mixing |
| Affine constant | $c = 0x63$ | S-Box construction |
| Total AES S-Box size | $16 \times 16 = 256$ bytes | Byte substitution table |
| Shift offsets (rows 0–3) | $0, 1, 2, 3$ bytes | Left cyclic shift |
| InvShiftRows offsets | $0, 3, 2, 1$ bytes | Right cyclic shift |

### 2.5 Inverse Cipher (Decryption)

The AES decryption is not identical to encryption in code order, but the **mathematical steps are inverses of the encryption steps** executed in **reverse order**:

$$\text{InvSubBytes} \rightarrow \text{InvShiftRows} \rightarrow \text{AddRoundKey} \rightarrow \text{InvMixColumns}$$

with the final round omitting InvMixColumns (mirroring the encryption side). The **Inverse S-Box** is the lookup inverse of the forward S-Box, and **InvMixColumns** uses the constant matrix $\{0B\}, \{0D\}, \{09\}, \{0E\}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalent Inverse Cipher (KTU 2024 Highlight):** By reordering operations, AES decryption can be implemented with the same round structure as encryption, which is highly valuable for **hardware implementations in IoT chips** that need a single unified datapath.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility in IoT

- **Hardware AES co-processors** are embedded in nearly every modern IoT SoC (ESP32, STM32, nRF52) to deliver **AES-128 at < 100 pJ/bit**.
- **AES-CCM, AES-GCM, AES-CBC** are the dominant authenticated and confidentiality modes used in protocols like **TLS 1.3, Zigbee, BLE, LoRaWAN, and MQTT-SN**.
- The Rijndael S-Box and MixColumns matrix are **constant-time**, side-channel-friendly when implemented correctly, making AES a baseline for **side-channel-resistant IoT cryptography**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: SubBytes Transformation of the Byte 0x53

We derive the S-Box value of input byte $b = 0x53$ (binary: $01010011$) using the **GF($2^8$) multiplicative inverse + affine transform** procedure.

**Step 1: Represent as a polynomial.**

$$
0x53 = 01010011_2 \;\Rightarrow\; b(x) = x^6 + x^4 + x + 1
$$

**Step 2: Compute the multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ modulo $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$.**

Using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm (or AES-specific pre-computed tables), the inverse is:

$$
b^{-1}(x) = x^7 + x^6 + x^5 + x^4 + x^2 + 1 = 0xED \quad (\text{hex: } 0xED = 11101101_2)
$$

> *Verification fact:* The AES S-Box entry at row 5, column 3 is $0xED$. ✓

**Step 3: Apply the affine transformation.**

For each bit $i$ (with $i = 0$ as LSB):

$$
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4)\bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5)\bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6)\bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7)\bmod 8} \oplus c_i
$$

where $c = 0x63 = 01100011_2$. Let $b = 0xED = 11101101_2$, so $b_7 b_6 b_5 b_4 b_3 b_2 b_1 b_0 = 11101101$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
b'_0 &= b_0 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus c_0 \\
&= 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \;=\; 1 \\[4pt]
b'_1 &= b_1 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus c_1 \\
&= 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \;=\; 1 \\[4pt]
b'_2 &= b_2 \oplus b_6 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus c_2 \\
&= 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \;=\; 0 \\[4pt]
b'_3 &= b_3 \oplus b_7 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus c_3 \\
&= 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \;=\; 1 \\[4pt]
b'_4 &= b_4 \oplus b_0 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus c_4 \\
&= 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \;=\; 0 \\[4pt]
b'_5 &= b_5 \oplus b_1 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus c_5 \\
&= 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \;=\; 1 \\[4pt]
b'_6 &= b_6 \oplus b_2 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus c_6 \\
&= 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \;=\; 0 \\[4pt]
b'_7 &= b_7 \oplus b_3 \oplus b_4 \oplus b_5 \oplus b_6 \oplus c_7 \\
&= 1 \oplus 0 \oplus 0 \oplus 1 \oplus 1 \oplus 0 \;=\; 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading $b'_7 b'_6 b'_5 b'_4 b'_3 b'_2 b'_1 b'_0 = 10101011_2 = 0xAB$.

> **Result:** $S(0x53) = 0xAB$. ✓ *(Matches the official AES S-Box.)*

### 3.2 Derivation 2: MixColumns on a Single Column

Consider a State column $c = \begin{bmatrix} 0xDB \quad 0x13 \quad 0x53 \quad 0x45 \end{bmatrix}^T$. We compute the output column $c'$ using the MixColumns matrix.

**Step 1: Set up the matrix multiplication.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} s'_{0,c} \\ s'_{1,c} \\ s'_{2,c} \\ s'_{3,c} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0xDB \\ 0x13 \\ 0x53 \\ 0x45 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 2: Compute row 0.**

$$
s'_{0,c} = (\{02\} \cdot 0xDB) \oplus (\{03\} \cdot 0x13) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x53) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x45)
$$

Compute the multiplications in $\text{GF}(2^8)$:

- $\{02\} \cdot 0xDB$: Since the MSB of $0xDB$ is 1, shift left and XOR with $0x1B$.
  - $0xDB \ll 1 = 0x1B6 \Rightarrow 0xB6$, then XOR with $0x1B$: $0xB6 \oplus 0x1B = 0xAD$.
- $\{03\} \cdot 0x13 = (\{02\} \cdot 0x13) \oplus 0x13 = 0x26 \oplus 0x13 = 0x35$.
- $\{01\} \cdot 0x53 = 0x53$.
- $\{01\} \cdot 0x45 = 0x45$.

$$
s'_{0,c} = 0xAD \oplus 0x35 \oplus 0x53 \oplus 0x45
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xAD \oplus 0x35 &= 0x98 \\
0x98 \oplus 0x53 &= 0xCB \\
0xCB \oplus 0x45 &= 0x8E
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute row 1.**

$$
s'_{1,c} = (\{01\} \cdot 0xDB) \oplus (\{02\} \cdot 0x13) \oplus (\{03\} \cdot 0x53) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x45)
$$

- $\{02\} \cdot 0x13 = 0x26$ (MSB = 0, just shift).
- $\{03\} \cdot 0x53 = (\{02\} \cdot 0x53) \oplus 0x53 = 0xA6 \oplus 0x53 = 0xF5$.

$$
s'_{1,c} = 0xDB \oplus 0x26 \oplus 0xF5 \oplus 0x45
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xDB \oplus 0x26 &= 0xFD \\
0xFD \oplus 0xF5 &= 0x08 \\
0x08 \oplus 0x45 &= 0x4D
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute row 2.**

$$
s'_{2,c} = (\{01\} \cdot 0xDB) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x13) \oplus (\{02\} \cdot 0x53) \oplus (\{03\} \cdot 0x45)
$$

- $\{02\} \cdot 0x53 = 0xA6$.
- $\{03\} \cdot 0x45 = (\{02\} \cdot 0x45) \oplus 0x45 = 0x8A \oplus 0x45 = 0xCF$.

$$
s'_{2,c} = 0xDB \oplus 0x13 \oplus 0xA6 \oplus 0xCF
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0xDB \oplus 0x13 &= 0xC8 \\
0xC8 \oplus 0xA6 &= 0x6E \\
0x6E \oplus 0xCF &= 0xA1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Compute row 3.**

$$
s'_{3,c} = (\{03\} \cdot 0xDB) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x13) \oplus (\{01\} \cdot 0x53) \oplus (\{02\} \cdot 0x45)
$$

- $\{03\} \cdot 0xDB = (\{02\} \cdot 0xDB) \oplus 0xDB = 0xAD \oplus 0xDB = 0x76$.
- $\{02\} \cdot 0x45 = 0x8A$.

$$
s'_{3,c} = 0x76 \oplus 0x13 \oplus 0x53 \oplus 0x8A
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
0x76 \oplus 0x13 &= 0x65 \\
0x65 \oplus 0x53 &= 0x36 \\
0x36 \oplus 0x8A &= 0xBC
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result Column:**

$$
c' = \begin{bmatrix} 0x8E \\ 0x4D \\ 0xA1 \\ 0xBC \end{bmatrix}
$$

> ✓ This matches the official FIPS-197 worked example (Section 4.1.2 in the standard).

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Rcon[9] = 0x1B and Rcon[10] = 0x36

The Round Constants are generated by successive multiplication by $x$ (= $\{02\}$) in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ modulo $m(x)$.

**Step 1: Initialize.** $RC[1] = 0x01 = x^0$.

**Step 2: Compute $RC[2] = \{02\} \cdot 0x01 = 0x02$.** (No XOR with $0x1B$ since MSB of 0x01 is 0.)

**Step 3: Continue doubling:**

$$
RC[j] = \{02\} \cdot RC[j-1] \;\; \text{(XOR with } 0x1B \text{ if MSB of } RC[j-1] = 1\text{)}
$$

Tracking until $RC[9]$:

- $RC[1] = 0x01$
- $RC[2] = 0x02$
- $RC[3] = 0x04$
- $RC[4] = 0x08$
- $RC[5] = 0x10$
- $RC[6] = 0x20$
- $RC[7] = 0x40$
- $RC[8] = 0x80$ (binary $10000000$, MSB = 1)
- $RC[9] = \{02\} \cdot 0x80 = 0x100 \oplus 0x1B = 0x1B$ ✓

**Step 4: Compute $RC[10]$:**

- $\{02\} \cdot 0x1B$: MSB of $0x1B$ is 0, so just shift: $0x1B \ll 1 = 0x36$.
- $RC[10] = 0x36$ ✓

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Number of Rounds Justification

For AES, the number of rounds $N_r$ is given by:

$$
N_r = \max(N_k, N_b) + 6
$$

For all standard AES variants, $N_b = 4$ and $N_k \in \{4, 6, 8\}$:

- **AES-128:** $N_r = \max(4,4) + 6 = 10$
- **AES-192:** $N_r = \max(6,4) + 6 = 12$
- **AES-256:** $N_r = \max(8,4) + 6 = 14$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "+6"?** The extra rounds provide a **security margin** against future cryptanalytic breakthroughs. Even if an attack could marginally weaken 4 rounds, the cipher would still be secure.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: AES vs DES (High-Yield Comparison)

| Property | DES | AES (Rijndael) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Year / Origin | 1977 (IBM/NBS) | 2001 (NIST FIPS-197) |
| Block size | **64 bits** | **128 bits** |
| Key size | **56 bits** | **128 / 192 / 256 bits** |
| Structure | Feistel network | **Substitution-Permutation Network (SPN)** |
| Rounds | 16 | **10 / 12 / 14** |
| Primitive ops | XOR, S-Box, Permutation, Expansion | **SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey** |
| S-Box size | 8 $\times$ 4 $\times$ 2 = 32 entries | $16 \times 16 = 256$ entries |
| Cryptanalysis status | Broken (brute force feasible) | **Secure (no practical attacks)** |
| IoT suitability | Low (deprecated) | High (hardware-accelerated in chips) |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 AES-128 Full Encryption Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete encryption flow for AES-128, including the Key Expansion side-process that runs in parallel.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Plaintext 128 bits"] --> B["Initial State 4x4 Matrix"]
    B --> C["AddRoundKey with Round Key 0"]
    C --> D["Round 1 SubBytes"]
    D --> E["Round 1 ShiftRows"]
    E --> F["Round 1 MixColumns"]
    F --> G["Round 1 AddRoundKey"]
    G --> H["Round 2 to Round 9 Loop"]
    H --> I["Round 10 SubBytes"]
    I --> J["Round 10 ShiftRows"]
    J --> K["Round 10 AddRoundKey Final Round No MixColumns"]
    K --> L["Ciphertext 128 bits"]

    M["Master Key 128 bits"] --> N["Key Expansion Module"]
    N --> O["Round Key 0"]
    N --> P["Round Key 1 to Round Key 10"]
    O --> C
    P --> G
    P --> H
    P --> K
```

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of One AES Round (SPN Layer Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["State Input 128 bits"] --> SB["SubBytes Layer 16 parallel S-Box lookups"]
    SB --> SR["ShiftRows Permutation"]
    SR --> MC["MixColumns GF 2 power 8 Matrix Multiply"]
    MC --> ARK["AddRoundKey XOR with Round Key"]
    ARK --> SO["State Output 128 bits"]

    RK["Round Key 128 bits"] --> ARK
```

### 4.3 ShiftRows Permutation Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Before["Before ShiftRows"]
        R0A["Row 0: A E I M"]
        R1A["Row 1: B F J N"]
        R2A["Row 2: C G K O"]
        R3A["Row 3: D H L P"]
    end

    subgraph After["After ShiftRows"]
        R0B["Row 0: A E I M (no shift)"]
        R1B["Row 1: F J N B (left 1)"]
        R2B["Row 2: K O C G (left 2)"]
        R3B["Row 3: P D H L (left 3)"]
    end

    Before --> After
```

### 4.4 AES Key Expansion Data Flow (AES-128)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    KW0["W0 W1 W2 W3 from Master Key"] --> NX["Next Word Index i 4 to 43"]
    NX --> CK{"i mod Nk = 0?"}
    CK -- "Yes" --> ROT["RotWord shift left 1 byte"]
    ROT --> SUB["SubWord apply S-Box"]
    SUB --> RC["XOR with Rcon i divided by Nk"]
    RC --> X1["temp = result"]
    CK -- "No" --> X2["temp = W i minus 1"]
    X1 --> X3["W i = W i minus Nk XOR temp"]
    X2 --> X3
    X3 --> NX
    X3 --> OUT["Expanded Key 44 Words = 1408 bits"]
```

### 4.5 Decryption Pipeline (Inverse Cipher)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CIP["Ciphertext 128 bits"] --> ISB["InvSubBytes"]
    ISB --> ISR["InvShiftRows right shifts 0 3 2 1"]
    ISR --> ARK1["AddRoundKey with Round Key Nr"]
    ARK1 --> MC{"Round index is final?"}
    MC -- "No" --> IMC["InvMixColumns with constants 0B 0D 09 0E"]
    IMC --> ARK2["AddRoundKey with next round key"]
    ARK2 --> ISB
    MC -- "Yes" --> PT["Plaintext 128 bits"]
```

### 4.6 AES Operating Modes for IoT Protocols (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT["Plaintext Blocks"] --> MD["Mode Wrapper"]
    MD --> AES["AES Core Engine 128 bit block cipher"]
    KEY["Secret Key"] --> AES
    AES --> MD
    MD --> CT["Ciphertext + Auth Tag"]

    MD -.->|CBC| M1["Used in TLS Cipher Block Chaining"]
    MD -.->|CTR| M2["Used in IoT lightweight streaming"]
    MD -.->|GCM| M3["Used in BLE Zigbee Authenticated Encryption"]
    MD -.->|CCM| M4["Used in LoRaWAN IEEE 802.15.4"]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Q: List the four transformations used in each round of the AES encryption algorithm. Why is the MixColumns transformation omitted in the final round?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four transformations of a standard AES round are:
1. **SubBytes** — Non-linear byte substitution using the $16 \times 16$ S-Box.
2. **ShiftRows** — Cyclic left shift of rows by 0, 1, 2, 3 bytes.
3. **MixColumns** — Matrix multiplication of each column in $\text{GF}(2^8)$.
4. **AddRoundKey** — XOR of the State with the round key.

**Reason for omitting MixColumns in the final round:** MixColumns is a linear operation, and its absence in the final round makes the cipher and its inverse algebraically symmetric. It also allows the inverse cipher to be implemented with the same round structure (just reversing the order of operations), which is critical for **hardware-friendly IoT implementations**.

*(1 mark for listing 4 operations, 1 mark for description, 1 mark for justification.)*

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between the AES S-Box and the DES S-Box in terms of size, structure, and design principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | DES S-Box | AES S-Box |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Size | 8 $\times$ 4 = 32 entries per box $\times$ 8 boxes | $16 \times 16 = 256$ entries (single box) |
| Input size | 6 bits | 8 bits |
| Output size | 4 bits | 8 bits |
| Design | Randomly chosen, design criteria kept secret | Algebraic: multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ + affine transform |
| Reversibility | Not invertible (4-bit output from 6-bit input) | Invertible (bijection) |

The AES S-Box is **bijective** (a permutation of all 256 byte values), while DES S-Boxes are many-to-one.

*(1 mark per correct point of comparison.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Note:** Part B Module-1 questions carry **14 marks** with **internal choice**. They are split into sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the AES algorithm in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the key expansion process. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Introduction (1 mark):**
AES is a symmetric block cipher standardized as FIPS-197. It has a 128-bit block and supports 128/192/256-bit keys with 10/12/14 rounds respectively. All operations are performed on a $4 \times 4$ State matrix.

**(ii) Round Structure (2 marks):**
Each round (except the last) consists of:
1. SubBytes — non-linear substitution via the 256-byte S-Box (built from multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ + affine transform with constant $0x63$).
2. ShiftRows — cyclic left shifts of rows 1, 2, 3 by 1, 2, 3 positions.
3. MixColumns — column-wise multiplication by the fixed matrix
$$
\begin{bmatrix} 02 & 03 & 01 & 01 \\ 01 & 02 & 03 & 01 \\ 01 & 01 & 02 & 03 \\ 03 & 01 & 01 & 02 \end{bmatrix}
$$
4. AddRoundKey — XOR with the 128-bit round key.

The final round omits MixColumns.

**(iii) Key Expansion (3 marks):**
For AES-128, the 128-bit key is split into 4 words $W[0] \dots W[3]$. The schedule generates $W[4] \dots W[43]$ (44 words total, 1408 bits) using:
- $W[i] = W[i-4] \oplus W[i-1]$ (for $i \bmod 4 \ne 0$)
- $W[i] = W[i-4] \oplus \text{SubWord}(\text{RotWord}(W[i-1])) \oplus \text{Rcon}[i/4]$ (for $i \bmod 4 = 0$)

Each round key is 4 consecutive words of this expanded key.

**(iv) Decryption (1 mark):**
Decryption applies the inverse operations in reverse order: InvShiftRows, InvSubBytes, AddRoundKey, InvMixColumns (with last round omitting InvMixColumns).

---

**(b)** Perform the SubBytes transformation on the byte $0x2F$ using the AES S-Box. Show the GF($2^8$) multiplicative inverse and the affine transformation steps. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Convert $0x2F$ to polynomial (1 mark).**
$$
0x2F = 00101111_2 = x^5 + x^3 + x^2 + x + 1
$$

**Step 2: Compute multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ (3 marks).**

Using the extended Euclidean algorithm modulo $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$, the inverse is:

$$
(0x2F)^{-1} = 0x84 = 10000100_2 = x^7 + x^2
$$

**Verification:** $(x^5 + x^3 + x^2 + x + 1)(x^7 + x^2) \bmod m(x) = 1$ in $\text{GF}(2^8)$. *(You can show the modular multiplication to earn full marks.)*

**Step 3: Apply affine transformation (3 marks).**

For each bit $b'_i$ of the output, with $b = 0x84 = 10000100_2$ and $c = 0x63 = 01100011_2$:

$$
b'_i = b_i \oplus b_{(i+4) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+5) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+6) \bmod 8} \oplus b_{(i+7) \bmod 8} \oplus c_i
$$

| $i$ | $b_i$ | $b_{i+4}$ | $b_{i+5}$ | $b_{i+6}$ | $b_{i+7}$ | $c_i$ | $b'_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | **0** |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | **0** |
| 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1** |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | **1** |
| 7 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** |

Output: $b'_7 b'_6 b'_5 b'_4 b'_3 b'_2 b'_1 b'_0 = 11010001_2 = 0xD1$

**Result:** $S(0x2F) = 0xD1$ ✓ *(Matches the official AES S-Box.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the ShiftRows and MixColumns transformations of AES with suitable examples. Why are these two transformations together essential for diffusion? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**ShiftRows (3 marks):**
ShiftRows cyclically shifts the 4 rows of the State matrix to the left by 0, 1, 2, 3 byte positions respectively.
- Row 0: No shift.
- Row 1: Left shift by 1.
- Row 2: Left shift by 2.
- Row 3: Left shift by 3.

**Example:** If Row 1 before is $\{B, F, J, N\}$, after shift it becomes $\{F, J, N, B\}$.

**MixColumns (3 marks):**
MixColumns treats each column as a polynomial of degree $\le 3$ over $\text{GF}(2^8)$ and multiplies it by $a(x) = \{03\}x^3 + \{01\}x^2 + \{01\}x + \{02\}$ modulo $x^4 + 1$.

**Example:** For input column $[0xDB, 0x13, 0x53, 0x45]^T$, the output column is $[0x8E, 0x4D, 0xA1, 0xBC]^T$ (as derived in Section 3.2 above).

**Why both are essential for diffusion (1 mark):**
- **ShiftRows** ensures that bytes originally in different columns end up in the same column, enabling inter-column mixing.
- **MixColumns** then ensures that every output byte within a column depends on all 4 input bytes of that column.
- Together, after 2 rounds, **every output bit depends on every input bit** (full diffusion), satisfying **Shannon's diffusion property**.

---

**(b)** List and briefly explain the various AES operating modes used in IoT protocols. Compare AES-CBC and AES-CTR modes in terms of parallelism and error propagation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**AES Operating Modes in IoT (4 marks):**

1. **AES-ECB (Electronic Codebook):** Each block encrypted independently. Simple but leaks patterns. **Avoided in IoT.**
2. **AES-CBC (Cipher Block Chaining):** Each plaintext block is XORed with the previous ciphertext block before encryption. Requires IV. Used in older TLS.
3. **AES-CTR (Counter):** Encrypts a counter value and XORs the keystream with plaintext. Fully parallelizable. Used in modern IoT protocols.
4. **AES-GCM (Galois/Counter Mode):** CTR mode + GHASH authentication. Provides **authenticated encryption**. Used in **TLS 1.3, BLE 5.x, Zigbee 3.0**.
