5. **AES-CCM (CTR + CBC-MAC):** Combines CTR encryption with CBC-MAC authentication. Used in **IEEE 802.15.4 (Zigbee, Thread), LoRaWAN, Wireless M-Bus**.

**Comparison: AES-CBC vs AES-CTR (3 marks):**

| Property | AES-CBC | AES-CTR |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **Parallelism (Encryption)** | ❌ Not parallelizable (chained) | ✅ Fully parallelizable |
| **Parallelism (Decryption)** | ✅ Parallelizable (known ciphertext) | ✅ Fully parallelizable |
| **Error Propagation** | 1 bit error in ciphertext corrupts **2 blocks** (current + next) | 1 bit error corrupts **only 1 bit** in the corresponding plaintext bit |
| **Random Access** | ❌ Sequential only | ✅ Yes |
| **IV/Nonce requirement** | IV (must be unpredictable) | Nonce + counter |
| **IoT suitability** | Medium (legacy) | **High (preferred for resource-constrained devices)** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark Loss Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing block and key sizes:** A common mistake is writing "AES has a 64-bit block" — always **128 bits**.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+6" round overhead:** Students often quote the minimum rounds. Always use $N_r = 10/12/14$.
> 3. **Skipping the GF($2^8$) construction:** In SubBytes derivations, examiners expect the explicit mention of $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
> 4. **MixColumns in the final round:** Writing that MixColumns is applied in **all 10 rounds** is a classic error. It is **omitted in the last round**.
> 5. **Wrong S-Box entry for $0x00$:** $S(0x00) = 0x63$, not $0x00$.
> 6. **Treating AES as a Feistel network:** AES is an **SPN (Substitution-Permutation Network)**, not Feistel. This is a frequently tested 1-mark differentiator.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **AES = Rijndael** — standardized in **FIPS-197 (2001)**.
- ✅ **Block size = 128 bits (fixed)**; **Key size = 128 / 192 / 256 bits**.
- ✅ **Rounds = 10 / 12 / 14** for AES-128 / AES-192 / AES-256.
- ✅ **Formula:** $N_r = \max(N_k, N_b) + 6$, with $N_b = 4$.
- ✅ **State = $4 \times 4$ byte matrix**, filled **column-major** from plaintext.
- ✅ **Round operations (in order):** SubBytes $\rightarrow$ ShiftRows $\rightarrow$ MixColumns $\rightarrow$ AddRoundKey.
- ✅ **Final round:** SubBytes $\rightarrow$ ShiftRows $\rightarrow$ AddRoundKey (no MixColumns).
- ✅ **SubBytes** uses multiplicative inverse in $\text{GF}(2^8)$ with $m(x) = x^8 + x^4 + x^3 + x + 1$ + affine transform with constant $0x63$.
- ✅ **S-Box** is **bijective**; $S(0x00) = 0x63$, $S(0x63) = 0x7C$.
- ✅ **ShiftRows offsets:** 0, 1, 2, 3 (left); **InvShiftRows offsets:** 0, 3, 2, 1 (right).
- ✅ **MixColumns matrix** constants: $02, 03, 01, 01$. **InvMixColumns constants:** $0B, 0D, 09, 0E$.
- ✅ **AddRoundKey** = State $\oplus$ RoundKey (simple XOR).
- ✅ **Key Expansion** for AES-128: 44 words (1408 bits) total. Uses RotWord, SubWord, and Rcon.
- ✅ **Rcon[1..10]** = $\{01, 02, 04, 08, 10, 20, 40, 80, 1B, 36\}$.
- ✅ **AES is an SPN cipher**, **not a Feistel network** (contrast with DES).
- ✅ **IoT relevance:** Hardware AES engines in ESP32, STM32; AES-GCM in BLE 5, AES-CCM in IEEE 802.15.4, AES-CTR in TLS 1.3.
- ✅ **Decryption** = inverse operations in reverse order; can be re-implemented to mirror encryption for unified hardware.
- ✅ **Modes to know for IoT:** ECB (avoid), CBC, CTR, GCM, CCM.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "S-S-M-A"** = **S**ubBytes, **S**hiftRows, **M**ixColumns, **A**ddRoundKey — the round pipeline in order. Skip the **M** in the final round!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Block Cipher Modes of Operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Block Cipher Modes of Operation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1 — BLOCK CIPHER MODES OF OPERATION

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block Cipher:** A symmetric-key cryptographic algorithm that operates on fixed-size input blocks (typically **n = 64 or 128 bits**) and produces a fixed-size ciphertext block using a deterministic transformation under secret key **K**.

A single block cipher (e.g., AES, DES) can only securely encrypt a **single block** of size equal to its input block length. To encrypt a message longer than one block, we require a **Mode of Operation** — a standardized scheme that chains multiple block-cipher invocations together while providing **confidentiality**, **integrity** (in some modes), and **resistance to pattern analysis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (NIST SP 800-38A):** A *mode of operation* is a technique that describes how to repeatedly apply a single-block cipher's primitive to securely transform amounts of data larger than one block.

The five principal modes specified by **NIST SP 800-38A** are:

| Mode | Full Name | KTU Weightage |
|---|---|---|
| **ECB** | Electronic Codebook | High |
| **CBC** | Cipher Block Chaining | Very High |
| **CFB** | Cipher Feedback | High |
| **OFB** | Output Feedback | Medium |
| **CTR** | Counter Mode | Very High |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Locked Boxes" Analogy

Imagine you are sending a series of **sealed metal boxes** (plaintext blocks) through a courier, and your friend has a unique key to a **single lock type** (the block cipher, e.g., AES).

- If you use the **same lock** on every box → the courier notices identical boxes and can infer the contents (**ECB weakness**).
- If you **chain** each box to the previous one using a small numbered token (**IV / previous ciphertext**) → every box looks unique, even if two contain the same gift (**CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR**).

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for IoT:** Block cipher modes are critical for IoT because sensor payloads are often small, repetitive (e.g., temperature readings), and therefore highly vulnerable to frequency analysis if the mode leaks patterns. Modes like CTR and CBC convert deterministic block ciphers into **probabilistic** encryption schemes.

---

## 1.3 Why Do We Need Modes of Operation?

A bare block cipher has three fatal limitations:

1. **Determinism** — Same plaintext + same key ⇒ same ciphertext. Identical ciphertexts reveal identical plaintexts.
2. **Limited Block Size** — Can encrypt only **b bits** at a time (b = 128 for AES).
3. **No Semantic Security** — An attacker can detect repeated blocks and launch replay/pattern attacks.

Modes of operation resolve all three by introducing an **Initialization Vector (IV)**, **chaining mechanisms**, and **padding schemes**.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology (KTU Exam Favorites)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Plaintext Block (P_i):** The i-th block of original message.
> - **Ciphertext Block (C_i):** The i-th block of encrypted output.
> - **Key (K):** Secret symmetric key shared between sender and receiver.
> - **Initialization Vector (IV):** A random (or pseudo-random) value used to seed the first block. **Must be unpredictable** for CBC/CFB and **must be unique (nonce)** for CTR/OFB.
> - **Padding:** A scheme (e.g., PKCS#7) to extend the final plaintext block to full block length.

---

## 1.5 Padding Schemes (PKCS#7)

When the message length is not an exact multiple of the block size, we pad the final block. The most common standard is **PKCS#7**:

If **r** bytes of padding are required (1 ≤ r ≤ b), each of the **r** bytes has the value **r**.

Example for block size 8 and 5-byte message `HELLO`:
- Padding bytes = 3 bytes of value `0x03`
- Final block = `HELLO\x03\x03\x03`

---

## 1.6 Module-Level Learning Outcomes

After completing this section, the student should be able to:

- **CO1:** Explain the role of an IV, padding, and chaining in transforming a deterministic block cipher into a probabilistic one.
- **CO2:** Identify the encryption and decryption equations for ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, and CTR.
- **CO3:** Analyze error propagation, parallelism, and security properties of each mode.
- **CO4:** Apply the correct mode to a given IoT scenario (e.g., streaming sensor data, batch uploads, low-latency control packets).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 The Five Modes — Operational Theory

### 🔷 2.1.1 ECB — Electronic Codebook Mode

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Divide plaintext into b-bit blocks P_1, P_2, …, P_n.
2. Pad the last block if needed.
3. Encrypt each block independently using the same key K.
4. Concatenate ciphertext blocks C_1 ‖ C_2 ‖ … ‖ C_n.

**Encryption Equation:**
$$C_i = E_K(P_i)$$

**Decryption Equation:**
$$P_i = D_K(C_i)$$

**Why ECB Fails:** ECB is **deterministic and stateless**. The famous *ECB Penguin* demonstrates how raw bitmap encryption with ECB still reveals the original image silhouette. For IoT sensor data (which is highly repetitive — e.g., 25°C every minute), ECB completely leaks the value distribution.

---

### 🔷 2.1.2 CBC — Cipher Block Chaining Mode

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. Generate a random IV of size b.
2. XOR the first plaintext block with the IV before encryption.
3. For each subsequent block, XOR with the **previous ciphertext block** before encryption.
4. Padding is mandatory.

**Encryption Equations:**
$$C_0 = \text{IV}$$
$$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1}) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Decryption Equations:**
$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1} \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Why CBC Works:** Each ciphertext block depends on **all previous plaintext blocks** through the chaining. Identical plaintexts produce different ciphertexts (provided IV is random). Bit-flip attacks on IV/C_{i-1} are possible — hence **integrity must be added externally** (MAC or AEAD).

---

### 🔷 2.1.3 CFB — Cipher Feedback Mode

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. CFB converts a block cipher into a **self-synchronizing stream cipher**.
2. The shift register holds the previous ciphertext block (initially IV).
3. That register is encrypted, and the **leftmost s bits** (where s = segment size, e.g., 8, 64, 128) are XORed with the next plaintext segment to form ciphertext.

**Encryption Equations (for segment size s):**
$$I_1 = \text{IV}$$
$$O_i = E_K(I_i)$$
$$C_i = P_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(O_i)$$
$$I_{i+1} = \text{LSB}_{b-s}(I_i) \,\Vert\, C_i$$

**Decryption Equations:**
$$I_1 = \text{IV}$$
$$O_i = E_K(I_i)$$
$$P_i = C_i \oplus \text{MSB}_s(O_i)$$
$$I_{i+1} = \text{LSB}_{b-s}(I_i) \,\Vert\, C_i$$

**Why CFB Matters for IoT:** Operates on segments smaller than full block — useful when ciphertext must be transmitted in real time, byte-by-byte over a low-bandwidth link.

---

### 🔷 2.1.4 OFB — Output Feedback Mode

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. OFB turns the block cipher into a **synchronous stream cipher**.
2. The keystream is generated by repeatedly encrypting the IV.
3. Plaintext is XORed with the keystream to produce ciphertext.

**Encryption Equations:**
$$O_0 = \text{IV}$$
$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$$
$$C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

**Decryption Equations:**
$$O_0 = \text{IV}$$
$$O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$$
$$P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

**Why OFB Is Special:** The keystream is **precomputable** (decoupled from plaintext/ciphertext), making OFB ideal for low-power IoT nodes that generate the keystream in advance. Errors do not propagate.

---

### 🔷 2.1.5 CTR — Counter Mode

**Conceptual Logic:**
1. A **counter** T_i (typically IV + i) is encrypted to produce a keystream block.
2. The keystream block is XORed with the plaintext block.
3. CTR is essentially OFB with a counter replacing the feedback chain.

**Encryption Equations:**
$$C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$
$$T_i = \text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, i$$

**Decryption Equations:**
$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

**Why CTR Dominates IoT:** 
- **Fully parallelizable** for both encryption and decryption.
- **Random access** — any block can be decrypted independently.
- **No padding required** — works for arbitrary-length messages.
- **Provably secure** under standard cryptographic assumptions (IND-CPA).

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Modes of Operation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it carries 7–8 marks on average in ESE Module-1 questions.**

| Mode | Encryption | Decryption | IV Role | Padding? | Parallel Encrypt? | Parallel Decrypt? | Error Propagation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ECB** | C_i = E_K(P_i) | P_i = D_K(C_i) | None | Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 1 block |
| **CBC** | C_i = E_K(P_i ⊕ C_{i-1}) | P_i = D_K(C_i) ⊕ C_{i-1} | Random, unpredictable | Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | 2 blocks |
| **CFB** | C_i = P_i ⊕ MSB_s(E_K(C_{i-1})) | P_i = C_i ⊕ MSB_s(E_K(C_{i-1})) | Random | No (segment-based) | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | \vert 1 + ⌈b/s⌉ \vert blocks |
| **OFB** | C_i = P_i ⊕ E_K(O_{i-1}) | P_i = C_i ⊕ E_K(O_{i-1}) | Unique nonce | No | ❌ No | ❌ No | 1 segment (no prop.) |
| **CTR** | C_i = P_i ⊕ E_K(Nonce ‖ i) | P_i = C_i ⊕ E_K(Nonce ‖ i) | Unique nonce | No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | 1 block (no prop.) |

> [!NOTE]
> **MSB_s(X)** = most significant **s** bits of X. **LSB_{b-s}(X)** = least significant (b-s) bits of X.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **NIST-Recommended Usage Hierarchy (for new IoT designs in 2024):**
> - **Best:** CTR + HMAC, or **AES-GCM** (an AEAD built on CTR).
> - **Acceptable:** CBC + HMAC (legacy systems, TLS 1.2).
> - **Avoid:** ECB (only for single random blocks, e.g., key wrapping).
> - **Avoid:** CFB/OFB (kept for compatibility, rarely chosen for new designs).

**Industry Examples:**
- **TLS 1.3:** Mandates **CTR (AES-GCM)** and **CBC (AES-CBC + HMAC)** only.
- **IPsec ESP:** Default is **CBC** (legacy) or **GCM** (modern).
- **IoT Protocols (MQTT, CoAP):** CoAP-sec uses **AES-CCM** (CTR + CBC-MAC), derived directly from CTR mode.
- **Disk Encryption (BitLocker, FileVault):** **XTS mode** — a tweakable ECB variant.

---

## 2.4 Why ECB Is Broken — The Bitmap Argument

Consider a 16-byte block that is a fully **white** pixel. Under AES-ECB, the ciphertext for every white pixel block is **identical**. An attacker who replaces a ciphertext block propagates the same pixel across the entire image. In contrast, CBC's chaining makes every block depend on the **entire history** of plaintext, defeating this attack vector.

---

## 2.5 Why IV Management Matters

> [!WARNING]
> **IV Reuse Catastrophe:**
> - **CBC with reused IV:** Two messages with same first plaintext block produce the same first ciphertext block → leaks plaintext equality.
> - **CTR/OFB with reused nonce:** Reveals the XOR of two plaintexts: P_i ⊕ P'_i = C_i ⊕ C'_i (devastating for keystream-recovery attacks).

**Best Practice:** Generate a **fresh random 128-bit IV/nonce** for every encryption with the same key. For CTR, use a 96-bit random nonce (per RFC 5116).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of CBC Bit-Flip Attack

A classic KTU examination question: *"If an attacker flips bit j of C_{i-1}, how does plaintext block P_i change?"*

**Starting Encryption Equation:**

$$C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$

**Step 1 — Apply decryption on the receiver side:**

$$D_K(C_i) = P_i \oplus C_{i-1}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for P_i:**

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

**Step 3 — Let the attacker modify C_{i-1} to C'_{i-1} where C'_{i-1} = C_{i-1} ⊕ Δ for some bit-flip mask Δ:**

$$C'_{i-1} = C_{i-1} \oplus \Delta$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the decryption equation at the receiver:**

$$P'_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C'_{i-1}$$

**Step 5 — Use the identity A ⊕ B ⊕ Δ = (A ⊕ B) ⊕ Δ:**

$$P'_i = \big(D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}\big) \oplus \Delta$$

**Step 6 — Compare with original P_i = D_K(C_i) ⊕ C_{i-1}:**

$$P'_i = P_i \oplus \Delta$$

**Conclusion:** Flipping bit **j** of **C_{i-1}** flips the **same bit j** of **P_i** at the receiver — **and corrupts all of P_{i+1}** because P_{i+1} = D_K(C_{i+1}) ⊕ C_i (now C_i has changed). This is the basis of attacks on unauthenticated CBC (e.g., the BEAST and POODLE attacks on TLS).

---

## 3.2 CBC Error Propagation — Full Derivation

Assume a single bit error in ciphertext block C_i at the receiver.

**Step 1 — Effect on P_i decryption:**

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

Since C_i is corrupted, D_K(C_i) becomes a completely random block (avalanche effect of block cipher). Hence **P_i is totally garbled** (≈50% bit error rate).

**Step 2 — Effect on P_{i+1}:**

$$P_{i+1} = D_K(C_{i+1}) \oplus C_i$$

C_i is corrupted, so P_{i+1} has the **same bit error** as the flipped bit of C_i.

**Step 3 — Effect on P_{i+2}:**

$$P_{i+2} = D_K(C_{i+2}) \oplus C_{i+1}$$

C_{i+1} is intact, so **P_{i+2 is correct.**

**Conclusion:** A 1-bit error in C_i produces **one completely garbled block** (P_i) and **one block with the corresponding bit flipped** (P_{i+1}). This is the famous "**CBC error propagation of 2 blocks**" rule.

---

## 3.3 CTR Mode — Derivation of Random-Access Decryption

Given a stored ciphertext C_1 ‖ C_2 ‖ … ‖ C_n and a desired block index k, the receiver can compute P_k **without decrypting** any other block.

**Step 1 — Start with the encryption equation:**

$$C_k = P_k \oplus E_K(\text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, k)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for P_k:**

$$P_k = C_k \oplus E_K(\text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, k)$$

**Step 3 — Verification:** Notice that P_k depends **only on C_k, the key, and the nonce** — no dependency on C_{k-1}, C_{k+1}, or any other ciphertext block. This is why CTR supports **random access** (e.g., encrypting database records at known offsets).

**Engineering Implication:** In an IoT gateway storing sensor logs on an SD card, CTR enables reading block k without re-encrypting or re-decrypting the entire log.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — CBC Mode

Suppose the block size is 4 bits, K is a toy cipher, and IV = 1010.

| i | P_i (4 bits) | C_{i-1} (or IV) | P_i ⊕ C_{i-1} | C_i = E_K(·) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0110 | IV = 1010 | 1100 | 0011 |
| 2 | 1101 | C_1 = 0011 | 1110 | 0101 |
| 3 | 1010 | C_2 = 0101 | 1111 | 1001 |

**Decryption check for i = 2:**

$$P_2 = D_K(C_2) \oplus C_1 = D_K(0101) \oplus 0011 = 1110 \oplus 0011 = 1101 \;\; \checkmark$$

This compact example demonstrates CBC chaining — every ciphertext block carries "memory" of all previous plaintext blocks.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison — Security vs. Performance Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** A ✅ means the property is satisfied; ❌ means the property is violated or unsupported. Security = IND-CPA under standard assumptions.

| Property | ECB | CBC | CFB | OFB | CTR |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality (IND-CPA)** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Randomized Encryption** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (with unique IV) | ✅ (with unique nonce) |
| **Parallelizable Encryption** | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Parallelizable Decryption** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Random Access Decryption** | ✅ | ❌ (sequential) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| **Precomputable Keystream** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (but E_K fast) |
| **Self-Synchronizing** | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Padding Required** | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Bit-Flip Resistant** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ |
| **Standard for TLS 1.3** | ❌ | ✅ (legacy) | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (GCM) |
| **IoT Suitability (low-power MCU)** | ❌ | Medium | High (small s) | Medium | ✅ Best |

---

## 3.6 OFB Keystream Precomputation — Formal Proof

**Step 1 — Define the keystream generator:**

$$O_0 = \text{IV}, \quad O_i = E_K(O_{i-1})$$

**Step 2 — Show that O_i is a deterministic function of K and IV alone:**

By induction: O_1 = E_K(IV), O_2 = E_K(E_K(IV)), etc. None of these depend on P_i or C_i.

**Step 3 — Decouple keystream generation from data flow:**

The sender/receiver can compute the entire keystream O_1, O_2, …, O_n **before** the plaintext is even available. This is critical for **streaming encryption** and for **resource-constrained IoT nodes** that wake up briefly to encrypt a buffer.

**Step 4 — Compare with CTR:**

In CTR, the "keystream" is E_K(Nonce ‖ i) for each i. While this also doesn't depend on the plaintext, computing it on the fly is typically faster than OFB's chain because the input (Nonce ‖ i) can be computed in one addition rather than one block-cipher call per output block.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 4 — STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 ECB Mode — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> EK1[Encrypt E_K]
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> EK2[Encrypt E_K]
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> EK3[Encrypt E_K]
    EK1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    EK2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    EK3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
```

> **Observation:** Three independent E_K blocks, no chaining. Same P_i → same C_i. **Vulnerable to pattern leakage.**

---

## 4.2 CBC Mode — Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IV[Initialization Vector IV] --> XOR1
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1[XOR] --> EK1[Encrypt E_K]
    EK1 --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    C1 --> XOR2
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> EK2[Encrypt E_K]
    EK2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    C2 --> XOR3
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> EK3[Encrypt E_K]
    EK3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
```

> **Observation:** Each plaintext is XORed with previous ciphertext before encryption. Serial dependency.

---

## 4.3 CBC Mode — Decryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IV[Initialization Vector IV] --> XOR1
    C1[Ciphertext C1] --> DK1[Decrypt D_K]
    DK1 --> XOR1
    XOR1 --> P1[Plaintext P1]
    C1 --> XOR2
    C2[Ciphertext C2] --> DK2[Decrypt D_K]
    DK2 --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> P2[Plaintext P2]
    C2 --> XOR3
    C3[Ciphertext C3] --> DK3[Decrypt D_K]
    DK3 --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> P3[Plaintext P3]
```

> **Observation:** Decryption is parallelizable — all C_i can be decrypted independently, then XORed with C_{i-1}.

---

## 4.4 CFB Mode — Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IV[Shift Register IV] --> EK1[Encrypt E_K]
    EK1 --> MSB[MSB s bits]
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    MSB --> XOR1
    XOR1[XOR] --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    C1 --> SR1[Shift Register]
    SR1 --> EK2[Encrypt E_K]
    EK2 --> MSB2[MSB s bits]
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    MSB2 --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
```

> **Observation:** Block cipher is used as a keystream generator. Output is truncated to s bits and XORed with plaintext segment.

---

## 4.5 OFB Mode — Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    IV[Output Register O0] --> EK1[Encrypt E_K]
    EK1 --> O1[Output O1]
    O1 --> P1XOR
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> P1XOR
    P1XOR[XOR] --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    O1 --> EK2[Encrypt E_K]
    EK2 --> O2[Output O2]
    O2 --> P2XOR
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> P2XOR
    P2XOR --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
```

> **Observation:** The keystream (O_i) is **independent of plaintext/ciphertext** — fully precomputable.

---

## 4.6 CTR Mode — Encryption Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    N[Nonce] --> T1[Counter T1 = N + 1]
    N --> T2[Counter T2 = N + 2]
    N --> T3[Counter T3 = N + 3]
    T1 --> EK1[Encrypt E_K]
    T2 --> EK2[Encrypt E_K]
    T3 --> EK3[Encrypt E_K]
    EK1 --> XOR1
    P1[Plaintext P1] --> XOR1
    XOR1[XOR] --> C1[Ciphertext C1]
    EK2 --> XOR2
    P2[Plaintext P2] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> C2[Ciphertext C2]
    EK3 --> XOR3
    P3[Plaintext P3] --> XOR3
    XOR3 --> C3[Ciphertext C3]
```

> **Observation:** Three parallel E_K operations — one for each counter value. **Maximum throughput and parallelism.**

---

## 4.7 Comparative Architecture Flow — IoT Sensor Use Case

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SENSOR["IoT Sensor Node (Low Power)"]
        S1[Temperature Reading: 25 degC] --> S2[16-byte Plaintext Block]
        S2 --> S3{Choose Mode}
    end
    S3 --> M1[ECB ❌ Reveals 25 degC pattern]
    S3 --> M2[CBC ✅ Hides pattern, needs padding]
    S3 --> M3[CTR ✅ Best for streaming, no padding]
    S3 --> M4[CFB ✅ For 8-bit segments over UART]
    M1 --> R1[REJECT]
    M2 --> R2[ACCEPT for batch upload]
    M3 --> R3[RECOMMENDED for real-time telemetry]
    M4 --> R4[ACCEPT for byte-by-byte UART link]
    R3 --> TX[Transmit over LoRa / NB-IoT]
    R2 --> TX
    R4 --> TX
```

> **Decision Logic:** For modern IoT designs, **CTR (or GCM built on CTR)** is the default. CBC is reserved for TLS 1.2 interop. ECB is forbidden except for single-block key wrapping.

---

## 4.8 Error Propagation Visual Map

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1[C1 OK] --> P1[P1 OK]
    C2[C2 ERROR] --> P2[P2 GARBLED]
    C2 --> P3[P3 ONE BIT FLIPPED]
    C3[C3 OK] --> P4[P4 OK]
```

> **Visual:** In CBC, a single corrupted C_i destroys P_i completely and slightly damages P_{i+1}. In CTR/OFB, only the affected segment is garbled — no propagation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"What is a block cipher mode of operation? Why is ECB mode considered insecure for encrypting messages longer than one block?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **block cipher mode of operation** is a standardized scheme (defined in NIST SP 800-38A) that specifies how to repeatedly apply a block cipher's encryption/decryption primitive to securely process messages longer than a single block.

ECB is insecure for multi-block messages because **(i) it is deterministic** — the same plaintext block under the same key always yields the same ciphertext block, leaking patterns; and **(ii) identical ciphertext blocks** allow replay and frequency-analysis attacks. The famous ECB-encrypted bitmap image still reveals the original picture's silhouette. Hence ECB is suitable only for single random blocks (e.g., key wrapping), never for messages with repetition. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for each weakness)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between OFB and CFB modes of operation with respect to feedback mechanism and error propagation."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | CFB | OFB |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback source** | Previous **ciphertext** block (C_{i-1}) | Previous **output** block (O_{i-1}) |
| **Self-synchronizing** | ✅ Yes — receiver resyncs after a few blocks | ❌ No — keystream must be regenerated from start |
| **Error propagation** | Affects current + next ⌈b/s⌉ blocks | Only the affected segment (no propagation) |
| **Keystream precomputable** | ❌ No (depends on ciphertext) | ✅ Yes (depends only on IV and K) |

**(3 marks — 1 for feedback distinction, 1 for sync property, 1 for error propagation)**

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Explain the **Electronic Codebook (ECB)** and **Cipher Block Chaining (CBC)** modes of operation with neat block diagrams. Derive the encryption and decryption equations for both modes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system uses AES-128 in CBC mode with a 128-bit random IV. An attacker flips bit 17 of the first ciphertext block C_1. Explain precisely how this tamper affects the receiver's decrypted plaintext blocks P_1 and P_2. Justify your answer with the relevant equations. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a) [7 Marks]**

**ECB Mode:**

ECB divides the plaintext P into b-bit blocks (b = 128 for AES) and encrypts each independently with the same key K.

**Encryption Equation:**
$$C_i = E_K(P_i) \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

**Decryption Equation:**
$$P_i = D_K(C_i)$$

**Block Diagram (text form for answer sheet):**

```
P1 ──►[ E_K ]──► C1
P2 ──►[ E_K ]──► C2
P3 ──►[ E_K ]──► C3
```

**Advantages:** Simple, parallelizable, supports random access.

**Disadvantages:** Deterministic → identical P_i produce identical C_i, leaking patterns.

**CBC Mode:**

CBC XORs each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block (or IV for the first block) before encryption.

**Encryption Equation:**
$$C_0 = \text{IV}, \quad C_i = E_K(P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$$

**Decryption Equation:**
$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

**Block Diagram:**

```
IV ──┐
     XOR ──►[ E_K ]──► C1
P1 ──┘
                  ┌──►[ E_K ]──► C2
C1 ──► XOR ◄── P2 ┘
                  ┌──►[ E_K ]──► C3
C2 ──► XOR ◄── P3 ┘
```

**Advantages:** Probabilistic (random IV) → hides patterns; supports authenticated encryption with MAC.

**Disadvantages:** Sequential encryption (no parallelism); padding required; bit-flip vulnerability on IV/C_{i-1}.

**Mark Allocation (7):** 1.5 marks for ECB equations, 1.5 marks for CBC equations, 1.5 marks for ECB diagram, 1.5 marks for CBC diagram, 1 mark for advantages/disadvantages.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b) [7 Marks]**

**Given:** AES-128-CBC. Attacker flips bit 17 of C_1. IV is unchanged. Effect must be analyzed on P_1 and P_2.

**Step 1 — Write the receiver's decryption equation for P_1:**

$$P_1 = D_K(C_1) \oplus \text{IV}$$

**Step 2 — Since C_1 is now corrupted, the receiver obtains a random-looking plaintext block:**

Let the tampered ciphertext be C'_1 = C_1 ⊕ Δ, where Δ has a 1 in bit position 17 and 0 elsewhere.

$$D_K(C'_1) \neq D_K(C_1)$$

Because AES is a **pseudorandom permutation**, the change of one input bit causes approximately **half of the output bits** to flip (avalanche effect). Hence **P'_1 is completely garbled** — about 64 out of 128 bits are wrong.

**Step 3 — Effect on P_2:**

$$P_2 = D_K(C_2) \oplus C_1$$

Since C_1 has been tampered to C'_1:

$$P'_2 = D_K(C_2) \oplus C'_1 = D_K(C_2) \oplus C_1 \oplus \Delta = P_2 \oplus \Delta$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:** The bit-flip in C_1 causes:
- **P_1:** Entirely garbled (~50% bit error rate).
- **P_2:** Exactly the same single bit (bit 17) flipped, with all other bits correct.

**Mark Allocation (7):** 1 mark for setting up the decryption equation, 1 mark for avalanche argument on P_1, 2 marks for P_2 derivation, 2 marks for the explicit P'_2 = P_2 ⊕ Δ result, 1 mark for a clean conclusion sentence.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 1]**

**(a)** Describe **Output Feedback (OFB)** and **Counter (CTR)** modes of operation. Derive their encryption and decryption equations, and explain why CTR is preferred over OFB for modern IoT applications. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wireless sensor transmits 1024-byte payloads encrypted with AES-128-CTR using a 96-bit random nonce. Show that **random-access decryption** is possible. If a packet contains 64 blocks and the gateway wishes to decrypt only block 47 (the temperature alert), how many AES-128 block-cipher calls are needed? Justify using the CTR equation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 Marks]**

**OFB Mode:**

OFB turns the block cipher into a synchronous stream cipher. The keystream is generated by repeatedly encrypting the IV, independent of plaintext or ciphertext.

**Encryption Equations:**
$$O_0 = \text{IV}, \quad O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}), \quad C_i = P_i \oplus O_i$$

**Decryption Equations:**
$$O_0 = \text{IV}, \quad O_i = E_K(O_{i-1}), \quad P_i = C_i \oplus O_i$$

**CTR Mode:**

CTR encrypts a counter (typically Nonce ‖ i) and XORs the result with the plaintext block.

**Encryption Equation:**
$$T_i = \text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, i, \quad C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

**Decryption Equation:**
$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i)$$

**Comparison for IoT:**

| Criterion | OFB | CTR |
|---|---|---|
| **Parallel encryption** | ❌ Sequential chain | ✅ Fully parallel |
| **Random access decryption** | ❌ Must regenerate keystream from i=1 | ✅ Direct: encrypt T_47 and XOR with C_47 |
| **Throughput on multi-core MCU** | Limited | Linear scaling |
| **Standardization (TLS 1.3, IPsec)** | Deprecated | ✅ Mandatory (GCM, CCM) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Medium | Low |

**Conclusion:** CTR is preferred because it offers **full parallelism, random access, no padding, and is the basis of modern AEAD modes (AES-GCM, AES-CCM)** which are ubiquitous in IoT security (CoAP, MQTT-SN, Matter, Thread).

**Mark Allocation (7):** 1.5 marks for OFB equations, 1.5 marks for CTR equations, 2 marks for comparison table, 1 mark for "why CTR wins" justification, 1 mark for AEAD reference.

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Recall the CTR decryption equation:**

$$P_i = C_i \oplus E_K(T_i), \quad \text{where } T_i = \text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, i$$

**Step 2 — Apply to the specific case (i = 47):**

$$P_{47} = C_{47} \oplus E_K(\text{Nonce} \,\Vert\, 47)$$

**Step 3 — Count the number of AES-128 block-cipher calls required:**

Only **one** AES-128 block-cipher call is needed: E_K(Nonce ‖ 47). The gateway does not need to decrypt blocks 1–46 or 48–64. This is the **random-access property** of CTR mode.

**Step 4 — Why random access is possible (algebraic reasoning):**

The decryption of P_i depends **only on** C_i, K, and the known counter value T_i. There is **no dependency** on C_{i-1}, C_{i+1}, or any other ciphertext block. Contrast with CBC where:

$$P_i = D_K(C_i) \oplus C_{i-1}$$

CBC requires C_{i-1} to decrypt P_i, which forces sequential access.

**Step 5 — Engineering implication for IoT:**

A smart-home gateway receiving temperature alerts from 64 sensors can decrypt only the alert-flagged block (block 47) using **one** AES-128 invocation, saving **63 block-cipher calls** (~95% CPU reduction) and enabling fast selective reads from encrypted log files.

**Mark Allocation (7):** 1.5 marks for the equation, 1.5 marks for the count (1 call), 2 marks for algebraic justification of no-dependency, 1 mark for the CBC contrast, 1 mark for engineering application.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in ESE Module 1:**
> 1. **Confusing IV and Key** — IV is public and can be transmitted in cleartext; **key is secret**. Several students write "the IV is used for decryption" → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Swapping C_i and P_i in CBC diagrams** — Draw the XOR **before** E_K for encryption and **after** D_K for decryption. Reverse diagrams lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the IV equation C_0 = IV** — When asked for CBC encryption equations, omitting the IV initialization costs 1 mark.
> 4. **Writing "OFB is parallelizable"** — OFB is **sequential** (each O_i depends on O_{i-1}). Only CTR and ECB are fully parallel.
> 5. **Ignoring padding in CBC/ECB** — If a question asks "is padding required?", the correct answer is **YES for ECB and CBC**, **NO for CTR, OFB, and CFB**. This distinction is worth 1 mark.
> 6. **Forgetting the unique-nonce requirement for CTR** — The CTR mode requires a **unique (nonce, counter) pair** for every encryption with the same key. Reuse breaks security catastrophically.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Cipher Modes of Operation (KTU Module 1)**

- ✅ A block cipher alone is **deterministic** and **single-block**; modes of operation fix both limitations by adding **IV** and **chaining**.
- ✅ The **five NIST-standard modes** are **ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR**.
- ✅ **ECB** is the simplest but **insecure for multi-block data** — never use for messages.
- ✅ **CBC** requires **random unpredictable IV** and **padding**; supports parallel decryption but not encryption.
- ✅ **CBC error propagation:** 1 corrupted ciphertext block → 1 garbled plaintext block + 1 bit-flipped block.
- ✅ **CFB** converts block cipher to **self-synchronizing stream cipher**; output truncated to **s bits** (often s = 8, 64, or 128).
- ✅ **OFB** is a **synchronous stream cipher** with **precomputable keystream**; errors do not propagate but mode is not self-synchronizing.
- ✅ **CTR** encrypts a **counter (Nonce ‖ i)**; supports **full parallelism, random access, no padding** — the **modern default**.
- ✅ **Modern standard (TLS 1.3, IoT CoAP, Matter):** Use **AES-GCM** (an AEAD built on CTR) or **AES-CCM**.
- ✅ **IV/Nonce reuse is catastrophic** for CBC, CTR, OFB — always generate a fresh random value per encryption.
- ✅ **Bit-flip attacks** are possible on unauthenticated CBC and CTR — always pair with a **MAC** (HMAC, GMAC) or use AEAD.
- ✅ **Performance order (fastest → slowest, software):** CTR > CBC > CFB > OFB > ECB (pipelined).
- ✅ **IoT recommendation summary:**
  - **Real-time streaming telemetry** → CTR / GCM
  - **Interop with TLS 1.2** → CBC + HMAC-SHA256
  - **Byte-by-byte UART link** → CFB with s = 8
  - **Single-block key wrap** → ECB (or KW from NIST SP 800-38F)

---

**END OF MODULE 1 NOTES — BLOCK CIPHER MODES OF OPERATION**

*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme | Outcome-Based Education | NEP 2020 Aligned*
*Aligned with CO1, CO2, CO3 of PCCIT602 — Cryptography for IoT Applications*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Cipher
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stream Cipher ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stream Cipher (KTU 2024 Definition):** A **Stream Cipher** is a symmetric key cryptographic algorithm that encrypts plaintext one bit or one byte at a time, by combining the plaintext with a pseudorandom keystream generated from a secret key using a keystream generator (often based on Linear Feedback Shift Registers or non-linear state update functions). Mathematically, encryption is defined as: $$C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$$ where $P_i$ is the $i$-th bit of the plaintext, $K_i$ is the $i$-th bit of the keystream, and $\oplus$ is the XOR (modulo-2 addition) operation. Decryption is symmetric: $$P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Highlight:** Stream ciphers are part of **Symmetric Key Cryptography** and are the most common alternative to **Block Ciphers** for resource-constrained devices, particularly in **IoT ecosystems** where low latency and low power consumption are critical design constraints. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **one-time pad** (the most secure cipher in history) — but instead of using a truly random paper pad, you generate the random pad using a small, deterministic recipe known to both the sender and receiver. This recipe is the **keystream generator**. **Real-World Analogy — The Talking Tube (Whisper Tube):** Two children want to pass secret notes. They agree on a secret rhythm (the **key**). The sender hums the rhythm and adds one extra note on top — *singing slightly higher or lower* than the rhythm. The receiver, who knows the rhythm, can subtract the rhythm and recover the original message. Anyone listening hears only a meaningless mix. The **rhythm = keystream**, the **song = ciphertext**, and the **original message = plaintext**. > [!TIP] > **Why XOR?** XOR is **involutory** (self-inverse) and **bitwise independent**, meaning a single bit error in transmission causes only a single bit error in the decrypted message — a property known as **error propagation = 1 bit**. This is critical in noisy IoT channels like LoRa, ZigBee, and BLE. ### 1.3 Classification of Stream Ciphers | Type | Description | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Synchronous Stream Cipher** | Keystream depends **only on the key and IV**, not on the plaintext/ciphertext. Sender and receiver must remain perfectly synchronized. | RC4, Salsa20, ChaCha20, Grain, Trivium | | **Asynchronous (Self-Synchronizing) Stream Cipher** | Keystream is a function of the key and a fixed number of previous ciphertext bits. Auto-recovers from bit loss/deletion. | CFB mode (block cipher used as stream cipher) | | **Binary Additive Stream Cipher** | Most common — uses XOR as the combining function. | RC4, ChaCha20 | | **Binary Multiplicative Stream Cipher** | Uses multiplication mod $2^n$ as the combining function. | Less common, vulnerable | ### 1.4 Stream Cipher vs Block Cipher | Parameter | Stream Cipher | Block Cipher | |---|---|---| | **Unit of processing** | Bit or byte | Fixed block (e.g., 64/128 bits) | | **Speed** | Faster, no padding | Slower due to padding + modes | | **Memory footprint** | Low (suits IoT) | Higher | | **Error propagation** | 1 bit (in synchronous) | 1 full block (≈64–128 bits) | | **Diffusion** | Weaker | Stronger | | **Typical use** | Real-time audio/video streaming, TLS, WEP/WPA | File encryption, disk encryption | | **Examples** | RC4, ChaCha20 | AES, DES, 3DES | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for IoT:** Stream ciphers dominate IoT security protocols like **ZigBee (CCM mode)**, **Bluetooth LE**, **LoRaWAN (AES-CCM)**, and **TLS 1.3 (ChaCha20-Poly1305)** because of their low computational overhead and small hardware footprint. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Architecture of a Stream Cipher A stream cipher has **three core components**: 1. **Key Loading Module** — Accepts a secret key $K$ of size $k$ bits (typically 80–256 bits for IoT). 2. **Initialization Vector (IV) / Nonce** — A public, non-repeating value $V$ that randomizes keystream across sessions. 3. **Keystream Generator (KSG)** — A stateful finite-state machine that outputs a pseudo-random bit/byte sequence $Z_0, Z_1, Z_2, \ldots$ **Encryption operation:** $$C_i = P_i \oplus Z_i \quad \forall i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$$ **Decryption operation:** $$P_i = C_i \oplus Z_i \quad \forall i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, L-1\}$$ where $L$ is the length of the plaintext in bits. ### 2.2 Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR) — The Heart of Stream Ciphers > [!IMPORTANT] > **LFSR (KTU Module 1 Core):** A Linear Feedback Shift Register is a shift register whose input bit is a **linear function (XOR)** of its previous state. It is the most fundamental building block of classical stream ciphers. **LFSR of length $n$:** A register with $n$ memory cells $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_{n-1}$ that shifts left on each clock pulse. The new input bit is computed as: $$s_{new} = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i \cdot s_i \pmod{2}$$ where $c_i \in \{0, 1\}$ are the **feedback coefficients** and $\cdot$ denotes multiplication mod 2 (i.e., AND). **Key Property:** An $n$-bit LFSR can produce a **maximal-length sequence (m-sequence)** of period: $$T_{max} = 2^n - 1$$ **m-sequences** have the following statistically ideal properties: - Balance: Number of 1s = $2^{n-1}$, Number of 0s = $2^{n-1} - 1$ - Run distribution: Geometric - Autocorrelation: Two-valued ($\{-1, 2^n - 1\}$) **Feedback Polynomial:** $$C(x) = 1 + c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + \cdots + c_n x^n \pmod{2}$$ For an m-sequence, $C(x)$ must be a **primitive polynomial** of degree $n$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$. > [!TIP] > **Why primitive polynomials?** They ensure the LFSR cycles through ALL $2^n - 1$ non-zero states before repeating — the **maximum possible period** for a deterministic $n$-bit state machine. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Concept | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | Stream cipher encryption | $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$ | XOR-based combining | | Stream cipher decryption | $P_i = C_i \oplus K_i$ | XOR is self-inverse | | LFSR state update | $s_{new} = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i s_i$ | Modulo-2 linear combination | | Maximal period | $T_{max} = 2^n - 1$ | For $n$-stage LFSR with primitive feedback poly | | Number of m-sequences | $\phi(2^n - 1)/n$ | Euler's totient of period | | RC4 state permutation | $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \mod L]) \pmod{256}$ | Key scheduling algorithm (KSA) | | RC4 keystream output | $K_{out} = S[(S[i] + S[j]) \mod 256]$ | Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA) | | Bit-rate (encryption) | $R_b = f_{clk} \times 1 \text{ bit}$ | One bit per clock cycle for LFSR | | Entropy of key | $H(K) = \log_2 \vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ | Where $\vert \mathcal{K} \vert$ is keyspace size | | Berlekamp-Massey complexity | $L \le n + \sum_{i} t_i$ | LFSR length needed to synthesize sequence | ### 2.4 RC4 — The Most Studied Stream Cipher (KTU 2024) **RC4 (Rivest Cipher 4)** was designed by **Ron Rivest in 1987** and was a trade secret of RSA Security until 1994, when it was leaked on the internet. **Components:** - State: Permutation $S$ of 256 bytes ($S[0], S[1], \ldots, S[255]$) - Index pointers: $i$ and $j$ (both 0–255) - Key $K$: 1 to 256 bytes **KSA (Key Scheduling Algorithm) — Initialize S:** 1. $S[i] = i$ for $i = 0, 1, \ldots, 255$ 2. $j = 0$ 3. For $i = 0$ to $255$: - $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \mod L]) \mod 256$ - Swap $S[i] \leftrightarrow S[j]$ **PRGA (Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm) — Generate Keystream:** 1. Initialize $i = 0, j = 0$ 2. Loop: - $i = (i + 1) \mod 256$ - $j = (j + S[i]) \mod 256$ - Swap $S[i] \leftrightarrow S[j]$ - Output $K_{out} = S[(S[i] + S[j]) \mod 256]$ - $C_n = P_n \oplus K_{out}$ > [!WARNING] > **RC4 Status:** Prohibited in **TLS 1.3** (RFC 8446, 2018). Vulnerabilities include **bias in early keystream bytes**, **Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir attack**, and **Klein's Mantin's biases**. Used mainly in legacy systems. Modern replacements: **ChaCha20, Salsa20, Grain-128, Trivium**. ### 2.5 Modern Stream Ciphers for IoT | Cipher | Year | Key Size | Nonce Size | Status | IoT Use | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | ChaCha20 | 2008 | 256 bits | 64 bits | TLS 1.3 standard | High | | Salsa20 | 2005 | 256 bits | 64 bits | eSTREAM portfolio | Moderate | | Grain-128 | 2008 | 128 bits | 96 bits | ISO/IEC 29192-3 | High (RFID) | | Trivium | 2008 | 80 bits | 80 bits | eSTREAM portfolio | Moderate | | MICKEY | 2005 | 80 bits | Variable | eSTREAM portfolio | Moderate | | HC-128 | 2008 | 128 bits | 64 bits | eSTREAM portfolio | Moderate | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN):** Stream ciphers in **802.15.4 / ZigBee** for low-power mesh nodes. - **Bluetooth LE:** AES-CCM (block mode) but legacy E0 stream cipher in Bluetooth Classic. - **Cellular IoT (NB-IoT, LTE-M):** Stream ciphers for real-time signaling encryption (SNOW 3G, ZUC). - **RFIDs:** Ultra-lightweight stream ciphers (Grain-128) for passive tags. - **TLS 1.3:** ChaCha20-Poly1305 as mandatory AEAD suite. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why XOR is the Perfect Stream Cipher Operation **Claim:** XOR is the ideal combining function for symmetric encryption when the keystream is uniformly random. **Proof (one-time pad equivalence):** Given $C = P \oplus K$ where $K$ is uniformly random over $\{0,1\}^n$, we want to show that for any fixed ciphertext $c$ and any plaintext $p$, we have: $$P(P = p \mid C = c) = P(P = p)$$ That is, $C$ reveals zero information about $P$ (perfect secrecy, Shannon 1949). **Step 1:** For any plaintext bit $p$ and ciphertext bit $c$, the keystream bit is uniquely determined as: $$k = p \oplus c$$ **Step 2:** Since $K$ is uniformly random, $K_i$ is independent and identically distributed (i.i.d.) with: $$P(K_i = 0) = P(K_i = 1) = \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 3:** Therefore, for any $(p, c)$: $$ \begin{aligned} P(C = c \mid P = p) &= P(P \oplus K = c \mid P = p) \\ &= P(K = p \oplus c) \\ &= P(K = k) \\ &= \frac{1}{2^n} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4:** Hence: $$ \begin{aligned} P(P = p \mid C = c) &= \frac{P(C = c \mid P = p) P(P = p)}{P(C = c)} \\ &= \frac{(1/2^n) \cdot P(P = p)}{1/2^n} \\ &= P(P = p) \end{aligned}$$ **Conclusion:** Posterior = Prior, so $C$ and $P$ are statistically independent. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **One-Time Pad theorem**. For a stream cipher to achieve the same property, the keystream $K$ must be **truly random** AND **used exactly once**. Pseudorandom keystreams weaken this guarantee to **computational security**. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: LFSR Period for a 4-bit LFSR with Feedback Polynomial $x^4 + x + 1$ **Setup:** Consider a 4-stage LFSR with state vector $(s_3, s_2, s_1, s_0)$ and feedback polynomial $C(x) = x^4 + x + 1$, i.e., $c_1 = 1, c_0 = 1$, all other $c_i = 0$. Thus the recursion is: $$s_4 = s_1 \oplus s_0$$ **Step 1 — Verify primitivity:** The polynomial $x^4 + x + 1$ has no roots in $\mathbb{F}_2$ (evaluate at $x=0$: 1, at $x=1$: 1). It must also not factor as product of lower-degree irreducibles. The only irreducible polynomials of degree $< 4$ over $\mathbb{F}_2$ are $x, x+1, x^2+x+1, x^3+x+1, x^3+x^2+1$. None divides $x^4 + x + 1$. Hence $x^4 + x + 1$ is **irreducible**. **Step 2 — Check primitive:** $x^4 + x + 1$ must divide $x^M + 1$ for $M = 2^4 - 1 = 15$ and for no smaller $M \in \{1, 3, 5, 7\}$. Computing $x^{15} \pmod{x^4+x+1}$ yields 1, confirming $x^4 + x + 1$ is primitive. **Step 3 — Maximum period:** $$T_{max} = 2^4 - 1 = 15$$ **Step 4 — Trace the cycle** (initial state = 0001): | Clock | $s_3$ | $s_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_0$ | Output | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | | 2 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 3 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 4 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 5 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 6 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 7 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 8 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 9 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | | 10 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 | | 11 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | | 12 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 13 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | | 14 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | | 15 | — | — | — | — | (back to 0001) | **Output sequence:** 1, 1, 0, 1, 0, 0, 1, 0, 1, 1, 1, 0, 0, 0, 1 — period **15** ✓ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example: RC4 Encryption of "HELLO" **Setup:** Key = "KEY" (3 bytes: K=0x4B, E=0x45, Y=0x59), Plaintext = "HELLO" (5 bytes). **Step 1 — KSA (Key Scheduling):** Initialize: $S = [0, 1, 2, 3, ..., 255]$, $j = 0$. For $i = 0$ to $255$: $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \mod 3]) \mod 256$, swap $S[i] \leftrightarrow S[j]$. After KSA, $S$ is a shuffled permutation. **Step 2 — PRGA (Keystream generation for "HELLO"):** | Step | $i$ | $j$ | $S[(S[i]+S[j]) \mod 256]$ | Plaintext | Ciphertext | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1 | 0+... = varies | varies | H (0x48) | varies | | 2 | 2 | ... | ... | E (0x45) | ... | | 3 | 3 | ... | ... | L (0x4C) | ... | | 4 | 4 | ... | ... | L (0x4C) | ... | | 5 | 5 | ... | ... | O (0x4F) | ... | Each output byte is XORed with the corresponding plaintext byte to produce ciphertext. **Step 3 — Decryption:** Since XOR is self-inverse, the receiver regenerates the same keystream and XORs with ciphertext to recover plaintext. > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip:** KTU questions often ask for the first 1–2 KSA iterations. Memorize the KSA and PRGA flow and practice tracing on small examples. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: LFSR Stream Cipher Strengths vs Weaknesses | Property | Strength | Weakness | |---|---|---| | **Period** | $2^n - 1$ (very long for $n \geq 80$) | Determined by $n$ — not infinite | | **Linear complexity** | High if cascaded/non-linear | Single LFSR: very low ($L = n$), Berlekamp-Massey attack feasible | | **Statistical randomness** | m-sequence properties | Predictable once 2L output bits known | | **Hardware efficiency** | Few XOR gates + shift register | Vulnerable to correlation attacks | | **Mathematical simplicity** | Easy to implement | Easily broken with known attacks | | **IoT suitability** | Low power, low area | Must be combined with non-linear combining function | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** A **single LFSR is NEVER cryptographically secure**, even if it has a 100-bit period. Always use **non-linear combinations** (multiple LFSRs, irregular clocking, or non-linear filters). Examples: **Geffe Generator** (3 LFSRs + multiplexer), **Grain-128** (LFSR + NFSR), **Trivium** (3 NFSRs). --- ### 3.5 Comparative Complexity Analysis **Hardware Gate Count (ASIC synthesis estimates, 65nm CMOS):** | Cipher | Logic Gates | Power (µW @ 10MHz) | Throughput | |---|---|---|---| | RC4 (basic) | ~650 | 50 | 1 byte/cycle | | ChaCha20 (1 round) | ~1,200 | 90 | 64 bytes/cycle | | Grain-128 | ~800 | 30 | 1 bit/cycle | | AES-128 (1 block) | ~2,400 | 180 | 16 bytes/cycle | **Observation:** Stream ciphers (Grain-128) consume **~5–6× less power** than AES — the primary reason for their dominance in battery-powered IoT. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Stream Cipher Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Secret Key K
80 to 256 bits] --> D[Keystream Generator
KSG / PRG] E[Initialization Vector IV / Nonce] --> D D --> F[Keystream Z sub i] B[Plaintext P sub i] --> C[XOR Combiner] F --> C C --> G[Ciphertext C sub i] H[Receiver's KSG
with same K and IV] --> F2[Identical Keystream] G --> I[XOR Combiner] F2 --> I I --> J[Recovered Plaintext P sub i] ``` ### 4.2 LFSR Internal Structure (4-bit example, polynomial $x^4 + x + 1$) ```mermaid flowchart LR S3[Stage 3 s3] --> S2[Stage 2 s2] S2 --> S1[Stage 1 s1] S1 --> S0[Stage 0 s0] S0 --> OUT[Output Bit] S0 --> XOR1[XOR Gate] S1 --> XOR1 XOR1 --> INJ[Feedback Input] INJ --> S3 ``` ### 4.3 RC4 KSA + PRGA Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB INITK[Initialize S 0 to 255 = i] --> KSA1[Set j = 0] KSA1 --> KSA2{For i = 0 to 255} KSA2 --> KSA3[Update j = j + S i + K i mod L mod 256] KSA3 --> KSA4[Swap S i with S j] KSA4 --> KSA2 KSA2 --> PRGA1[Set i = 0, j = 0] PRGA1 --> PRGA2[Update i = i + 1 mod 256] PRGA2 --> PRGA3[Update j = j + S i mod 256] PRGA3 --> PRGA4[Swap S i with S j] PRGA4 --> PRGA5[Output K out = S S i + S j mod 256] PRGA5 --> PRGA6[C n = P n XOR K out] PRGA6 --> PRGA2 ``` ### 4.4 Stream Cipher vs Block Cipher — Functional Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SC[Stream Cipher Path] PS[Plaintext Stream] --> XS[XOR with Keystream] KS[Keystream from PRG] --> XS XS --> CS[Ciphertext Stream] end subgraph BC[Block Cipher Path] PB[Plaintext Block 128 bits] --> EB[Block Encryptor AES] KB[Round Keys] --> EB EB --> CB[Ciphertext Block] end ``` ### 4.5 Modern IoT Stream Cipher — Grain-128 Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[80-bit Key] --> LFSR1[128-bit LFSR] B[96-bit IV] --> LFSR1 LFSR1 --> L1[Linear Update] LFSR1 --> NFSR1[128-bit NFSR] NFSR1 --> N1[Non-linear Update] L1 --> H[Output Function h x] N1 --> H H --> KGEN[Keystream Bit z] NFSR1 --> N1 ``` ### 4.6 Synchronous vs Asynchronous Stream Cipher ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SYNC[Synchronous Stream Cipher] K1[Key K] --> KSG1[Keystream Generator] IV1[IV] --> KSG1 KSG1 --> Z1[Keystream z i] Z1 --> X1[XOR with P i] P1[P i] --> X1 X1 --> C1[C i] end subgraph ASYNC[Asynchronous Stream Cipher] K2[Key K] --> KSG2[Stateful KSG] CB[Previous Ciphertext Bits C i-1 ... C i-t] --> KSG2 KSG2 --> Z2[Keystream z i] Z2 --> X2[XOR with P i] P2[P i] --> X2 X2 --> C2[C i] end ``` ### 4.7 Hierarchical Topic Map — Stream Cipher Ecosystem ```mermaid mindmap root((Stream Cipher)) Basics XOR Combining Symmetric Key One Time Pad Origin Types Synchronous Self Synchronizing Building Blocks LFSR NFSR Non Linear Combining Famous Ciphers RC4 ChaCha20 Salsa20 Grain 128 Trivium IoT Applications ZigBee 802 15 4 Bluetooth LE LoRaWAN RFID Tags Attacks Berlekamp Massey Correlation Attack Fluhrer Mantin Shamir Algebraic Attack ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]** > **Define a Stream Cipher. How is it different from a Block Cipher?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A stream cipher is a symmetric key cipher that encrypts plaintext one bit or byte at a time by XORing with a pseudorandom keystream generated from a secret key. The encryption rule is $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$. Difference from block cipher: - **Processing unit:** Stream = bit/byte; Block = 64/128-bit block. - **Speed:** Stream is faster; no padding overhead. - **Error propagation:** Stream = 1 bit; Block = 1 full block. - **Memory:** Stream requires less memory; suitable for IoT. - **Examples:** Stream — RC4, ChaCha20; Block — AES, DES. **(Any 2 differences — 2 marks)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Expected]** > **What is an LFSR? Why is a primitive feedback polynomial important?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **Linear Feedback Shift Register (LFSR)** is a shift register whose input is a linear (XOR) function of its previous state. It generates a pseudo-random binary sequence of period up to $2^n - 1$ for an $n$-stage LFSR. **(1 mark)** The **feedback polynomial** $C(x) = 1 + c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + \cdots + c_n x^n$ defines the recurrence. **(1 mark)** A **primitive polynomial** is important because: - It is irreducible over $\mathbb{F}_2$ (cannot be factored). - It generates the **maximal period** $T = 2^n - 1$, producing an **m-sequence** with ideal statistical randomness properties (balance, run distribution, 2-level autocorrelation). **(1 mark)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Question A (Module 1 Internal Choice — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Q2A]** **Q.A.(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a stream cipher. Discuss the role of the keystream generator. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks):** A stream cipher consists of three main components: the **secret key K**, the **keystream generator (KSG)**, and the **combining function (XOR)**. The plaintext $P$ enters bit-by-bit (or byte-by-byte) and is XORed with the keystream output $Z_i$ to produce ciphertext $C_i = P_i \oplus Z_i$. The KSG takes the key $K$ and an optional **IV/nonce** as inputs. **Step 2 — Role of KSG (2 marks):** The keystream generator is the cryptographic heart of the stream cipher. Its design must ensure: - **Long period** (typically $2^{80}$ or more). - **High linear complexity** (resistance to Berlekamp-Massey attack). - **Statistical randomness** (balance, run distribution). - **Avalanche effect** (small change in $K$ changes $Z$ significantly). **Step 3 — Why synchronization matters (2 marks):** In **synchronous** stream ciphers, both sender and receiver must maintain **bit-level synchronization** because loss of even one bit causes all subsequent decryption to fail. The IV must be **unique per session** to prevent keystream reuse, which is the primary weakness of stream ciphers (e.g., the famous WEP attack on Wi-Fi). --- **Q.A.(b)** Explain the RC4 algorithm with its KSA and PRGA. Why was RC4 removed from TLS 1.3? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — RC4 Overview (1 mark):** RC4 is a variable-key-size stream cipher designed by Ron Rivest in 1987. It uses a 256-byte state array $S$, two 8-bit indices $i, j$, and a key $K$ of 1–256 bytes. It consists of two phases: **KSA** and **PRGA**. **Step 2 — KSA (2 marks):** - Initialize $S[i] = i$ for $i = 0, \ldots, 255$. - Set $j = 0$. - For $i = 0$ to $255$: - $j = (j + S[i] + K[i \mod L]) \mod 256$ - Swap $S[i]$ and $S[j]$. **Step 3 — PRGA (2 marks):** - Initialize $i = 0, j = 0$. - Loop: - $i = (i + 1) \mod 256$ - $j = (j + S[i]) \mod 256$ - Swap $S[i]$ and $S[j]$ - Output keystream byte $K_{out} = S[(S[i] + S[j]) \mod 256]$ - $C_n = P_n \oplus K_{out}$ **Step 4 — Why RC4 was removed from TLS 1.3 (2 marks):** RC4 was removed due to multiple cryptanalytic attacks: - **Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir attack (2001):** Exploits weak keys. - **Klein's attack (2005):** Exploits biases in second and third keystream bytes. - **Mantin's bias (2005):** Predictable patterns in first keystream bytes. - **RFC 7465 (2015)** prohibited RC4 in all TLS versions; **RFC 8446 (TLS 1.3, 2018)** removed it entirely. Modern alternatives: **ChaCha20-Poly1305** and **AES-GCM**. --- > **Question B (Module 1 Internal Choice — 14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Expected]** **Q.B.(a)** Explain the construction and operation of an LFSR. Derive the maximum period and prove it using the characteristic polynomial. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Construction (2 marks):** An LFSR consists of $n$ flip-flops (stages) $s_0, s_1, \ldots, s_{n-1}$ connected in series, with feedback paths determined by coefficients $c_0, c_1, \ldots, c_{n-1} \in \{0, 1\}$. On each clock pulse, the contents shift right, and the new leftmost bit is computed as: $$s_{new} = \bigoplus_{i=0}^{n-1} c_i \cdot s_i \pmod{2}$$ The rightmost bit $s_0$ is the output. **Step 2 — Characteristic Polynomial (2 marks):** The feedback is described by the characteristic polynomial: $$C(x) = c_0 + c_1 x + c_2 x^2 + \cdots + c_{n-1} x^{n-1} \pmod{2}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum Period Derivation (3 marks):** The LFSR state space has $2^n$ possible states. The all-zero state is **terminal** (once reached, it stays at all-zeros because XOR of all zeros is zero), so the maximum period for a non-trivial cycle is $2^n - 1$. This maximum is achieved if and only if $C(x)$ is a **primitive polynomial** over $\mathbb{F}_2$, i.e., irreducible with the property that $x^{2^n-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{C(x)}$ and no smaller $M$ satisfies $x^M \equiv 1 \pmod{C(x)}$. **Theorem:** The output of an $n$-stage LFSR has maximum period $2^n - 1$ iff $C(x)$ is primitive. **Proof sketch:** Primitive polynomials are the generators of the cyclic group $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}^*$, which has order $2^n - 1$. Since the LFSR state update corresponds to multiplication by $x$ in $\mathbb{F}_{2^n}$, repeated application cycles through all $2^n - 1$ non-zero elements, giving period $2^n - 1$. $\blacksquare$ **Example:** For $n = 4$, $C(x) = x^4 + x + 1$ is primitive, period = 15. --- **Q.B.(b)** Compare synchronous and asynchronous stream ciphers. Explain the Geffe generator and its weaknesses. Why is a single LFSR not secure for cryptography? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Comparison (2 marks):** | Feature | Synchronous | Asynchronous | |---|---|---| | Keystream depends on | Key + IV only | Key + previous ciphertext | | Synchronization | Strict (bit-level) | Self-recovering | | Error recovery | Loses all bits after an error | Recovers after $t$ bits | | Vulnerability | Keystream reuse attacks | Chosen-ciphertext attacks | **Step 2 — Geffe Generator (2 marks):** The **Geffe generator** (1973) uses three LFSRs (LFSR-1, LFSR-2, LFSR-3) and a 2-to-1 multiplexer. The multiplexer selects one of three LFSR outputs based on the output of LFSR-2. Mathematically, the output is: $$z_i = (s_1 \land \bar{s_2}) \oplus (s_2 \land s_3)$$ where $s_1, s_2, s_3$ are bits from the three LFSRs. **Step 3 — Weakness of Geffe Generator (2 marks):** The Geffe generator suffers from **correlation attack** (Siegenthaler, 1985). The output $z$ is correlated with $s_1$ and $s_3$ individually with probability 75%, not the ideal 50%. This allows an attacker to recover each LFSR's initial state independently. Specifically: $$P(z = s_1) = 0.75, \quad P(z = s_3) = 0.75$$ By combining the three correlation attacks, the secret key can be recovered with only $2^{40}$ trials for a generator with $2^{40}$ effective key size. **Step 4 — Why a Single LFSR is Not Secure (1 mark):** A single LFSR's output has **linear complexity equal to its stage count** $n$. The **Berlekamp-Massey algorithm** can recover the LFSR's feedback polynomial and initial state from any $2n$ consecutive output bits. This makes the entire keystream predictable from a small sample. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing LFSR period with security:** Maximum period ($2^n - 1$) does NOT equal security. Linear complexity matters more. > 2. **Forgetting IV uniqueness:** Always state that the IV/nonce MUST be unique per session — this is the #1 exam-mark distinguisher. > 3. **Calling RC4 "secure":** RC4 is cryptographically broken. Do not recommend it for new designs. > 4. **Confusing self-synchronizing with synchronous:** Self-synchronizing (asynchronous) ciphers use ciphertext feedback — students often mix these up. > 5. **Skipping one blank line around `$$` blocks:** Causes KTU answer-script rendering errors. > 6. **Writing "m-sequence" without defining it:** Examiners want the formula $2^n - 1$ and the term **primitive polynomial**. > 7. **Forgetting to mention ChaCha20 in modern stream ciphers:** RC4 is legacy; mention at least one modern alternative. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Stream Cipher (Module 1)** - **Definition:** Encrypts one bit/byte at a time using XOR with a pseudorandom keystream. **(Recall: $C_i = P_i \oplus K_i$)** - **One-Time Pad Equivalence:** XOR-based stream cipher achieves perfect secrecy iff keystream is truly random and used once. - **Two main types:** Synchronous (keystream from key+IV) and Self-synchronizing (keystream from key + previous ciphertext). - **LFSR Building Block:** Linear recurrence $s_{new} = \bigoplus c_i s_i$; max period $2^n - 1$ if feedback polynomial is **primitive**. - **m-Sequence Properties:** Balance, ideal autocorrelation, run distribution, shift-and-add property. - **RC4 Components:** 256-byte state $S$, 8-bit indices $i, j$, KSA (key mixing), PRGA (keystream output). **Removed from TLS 1.3 (RFC 8446).** - **Modern IoT Ciphers:** ChaCha20 (TLS 1.3), Grain-128 (RFID, ISO 29192-3), Trivium (eSTREAM), Salsa20. - **Attacks to Know:** - **Berlekamp-Massey:** Synthesizes LFSR from $2n$ bits. - **Correlation Attack (Siegenthaler):** Exploits non-zero correlation in combining function. - **Fluhrer-Mantin-Shamir (FMS):** Weak-key attack on RC4. - **Algebraic Attack:** Solves nonlinear equations on output. - **Keystream Reuse:** Same keystream XORed with two plaintexts recovers $P_1 \oplus P_2$. - **IoT Use Cases:** ZigBee, Bluetooth LE, LoRaWAN, RFID, NB-IoT, TLS 1.3, 5G (SNOW 3G, ZUC). - **Stream vs Block:** Stream = bit/byte, low power, 1-bit error; Block = 128 bits, high security margin, full-block error. - **Why XOR?** Self-inverse, involutory, cheap in hardware, mathematically elegant. - **Key Sizes to Remember:** IoT = 80–128 bits; Modern = 128–256 bits; Avoid RC4 entirely. - **KTU Mantra:** *"Stream cipher = XOR + Pseudorandom Keystream + Synchronization. Never reuse a keystream. Always combine multiple LFSRs non-linearly."*

topics.title: Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles of Public Key Cryptosystems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC)**, also called an **Asymmetric Cryptosystem**, is a cryptographic framework in which a pair of mathematically related but distinct keys — a **Public Key (KU)** that is openly distributed and a **Private Key (KR)** that is kept secret by the owner — are used to perform encryption, decryption, digital signing, and key exchange operations. The defining mathematical property is that it must be **computationally infeasible** to derive the private key from the corresponding public key.

Formally, a Public Key Cryptosystem is a 5-tuple:

$$\mathcal{PKC} = (\mathcal{M}, \mathcal{C}, \mathcal{K}, \mathcal{E}, \mathcal{D})$$

Where:
- $\mathcal{M}$ = Message (Plaintext) space
- $\mathcal{C}$ = Ciphertext space
- $\mathcal{K}$ = Key space (containing ordered key pairs $(KU_i, KR_i)$)
- $\mathcal{E}$ = Encryption algorithm: $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$
- $\mathcal{D}$ = Decryption algorithm: $M = D_{KR_b}(C)$

The subscript **b** denotes the **receiver's** keys in an encryption context, because in PKC the receiver's public key encrypts and the receiver's private key decrypts.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Every public key system is built upon **one-way functions with a trapdoor**. The trapdoor is the secret information (the private key) that makes the inverse computation easy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Mailbox Model

Imagine a city full of **open mailboxes** (public keys) on the street, each with a clearly visible label showing the owner's name. Anyone in the city can:

1. **Drop a letter (plaintext)** into any mailbox, but only the **mailbox owner** has the private physical key to retrieve the letters.
2. **Verify a signed letter (digital signature)** by checking a publicly verifiable seal that only the owner can produce.

Two critical security properties emerge:
- **Confidentiality:** Only the receiver can read what was sent (drop letter scenario).
- **Authentication/Non-repudiation:** Anyone can verify the sender, but only the sender can create the seal (signing scenario).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why PKC matters for IoT:** In IoT, devices are resource-constrained. Public key systems like **ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography)** allow strong security with smaller key sizes — making them practical for sensors and embedded gateways.

## 1.3 Symmetric vs. Asymmetric — A First Comparison

| Property | Symmetric Key | Public Key |
|---|---|---|
| Keys Used | Single shared secret K | Pair (KU, KR) |
| Key Distribution | Major problem | Solved (public key is open) |
| Speed | Very Fast | Slower (100–1000x) |
| Key Count | n(n-1)/2 keys for n users | Only 2n keys for n users |
| Confidentiality | Yes | Yes |
| Authentication | Difficult | Yes (via signatures) |
| Non-Repudiation | No | Yes |
| Typical Algorithms | AES, 3DES, ChaCha20 | RSA, ECC, ElGamal, DH |

## 1.4 Six Essential Components of a Public Key Cryptosystem

A complete PKC framework consists of:

1. **Plaintext (M):** The readable input message.
2. **Encryption Algorithm (E):** Performs mathematical transformations on M.
3. **Public Key (KU):** Known publicly, used by sender.
4. **Private Key (KR):** Known only to receiver, used for decryption.
5. **Ciphertext (C):** The scrambled output, transmitted over insecure channel.
6. **Decryption Algorithm (D):** Inverts encryption using the private key.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the components of a public key cryptosystem", always write the above six. Examiners award **½ mark per correct point** up to 3 marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Mathematical Foundation — One-Way Functions

The entire security of public key cryptography rests on the concept of a **One-Way Function (OWF)**.

### 2.1.1 Definition of One-Way Function

A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is called a **one-way function** if:

1. For every $x \in X$, computing $y = f(x)$ is **easy** (polynomial time).
2. For almost every $y \in \text{Range}(f)$, finding any $x$ such that $f(x) = y$ is **hard** (infeasible in polynomial time).

### 2.1.2 Definition of Trapdoor One-Way Function

A function $f: X \rightarrow Y$ is a **trapdoor one-way function** if:

1. For every $x \in X$, computing $y = f(x)$ is **easy**.
2. For almost every $y \in \text{Range}(f)$, computing $x = f^{-1}(y)$ is **hard** *without* knowledge of the trapdoor $t$.
3. With knowledge of the trapdoor $t$, computing $x = f^{-1}(y)$ becomes **easy**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **private key IS the trapdoor**. This is the most important conceptual point in the entire module.

### 2.1.3 Intuition Behind One-Way Functions

Think of a function like a **meat grinder**:
- **Forward direction** (pushing meat in): Easy. — $f(x)$ is easy.
- **Reverse direction** (reconstructing the meat): Extremely hard without the original mould/template. — $f^{-1}(y)$ is hard.

The **private key is the mould** that makes the inverse operation easy.

## 2.2 The Three Formal Requirements of a Public Key Cryptosystem

For a cryptosystem to be considered secure, **Whitfield Diffie and Martin Hellman** (1976) defined three essential requirements:

### Requirement 1 — Computational Feasibility
Generating a key pair $(KU_b, KR_b)$ must be computationally easy.

$$\text{Generate}(1^k) \rightarrow (KU_b, KR_b) \quad \text{in polynomial time}$$

### Requirement 2 — Easy Encryption and Decryption
Both encryption and decryption operations must be efficient.

$$C = E_{KU_b}(M) \quad \text{easy} \quad \forall M \in \mathcal{M}$$
$$M = D_{KR_b}(C) \quad \text{easy} \quad \forall C \in \mathcal{C}$$

### Requirement 3 — Infeasible Inversion Without Private Key
An adversary knowing $KU_b$ and $C$ must find it computationally infeasible to recover $M$.

$$\Pr\big[D_{KU_b}(C) = M\big] < \varepsilon \quad \text{(negligible probability)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additional Implicit Requirements (often asked in 14-mark questions):**
> 4. **Decryption Consistency:** $D_{KR_b}(E_{KU_b}(M)) = M$ for all $M$.
> 5. **Public Key Authentication:** The public key must be bound to a user's identity (via PKI/certificates).

## 2.3 Applications of Public Key Cryptography

Public key systems enable **four major cryptographic services**:

| Application | Key Used to Encrypt | Key Used to Decrypt | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confidentiality** | Receiver's Public Key | Receiver's Private Key | Only receiver reads |
| **Authentication** | Sender's Private Key | Sender's Public Key | Verifies sender identity |
| **Integrity + Non-Repudiation** | Sender signs (hash encrypted by Private Key) | Anyone verifies with Public Key | Message not altered, sender cannot deny |
| **Key Exchange** | (Diffie-Hellman or RSA-wrapped) | (Asymmetric) | Securely share symmetric keys |

## 2.4 General Workflow — Encryption for Confidentiality

**Setup Phase:**
- Receiver generates $(KU_b, KR_b)$.
- Receiver publishes $KU_b$ in a directory; keeps $KR_b$ secret.

**Communication Phase:**
1. Sender obtains $KU_b$ from public directory.
2. Sender computes ciphertext: $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$.
3. Sender transmits $C$ over insecure channel.
4. Receiver applies private key: $M = D_{KR_b}(C)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Context:** In an IoT scenario, the **gateway** acts as a Certificate Authority and publishes its public key. **Sensor nodes** encrypt telemetry with the gateway's public key before transmission over LoRa/WiFi/Bluetooth.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Encryption (Confidentiality) | $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$ | Sender encrypts with receiver's public key |
| Decryption (Confidentiality) | $M = D_{KR_b}(C)$ | Receiver decrypts with own private key |
| Signing | $S = E_{KR_a}(H(M))$ | Sender signs hash with own private key |
| Verification | $H(M) = D_{KU_a}(S)$ | Verifier checks signature with sender's public key |
| One-Way Property | $f(x) \text{ easy}, \; f^{-1}(y) \text{ hard}$ | Defines OWF |
| Trapdoor | $f^{-1}(y) \text{ easy} \iff \text{trapdoor } t \text{ known}$ | Trapdoor = Private Key |
| Key Count (Symmetric) | $\dfrac{n(n-1)}{2}$ | Keys required for n users |
| Key Count (Asymmetric) | $2n$ | Keys required for n users |
| Consistency Equation | $D_{KR_b}\big(E_{KU_b}(M)\big) = M$ | Must hold for all M |
| RSA Modulus (preview) | $n = p \cdot q$ | Product of two large primes |
| RSA φ (preview) | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Euler's totient for n = pq |
| RSA Encryption (preview) | $C = M^e \bmod n$ | Public exponent e |
| RSA Decryption (preview) | $M = C^d \bmod n$ | Private exponent d |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse the keys in a question:** When "Sender encrypts" → it is the **RECEIVER's public key**. When "Receiver verifies a signature" → it is the **SENDER's public key**.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern IoT deployments, PKC enables:
- **TLS 1.3 handshakes** in MQTT brokers used by AWS IoT Core and Azure IoT Hub.
- **Secure boot** in microcontroller firmware (signature verification using ECC public keys burned into ROM).
- **Over-the-air (OTA) firmware updates** verified by manufacturer-signed images.
- **Device identity** through X.509 certificates tied to ECC public keys.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Analysis: The Decryption Consistency Derivation

We need to formally verify that for any PKC with keys $(KU_b, KR_b)$ and plaintext $M$, the round-trip operation returns the original message.

### Step 1 — Define the encryption function

The encryption algorithm $E$ is a deterministic (or randomized) mapping that uses the public key $KU_b$ to transform plaintext $M$ into ciphertext $C$.

$$C = E_{KU_b}(M) \quad \text{--- (Definition of Encryption)}$$

This equation states that the ciphertext $C$ is the output of the encryption algorithm $E$ when applied to plaintext $M$ using the public key $KU_b$.

### Step 2 — Apply the decryption function to the ciphertext

We now pass the ciphertext $C$ through the decryption algorithm $D$ using the private key $KR_b$.

$$D_{KR_b}(C) = D_{KR_b}\big(E_{KU_b}(M)\big) \quad \text{--- (Substitution of Step 1)}$$

This equation replaces the variable $C$ in the decryption function with the expression $E_{KU_b}(M)$ obtained in Step 1.

### Step 3 — Apply the definition of inverse functions

By the **fundamental design of public key cryptography**, the pair of algorithms $(E, D)$ is constructed such that $D$ is the **mathematical inverse** of $E$ when used with the matching key pair. This is the **trapdoor inverse** property.

$$D_{KR_b}\big(E_{KU_b}(M)\big) = M \quad \text{--- (Inverse Function Property)}$$

This step uses the inverse property — that decryption inverts encryption — which holds **if and only if** the correct private key $KR_b$ is used.

### Step 4 — Formally state the consistency identity

Combining all the above, we obtain the **Decryption Consistency Identity** that must hold for all valid $M$ in the message space:

$$\forall M \in \mathcal{M}: \quad D_{KR_b}\big(E_{KU_b}(M)\big) = M$$

This is the formal mathematical statement that any sound public key cryptosystem must satisfy.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Key Count Comparison

### 3.2.1 Symmetric Key Count

In a symmetric system, every pair of communicating users must share a **unique secret key**. The number of such unordered pairs among $n$ users is given by the combination formula:

$$K_{\text{sym}} = \binom{n}{2} = \frac{n!}{2!(n-2)!} = \frac{n(n-1)}{2}$$

For $n = 10$ users:
$$K_{\text{sym}} = \frac{10 \cdot 9}{2} = 45 \text{ keys}$$

### 3.2.2 Asymmetric Key Count

In an asymmetric system, each user possesses a **key pair** — one public and one private. So the total number of keys is:

$$K_{\text{asym}} = 2n$$

For $n = 10$ users:
$$K_{\text{asym}} = 2 \cdot 10 = 20 \text{ keys}$$

### 3.2.3 Scalability Ratio

The ratio of symmetric to asymmetric key counts grows linearly with $n$:

$$\text{Ratio}(n) = \frac{K_{\text{sym}}}{K_{\text{asym}}} = \frac{n(n-1)}{4n} = \frac{n-1}{4}$$

For $n = 100$: Ratio = $\frac{99}{4} = 24.75$ — showing how PKC drastically reduces key management overhead.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Conceptual Application of OWF (Number Factorisation)

Consider the classic example of multiplication vs. factorisation.

### Step 1 — Define the forward function

Let $p = 61$ and $q = 53$ be two large primes. Define:

$$f(p, q) = p \cdot q = 61 \times 53$$

### Step 2 — Compute the forward value (easy)

$$f(p, q) = 61 \times 53 = 3233$$

Computing the product is trivial — even for very large primes with hundreds of digits.

### Step 3 — Attempt the inverse (hard without trapdoor)

Now an adversary who knows only $n = 3233$ must find $p$ and $q$. The naive approach requires trial division:

$$\text{Check } d = 2, 3, 5, 7, 11, \ldots \text{ up to } \sqrt{3233} \approx 56.86$$

Trial dividing: $3233 / 53 = 61$ ✓

For larger numbers (e.g., 2048-bit RSA modulus), this becomes **computationally infeasible** — no known classical algorithm can factor it in reasonable time.

### Step 4 — Inversion with trapdoor (easy)

If the adversary **knows** $p = 61$ (the trapdoor), they can compute:
$$q = n / p = 3233 / 61 = 53$$

Thus, the **trapdoor** transforms an infeasible problem into a trivial one.

> [!NOTE]
> **IoT Relevance:** RSA with 2048-bit keys is considered secure. ECC with only **256-bit keys** provides equivalent security — this is why IoT prefers ECC.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trapdoor Functions vs. Security Levels

| Algorithm | Underlying Hard Problem | Trapdoor (Private Key) | Key Size for 128-bit Security |
|---|---|---|---|
| RSA | Integer Factorisation | Knowledge of $p, q$ | 3072 bits |
| Diffie-Hellman | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | Knowledge of $x$ in $g^x$ | 3072 bits |
| DSA | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | Knowledge of $x$ in $g^x$ | 3072 bits |
| ECC (ECDSA, ECDH) | Elliptic Curve DLP (ECDLP) | Knowledge of scalar $k$ | 256 bits |
| ElGamal | Discrete Logarithm (DLP) | Knowledge of $x$ in $g^x$ | 3072 bits |
