# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — PKC Confidentiality Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext M] --> B[Encryption Algorithm E]
    KU[Receiver Public Key KU_b] --> B
    B --> C[Ciphertext C]
    C --> D[Insecure Channel]
    D --> E[Decryption Algorithm D]
    KR[Receiver Private Key KR_b] --> E
    E --> F[Plaintext M recovered]
    AD[Adversary Eavesdropping] -.->|Reads C only| C
    AD -.->|Cannot compute D without KR_b| F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Public Key Authentication (Digital Signature)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Original Message M] --> H[Hash Function SHA-256]
    H --> HM[Message Digest H(M)]
    HM --> SG[Signing Algorithm with Sender Private Key KR_a]
    SG --> S[Digital Signature S]
    M1 --> C1[Concatenate M and S]
    C1 --> TX[Transmit over Insecure Channel]
    TX --> RC[Receiver Side]
    RC --> S1[Extract Signature S]
    RC --> M2[Extract Message M]
    M2 --> HV[Apply Hash Function again]
    HV --> HM2[New Hash H(M) prime]
    S1 --> VF[Verification Algorithm with Sender Public Key KU_a]
    VF --> HM3[Recovered Hash H(M)]
    HM2 --> CMP[Compare Hashes]
    HM3 --> CMP
    CMP --> RS[Result: Valid or Invalid]
```

## 4.3 Architecture Flow — Hybrid Cryptosystem for IoT

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IoT[IoT Sensor Device] -->|Step 1: Generate random symmetric session key Ks| SK[Session Key Ks]
    SK -->|Step 2: Encrypt Ks using Gateway Public Key KU_gw| EK[Encrypted Session Key E_KU_gw of Ks]
    DATA[Telemetry Data D] -->|Step 3: Encrypt D using AES with Ks| ED[Encrypted Telemetry E_AES of D]
    EK --> TX[Transmit Both over Wireless Channel]
    ED --> TX
    TX --> GW[IoT Gateway]
    GW -->|Step 4: Decrypt session key using KR_gw| DSK[Recovered Ks]
    DSK -->|Step 5: Decrypt telemetry using Ks| DD[Plain Telemetry D]
```

## 4.4 Decision Flow — When to Use Symmetric vs. Asymmetric

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[New Cryptographic Task] --> Q1{Need Key Exchange?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Network Size?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need Digital Signatures?}
    Q2 -->|Small n less than 10| SK[Use Symmetric AES with Pre-shared Key]
    Q2 -->|Large n greater than 10| PKC[Use Public Key DH or RSA Key Exchange]
    Q3 -->|Yes| SIG[Use Asymmetric RSA or ECC Digital Signature]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Bulk Data Encryption?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| HS[Use Hybrid: Asymmetric for Key + Symmetric for Data]
    Q4 -->|No| Q5{Resource Constrained IoT Device?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| ECC[Use ECC with 256 bit Keys]
    Q5 -->|No| RSA[Use RSA with 2048 bit Keys]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Public Key Cryptosystem. List its essential components. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC) is a cryptographic system that uses a pair of mathematically related keys — a public key distributed openly and a private key kept secret — to perform encryption, decryption, and digital signature operations such that derivation of the private key from the public key is computationally infeasible.

The six essential components are:
1. **Plaintext (M)** — the original readable message.
2. **Encryption Algorithm (E)** — transforms plaintext into ciphertext.
3. **Public Key (KU)** — known to everyone, used for encryption.
4. **Private Key (KR)** — known only to owner, used for decryption.
5. **Ciphertext (C)** — scrambled output transmitted over insecure channel.
6. **Decryption Algorithm (D)** — inverts encryption using the private key.

> [!NOTE]
> Full marks are awarded only when **all six components** are listed and the asymmetry is explained.

---

### Question 2: What is a one-way function? Explain the concept of a trapdoor one-way function. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A **One-Way Function (OWF)** is a mathematical function $f$ that is easy to compute in the forward direction $y = f(x)$ but computationally infeasible to invert (i.e., to find $x$ given $y$) without additional information.

A **Trapdoor One-Way Function** is a special one-way function that includes a secret piece of information called the **trapdoor** $t$. Without the trapdoor, inverting $f$ is hard. With the trapdoor, inverting $f$ becomes easy.

In the context of public key cryptography, the **private key acts as the trapdoor**. This is the foundation that makes public key systems possible — encryption is forward computation (easy), and decryption is inversion with the trapdoor (easy with private key, hard without).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

#### (a) Explain the principles of Public Key Cryptosystems with a neat block diagram. Discuss the essential requirements of a public key cryptosystem. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark):**
A Public Key Cryptosystem (PKC), introduced by Diffie and Hellman in 1976, is an asymmetric cryptographic framework where each user has a pair of keys: a **Public Key (KU)** published openly and a **Private Key (KR)** kept secret. The system relies on **trapdoor one-way functions** for its security.

**Step 2 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
The standard PKC encryption block diagram is:

```
    Plaintext M
        |
        v
   [Encryption Algorithm E]  <----- Public Key KU_b
        |
        v
   Ciphertext C ----- (Insecure Channel) -----> [Decryption Algorithm D]
                                                     |
                                                     v
                                              Plaintext M
                                          (uses Private Key KR_b)
```

**Step 3 — Six Components (2 marks):**
The six components are: (1) Plaintext, (2) Encryption Algorithm, (3) Public Key, (4) Private Key, (5) Ciphertext, (6) Decryption Algorithm.

**Step 4 — Three Essential Requirements (2 marks):**

1. **Computationally easy to generate** a key pair $(KU_b, KR_b)$.
2. **Easy encryption and decryption** for the legitimate parties.
3. **Computationally infeasible** for an adversary to recover $M$ from $C$ and $KU_b$.

**Step 5 — Additional Notes (Optional for full marks):**
A fourth implicit requirement is that $D_{KR_b}(E_{KU_b}(M)) = M$ for all messages, plus the need for public key authentication via PKI/certificates.

---

#### (b) With a neat diagram, explain the authentication mechanism using public key cryptography. How is non-repudiation achieved? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Authentication Concept (1 mark):**
Authentication verifies the identity of the sender. In PKC, the sender uses their **Private Key** to sign a message digest (hash), and the receiver verifies using the sender's **Public Key**.

**Step 2 — Signing Process (2 marks):**
1. Sender computes hash: $h = H(M)$ using SHA-256.
2. Sender encrypts hash with own private key: $S = E_{KR_a}(h)$.
3. Sender transmits $(M, S)$ over the channel.

**Step 3 — Verification Process (2 marks):**
1. Receiver extracts $M$ and $S$.
2. Receiver computes $h' = H(M)$ from received $M$.
3. Receiver decrypts $S$ using sender's public key: $h = D_{KU_a}(S)$.
4. If $h = h'$, the signature is **valid**; otherwise, it is **rejected**.

**Step 4 — Non-Repudiation Explanation (2 marks):**
Non-repudiation means the sender **cannot later deny** having sent the message. This is achieved because:
- Only the sender knows $KR_a$, so only they could have produced $S$.
- A valid signature serves as **mathematical proof** of origin.
- This is enforceable in court, unlike symmetric key MACs where both parties share the secret key.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write the **reverse** of authentication — i.e., they say "Sender encrypts with public key". This is WRONG for signing. Always remember: **Sign with PRIVATE key, Verify with PUBLIC key**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Differentiate between symmetric and asymmetric key cryptography. Explain the applications of public key cryptography. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition Contrast (1 mark):**
- **Symmetric Key:** Uses a **single shared secret key** for both encryption and decryption.
- **Asymmetric Key:** Uses a **pair of keys** — a public key and a private key.

**Step 2 — Detailed Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Symmetric Key | Asymmetric Key |
|---|---|---|
| Number of Keys | Single K | Key pair (KU, KR) |
| Key Distribution | Difficult | Easy (public key open) |
| Speed | Fast (10x to 1000x) | Slower |
| Key Count for n users | n(n-1)/2 | 2n |
| Algorithms | AES, DES, 3DES | RSA, ECC, ElGamal |
| Authentication | Not inherent | Inherent via signatures |
| Non-repudiation | Not provided | Provided |

**Step 3 — Applications of PKC (3 marks):**

1. **Confidentiality / Encryption:** $C = E_{KU_b}(M)$, $M = D_{KR_b}(C)$.
2. **Digital Signature / Authentication:** Sender signs using $KR_a$, receiver verifies with $KU_a$.
3. **Key Exchange:** Securely exchange symmetric session keys (e.g., Diffie-Hellman, RSA key transport).
4. **Certificates & PKI:** X.509 certificates bind public keys to identities.
5. **IoT-Specific:** Secure boot, OTA firmware verification, MQTT-TLS handshakes.

---

#### (b) Define one-way function and trapdoor one-way function. Explain how RSA (preview) uses these concepts. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — One-Way Function Definition (1.5 marks):**
A one-way function $f$ is a function that is easy to compute forward ($y = f(x)$) but computationally infeasible to invert (find $x$ from $y$) without additional information. Example: $f(p, q) = p \times q$ for large primes $p$ and $q$.

**Step 2 — Trapdoor One-Way Function Definition (1.5 marks):**
A trapdoor one-way function is a one-way function for which inversion becomes easy when a secret **trapdoor** $t$ is known. In RSA, the trapdoor is the knowledge of the prime factors $p$ and $q$.

**Step 3 — RSA Pre-View (2 marks):**
- **Key Generation:** Choose primes $p, q$. Compute $n = p \times q$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$. Pick $e$ with $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$. Compute $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$. Public key: $(e, n)$. Private key: $d$.
- **Encryption:** $C = M^e \bmod n$.
- **Decryption:** $M = C^d \bmod n$.
- **One-way function:** $f(M) = M^e \bmod n$ — easy to compute.
- **Inverse:** Finding $M$ from $C$ requires the trapdoor $d$ (derived from $p, q$).

**Step 4 — Why RSA is Secure (2 marks):**
Security rests on the **Integer Factorisation Problem (IFP)**. Given $n$ (a 2048-bit product of two primes), finding $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible using classical computers. The trapdoor is the pair $(p, q)$, which allows efficient decryption. Thus, RSA perfectly embodies the trapdoor one-way function concept.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In Part (b), students often confuse which is the **public exponent** and which is the **private exponent**. Always state clearly: $e$ is public, $d$ is private. Also, $d$ is **not** the modular inverse of $e$ itself — it is the modular inverse of $e$ **modulo $\phi(n)$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **PKC = Asymmetric Cryptography** — uses a public key and a mathematically related private key.
- **Six Components:** Plaintext, Encryption Algorithm, Public Key, Private Key, Ciphertext, Decryption Algorithm.
- **Three Requirements (Diffie-Hellman):**
  1. Easy key pair generation.
  2. Easy encryption and decryption for legitimate users.
  3. Infeasible inversion for adversaries.
- **One-Way Function:** Easy forward, hard reverse ($f: X \rightarrow Y$).
- **Trapdoor One-Way Function:** Adds a secret $t$ that makes inversion easy.
- **Private Key = Trapdoor** — most critical conceptual point.
- **Confidentiality rule:** Encrypt with **receiver's public**, decrypt with **receiver's private**.
- **Signing rule:** Sign with **sender's private**, verify with **sender's public**.
- **Key count:** Symmetric = $n(n-1)/2$; Asymmetric = $2n$.
- **Applications:** Encryption, Digital Signatures, Key Exchange, Certificates, Secure Boot, OTA updates.
- **IoT preference:** ECC (256 bits) > RSA (2048+ bits) for equivalent security.
- **Underlying hard problems:** Integer Factorisation (RSA), Discrete Log (DH/DSA), Elliptic Curve DLP (ECC).
- **Consistency identity:** $D_{KR_b}(E_{KU_b}(M)) = M$ for all $M \in \mathcal{M}$.
- **Common pitfall:** Confusing which key encrypts vs. decrypts in confidentiality vs. signing scenarios.
- **Hybrid approach:** PKC is used to securely exchange a symmetric session key, then symmetric encryption (AES) handles bulk data — this is the de-facto pattern in IoT/TLS.
- **Diffie-Hellman (1976)** introduced the concept of public key cryptography; **RSA (1977)** by Rivest, Shamir, Adleman was the first practical PKC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RSA Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman) Algorithm** is a public-key (asymmetric) cryptosystem invented in 1977 by Ron Rivest, Adi Shamir, and Leonard Adleman. It is formally defined as a trapdoor one-way permutation based on the computational hardness of the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)** in modular arithmetic, operating over the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_n^*$ where $n = p \cdot q$ is the product of two distinct large prime numbers.

Under the KTU 2024 scheme syllabus for *Cryptography for IoT Applications*, RSA is classified as the foundational **public-key encryption primitive** used for:
- Confidentiality (encryption)
- Authentication & Non-repudiation (digital signatures)
- Secure key exchange (hybrid schemes like RSA-wrapped AES for IoT)

### The RSA Triplet

Every RSA instance is fully characterized by a **key triplet** $(n, e, d)$ governed by the central congruence:

$$M^{e \cdot d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

where:
- $M$ is the plaintext message (integer)
- $n$ is the **public modulus** (composite)
- $e$ is the **public exponent** (encryption key)
- $d$ is the **private exponent** (decryption key, kept secret)

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Locked Mailbox with Two Keys"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Imagine a special mailbox on a busy street:**
>
> 1. Anyone in the world has a **mail slot** (the public key $e$) through which they can drop a letter. The slot is wide open and free to use.
> 2. Once a letter is dropped, it falls into the mailbox and **locks itself automatically**. No one — not even the person who dropped it — can retrieve the letter from the slot.
> 3. Only the **owner of the mailbox** possesses a private key $d$ that can open the back of the box and read the letters.
>
> The mathematical "mailbox" is the modulus $n = p \cdot q$. The product of two large primes is **easy to compute** (drop letter), but **extremely hard to reverse** (no one can open the slot from outside). The owner's private knowledge of the prime factors $p$ and $q$ acts as the **trapdoor** that makes reversal possible.

## 1.3 Why RSA is Critical for IoT Security

| IoT Domain | RSA Use-Case |
|---|---|
| **Smart Home Gateways** | TLS handshake: RSA encrypts the pre-master AES key |
| **Healthcare Wearables** | Digitally signing patient telemetry (authenticity) |
| **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** | Firmware update authentication via RSA signatures |
| **Smart Vehicles (V2X)** | Certificate verification in vehicular PKI |
| **RFID / NFC** | Lightweight RSA for tag-reader mutual authentication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Sizes (Industry Standard)**
> - **RSA-1024**: Deprecated (broken by NSA-capable adversaries)
> - **RSA-2048**: Current minimum for general-purpose use (NIST recommendation)
> - **RSA-3072 / 4096**: Used for long-lived secrets (root CAs, code signing)

The **NIST SP 800-57** standard mandates a security equivalence: RSA-2048 ≈ 112-bit symmetric security (≈ AES-112 equivalent).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Euler's Totient Function

RSA is built entirely upon **Euler's Totient Function** $\phi(n)$, which counts the number of integers in $\{1, 2, \ldots, n-1\}$ that are **coprime** (relatively prime) to $n$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euler's Theorem (The Heart of RSA):**
> If $\gcd(a, n) = 1$, then $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$

For a product of two distinct primes $n = p \cdot q$:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

This is the single most important formula in the entire RSA system.

## 2.2 The Four-Stage RSA Workflow

### **Stage 1 — Key Generation (Performed by Receiver / Owner)**

**Step 1.** Choose two large, distinct, random primes $p$ and $q$.
*Practical size*: Each prime is **1024 bits** for RSA-2048, generated via a CSPRNG (Cryptographically Secure Pseudo-Random Number Generator) and tested via Miller-Rabin primality test.

**Step 2.** Compute the modulus:
$$n = p \cdot q$$

**Step 3.** Compute Euler's totient:
$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

**Step 4.** Choose a public exponent $e$ such that:
$$1 < e < \phi(n) \quad \text{and} \quad \gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$$

**Step 5.** Compute the private exponent $d$ as the **modular multiplicative inverse** of $e$ modulo $\phi(n)$:
$$e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$$

This is computed using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.

### **Stage 2 — Public Key Publication**

The public key is the tuple $\boxed{(n, e)}$ and is openly distributed.
The private key is the tuple $\boxed{(n, d)}$ (or sometimes stored as $(p, q, d)$ for CRT optimization).

### **Stage 3 — Encryption (Performed by Sender)**

The sender converts plaintext $M$ to an integer with $0 < M < n$ and computes the ciphertext:

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

### **Stage 4 — Decryption (Performed by Receiver)**

The receiver uses their private key $d$:

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

## 2.3 The "Why" — Proof of Correctness

The decryption recovers $M$ because:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C^{d} &\equiv (M^{e})^{d} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} \pmod{n} \quad \text{(since } e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)\text{)} \\
&\equiv M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \pmod{n} \\
&\equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \pmod{n} \quad \text{(by Euler's Theorem, valid if } \gcd(M,n)=1\text{)} \\
&\equiv M \pmod{n}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Formula / Definition | Purpose | Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | Public composite | $p, q$ large primes |
| Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Group order | Used for key generation |
| Public exponent | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ | Encryption | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| Private exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Decryption (trapdoor) | Computed via EEA |
| Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | Sender operation | $0 \le M < n$ |
| Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Receiver operation | Recovers plaintext |
| Signature | $S \equiv M^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Sign with private key | Verified: $M \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ |
| Security bit-length | $n \ge 2048$ bits | NIST minimum | $p, q \ge 1024$ bits each |
| Mod size relation | $\vert n \vert = \vert p \vert + \vert q \vert$ bits | Bit-length identity | Approximate |
| Common $e$ | $e = 65537 = 2^{16}+1$ | Fermat prime $F_4$ | Fast encryption, secure |

## 2.5 Security Basis — Why RSA is Hard to Break

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardness Assumption (Integer Factorization Problem — IFP):**
> Given $n = p \cdot q$, recovering $p$ and $q$ is computationally infeasible for $\vert n \vert \ge 2048$ bits using any known classical algorithm.
>
> The best classical algorithm is the **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)**, with sub-exponential complexity:
> $$L_n\left[\tfrac{1}{3},\, \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{64}{9}}\right] = \exp\!\left((\,\sqrt[3]{1.9229\ldots}\, + o(1)) \, (\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}\right)$$

If a cryptanalyst could factor $n$, they could compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ and then derive $d$ from $e$ via the Extended Euclidean Algorithm. The **entire security of RSA collapses** under factorization.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in IoT

- **TLS 1.2/1.3 Handshake**: RSA signs the server's certificate, encrypts the pre-master secret in cipher suites like `TLS_RSA_WITH_AES_128_CBC_SHA`.
- **X.509 Certificates**: Every IoT device's identity certificate contains an RSA public key.
- **Firmware Signing**: Manufacturers sign firmware updates with RSA; the device verifies with the embedded public key in ROM.
- **Lightweight RSA Variants**: For constrained IoT devices, RSA is used with $e = 3$ and **multi-prime RSA** or **Rabin-Williams** for bandwidth savings.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Complete RSA Round Trip

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** Alice wants to receive a confidential message $M = 42$ from Bob. Use a small RSA instance to demonstrate key generation, encryption, and decryption.

### **Part 1 — Key Generation**

**Step 1.1:** Choose two small distinct primes.

$$p = 11, \quad q = 13$$

**Step 1.2:** Compute the modulus.

$$
n = p \cdot q = 11 \times 13 = 143
$$

**Step 1.3:** Compute Euler's totient.

$$
\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1) = (10)(12) = 120
$$

**Step 1.4:** Choose a public exponent $e$ such that $\gcd(e, 120) = 1$ and $1 < e < 120$.

Try $e = 7$. Check: $\gcd(7, 120) = 1$ (since $120 = 2^3 \cdot 3 \cdot 5$ and $7$ is coprime to all of them). ✓

$$e = 7$$

**Step 1.5:** Compute the modular inverse $d = e^{-1} \pmod{120}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.

We must find $d$ such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{120}$.

**Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

| Iteration | Equation | Quotient | Remainder |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $120 = 7 \cdot 17 + 1$ | 17 | 1 |
| 2 | $7 = 1 \cdot 7 + 0$ | 7 | 0 |

**Back-substitute** from the first row:

$$
1 = 120 - 7 \cdot 17
$$

Rearranging:

$$
1 = 120 \cdot 1 + 7 \cdot (-17)
$$

Thus, modulo 120:

$$
7 \cdot (-17) \equiv 1 \pmod{120}
$$

So $d = -17 \equiv 120 - 17 = 103 \pmod{120}$.

$$
\boxed{d = 103}
$$

**Verification:** $7 \times 103 = 721 = 6 \times 120 + 1 = 721$. Indeed, $721 \bmod 120 = 1$. ✓

**Generated Keys:**
- **Public Key:** $(n, e) = (143, 7)$
- **Private Key:** $d = 103$

### **Part 2 — Encryption by Bob**

Bob wishes to send $M = 42$. He retrieves Alice's public key $(143, 7)$ and computes:

$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 42^{7} \pmod{143}
$$

We evaluate this using **successive modular squaring** (a.k.a. square-and-multiply).

**Express 7 in binary:** $7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = (111)_2$

**Compute successive squares modulo 143:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
42^{1} &\equiv 42 \pmod{143} \\
42^{2} &\equiv 1764 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

To find $1764 \bmod 143$:
- $143 \times 12 = 1716$
- $1764 - 1716 = 48$

$$
42^{2} \equiv 48 \pmod{143}
$$

$$
42^{4} \equiv 48^{2} = 2304 \pmod{143}
$$

- $143 \times 16 = 2288$
- $2304 - 2288 = 16$

$$
42^{4} \equiv 16 \pmod{143}
$$

Now combine according to bits of $7 = (111)_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &\equiv 42^{4} \cdot 42^{2} \cdot 42^{1} \pmod{143} \\
&\equiv 16 \cdot 48 \cdot 42 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$
16 \cdot 48 = 768
$$

$768 \bmod 143$: $143 \times 5 = 715$, remainder $= 53$.

$$
16 \cdot 48 \equiv 53 \pmod{143}
$$

Then:

$$
53 \cdot 42 = 2226
$$

$2226 \bmod 143$: $143 \times 15 = 2145$, remainder $= 81$.

$$
\boxed{C = 81}
$$

So Bob transmits ciphertext $C = 81$ to Alice.

### **Part 3 — Decryption by Alice**

Alice uses her private key $d = 103$:

$$
M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 81^{103} \pmod{143}
$$

**Direct evaluation is infeasible by hand** ($81^{103}$ is enormous), so we use **Euler's theorem shortcut** combined with reduction of the exponent.

**Step 3.1:** Since $\phi(143) = 120$ and $\gcd(81, 143) = 1$:

$$81^{120} \equiv 1 \pmod{143}$$

**Step 3.2:** Reduce exponent 103 modulo 120. Since $103 < 120$, no reduction possible.

But for pedagogical clarity, suppose $d$ were $223$. Then $223 \bmod 120 = 103$. So our exponent is already in reduced form.

**Step 3.3:** Apply square-and-multiply on $81^{103} \bmod 143$:

Express $103$ in binary: $103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (1100111)_2$

**Successive squares:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
81^{1} &\equiv 81 \pmod{143} \\
81^{2} &= 6561; \quad 6561 = 45 \cdot 143 + 126 \implies 81^{2} \equiv 126 \pmod{143} \\
81^{4} &\equiv 126^{2} = 15876; \quad 15876 = 111 \cdot 143 + 3 \implies 81^{4} \equiv 3 \pmod{143} \\
81^{8} &\equiv 3^{2} = 9 \pmod{143} \\
81^{16} &\equiv 9^{2} = 81 \pmod{143} \\
81^{32} &\equiv 81^{2} \equiv 126 \pmod{143} \\
81^{64} &\equiv 126^{2} \equiv 3 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Combine via binary representation of 103 = 64 + 32 + 4 + 2 + 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M &\equiv 81^{64} \cdot 81^{32} \cdot 81^{4} \cdot 81^{2} \cdot 81^{1} \pmod{143} \\
&\equiv 3 \cdot 126 \cdot 3 \cdot 126 \cdot 81 \pmod{143}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Multiply left to right:**

- $3 \cdot 126 = 378$; $378 \bmod 143$: $143 \times 2 = 286$, $378 - 286 = 92$. So $\equiv 92$.
- $92 \cdot 3 = 276$; $276 \bmod 143 = 276 - 286 = -10 \equiv 133 \pmod{143}$.
- $133 \cdot 126 = 16758$; $143 \times 117 = 16731$; $16758 - 16731 = 27$. So $\equiv 27$.
- $27 \cdot 81 = 2187$; $143 \times 15 = 2145$; $2187 - 2145 = 42$. So $\equiv 42$.

$$
\boxed{M = 42}
$$

**Alice successfully recovered Bob's plaintext message!** ✓

## 3.2 Proof of Decryption Correctness (Formal)

We must show $C^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$.

**Given:** $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, meaning $e \cdot d = 1 + k \cdot \phi(n)$ for some integer $k \ge 0$.

**Start with ciphertext:**

$$
C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}
$$

**Raise both sides to the $d$-th power:**

$$
C^{d} \equiv M^{e \cdot d} \pmod{n}
$$

**Substitute the congruence relation:**

$$
M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot M^{k \cdot \phi(n)} = M \cdot \left(M^{\phi(n)}\right)^{k}
$$

**By Euler's Theorem**, since $\gcd(M, n) = 1$:

$$
M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}
$$

**Therefore:**

$$
M \cdot \left(M^{\phi(n)}\right)^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n} \quad \blacksquare
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common RSA Attack Vectors (for KTU perspective)

| Attack | Target | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Brute-force factoring** | $n$ | Use $\vert n \vert \ge 2048$ bits |
| **Common modulus attack** | Same $n$ for two users | Each user generates unique $p, q$ |
| **Low-exponent ($e=3$) broadcast** | Same $M$ sent to 3 recipients with $e=3$ | Use **OAEP padding** + randomized salts |
| **Wiener's attack** | Small $d$ (e.g., $d < n^{1/4}/3$) | Ensure $d \ge n^{1/4}/3$ |
| **Håstad's broadcast** | $e$ small, $M$ small | Apply **random padding** before encryption |
| **Side-channel timing** | Square-and-multiply timing | Use **constant-time** modular exponentiation |
| **Chosen-ciphertext (CCA)** | Adaptive decryption queries | Use **RSA-OAEP** or **RSA-PSS** padding |
| **Quantum (Shor's algorithm)** | Integer factorization | Migrate to **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (post-quantum) |

## 3.4 CRT-Based RSA Decryption (Chinese Remainder Theorem Speedup)

For real-world RSA, decryption is accelerated by a factor of **~4×** using CRT. Instead of computing $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ directly, compute:

$$
M_p \equiv C^{d \bmod (p-1)} \pmod{p}
$$

$$
M_q \equiv C^{d \bmod (q-1)} \pmod{q}
$$

Then reconstruct $M$ using CRT:

$$
M \equiv M_q + q \cdot \left( (M_p - M_q) \cdot q^{-1} \bmod p \right) \pmod{n}
$$

This is widely used in smart cards and IoT security modules (e.g., TPM chips).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RSA End-to-End Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Key Generation"] --> B["Pick two large distinct primes p and q"]
    B --> C["Compute modulus n = p times q"]
    C --> D["Compute Euler totient phi_n = p_minus_1 times q_minus_1"]
    D --> E["Choose public exponent e with gcd e phi_n = 1"]
    E --> F["Compute private exponent d = e inverse mod phi_n using Extended Euclidean Algorithm"]
    F --> G["Publish public key as tuple n e"]
    F --> H["Keep private key d secret"]
    G --> I["Sender receives public key n e"]
    I --> J["Sender converts plaintext M to integer with 0 lt M lt n"]
    J --> K["Compute ciphertext C = M power e mod n via square and multiply"]
    K --> L["Transmit ciphertext C over insecure channel"]
    L --> M["Receiver obtains C"]
    H --> M
    M --> N["Receiver computes M_recovered = C power d mod n"]
    N --> O["Recovered plaintext equals original M"]
    O --> P["End of secure communication"]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 RSA Functional Architecture — Block-Level View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph KEYGEN["Key Generation Module"]
        PRIME["Prime Generator\np and q"] --> MODN["Modulus Calculator\nn = p times q"]
        PRIME --> TOT["Totient Calculator\nphi_n = p-1 times q-1"]
        MODN --> PUBE["Public Exponent Selector\ne coprime to phi_n"]
        TOT --> PUBE
        PUBE --> PRIVD["Private Exponent Solver\nd = e inverse mod phi_n\nExtended Euclidean Algorithm"]
    end

    subgraph ENCRYPT["Encryption Side - Sender"]
        PLT["Plaintext M"] --> PAD["OAEP Padding\nAdd randomness"] --> MEXP["Modular Exponentiator\nC = M to power e mod n"]
    end

    subgraph DECRYPT["Decryption Side - Receiver"]
        CEXP["Modular Exponentiator\nM = C to power d mod n"] --> UNPAD["OAEP Unpadding\nRemove padding"] --> REC["Recovered M"]
    end

    subgraph PUBCH["Public Channel - Insecure"]
        TRANS["Ciphertext C transmission"]
    end

    PRIVD --> MEXP
    PUBE --> MEXP
    MEXP --> TRANS
    TRANS --> CEXP
    PRIVD --> CEXP

    style PRIME fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,color:#ffffff
    style MODN fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,color:#ffffff
    style TOT fill:#0e7490,stroke:#083344,color:#ffffff
    style PUBE fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
    style PRIVD fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
    style PLT fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style PAD fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
    style MEXP fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style CEXP fill:#047857,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
    style UNPAD fill:#047857,stroke:#064e3b,color:#ffffff
    style REC fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff
    style TRANS fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 RSA Decryption — Mathematical Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Original Plaintext M"] --> E1["Raise to public exponent e\nC = M to power e"]
    E1 --> MOD1["Reduce modulo n\nC in range 0 to n_minus_1"]
    MOD1 --> C1["Ciphertext C transmitted"]
    C1 --> E2["Raise to private exponent d\nM_recovered = C to power d"]
    E2 --> MOD2["Reduce modulo n\nApply Euler theorem internally"]
    MOD2 --> M2["Recovered M equals original M"]

    M1 -.->|"Trapdoor knowledge\nof p and q enables d"| E2

    style M1 fill:#0d9488,stroke:#134e4a,color:#ffffff
    style E1 fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style MOD1 fill:#1d4ed8,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#b45309,stroke:#78350f,color:#ffffff
    style E2 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
    style MOD2 fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3b0764,color:#ffffff
    style M2 fill:#15803d,stroke:#14532d,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*State the mathematical foundation of the RSA algorithm. Why is RSA considered an asymmetric cryptosystem?*

**Model Answer:**

RSA is mathematically grounded in **Euler's Totient Theorem**, which states that for any integer $a$ coprime to $n$, $a^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$, where $\phi(n)$ is Euler's totient function. For a composite modulus $n = p \cdot q$ (product of two distinct primes), $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.

RSA is **asymmetric** because it employs a pair of mathematically linked but functionally distinct keys:
- A **public key** $(n, e)$ used for encryption, freely distributed.
- A **private key** $d$ used for decryption, kept secret by the owner.

The asymmetry arises from the fact that computing $d$ from $e$ requires knowledge of $\phi(n)$, which in turn requires factorization of $n$. This factorization is computationally infeasible for sufficiently large primes, making it a **trapdoor function**.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*What is the role of the public exponent $e = 65537$ in RSA? Why is this specific value widely used in practice?*

**Model Answer:**

The public exponent $e = 65537$ (a.k.a. $F_4$, the fourth Fermat prime) is the most widely used encryption exponent in production RSA systems. Its roles are:

1. **Encryption efficiency:** Since $65537 = 2^{16} + 1$, its binary representation has only **two 1-bits** (at positions 0 and 16). This means square-and-multiply exponentiation requires only **17 squarings and 1 multiplication**, drastically speeding up encryption compared to using a large random $e$.

2. **Security:** $65537$ is **Fermat prime**, satisfying $\gcd(65537, \phi(n)) = 1$ for all valid $n$ (since 65537 is prime itself and $\phi(n)$ is even, but $65537$ is odd).

3. **Resistance to small-exponent attacks:** Unlike $e = 3$ which is vulnerable to Håstad's broadcast attack, $e = 65537$ is large enough to resist such attacks when combined with proper OAEP padding.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Perform RSA key generation for $p = 17$ and $q = 11$, choosing $e = 7$. Compute the full public and private keys and demonstrate encryption and decryption of the plaintext message $M = 88$. Show all intermediate steps. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the security of RSA in terms of the Integer Factorization Problem (IFP). Discuss the **Wiener's attack** and state the recommended minimum bit-length of $n$ as per NIST guidelines. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Key Generation:**

Given $p = 17$, $q = 11$, $e = 7$.

Compute modulus:
$$n = p \cdot q = 17 \times 11 = 187$$

Compute Euler's totient:
$$\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 16 \times 10 = 160$$

**Verify** $\gcd(7, 160) = 1$ ✓ (since $160 = 2^5 \times 5$ and $7$ is coprime).

**Compute $d$** such that $7d \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$:

Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:

$$160 = 7 \times 22 + 6$$
$$7 = 6 \times 1 + 1$$
$$6 = 1 \times 6 + 0$$

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 7 - 6 \times 1$$
$$1 = 7 - (160 - 7 \times 22) \times 1 = 7 \times 23 - 160 \times 1$$

Therefore $7 \times 23 \equiv 1 \pmod{160}$, giving $d = 23$.

**Generated Keys:**
- **Public key:** $(n, e) = (187, 7)$
- **Private key:** $d = 23$

**Step 2 — Encryption of $M = 88$:**

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n} = 88^{7} \pmod{187}$$

Express $7 = 4 + 2 + 1 = (111)_2$. Compute successive squares:

$$
88^{1} \equiv 88 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
88^{2} = 7744; \quad 7744 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 41 = 7667, \quad 7744 - 7667 = 77
$$
$$
88^{2} \equiv 77 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
88^{4} \equiv 77^{2} = 5929; \quad 5929 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 31 = 5797, \quad 5929 - 5797 = 132
$$
$$
88^{4} \equiv 132 \pmod{187}
$$

Combine:
$$
C \equiv 88^{4} \cdot 88^{2} \cdot 88^{1} \equiv 132 \cdot 77 \cdot 88 \pmod{187}
$$

Compute step by step:
- $132 \cdot 77 = 10164$; $187 \times 54 = 10098$; $10164 - 10098 = 66$. So $\equiv 66 \pmod{187}$.
- $66 \cdot 88 = 5808$; $187 \times 31 = 5797$; $5808 - 5797 = 11$. So $\equiv 11 \pmod{187}$.

$$\boxed{C = 11}$$

**Step 3 — Decryption of $C = 11$:**

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n} = 11^{23} \pmod{187}$$

**Reduce exponent using Euler's theorem:** $11^{160} \equiv 1 \pmod{187}$, but $23 < 160$ so no reduction.

Express $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1 = (10111)_2$. Compute successive squares:

$$
11^{1} \equiv 11 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
11^{2} = 121 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
11^{4} \equiv 121^{2} = 14641; \quad 14641 \bmod 187: \quad 187 \times 78 = 14586, \quad 14641 - 14586 = 55
$$
$$
11^{4} \equiv 55 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
11^{8} \equiv 55^{2} = 3025; \quad 187 \times 16 = 2992, \quad 3025 - 2992 = 33
$$
$$
11^{8} \equiv 33 \pmod{187}
$$

$$
11^{16} \equiv 33^{2} = 1089; \quad 187 \times 5 = 935, \quad 1089 - 935 = 154
$$
$$
11^{16} \equiv 154 \pmod{187}
$$

Combine for $23 = 16 + 4 + 2 + 1$:

$$
M \equiv 11^{16} \cdot 11^{4} \cdot 11^{2} \cdot 11^{1} \equiv 154 \cdot 55 \cdot 121 \cdot 11 \pmod{187}
$$

- $154 \cdot 55 = 8470$; $187 \times 45 = 8415$; $8470 - 8415 = 55$. So $\equiv 55$.
- $55 \cdot 121 = 6655$; $187 \times 35 = 6545$; $6655 - 6545 = 110$. So $\equiv 110$.
- $110 \cdot 11 = 1210$; $187 \times 6 = 1122$; $1210 - 1122 = 88$. So $\equiv 88$.

$$\boxed{M = 88}$$

**Verification successful: Decryption recovered the original plaintext!** ✓

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Security of RSA — The Integer Factorization Problem (IFP):**

The security of RSA rests on the assumption that given the public modulus $n = p \cdot q$, it is computationally infeasible to recover the prime factors $p$ and $q$. This is known as the **Integer Factorization Problem (IFP)**.

If an attacker can factor $n$, they can compute:
1. $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$
2. The private exponent $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ (using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm)

This would allow them to decrypt any ciphertext, completely breaking the cryptosystem.

**Algorithmic Complexity of Factoring:**

The fastest classical algorithm for factoring is the **General Number Field Sieve (GNFS)**, with sub-exponential time complexity:
$$L_n\left[\tfrac{1}{3}, \sqrt[3]{\tfrac{64}{9}}\right] = \exp\!\left((\,1.923\ldots + o(1)) (\ln n)^{1/3} (\ln \ln n)^{2/3}\right)$$

**Wiener's Attack (Continued-Fraction Attack):**

In 1990, **Michael Wiener** demonstrated that if the private exponent $d$ is small, specifically:
$$d < \frac{n^{1/4}}{3}$$

then the attacker can recover $d$ efficiently using the **continued fraction expansion of $e/n$**. The attack has polynomial time complexity $O(\log n)$ and works because small $d$ makes $e/n \approx k/d$ for some small $k$, and the continued fraction reveals the small denominator $d$.

**Mitigation:** Choose $d$ sufficiently large (specifically $d \ge n^{1/4}/3$) by using large primes $p$ and $q$ such that $p - 1$ and $q - 1$ have large prime factors.

**NIST Recommended Bit-Lengths for $n$:**

| RSA Key Size | Symmetric Equivalent | Status / Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| RSA-1024 | 80-bit security | **DEPRECATED** (broken since 2010s) |
| **RSA-2048** | **112-bit security** | **Current minimum (NIST SP 800-57)** |
| RSA-3072 | 128-bit security | Recommended for sensitive applications |
| RSA-4096 | ~150-bit security | Long-lived root CA, code signing |

**Real-World Impact:** As of 2024, factoring RSA-2048 would require approximately **$10^{15}$ operations**, infeasible with current classical computers. However, **Shor's algorithm** on a sufficiently large quantum computer (~20 million noisy qubits for RSA-2048) could break it in polynomial time, motivating the migration to **post-quantum cryptography** (e.g., CRYSTALS-Kyber, FALCON).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the four-stage RSA algorithm in detail. In each stage, clearly state the inputs, outputs, and mathematical operations involved. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Bob chooses $p = 23$ and $q = 41$ and selects $e = 13$ as the public exponent. Compute $\phi(n)$, find the private key $d$, encrypt the message $M = 150$, and then show the decryption process. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**The Four-Stage RSA Algorithm:**

**Stage 1 — Key Generation:**

*Inputs:* Two large, distinct, random primes $p$ and $q$.
*Operations:*
- Compute $n = p \cdot q$.
- Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
- Compute $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$ using the Extended Euclidean Algorithm.

*Outputs:* Public key $(n, e)$ and private key $d$.

**Stage 2 — Key Distribution:**

The public key $(n, e)$ is published in a digital certificate (e.g., X.509) or a public key infrastructure (PKI) repository. The private key $d$ is stored securely (e.g., in a Hardware Security Module, TPM, or smart card) and never transmitted.

**Stage 3 — Encryption:**

*Input:* Plaintext message $M$ (integer with $0 < M < n$).
*Operation:* The sender computes:
$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$
using **square-and-multiply** modular exponentiation.

*Output:* Ciphertext $C$, transmitted over the insecure channel.

**Stage 4 — Decryption:**

*Input:* Received ciphertext $C$.
*Operation:* The receiver computes:
$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$
using the private key.

*Output:* Recovered plaintext $M$.

**Correctness:** Decryption works because $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, so by Euler's theorem:
$$C^{d} \equiv (M^{e})^{d} = M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $p = 23$, $q = 41$, $e = 13$, $M = 150$.

**Step 1 — Compute Modulus:**
$$n = 23 \times 41 = 943$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler's Totient:**
$$\phi(n) = (23 - 1)(41 - 1) = 22 \times 40 = 880$$

**Step 3 — Verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n))$:**
$\gcd(13, 880) = 1$ ✓ (since $880 = 2^4 \times 5 \times 11$ and $13$ is prime, coprime to all factors).

**Step 4 — Compute $d = 13^{-1} \pmod{880}$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm:**

| Step | Equation |
|---|---|
| 1 | $880 = 13 \times 67 + 9$ |
| 2 | $13 = 9 \times 1 + 4$ |
| 3 | $9 = 4 \times 2 + 1$ |
| 4 | $4 = 1 \times 4 + 0$ |

Back-substitute:
$$1 = 9 - 4 \times 2$$
$$1 = 9 - (13 - 9) \times 2 = 9 \times 3 - 13 \times 2$$
$$1 = (880 - 13 \times 67) \times 3 - 13 \times 2 = 880 \times 3 - 13 \times 201 - 13 \times 2$$
$$1 = 880 \times 3 - 13 \times 203$$

Thus $13 \times (-203) \equiv 1 \pmod{880}$, so:
$$d = -203 \equiv 880 - 203 = 677 \pmod{880}$$

**Verification:** $13 \times 677 = 8801 = 10 \times 880 + 1$. ✓

$$\boxed{d = 677}$$

**Step 5 — Encryption of $M = 150$:**

$$C \equiv 150^{13} \pmod{943}$$

Express $13 = 8 + 4 + 1 = (1101)_2$. Compute successive squares:

$$
150^{1} \equiv 150 \pmod{943}
$$

$$
150^{2} = 22500; \quad 22500 \bmod 943: \quad 943 \times 23 = 21689, \quad 22500 - 21689 = 811
$$
$$
150^{2} \equiv 811 \pmod{943}
$$

$$
150^{4} \equiv 811^{2} = 657721; \quad 943 \times 697 = 657271, \quad 657721 - 657271 = 450
$$
$$
150^{4} \equiv 450 \pmod{943}
$$

$$
150^{8} \equiv 450^{2} = 202500; \quad 943 \times 214 = 201802, \quad 202500 - 201802 = 698
$$
$$
150^{8} \equiv 698 \pmod{943}
$$

Combine for $13 = 8 + 4 + 1$:
$$
C \equiv 150^{8} \cdot 150^{4} \cdot 150^{1} \equiv 698 \cdot 450 \cdot 150 \pmod{943}
$$

- $698 \cdot 450 = 314100$; $943 \times 333 = 314019$; $314100 - 314019 = 81$. So $\equiv 81 \pmod{943}$.
- $81 \cdot 150 = 12150$; $943 \times 12 = 11316$; $12150 - 11316 = 834$. So $\equiv 834 \pmod{943}$.

$$\boxed{C = 834}$$

**Step 6 — Decryption of $C = 834$:**

$$M \equiv 834^{677} \pmod{943}$$

**Reduce exponent via Euler's theorem:** $677 \bmod 880 = 677$ (no reduction possible, $677 < 880$).

We can additionally reduce the base by computing $\gcd(834, 943)$:
$\gcd(834, 943) = 1$ (since $943 = 23 \times 41$ and $834 = 2 \times 3 \times 139$, all coprime). So Euler's theorem applies directly.

Express $677 = 512 + 128 + 32 + 4 + 1 = (1010100101)_2$.

For brevity, the full computation (which involves 10 successive squarings) yields:
$$\boxed{M = 150}$$

confirming successful decryption.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in RSA Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to verify $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$** — Without this, $e$ has no modular inverse, and key generation fails. *Always show this check explicitly.*
>
> 2. **Incorrect Extended Euclidean back-substitution** — A single sign error propagates and gives a wrong $d$. *Verify: $e \cdot d \bmod \phi(n)$ MUST equal exactly 1.*
>
> 3. **Using textbook RSA (no padding) in security discussions** — The "textbook RSA" $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ is **deterministic and insecure**. Real systems use **OAEP** or **PSS padding**. *Mention this explicitly in 14-mark answers for full credit.*
>
> 4. **Confusing the order of public/private exponents in signatures** — **Encryption:** $C = M^{e} \bmod n$ (use receiver's public $e$). **Decryption:** $M = C^{d} \bmod n$ (use receiver's private $d$). **Signing:** $S = M^{d} \bmod n$ (use signer's private $d$). **Verification:** $M = S^{e} \bmod n$ (use signer's public $e$).
>
> 5. **Not specifying the modulus size in security questions** — Stating "RSA is secure" without saying "RSA with $n \ge 2048$ bits" loses marks.
>
> 6. **Computational errors in modular arithmetic** — A single mistake in $a \bmod n$ cascades. *Always show the quotient multiplication explicitly*: e.g., "$\,143 \times 12 = 1716$, $1764 - 1716 = 48$".

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RSA Algorithm — Key Takeaways for KTU 2024 Exam:**
>
> - **Inventors:** Rivest, Shamir, Adleman (1977). **Type:** Public-key (asymmetric) cryptosystem.
> - **Security basis:** Integer Factorization Problem (IFP) — factoring $n = p \cdot q$ is hard for $\vert n \vert \ge 2048$ bits.
> - **Totient formula:** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ for $n = p \cdot q$ (two distinct primes).
> - **Key generation:** Compute $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ using **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
> - **Public exponent constraint:** $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ and $1 < e < \phi(n)$.
> - **Standard $e$:** $e = 65537$ (Fermat prime $F_4$, fast and secure).
> - **Encryption:** $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$.
> - **Decryption:** $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$.
> - **Core proof:** $C^{d} = M^{e \cdot d} = M^{1 + k\phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ (Euler's Theorem).
> - **Square-and-Multiply** is used to compute modular exponentiation efficiently in $O(\log e)$ multiplications.
> - **Decryption speedup:** Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) gives ~4× faster decryption by computing modulo $p$ and $q$ separately.
> - **Vulnerabilities:** Low $d$ (Wiener), low $e$ with no padding (Håstad), timing attacks, and future **quantum (Shor's algorithm)**.
> - **Padding is mandatory:** Real systems use **RSA-OAEP** for encryption and **RSA-PSS** for signatures.
> - **NIST recommended:** **RSA-2048** minimum (≈ 112-bit security); RSA-3072 or 4096 for long-lived secrets.
> - **IoT relevance:** Foundation of TLS handshakes, X.509 certificates, firmware signing, and PKI in IoT gateways.
> - **Post-quantum migration:** RSA will be replaced by **CRYSTALS-Kyber** (KEM) and **CRYSTALS-Dilithium** (signatures) under NIST PQC standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RSA Illustration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RSA Illustration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**RSA (Rivest–Shamir–Adleman)** is an **asymmetric / public-key cryptosystem** in which the encryption key is publicly distributed while the decryption (private) key is kept secret. Its security relies on the **computational intractability of factoring the product of two large prime numbers**. In an *RSA Illustration*, the abstract algorithm is concretely demonstrated through a complete worked numerical example covering **key generation, encryption, and decryption**, forming a high-weightage question in KTU end-semester evaluations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCCIT602 — Module 2):**
> Students must be able to demonstrate the **complete numerical flow of the RSA algorithm**, including prime selection, computation of Euler's totient, modular inverse for the private exponent, and modular exponentiation for ciphertext conversion. This is a **frequently repeated 14-mark ESE question**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Locked Mailbox"

Imagine an **IoT sensor node** deployed on a Kerala paddy field. Any authorized drone passing by must be able to send **authenticated firmware updates** to the sensor. Here is how RSA maps to this real-world scenario:

- **Public Key (e, n)** = the *open slot* of a transparent mailbox. Anyone (any drone) can see the address and drop a message in. No password needed.
- **Private Key (d, n)** = the *physical key* the sensor's owner alone possesses. Only the owner can open the mailbox and read what was dropped.
- **The locked message** = the firmware update encrypted as $C = M^{e} \bmod n$.
- **Cracking the lock without the key** = attempting to factor $n = p \times q$, a near-impossible task when $p$ and $q$ are 1024-bit primes.

This single analogy captures the **essence of asymmetric encryption** that the RSA illustration questions in KTU 2024 expect you to articulate.

## 1.3 Why RSA Illustration Matters in IoT

In **resource-constrained IoT devices** (think Class-0/Class-1 sensor motes with 8-bit microcontrollers), **full RSA with 2048-bit keys is computationally heavy**. However, lightweight variants of RSA, or hybrid schemes using **RSA for key exchange + AES for bulk data**, are heavily deployed. Understanding the **mathematical illustration** is the foundation for grasping ECC (Elliptic Curve Cryptography) — the preferred IoT alternative.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard RSA Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Public modulus** $n = p \times q$ where $p, q$ are large primes
> - **Euler's totient** $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ — valid ONLY when $n = pq$ with distinct primes
> - **Public exponent** $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$
> - **Private exponent** $d \equiv e^{-1} \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of RSA Illustration

Any KTU RSA illustration question has **three distinct phases** that you must present sequentially:

### **Phase 1 — Key Generation (Performed by the Receiver/Bob)**

1. **Prime Selection:** Choose two distinct large primes $p$ and $q$. For KTU illustration problems, these are **small primes (2-digit)** to keep arithmetic tractable.
2. **Modulus Computation:** Compute $n = p \times q$. This $n$ becomes part of **both** the public and private keys.
3. **Totient Computation:** Compute $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$.
4. **Public Exponent Selection:** Pick integer $e$ such that $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.
5. **Private Exponent Computation:** Compute $d$ satisfying $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi(n)$ using the **Extended Euclidean Algorithm**.
6. **Publish:** Public Key = $(e, n)$. **Keep Secret:** Private Key = $(d, n)$. **Destroy:** $p, q, \phi(n)$ (in real-world deployment).

### **Phase 2 — Encryption (Performed by the Sender/Alice)**

To send plaintext $M$ (where $0 \leq M < n$):

$$C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$$

Alice uses Bob's public key $(e, n)$ to compute ciphertext $C$.

### **Phase 3 — Decryption (Performed by Receiver/Bob)**

Bob recovers the original plaintext using his private key $(d, n)$:

$$M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$$

This works because of **Euler's Theorem** which states $M^{\phi(n)} \equiv 1 \pmod{n}$ when $\gcd(M, n) = 1$, leading to the elegant identity:

$$(M^{e})^{d} = M^{ed} = M^{1 + k \phi(n)} = M \cdot (M^{\phi(n)})^{k} \equiv M \cdot 1^{k} \equiv M \pmod{n}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RSA Works — The Mathematical Guarantee:**
> The chain $M \to C = M^{e} \bmod n \to M' = C^{d} \bmod n = M$ holds **if and only if** $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$. This is why Phase 1 step 5 is the **most critical computation** in any RSA illustration.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Step** | **Formula / Operation** | **Constraints / Conditions** | **Used In** |
|----------|-------------------------|------------------------------|-------------|
| Modulus | $n = p \cdot q$ | $p, q$ distinct large primes | Key Gen |
| Totient | $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$ | Valid only for $n = pq$ semiprime | Key Gen |
| Public Exponent | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ | $1 < e < \phi(n)$ | Key Gen |
| Private Exponent | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ | Solved via Extended Euclidean Algorithm | Key Gen |
| Encryption | $C \equiv M^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $0 \leq M < n$ | Encryption |
| Decryption | $M \equiv C^{d} \pmod{n}$ | Uses private key $(d, n)$ | Decryption |
| Signature Verify | $M \equiv S^{e} \pmod{n}$ | $S = M^{d} \bmod n$ | Digital Signature |
| Correctness Identity | $(M^{e})^{d} \equiv M \pmod{n}$ | By Euler's Theorem | Proof |
| Square-and-Multiply | $M^{e} = \prod M^{2^{i} \cdot b_{i}} \bmod n$ | $b_i$ are binary bits of $e$ | Fast Exponentiation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Never use the vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ inside LaTeX table cells. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Also, in KTU valuation, if you write $\phi(n) = n - 1$ instead of $(p-1)(q-1)$, you will lose **2 marks** because this only holds when $n$ itself is prime.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **TLS/SSL Handshakes:** RSA (or its successor X25519 ECDH) is used to securely exchange the symmetric session key for HTTPS browsing.
- **IoT Firmware Update Authentication:** Manufacturers sign firmware images with their private key; deployed IoT devices verify using the manufacturer's public key.
- **Digital Certificates (X.509):** Every digital certificate in PKI uses RSA signatures as the trust anchor.
- **Email Encryption (PGP/GPG):** RSA encrypts the symmetric key that encrypts the email body.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step RSA Illustration (Worked Numerical Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard RSA Illustration Problem:**
> *"In an RSA cryptosystem, given $p = 61$, $q = 53$, $e = 17$, and plaintext $M = 65$, perform key generation, encryption, and decryption. Verify your result."*

This is the **canonical example** appearing in KTU model question papers and university examinations. We will solve it with **exhaustive step-by-step arithmetic**.

## 3.1 Step 1 — Key Generation

### Sub-step 1.1: Compute Modulus $n$

$$n = p \times q = 61 \times 53$$

$$\begin{aligned}
61 \times 53 &= 61 \times 50 + 61 \times 3 \\
&= 3050 + 183 \\
&= 3233
\end{aligned}$$

So, $n = 3233$.

### Sub-step 1.2: Compute Euler's Totient $\phi(n)$

Since $n = p \cdot q$ with distinct primes, by the multiplicative property of $\phi$:

$$\phi(n) = (p - 1)(q - 1)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\phi(3233) &= (61 - 1) \times (53 - 1) \\
&= 60 \times 52 \\
&= 3120
\end{aligned}$$

So, $\phi(n) = 3120$.

### Sub-step 1.3: Verify Public Exponent $e = 17$

The condition is $1 < e < \phi(n)$ and $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$.

Check: $1 < 17 < 3120$ ✓

Compute $\gcd(17, 3120)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
3120 &= 17 \times 183 + 9 \\
17 &= 9 \times 1 + 8 \\
9 &= 8 \times 1 + 1 \\
8 &= 1 \times 8 + 0
\end{aligned}$$

GCD back-substitution gives $\gcd(17, 3120) = 1$ ✓

So $e = 17$ is a valid public exponent.

### Sub-step 1.4: Compute Private Exponent $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm

We need $d$ such that $17 \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

Continue the Euclidean chain backwards from above:

$$\begin{aligned}
1 &= 9 - 8 \times 1 \\
  &= 9 - (17 - 9 \times 1) \times 1 \\
  &= 9 \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\
  &= (3120 - 17 \times 183) \times 2 - 17 \times 1 \\
  &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 366 - 17 \times 1 \\
  &= 3120 \times 2 - 17 \times 367
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$17 \times (-367) \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$$

Taking modulo $3120$:

$$d = -367 \bmod 3120 = 3120 - 367 = 2753$$

**Verification:**

$$17 \times 2753 = 46801$$

$$46801 \div 3120 = 15 \text{ remainder } 1$$

$$46801 = 15 \times 3120 + 1 = 46800 + 1 \checkmark$$

So, $d = 2753$.

### Sub-step 1.5: Publish Keys

| **Key Type** | **Value** | **Visibility** |
|--------------|-----------|----------------|
| Public Key | $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$ | Published to everyone |
| Private Key | $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$ | Kept secret by Bob |
| Primes $p, q$ | $61, 53$ | Destroyed in real systems |

## 3.2 Step 2 — Encryption ($C = M^{e} \bmod n$)

Given plaintext $M = 65$, public key $(e, n) = (17, 3233)$:

$$C = 65^{17} \bmod 3233$$

We use the **Square-and-Multiply Algorithm** (binary exponentiation) since direct computation is infeasible.

**Binary expansion of 17:** $17 = 16 + 1 = (10001)_2$

So we compute $65^{1}, 65^{2}, 65^{4}, 65^{8}, 65^{16} \pmod{3233}$ and multiply the relevant ones.

### Sub-step 2.1: Compute Successive Squares

$$\begin{aligned}
65^{1} &\equiv 65 \pmod{3233} \\
65^{2} &\equiv 65 \times 65 = 4225 \\
  &\equiv 4225 - 3233 = 992 \pmod{3233} \\
65^{4} &\equiv 992 \times 992 = 984064 \\
  &\equiv 984064 \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $984064 \bmod 3233$:

$$984064 = 304 \times 3233 + r$$
$$304 \times 3233 = 304 \times 3000 + 304 \times 233 = 912000 + 70832 = 982832$$
$$r = 984064 - 982832 = 1232$$

So $65^{4} \equiv 1232 \pmod{3233}$.

Continuing:

$$\begin{aligned}
65^{8} &\equiv 1232 \times 1232 = 1517824 \\
  &\equiv 1517824 \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $1517824 \bmod 3233$:

$$469 \times 3233 = 469 \times 3000 + 469 \times 233 = 1407000 + 109277 = 1516277$$
$$r = 1517824 - 1516277 = 1547$$

So $65^{8} \equiv 1547 \pmod{3233}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
65^{16} &\equiv 1547 \times 1547 = 2393209 \\
  &\equiv 2393209 \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $2393209 \bmod 3233$:

$$740 \times 3233 = 740 \times 3000 + 740 \times 233 = 2220000 + 172420 = 2392420$$
$$r = 2393209 - 2392420 = 789$$

So $65^{16} \equiv 789 \pmod{3233}$.

### Sub-step 2.2: Combine Powers for $65^{17}$

Since $17 = 16 + 1$:

$$65^{17} = 65^{16} \times 65^{1}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
65^{17} &\equiv 789 \times 65 \pmod{3233} \\
  &= 51285 \\
  &\equiv 51285 \bmod 3233
\end{aligned}$$

Compute $51285 \bmod 3233$:

$$15 \times 3233 = 15 \times 3000 + 15 \times 233 = 45000 + 3495 = 48495$$
$$r = 51285 - 48495 = 2790$$

$$\boxed{C = 2790}$$

## 3.3 Step 3 — Decryption ($M = C^{d} \bmod n$)

Given ciphertext $C = 2790$, private key $(d, n) = (2753, 3233)$:

$$M = 2790^{2753} \bmod 3233$$

**Note:** The exponent 2753 is enormous for direct computation. In a KTU illustration, you do **NOT** need to compute this expansion by hand. Instead, you **demonstrate the procedure** and rely on the theoretical correctness of RSA. However, let us still verify with a manageable approach using the Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) — this is what real RSA implementations actually do.

### Sub-step 3.1: Decryption Modulo $p = 61$

Compute $M_p = C^{d} \bmod p = 2790^{2753} \bmod 61$.

First reduce base: $2790 \bmod 61$:
$$2790 = 45 \times 61 + 45 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2790 \equiv 45 \pmod{61}$$

By **Fermat's Little Theorem**: $a^{p-1} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$, so $a^{60} \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$.

Reduce exponent $2753 \bmod 60$:

$$2753 = 45 \times 60 + 53 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2753 \equiv 53 \pmod{60}$$

So:

$$M_p \equiv 45^{53} \bmod 61$$

Compute $45^{53} \bmod 61$ using repeated squaring:

$$\begin{aligned}
45^{1} &\equiv 45 \pmod{61} \\
45^{2} &\equiv 2025 \bmod 61 \\
  &\quad 2025 = 33 \times 61 + 12 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 45^{2} \equiv 12 \pmod{61} \\
45^{4} &\equiv 12^{2} = 144 \equiv 144 - 2(61) = 22 \pmod{61} \\
45^{8} &\equiv 22^{2} = 484 \equiv 484 - 7(61) = 484 - 427 = 57 \pmod{61} \\
45^{16} &\equiv 57^{2} = 3249 \equiv 3249 - 53(61) = 3249 - 3233 = 16 \pmod{61} \\
45^{32} &\equiv 16^{2} = 256 \equiv 256 - 4(61) = 256 - 244 = 12 \pmod{61}
\end{aligned}$$

Now $53 = 32 + 16 + 4 + 1$:

$$\begin{aligned}
45^{53} &= 45^{32} \cdot 45^{16} \cdot 45^{4} \cdot 45^{1} \\
  &\equiv 12 \times 16 \times 22 \times 45 \pmod{61} \\
  &= (12 \times 16) \times (22 \times 45) \\
  &= 192 \times 990
\end{aligned}$$

Reduce each factor mod 61:

$$192 = 3 \times 61 + 9 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 192 \equiv 9 \pmod{61}$$
$$990 = 16 \times 61 + 14 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 990 \equiv 14 \pmod{61}$$

So:

$$M_p \equiv 9 \times 14 = 126 \equiv 126 - 2(61) = 4 \pmod{61}$$

Therefore $M_p = 4$, i.e., plaintext $\equiv 4 \pmod{61}$.

### Sub-step 3.2: Decryption Modulo $q = 53$

Compute $M_q = 2790^{2753} \bmod 53$.

Reduce base: $2790 \bmod 53$:
$$2790 = 52 \times 53 + 34 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2790 \equiv 34 \pmod{53}$$

By Fermat: $a^{52} \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$.

Reduce exponent $2753 \bmod 52$:

$$2753 = 52 \times 52 + 49 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2753 \equiv 49 \pmod{52}$$

So $M_q \equiv 34^{49} \bmod 53$.

Since $34 \equiv -19 \pmod{53}$ and $49 = 48 + 1$:

$$34^{49} = 34^{48} \cdot 34 = (34^{2})^{24} \cdot 34$$

$$\begin{aligned}
34^{2} &= 1156 \equiv 1156 - 21(53) = 1156 - 1113 = 43 \pmod{53} \\
34^{4} &\equiv 43^{2} = 1849 \equiv 1849 - 34(53) = 1849 - 1802 = 47 \pmod{53} \\
34^{8} &\equiv 47^{2} = 2209 \equiv 2209 - 41(53) = 2209 - 2173 = 36 \pmod{53} \\
34^{16} &\equiv 36^{2} = 1296 \equiv 1296 - 24(53) = 1296 - 1272 = 24 \pmod{53} \\
34^{32} &\equiv 24^{2} = 576 \equiv 576 - 10(53) = 576 - 530 = 46 \pmod{53}
\end{aligned}$$

$49 = 32 + 16 + 1$:

$$34^{49} \equiv 46 \times 24 \times 34 \pmod{53}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
46 \times 24 &= 1104 \equiv 1104 - 20(53) = 1104 - 1060 = 44 \pmod{53} \\
44 \times 34 &= 1496 \equiv 1496 - 28(53) = 1496 - 1484 = 12 \pmod{53}
\end{aligned}$$

So $M_q = 12$, i.e., plaintext $\equiv 12 \pmod{53}$.

### Sub-step 3.3: Chinese Remainder Theorem Reconstruction

We have:
$$M \equiv 4 \pmod{61}$$
$$M \equiv 12 \pmod{53}$$

Using CRT: $M = M_p \cdot q \cdot (q^{-1} \bmod p) + M_q \cdot p \cdot (p^{-1} \bmod q) \pmod{n}$

Compute modular inverses:

$q^{-1} \bmod p$: Find $53^{-1} \bmod 61$ via extended Euclidean:
$$61 = 1 \times 53 + 8, \quad 53 = 6 \times 8 + 5, \quad 8 = 1 \times 5 + 3, \quad 5 = 1 \times 3 + 2, \quad 3 = 1 \times 2 + 1$$

Back-substitution:

$$1 = 3 - 2 = 3 - (5 - 3) = 2(3) - 5 = 2(8-5) - 5 = 2(8) - 3(5)$$
$$= 2(8) - 3(53 - 6\cdot 8) = 20(8) - 3(53) = 20(61 - 53) - 3(53) = 20(61) - 23(53)$$

So $53 \times (-23) \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$, hence $53^{-1} \equiv -23 \equiv 38 \pmod{61}$.

$p^{-1} \bmod q$: Find $61^{-1} \bmod 53$:
$$61 = 1 \times 53 + 8, \quad 53 = 6 \times 8 + 5 \Rightarrow 5 = 53 - 6(8) = 53 - 6(61-53) = 7(53) - 6(61)$$

So $61 \times (-6) \equiv 5 \pmod{53}$ — hmm, not 1. Continue:
$$5 = 8 - 3, \text{ where } 3 = 8 - 5 = 8 - (53 - 6\cdot 8) = 7(8) - 53 = 7(61-53) - 53 = 7(61) - 8(53)$$

Now: $1 = 8 - 5 = 8 - [7(61) - 8(53)] = 9(8) - 7(61) = 9(61-53) - 7(61) = 2(61) - 9(53)$

So $61 \times 2 \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$, hence $61^{-1} \equiv 2 \pmod{53}$.

Now reconstruct:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 4 \cdot 53 \cdot 38 + 12 \cdot 61 \cdot 2 \pmod{3233} \\
  &= 4 \times 2014 + 12 \times 122 \\
  &= 8056 + 1464 \\
  &= 9520
\end{aligned}$$

Reduce $9520 \bmod 3233$:

$$9520 = 2 \times 3233 + 3054$$

Wait: $2 \times 3233 = 6466$. $9520 - 6466 = 3054$. But $3054 > 3233$? No, $3054 < 3233$? Yes, $3054 < 3233$. So:

$$9520 \bmod 3233 = 3054 - 3233 = \text{negative}.$$

Let me recompute: $2 \times 3233 = 6466$. $9520 - 6466 = 3054$. Since $3054 < 3233$, this is the remainder.

But we expect $M = 65$. Let me recheck.

Actually $3054 \bmod 3233 = 3054$, which is wrong. Let me recheck the modular inverse of 53 mod 61.

Recheck: $53 \times 38 = 2014$. $2014 \bmod 61$: $2014 = 33 \times 61 + 1 = 2013 + 1$. So $2014 \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$ ✓

Recheck: $61 \times 2 = 122$. $122 \bmod 53 = 122 - 2(53) = 122 - 106 = 16$. That's not 1!

I made an error. Let me redo $61^{-1} \bmod 53$:

$61 \bmod 53 = 8$. So we need $8^{-1} \bmod 53$, i.e., find $x$ such that $8x \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$.

From Euclidean: $53 = 6 \times 8 + 5$, so $5 = 53 - 6(8)$.
$8 = 1 \times 5 + 3$, so $3 = 8 - 5 = 8 - [53 - 6(8)] = 7(8) - 53$.
$5 = 1 \times 3 + 2$, so $2 = 5 - 3 = [53 - 6(8)] - [7(8) - 53] = 2(53) - 13(8)$.
$3 = 1 \times 2 + 1$, so $1 = 3 - 2 = [7(8) - 53] - [2(53) - 13(8)] = 20(8) - 3(53)$.

So $8 \times 20 \equiv 1 \pmod{53}$, hence $61^{-1} \equiv 20 \pmod{53}$ (since $61 \equiv 8 \pmod{53}$).

Verify: $61 \times 20 = 1220$. $1220 \bmod 53$: $1220 = 23 \times 53 + 1 = 1219 + 1$. ✓

Now recompute:

$$\begin{aligned}
M &= 4 \cdot 53 \cdot 38 + 12 \cdot 61 \cdot 20 \pmod{3233} \\
  &= 8056 + 14640 \\
  &= 22696
\end{aligned}$$

Reduce $22696 \bmod 3233$:

$$22696 / 3233 \approx 7.02$$
$$7 \times 3233 = 22631$$
$$r = 22696 - 22631 = 65$$

$$\boxed{M = 65}$$

**Verification:** The decrypted plaintext is $M = 65$, which matches our original plaintext! ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway from Worked Example:** The CRT-based decryption is **4× faster** than direct $C^d \bmod n$ and is the method used in real-world RSA implementations. In KTU exams, the simpler direct approach $M = C^d \bmod n$ is acceptable; CRT is the **higher-order demonstration** that earns full marks.

## 3.4 Summary Table of the RSA Illustration

| **Step** | **Quantity** | **Value** | **Formula** |
|----------|--------------|-----------|-------------|
| Primes | $p, q$ | $61, 53$ | Selected |
| Modulus | $n$ | $3233$ | $n = p \cdot q$ |
| Totient | $\phi(n)$ | $3120$ | $(p-1)(q-1)$ |
| Public Exponent | $e$ | $17$ | $\gcd(e, \phi(n)) = 1$ |
| Private Exponent | $d$ | $2753$ | $e \cdot d \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$ |
| Plaintext | $M$ | $65$ | Given |
| Ciphertext | $C$ | $2790$ | $M^{e} \bmod n$ |
| Decrypted | $M'$ | $65$ | $C^{d} \bmod n$ |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 RSA Full Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Receiver Bob selects two distinct large primes p and q"] --> B["Compute modulus n = p multiplied by q"]
    B --> C["Compute Euler totient phi_n = p_minus_1 times q_minus_1"]
    C --> D["Choose public exponent e with gcd_e_phin equal to 1"]
    D --> E["Compute private exponent d such that e_times_d_mod_phin equals 1"]
    E --> F["Publish Public Key = (e, n) and keep Private Key = (d, n) secret"]
    F --> G["Sender Alice retrieves Bob's Public Key (e, n)"]
    G --> H["Alice encrypts plaintext M using C = M_power_e_mod_n"]
    H --> I["Ciphertext C is transmitted over insecure IoT channel"]
    I --> J["Bob receives Ciphertext C and applies Private Key d"]
    J --> K["Bob computes recovered M = C_power_d_mod_n"]
    K --> L["Verification: recovered M must equal original plaintext M"]
    L --> M["End: Secure communication established over insecure channel"]
```

## 4.2 RSA Key Generation Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input: primes p and q"] --> A2["Compute n = p times q"]
    A2 --> A3["Compute phi_n = p-1 times q-1"]
    A3 --> A4["Choose e coprime to phi_n"]
    A4 --> A5["Apply Extended Euclidean Algorithm"]
    A5 --> A6["Output: Public Key (e, n) and Private Key (d, n)"]
```

## 4.3 RSA Encryption / Decryption Sub-Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1["Plaintext M with 0 ≤ M less than n"] --> E2["Raise M to power e"]
    E2 --> E3["Take mod n to get Ciphertext C"]
    E3 --> E4["Transmit C over public channel"]
    E4 --> D1["Bob receives C"]
    D1 --> D2["Raise C to power d"]
    D2 --> D3["Take mod n to recover M"]
    D3 --> D4["Output: Decrypted plaintext equals original M"]
```

## 4.4 RSA in IoT — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IoT_Device_Side["IoT Device (Sensor Node — Class 1 Mote)"]
        SD1["Microcontroller Unit (8-bit / 32-bit)"]
        SD2["Lightweight RSA Engine or Hardware Crypto Accelerator"]
        SD3["Sensor Data Buffer M"]
    end
    subgraph Cloud_Server_Side["Cloud Server (Data Aggregator)"]
        CS1["RSA Public Key Store (e, n)"]
        CS2["RSA Encryption Module"]
        CS3["Encrypted Data Receiver"]
    end
    subgraph Communication_Layer["Communication Layer — Insecure Wireless"]
        WL1["LoRaWAN / Zigbee / BLE Channel"]
        WL2["Ciphertext Transmission C = M^e mod n"]
    end
    SD1 --> SD3
    SD3 --> SD2
    SD2 --> WL1
    WL1 --> WL2
    WL2 --> CS3
    CS1 --> CS2
    CS2 --> CS3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The architecture above shows the **IoT-to-Cloud** data path. In practice, IoT devices often use **ECDH or pre-shared symmetric keys** because full RSA-2048 is too slow for low-power motes. The diagram is illustrative of the **principle** of public-key encryption, which is the conceptual foundation that RSA illustration problems in KTU test.
