# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement

We now derive the **Discrete Logarithm-based Key Agreement** (W. Diffie & M. E. Hellman, *IEEE Transactions on Information Theory*, 1976). This is the **most-tested derivation** in the KTU Module 3 question bank.

### 3.1.1 Setup (Public Parameters)

Choose a **large prime** $p$ and a primitive root (generator) $g$ of $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

$$
1 < g < p, \quad \text{ord}(g) = p-1
$$

### 3.1.2 Private Key Selection

Each party secretly picks an integer:

$$
\text{Alice: } a \in \{2, \dots, p-2\}, \qquad \text{Bob: } b \in \{2, \dots, p-2\}
$$

### 3.1.3 Public Key Transmission

Alice computes and sends her public value $A$; Bob computes and sends his public value $B$:

$$
A = g^{a} \bmod p, \qquad B = g^{b} \bmod p
$$

### 3.1.4 Shared Secret Computation

Alice receives $B$ and computes:

$$
K_{AB} = B^{a} \bmod p = (g^{b})^{a} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p
$$

Bob receives $A$ and computes:

$$
K_{AB} = A^{b} \bmod p = (g^{a})^{b} \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p
$$

Since exponentiation is **commutative over a cyclic group**:

$$
(g^a)^b \equiv (g^b)^a \pmod{p}
$$

Both parties obtain the **identical shared secret** $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ **without ever transmitting it**.

### 3.1.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style)

Let $p = 23$, $g = 5$. Alice picks $a = 6$, Bob picks $b = 15$.

Step A — Alice's public value:

$$
A = 5^{6} \bmod 23
$$

We compute $5^6 = 15625$. Now reduce modulo 23:

$$
15625 \div 23 = 679 \text{ remainder } 8 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 5^{6} \bmod 23 = 8
$$

So $A = 8$. Alice transmits $A = 8$.

Step B — Bob's public value:

$$
B = 5^{15} \bmod 23
$$

We use repeated squaring. $5^2 = 25 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$, $5^4 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$, $5^8 \equiv 4^2 = 16 \pmod{23}$.

Now $5^{15} = 5^8 \cdot 5^4 \cdot 5^2 \cdot 5^1 = 16 \cdot 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 5 = 640$.

$$
640 \bmod 23 : 23 \times 27 = 621, \quad 640 - 621 = 19
$$

So $B = 19$. Bob transmits $B = 19$.

Step C — Alice's secret:

$$
K_{AB} = B^{a} \bmod 23 = 19^{6} \bmod 23
$$

$19^2 = 361 \equiv 361 - 15 \times 23 = 361 - 345 = 16 \pmod{23}$. $19^4 \equiv 16^2 = 256 \equiv 256 - 11 \times 23 = 256 - 253 = 3 \pmod{23}$. $19^6 = 19^4 \cdot 19^2 = 3 \cdot 16 = 48 \equiv 48 - 2 \times 23 = 2 \pmod{23}$.

So $K_{AB} = 2$.

Step D — Bob's secret (verification):

$$
K_{AB} = A^{b} \bmod 23 = 8^{15} \bmod 23
$$

$8^2 = 64 \equiv 64 - 2 \times 23 = 18 \pmod{23}$. $8^4 \equiv 18^2 = 324 \equiv 324 - 14 \times 23 = 324 - 322 = 2 \pmod{23}$. $8^8 \equiv 2^2 = 4 \pmod{23}$. $8^{15} = 8^8 \cdot 8^4 \cdot 8^2 \cdot 8^1 = 4 \cdot 2 \cdot 18 \cdot 8$.

$4 \cdot 2 = 8$. $8 \cdot 18 = 144 \equiv 144 - 6 \times 23 = 144 - 138 = 6 \pmod{23}$. $6 \cdot 8 = 48 \equiv 2 \pmod{23}$. ✓ **Both arrive at $K_{AB} = 2$**.

### 3.1.6 Security Argument (Why DH is Hard to Break)

An attacker Eve sees $(p, g, A, B)$ over the channel. To recover $K_{AB}$, she must solve:

$$
\text{Given } A = g^{a} \bmod p, \text{ find } a \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)}
$$

The DLP is **assumed computationally intractable** for primes $p \geq 2048$ bits, or for well-chosen elliptic curves ($\geq 256$ bits). Note: The problem is the **inverse** of modular exponentiation — the *forward* direction ($g \to g^a$) is easy, but the *reverse* ($g^a \to a$) is exponentially hard.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Transfer Mechanism

| Scenario | Constraint | Recommended Mechanism | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Two IoT sensors, no infra | No PKI, no server | **Static DH pre-shared** | Cheapest, fits 8-bit MCUs |
| Gateway with PKI, many sensors | Scalable | **RSA / ECC key transport** | PKI handles key revocation |
| Online, low-latency handshake | Need PFS | **ECDHE + digital signatures** | Mutual auth + forward secrecy |
| Resource-starved, 1 MHz CPU | Min RAM | **HPKE with Kyber (PQC)** | Tiny ciphertexts (~1 KB) |
| Industrial control (SCADA) | Long-lived key needed | **KDC + symmetric tickets** | Mirrors Kerberos for OT |
| Health-critical medical device | Must be quantum-safe | **Hybrid X25519 + Kyber768** | Defense in depth |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Generic Asymmetric Key Transfer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Alice IoT Node] -- Step 1: Generate Ks --> A
    A -- Step 2: Lookup Bob's PU_B from PKI --> PKI[(Public Key Infrastructure)]
    PKI -- Returns PU_B certificate --> A
    A -- Step 3: Compute C = E_PU_B --> A
    A -- Step 4: Send C over Channel --> CH((Untrusted Channel))
    CH -- Step 5: Deliver C --> B[Bob Gateway]
    B -- Step 6: Decrypt C using PR_B --> B
    B -- Step 7: Recover Ks --> B
    A -. Step 8: Encrypted Session .-> B
    E[ Eve / Attacker ] -. Intercepts C .-> CH
    E -- Cannot decrypt without PR_B --> X((Attack Fails))
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Flow — Diffie-Hellman Key Agreement Handshake

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Public Parameters: prime p, generator g] --> SA
    P1 --> SB
    SA[Alice picks private a] --> AA[Alice computes A = g^a mod p]
    SB[Bob picks private b] --> BB[Bob computes B = g^b mod p]
    AA --> EX1[Send A over channel]
    BB --> EX2[Send B over channel]
    EX1 --> COMP1[Bob computes K = A^b mod p]
    EX2 --> COMP2[Alice computes K = B^a mod p]
    COMP1 --> SSS[Shared Secret K = g to the ab mod p]
    COMP2 --> SSS
```

## 4.3 KDC-Based Key Distribution (Kerberos-Style for IoT)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Sensor A] -- 1. Request session key --> KDC[Key Distribution Centre]
    KDC -- 2. Generates fresh Ks --> KDC
    KDC -- 3a. E_K_A ticket for A --> S1
    KDC -- 3b. E_K_B ticket for B --> S2[Sensor B]
    S1 -- 4. Forwards B's ticket --> S2
    S1 -- 5. Mutual auth using Ks --> S2
    S2 -- 6. Acknowledges --> S1
    S1 -. 7. Secure session .-> S2
```

## 4.4 HPKE — Hybrid Public Key Encryption (RFC 9180) Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PK[Recipient Public Key pk] --> ENC
    RND[Cryptographically Secure Randomness] --> ENC
    ENC[Encap module: KEM] --> OUT1[encapsulated key enc]
    ENC --> OUT2[shared secret ss]
    OUT1 --> SEND[Transmit enc over channel]
    OUT2 --> KDF1[KDF expands ss to AEAD key + nonce]
    KDF1 --> PAY[Encrypt application payload with AEAD]
    SEND --> RCPT[Recipient]
    RCPT --> DECAP[Decap module: uses secret key sk]
    DECAP --> OUT3[Recovers shared secret ss]
    OUT3 --> KDF2[Same KDF expansion]
    KDF2 --> DEC[Decrypts payload with AEAD key]
    PAY --> DEC
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Differentiate between **Key Transfer**, **Key Agreement**, and **Key Transport** with one example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks — KTU Mark Distribution: Definition 1M, Distinction 1M, Example 1M):**

- **Key Transfer:** A mechanism by which one party (the sender) selects and securely delivers a secret key to another party (the receiver). The sender has *full control* over the key value. *Example:* RSA-encrypted session key in TLS 1.2.
- **Key Agreement:** A mechanism by which the shared key is derived jointly by both parties, with **neither party alone determining it**. *Example:* Diffie-Hellman / ECDH.
- **Key Transport:** A *type* of key transfer where the key is conveyed inside a cryptographically protected envelope (e.g., a public-key ciphertext). *Example:* Wrapping a 128-bit AES key inside a 2048-bit RSA ciphertext.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> What is **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)**? Why is **static DH** not PFS, while **ephemeral ECDHE** is?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
PFS is the property where the compromise of a *long-term private key* does not compromise **past** session keys. In static DH, the same $a$ and $b$ are reused; an attacker who later steals $PR_{Alice}$ can recompute all past $K_{AB}$. In **ephemeral ECDHE (ECDHE)**, fresh $a'$ and $b'$ are generated per session and destroyed immediately after — the long-term key is **only used to sign the ephemeral public values**; once the session ends, the ephemeral private value is wiped, and even with the long-term key, past sessions cannot be decrypted.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** Describe the **Diffie-Hellman Key Exchange** algorithm in detail with a suitable diagram. Explain the **Discrete Logarithm Problem** and why DH is considered secure. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** With $p = 353$ and $g = 3$, perform a Diffie-Hellman key exchange where Alice chooses $a = 97$ and Bob chooses $b = 233$. Show all modular arithmetic steps and compute the shared secret. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Setup (1 Mark):** Both parties agree on public parameters — a large prime $p$ and a primitive root $g$ modulo $p$.
2. **Private Key Generation (1 Mark):** Alice secretly picks $a$; Bob secretly picks $b$, both in $[2, p-2]$.
3. **Public Key Exchange (1 Mark):** Alice computes $A = g^a \bmod p$ and sends it. Bob computes $B = g^b \bmod p$ and sends it.
4. **Shared Secret Derivation (1 Mark):** Alice computes $K = B^a \bmod p$, Bob computes $K = A^b \bmod p$. Both arrive at $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$ by commutativity of exponentiation.
5. **DLP and Security (2 Marks):** The Discrete Logarithm Problem (given $g, p, A$, find $a$) is computationally intractable for $|p| \geq 2048$ bits. Best classical algorithms (Number Field Sieve) run in sub-exponential time; quantum Shor's algorithm breaks it in polynomial time, motivating the move to PQC.
6. **Diagram (1 Mark):** A clear sequence diagram showing the 3-message flow (params, $A$, $B$) and the final shared secret computation.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

Given: $p = 353$, $g = 3$, $a = 97$, $b = 233$.

**Step 1 — Compute $A = 3^{97} \bmod 353$ (2 Marks):**
Use repeated squaring. We decompose $97 = 64 + 32 + 1$ (i.e., $3^1 \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^{64}$).

- $3^1 = 3 \pmod{353}$
- $3^2 = 9 \pmod{353}$
- $3^4 = 81 \pmod{353}$
- $3^8 = 81^2 = 6561 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 18 = 6354$. $6561 - 6354 = 207$. So $3^8 \equiv 207 \pmod{353}$.
- $3^{16} = 207^2 = 42849 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 121 = 42713$. $42849 - 42713 = 136$. So $3^{16} \equiv 136 \pmod{353}$.
- $3^{32} = 136^2 = 18496 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 52 = 18356$. $18496 - 18356 = 140$. So $3^{32} \equiv 140 \pmod{353}$.
- $3^{64} = 140^2 = 19600 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 55 = 19415$. $19600 - 19415 = 185$. So $3^{64} \equiv 185 \pmod{353}$.

Now $3^{97} = 3^{64} \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^1 = 185 \cdot 140 \cdot 3 \pmod{353}$.

$185 \cdot 140 = 25900 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 73 = 25769$. $25900 - 25769 = 131$. So $185 \cdot 140 \equiv 131 \pmod{353}$.

$131 \cdot 3 = 393 \bmod 353 = 393 - 353 = 40$. So $A = 40$.

**Step 2 — Compute $B = 3^{233} \bmod 353$ (2 Marks):**
Decompose $233 = 128 + 64 + 32 + 8 + 1$.

- $3^{64} \equiv 185 \pmod{353}$ (from above)
- $3^{128} = 185^2 = 34225 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 96 = 33888$. $34225 - 33888 = 337$. So $3^{128} \equiv 337 \pmod{353}$.
- $3^{32} \equiv 140 \pmod{353}$ (from above)
- $3^8 \equiv 207 \pmod{353}$ (from above)
- $3^1 = 3 \pmod{353}$

Now $3^{233} = 3^{128} \cdot 3^{64} \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^8 \cdot 3^1 = 337 \cdot 185 \cdot 140 \cdot 207 \cdot 3 \pmod{353}$.

$337 \cdot 185 = 62345 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 176 = 62128$. $62345 - 62128 = 217$. So $337 \cdot 185 \equiv 217 \pmod{353}$.

$217 \cdot 140 = 30380 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 86 = 30358$. $30380 - 30358 = 22$. So result $\equiv 22 \pmod{353}$.

$22 \cdot 207 = 4554 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 12 = 4236$. $4554 - 4236 = 318$. So result $\equiv 318 \pmod{353}$.

$318 \cdot 3 = 954 \bmod 353$. $353 \times 2 = 706$. $954 - 706 = 248$. So $B = 248$.

**Step 3 — Compute shared secret (3 Marks):**
Alice computes $K = B^a \bmod 353 = 248^{97} \bmod 353$.
Bob computes $K = A^b \bmod 353 = 40^{233} \bmod 353$.

Both should yield **$K = 160$** (this is the standard textbook answer for these parameters). The full repeated squaring chain for $248^{97} \bmod 353$ follows the same $3^1 \cdot 3^{32} \cdot 3^{64}$ decomposition shown in Step 1, with intermediate values:

- $248^2 = 61504 \bmod 353 = 61504 - 174 \times 353 = 61504 - 61422 = 82$
- $248^4 = 82^2 = 6724 \bmod 353 = 6724 - 19 \times 353 = 6724 - 6707 = 17$
- $248^8 = 17^2 = 289$
- $248^{16} = 289^2 = 83521 \bmod 353 = 83521 - 236 \times 353 = 83521 - 83308 = 213$
- $248^{32} = 213^2 = 45369 \bmod 353 = 45369 - 128 \times 353 = 45369 - 45184 = 185$
- $248^{64} = 185^2 = 34225 \bmod 353 = 337$ (from above)

$248^{97} = 248^{64} \cdot 248^{32} \cdot 248^1 = 337 \cdot 185 \cdot 248 \pmod{353}$.

$337 \cdot 185 = 62345 \bmod 353 = 217$.
$217 \cdot 248 = 53816 \bmod 353 = 53816 - 152 \times 353 = 53816 - 53656 = 160$.

$$
\boxed{K = 160}
$$

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the **RSA-based Key Transport** mechanism with a neat diagram. Discuss the security considerations and the role of **PKCS#1 v2.2 (OAEP)** padding in preventing chosen-ciphertext attacks. **(7 Marks)**
> **(b)** In a smart-home IoT deployment, the gateway uses RSA with $p = 61$, $q = 53$, and public exponent $e = 17$. An IoT sensor must transport a 128-bit session key $K_s = 42$ to the gateway. Perform the complete key transport: compute $n$, $\phi(n)$, $d$, the ciphertext $C$, and verify decryption. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Concept (1 Mark):** RSA key transport is the most widely deployed asymmetric method for symmetric key delivery. The session key $K_s$ is encrypted with the receiver's public key $(e, n)$ and decrypted with the receiver's private key $d$.
2. **Protocol Flow (2 Marks):** (i) Sender retrieves receiver's public key from PKI/X.509 certificate. (ii) Sender computes $C = K_s^e \bmod n$. (iii) Sender transmits $C$ alongside $\text{ID}_{sender}$, timestamp, and a digital signature. (iv) Receiver computes $K_s = C^d \bmod n$. (v) Receiver verifies freshness (timestamp within window $\Delta$) and signature.
3. **PKCS#1 v2.2 / OAEP (2 Marks):** Textbook RSA ($C = M^e \bmod n$) is deterministic and vulnerable to CCA2 attacks. OAEP adds **two random oracles** (a hash chain) so that the same plaintext produces different ciphertexts each time, and any bit-flip in the ciphertext randomizes the entire plaintext on decryption — making adaptive chosen-ciphertext attacks infeasible.
4. **Security Considerations (1 Mark):** $n \geq 2048$ bits, use of OAEP, RSA-PSS for signing, key rotation, certificate revocation (CRL/OCSP), constant-time decryption to prevent side-channel leakage.
5. **Diagram (1 Mark):** Mermaid/Sequence diagram showing sender, PKI, receiver, and message arrows.

### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

Given: $p = 61$, $q = 53$, $e = 17$, $K_s = 42$.

**Step 1 — Compute modulus (1 Mark):**
$$
n = p \cdot q = 61 \cdot 53 = 3233
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Euler totient (1 Mark):**
$$
\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1) = 60 \cdot 52 = 3120
$$

**Step 3 — Compute private exponent $d$ via Extended Euclidean Algorithm (2 Marks):**
We need $d$ such that $d \cdot e \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, i.e., $17d \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$.

Apply the Extended Euclidean Algorithm:
- $3120 = 17 \cdot 183 + 9$ (since $17 \cdot 183 = 3111$, remainder $9$)
- $17 = 9 \cdot 1 + 8$
- $9 = 8 \cdot 1 + 1$
- $8 = 1 \cdot 8 + 0$

Back-substitute:
- $1 = 9 - 8 \cdot 1$
- $1 = 9 - (17 - 9) = 2 \cdot 9 - 17$
- $1 = 2 \cdot (3120 - 17 \cdot 183) - 17 = 2 \cdot 3120 - 17 \cdot 366 - 17 = 2 \cdot 3120 - 17 \cdot 367$

So $-367 \cdot 17 \equiv 1 \pmod{3120}$, hence $d \equiv -367 \equiv 3120 - 367 = 2753 \pmod{3120}$.

$$
d = 2753
$$

**Step 4 — Encrypt $K_s = 42$ (1.5 Marks):**
$$
C = K_s^e \bmod n = 42^{17} \bmod 3233
$$

Using repeated squaring: $42^2 = 1764$, $42^4 = 1764^2 = 3111696 \bmod 3233$. $3233 \times 962 = 3110146$. $3111696 - 3110146 = 1550$. So $42^4 \equiv 1550 \pmod{3233}$.

$42^8 \equiv 1550^2 = 2402500 \bmod 3233$. $3233 \times 743 = 2402119$. $2402500 - 2402119 = 381$. So $42^8 \equiv 381$.

$42^{16} \equiv 381^2 = 145161 \bmod 3233$. $3233 \times 44 = 142252$. $145161 - 142252 = 2909$. So $42^{16} \equiv 2909$.

$42^{17} = 42^{16} \cdot 42^1 = 2909 \cdot 42 = 122178 \bmod 3233$. $3233 \times 37 = 119621$. $122178 - 119621 = 2557$.

$$
C = 2557
$$

**Step 5 — Decrypt and verify (1.5 Marks):**
$$
M = C^d \bmod n = 2557^{2753} \bmod 3233
$$

By Euler's theorem, $2557^{2753} \equiv 42 \pmod{3233}$ (CRT-based optimization possible but not required). Confirming: $K_s$ recovered is $\mathbf{42}$. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting CRT optimization** in RSA decryption — for a 14-mark question, you must show **at least one optimization** (Fermat's little theorem, CRT, or the Chinese Remainder Theorem for $\bmod p$ and $\bmod q$ separately). Showing raw exponentiation loses **2 marks**.
> 2. **Skipping the timestamp and signature** in the protocol flow — full marks require the *complete* key transport protocol, not just the math.
> 3. **Missing OAEP discussion** — KTU 2024 specifically emphasizes padding schemes. A bare "RSA encryption" answer without OAEP loses **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Modular arithmetic errors in DH** — examiners are strict on showing the *intermediate* reductions. Final answer alone: **0 marks** for that step.
> 5. **Confusing "transfer" with "agreement"** — definitional confusion in Part (a) is the #1 reason students fail to cross 7/10.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Yield Summary — Memorize Before KTU Exam Day:**

- **Key Transfer** = sender controls the key and ships it (protected). **Key Agreement** = both parties jointly derive it. **Key Transport** = key inside a protected envelope.
- **Diffie-Hellman** shared secret is $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$. Security rests on the **Discrete Logarithm Problem (DLP)**. Computationally infeasible for $|p| \geq 2048$ bits.
- **ECDH** is the same idea over an elliptic curve group — yields a **point** $abG$ whose $x$-coordinate is hashed into the key. ~256-bit EC key ≈ 3072-bit RSA strength.
- **Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS)** requires **ephemeral** keys (DHE, ECDHE). TLS 1.3 mandates ECDHE — RSA key exchange is **deprecated**.
- **RSA Key Transport** formula: $C = K_s^e \bmod n$; decryption $K_s = C^d \bmod n$. Always use **OAEP padding** (PKCS#1 v2.2). Never use textbook RSA.
- **KDC / Kerberos model** uses a trusted third party distributing **tickets** — symmetric only, efficient for closed IoT networks.
- **HPKE (RFC 9180)** is the modern hybrid: combines a **KEM** (Key Encapsulation Mechanism) with a **DEM** (Data Encapsulation Mechanism) — used in TLS 1.3, MLS, and post-quantum IoT.
- **IoT Constraints** favor **ECDH-256** or **Kyber-768** (post-quantum) over RSA-2048. Bandwidth and energy budgets are 100–1000× tighter.
- **Diffie-Hellman limitations:** *No authentication*. Vulnerable to **man-in-the-middle** unless combined with signatures or pre-shared keys — this is why we have **STS (Station-to-Station)** and **TLS 1.3 signed ECDHE**.
- **Key freshness** must always be guaranteed via **nonces**, **timestamps**, or **sequence counters** — never accept a key without fresh authentication.
- **Post-Quantum (NIST 2024):** Kyber (now **ML-KEM**) for key encapsulation, Dilithium (now **ML-DSA**) for signatures — both selected for standardization and suitable for IoT key transfer.
- **NIST SP 800-57** defines key lifetimes, hierarchy (master → key-encryption → session), and destruction rules — expected to be cited in KTU theory answers.
- **Formulae to memorize cold:** $K = g^{ab} \bmod p$, $C = M^e \bmod n$, $ed \equiv 1 \pmod{\phi(n)}$, $\phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1)$, $n = pq$.
- **IoT-specific terms:** *AppKey* (LoRaWAN), *Network Session Key*, *Application Session Key* — all derived via **HKDF** from a transferred master key.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verifying Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Verifying Keys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Key Verification** is the cryptographic process of validating that a cryptographic key is *authentic*, *untampered*, *correctly generated*, and *bound to the claimed identity or purpose* before it is used in any secure communication, encryption, or authentication operation. In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)** systems, key verification is the critical pre-deployment and runtime step that prevents a node from accepting a forged, corrupted, or substituted key that an adversary could exploit to break the entire security chain.

Mathematically, key verification is a function:

$$ V : K \times M \rightarrow \{0, 1\} $$

Where $K$ is the key space, $M$ is the verification metadata (checksum, MAC tag, signature, certificate, or challenge), and the output is a Boolean decision: **1 (Valid)** or **0 (Invalid)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In IoT networks — which are inherently low-power, resource-constrained, and physically exposed — key verification is *not optional*. It is the gatekeeper that determines whether a sensor, actuator, or gateway can trust the cryptographic material it has received over an insecure channel such as BLE, LoRaWAN, Zigbee, or MQTT-over-TLS.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you receive a sealed envelope containing the password to a bank vault. Before you walk up to the vault and punch in the password, you would naturally do two things:

1. **Check the seal** — Is the envelope tampered with? (Integrity check)
2. **Check the sender** — Did this envelope really come from the bank manager, or could a forger have slipped it into your mailbox? (Authentication check)

**Key verification is exactly this "envelope check" for cryptographic keys.** The key itself is the password; the verification step ensures that the password was not corrupted in transit and that it genuinely originated from the trusted party.

A second analogy: a **biometric fingerprint on a key card**. Even if you hold the card, you wouldn't trust the door to open for you unless the fingerprint on the card matches the one stored in the door's database. A **key fingerprint** (a short hash of the key) plays this exact role in modern cryptography.

## 1.3 Why Key Verification Matters in IoT

| Threat Scenario | What Goes Wrong Without Verification |
|---|---|
| **Man-in-the-Middle (MITM)** during key exchange | Attacker substitutes their own key; victim encrypts with attacker's key |
| **Malicious firmware update** | Forged key injected into device memory; attacker decrypts all traffic |
| **Physical tampering of sensor node** | Replaced key; entire cluster compromised |
| **Replay of old key** | Stale, revoked, or expired key accepted as valid |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics in Key Verification:**
> - **Key Fingerprint Length:** Typically **128 bits (16 bytes)** for symmetric keys, **160–256 bits** for public keys (SHA-1 truncated or SHA-256).
> - **MAC Tag Length:** **64–128 bits** (e.g., CMAC, HMAC-SHA256 truncated).
> - **Verification Latency Budget (IoT):** Often **< 50 ms** on Class-1 devices (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six Pillars of Key Verification

Every key verification mechanism used in IoT cryptography falls into one of the following six categories. KTU examiners frequently test a student's ability to *classify a given method* and *state its security guarantee*.

### Pillar 1: Key Checksum / Non-Cryptographic Hash
A short fingerprint computed by hashing the key material.

$$ \text{Checksum} = H(K) $$

where $H$ is a hash function (MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, or a custom polynomial). Used to detect *accidental* corruption (bit-flips) but **NOT malicious tampering** because an attacker can recompute the checksum.

### Pillar 2: Keyed-Hash Message Authentication Code (HMAC/CMAC)
A keyed fingerprint — only the holder of a *master key* $K_M$ can produce or verify it.

$$ \text{Tag} = \text{MAC}_{K_M}(K) $$

$$ \text{Verification:} \quad \text{MAC}_{K_M}(K) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{Tag}_{\text{received}} $$

Provides **both integrity and authenticity** of the key.

### Pillar 3: Digital Signature over the Key
The trusted authority (CA / Key Distribution Center) signs the key with its private key. Verifier checks the signature using the authority's public key.

$$ \sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(K) $$

$$ \text{Verify:} \quad \text{VerSig}_{PK_{CA}}(K, \sigma) \stackrel{?}{=} \text{True} $$

### Pillar 4: Public Key Fingerprint
For asymmetric keys, the fingerprint of the *public key* is published out-of-band (e.g., printed on a device label, posted on a website, exchanged verbally).

$$ \text{Fingerprint} = \text{Truncate}_{n}(H(PK)) $$

Commonly $n = 160$ bits using SHA-1 (e.g., in SSH `ssh-keygen -lf`), or $n = 256$ bits using SHA-256 in PGP / TLS.

### Pillar 5: Challenge-Response (Interactive Verification)
The verifier sends a random nonce $N$; the prover must return a value that proves possession of the key without revealing it.

$$ \text{Prover response:} \quad R = \text{PRF}_{K}(N) \quad \text{or} \quad R = \text{MAC}_{K}(N) $$

### Pillar 6: Certificate-Based Verification (PKI / X.509)
A digitally signed certificate binds a public key to an identity. Verification chains up through a **trust anchor** (root CA).

$$ \text{Cert}_A = (\text{ID}_A, \, PK_A, \, \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(\text{ID}_A, PK_A)) $$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Verification Method | Mathematical Expression | Detects Corruption? | Detects Forgery? | Typical IoT Use Case | Output Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Key Checksum** | $C = H(K)$ | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Firmware flash integrity | 16–32 bytes |
| 2 | **HMAC of Key** | $T = \text{HMAC}_{K_M}(K)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Symmetric key provisioning | 32 bytes |
| 3 | **Digital Signature** | $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(K)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Public key infrastructure | 64–128 bytes |
| 4 | **Public Key Fingerprint** | $F = \text{Trunc}_{160}(H(PK))$ | ✅ Yes | ⚠ Partial | Device pairing, SSH | 20 bytes |
| 5 | **Challenge-Response** | $R = \text{MAC}_{K}(N)$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | Mutual authentication | 8–32 bytes |
| 6 | **X.509 Certificate** | $\text{VerSig}_{PK_{CA}}(\text{Cert})$ | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | TLS, MQTT over TLS | 1–4 KB |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** Notice that Pillar 1 (checksum) is the *only* method that detects corruption but NOT forgery. This distinction is a **favourite 3-mark question**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In deployed IoT ecosystems, key verification is the operational backbone of:

- **TLS / DTLS Handshakes** — Verifying server certificates during MQTT communication.
- **Zigbee & Z-Wave Trust Center** — Verifying the network key before joining a node.
- **LoRaWAN Join Procedure** — Verifying the `AppSKey` and `NwkSKey` derived from the `AppKey`.
- **Bluetooth LE Secure Pairing** — Just-Works, Passkey, and Out-of-Band (OOB) verification all involve key confirmation.
- **Secure Boot & Attestation** — Verifying the signing key of firmware images (matches Pillar 3).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Public Key Fingerprint (SSH-style)

The widely-deployed SSH key fingerprint is computed via the following steps.

**Step 1: Choose a hash algorithm.**
The de-facto standard for SSH is **MD5** (legacy) or **SHA-256** (modern). Let $H = \text{SHA-256}$.

**Step 2: Serialize the public key.**
The public key (e.g., RSA or Ed25519) is encoded in its canonical wire format. For an Ed25519 key:

$$
PK_{\text{bytes}} = \text{Encode}_{\text{Ed25519}}(PK) \quad \in \quad \{0,1\}^{256}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the hash digest.**

$$
D = \text{SHA-256}(PK_{\text{bytes}})
$$

**Step 4: Truncate the digest.**
For SHA-256 fingerprints, all **256 bits** are kept but rendered in Base58. For the older MD5 fingerprint, the digest is **truncated to 16 bytes (128 bits)** and rendered in hexadecimal.

$$
F_{128} = D \bmod 2^{128} \quad \text{(truncation)}
$$

**Step 5: Encode for human display.**

$$
F_{\text{display}} = \text{Base64}(D) \quad \text{or} \quad \text{Hex}(F_{128})
$$

**Verification:** The verifier independently computes $F' = \text{Hash}(PK')$ and compares:

$$
\text{Valid} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } F' = F \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: HMAC-based Key Confirmation (NIST SP 800-56A)

In a Diffie-Hellman key exchange, both parties derive a shared secret $Z$. They must then *confirm* that they have the same $Z$ *without revealing it*. The standard HMAC-based key confirmation works as follows.

**Step 1: Both parties compute the shared secret.**

$$
Z = g^{ab} \bmod p
$$

**Step 2: A key derivation function derives the working key.**

$$
K_{ab} = \text{KDF}(Z, \text{Transcript})
$$

A common construction is:

$$
K_{ab} = \text{HMAC}_{\text{salt}}(\text{TranscriptHash} \Vert \text{Counter})
$$

**Step 3: Party A (initiator) generates a confirmation tag.**

$$
\text{MacTag}_A = \text{HMAC}_{K_{ab}}(N_A \Vert \text{ID}_A \Vert \text{ID}_B)
$$

where $N_A$ is a fresh random nonce, and $\text{ID}_A, \text{ID}_B$ are the identifiers of the two parties.

**Step 4: Party B verifies the tag from A.**

$$
\text{Valid}_A = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } \text{HMAC}_{K_{ab}}(N_A \Vert \text{ID}_A \Vert \text{ID}_B) = \text{MacTag}_A^{\text{received}} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Party B reciprocates with its own tag** (mutual confirmation).

$$
\text{MacTag}_B = \text{HMAC}_{K_{ab}}(N_B \Vert \text{ID}_B \Vert \text{ID}_A)
$$

**Step 6: Party A verifies B's tag in the same manner.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this is secure:** An eavesdropper who observes $\text{MacTag}_A$ cannot derive $K_{ab}$ because HMAC is a *pseudorandom function* under standard cryptographic assumptions. An active MITM who tries to inject a different $Z$ would produce a different $K_{ab}$, and the HMAC tag check in Step 4 would fail with overwhelming probability $1 - 2^{-t}$, where $t$ is the tag length in bits.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Challenge-Response Verification of a Symmetric Key

This is the most common method in constrained IoT devices.

**Setup:** Prover (sensor node $S$) and Verifier (gateway $G$) share a symmetric key $K$ *a priori* (e.g., pre-provisioned at manufacture).

**Step 1: Verifier generates a fresh random challenge.**

$$
N \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{128}
$$

**Step 2: Prover computes the response using a keyed primitive.**

$$
R = \text{AES-CMAC}_{K}(N)
$$

**Step 3: Prover transmits $R$ to the verifier.**

**Step 4: Verifier independently computes the expected response.**

$$
R' = \text{AES-CMAC}_{K}(N)
$$

**Step 5: Verifier compares.**

$$
\text{Auth} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } R = R' \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

**Probability of an impostor passing:** $\Pr[\text{Impostor succeeds}] = 2^{-t}$, where $t$ is the MAC tag length. For CMAC-AES-128, $t = 128$, giving $\approx 3.4 \times 10^{-39}$ — cryptographically negligible.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Checksum | HMAC | Digital Signature | Fingerprint | Challenge-Resp | Certificate |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Detects bit-flips** | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Detects forgery** | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠ | ✅ | ✅ |
| **Requires shared secret** | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | ❌ (but needs trust anchor) |
| **Bandwidth overhead** | Tiny | Small | Large | Tiny | Small | Large |
| **IoT Suitability (Constrained)** | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐ | ⭐ |
| **Replay resistance** | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ (uses nonce) | ⚠ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Key Verification — Master Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Key Received] --> B{Is Key Public or Symmetric?}
    B -- Symmetric --> C[Compute MAC over Key using Master Key]
    B -- Public --> D[Fetch Certificate or Fingerprint]
    C --> E{Compare MAC Tag}
    D --> F{Verify Signature or Hash}
    E -- Match --> G[Challenge-Response Confirmation]
    E -- Mismatch --> X[REJECT Key - Log Error]
    F -- Valid --> G
    F -- Invalid --> X
    G --> H{Prover Responds Correctly?}
    H -- Yes --> I[ACCEPT Key - Store in Keystore]
    H -- No --> X
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: HMAC-based Key Verification in an IoT Gateway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SENDER[Sender / Key Distribution Center]
        KM1[Master Key Storage] --> MAC1[HMAC Engine]
        KEY[Symmetric Key K] --> MAC1
        MAC1 --> TAG[MAC Tag]
    end
    TAG --> CHAN[Insecure Channel]
    KEY --> CHAN
    subgraph RECEIVER[Receiver / IoT Node]
        KM2[Master Key Storage] --> MAC2[HMAC Engine]
        KEY2[Received Key K'] --> MAC2
        TAG2[Received Tag] --> CMP[Comparator]
        MAC2 --> CMP
        CMP --> OUT{Valid Boolean Decision}
    end
    OUT -- 1 --> KS[Keystore Insert]
    OUT -- 0 --> ERR[Error Handler]
```

## 4.3 Challenge-Response Protocol Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant V as Verifier (Gateway)
    participant P as Prover (Sensor Node)
    V->>V: Generate random nonce N
    V->>P: Send challenge N
    P->>P: Compute R = AES-CMAC_K(N)
    P->>V: Send response R
    V->>V: Compute R' = AES-CMAC_K(N)
    V->>V: Compare R == R'
    alt R matches R'
        V-->>P: ACCEPT (Key Verified)
    else Mismatch
        V-->>P: REJECT (Authentication Failed)
    end
```

## 4.4 Certificate Chain Verification (PKI for IoT)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Root CA Public Key - Trust Anchor] --> I1[Intermediate CA 1 Cert]
    I1 --> I2[Intermediate CA 2 Cert]
    I2 --> DEV[IoT Device Certificate]
    DEV --> PUBK[Device Public Key PK_dev]
    ROOT -. Verifies .-> I1
    I1 -. Verifies .-> I2
    I2 -. Verifies .-> DEV
    DEV -. Binds .-> PUBK
    PUBK --> RESULT{Chain Valid?}
    RESULT -- Yes --> TRUST[Trust PK_dev]
    RESULT -- No --> FAIL[Reject Connection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — 3 Mark Questions**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Differentiate between key checksum and HMAC-based key verification. State one limitation of using a plain key checksum.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Key Checksum | HMAC-based Verification |
|---|---|---|
| **Formula** | $C = H(K)$ | $T = \text{HMAC}_{K_M}(K)$ |
| **Secret required** | No | Yes (master key $K_M$) |
| **Detects corruption** | Yes | Yes |
| **Detects forgery** | No | Yes |

**Limitation of a plain checksum:** A checksum is computed using a *public, unkeyed* hash function. An adversary who intercepts the key $K$ can simply recompute $H(K)$ and substitute both the key and a valid-looking checksum. Hence, a plain checksum offers **integrity against accidental errors only**, not against deliberate tampering. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**What is a public key fingerprint? How is it computed and how is it typically verified in practice?**

**Model Answer:**

A **public key fingerprint** is a short, fixed-length digest of a public key, used to verify the key's identity out-of-band before it is trusted for cryptographic operations.

It is computed as:

$$ F = \text{Truncate}_{160 \text{ bits}}\bigl(\text{Hash}(PK_{\text{canonical-bytes}})\bigr) $$

The most common hash is SHA-256, with the result rendered as **Base64** (for SSH modern format) or hexadecimal (for older MD5 fingerprints).

**Verification in practice:** The verifier obtains the fingerprint through a *trusted out-of-band channel* — for example, printed on the back of an IoT device, exchanged verbally over a phone call, or published on a vendor's HTTPS-protected website — and compares it against the independently computed fingerprint of the received public key. If they match, the public key is considered authentic. **(3 marks)**

---

## **PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)**

### **Question A — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Suggested Question]**

**(a)** Explain the six major methods of cryptographic key verification with suitable mathematical expressions. Compare their security guarantees in a tabular format. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the HMAC-based key confirmation procedure used in the Diffie-Hellman key exchange (as per NIST SP 800-56A). Show the steps involved and explain why an eavesdropper or active MITM cannot defeat the scheme. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) The Six Methods of Key Verification**

1. **Key Checksum:** $C = H(K)$. Detects accidental bit errors, not forgery.
2. **HMAC of Key:** $T = \text{HMAC}_{K_M}(K)$. Provides integrity and authenticity under a shared master key.
3. **Digital Signature:** $\sigma = \text{Sign}_{SK_{CA}}(K)$. Binds the key to a trusted authority.
4. **Public Key Fingerprint:** $F = \text{Trunc}_n(H(PK))$. Used for out-of-band identification.
5. **Challenge-Response:** $R = \text{MAC}_K(N)$. Proves live possession of the key.
6. **X.509 Certificate:** $\text{VerSig}_{PK_{CA}}(\text{Cert})$. Full PKI trust chain.

The tabular comparison is given in **Section 2.3** of these notes (the analytical matrix). The student should reproduce the property-by-property table. **(7 marks — 1 mark for each method + 1 for table)**

**(b) HMAC-based Key Confirmation in DH Key Exchange**

**Step 1:** Parties A and B exchange DH public values $g^a$ and $g^b$ over an *unauthenticated* channel and both compute the shared secret:

$$ Z = g^{ab} \bmod p $$

**Step 2:** Both parties run a Key Derivation Function (KDF) over the shared secret and the protocol transcript to obtain the working MAC key:

$$ K_{ab} = \text{KDF}(Z, \text{TranscriptHash}) $$

**Step 3:** Party A generates a fresh random nonce $N_A$ and computes the confirmation tag:

$$ \text{MacTag}_A = \text{HMAC}_{K_{ab}}\bigl(N_A \Vert \text{ID}_A \Vert \text{ID}_B\bigr) $$

**Step 4:** Party B independently recomputes the same tag and compares it with the received $\text{MacTag}_A$. If the two match, A is confirmed to possess the same $K_{ab}$.

**Step 5:** Party B reciprocates with its own tag using a different nonce $N_B$. A performs the same verification, completing **mutual key confirmation**.

**Security Argument:**

- **Eavesdropper:** Sees $g^a$, $g^b$, and the MAC tag. Without solving the Computational Diffie-Hellman Problem (CDHP), the eavesdropper cannot derive $Z$, and therefore cannot compute $K_{ab}$ or forge a valid HMAC tag.
- **Active MITM:** If Mallory substitutes $g^a$ with $g^{a'}$, then A computes $Z_A = g^{a'b}$ while B computes $Z_B = g^{ab'}$, so the derived $K_{ab}$ values will differ. Any HMAC tag computed by A under $K_{ab}^A$ will not verify under $K_{ab}^B$, and the protocol aborts.
- **Replay:** Each tag uses a fresh nonce $N_A, N_B$, so a captured tag from a previous session cannot be reused. **(7 marks — 2 for steps 1-3, 2 for steps 4-5, 3 for security analysis)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the HMAC-based key verification procedure for a symmetric key received over an insecure IoT channel. Mention the role of the master key and the comparator. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a challenge-response authentication protocol for an IoT sensor node and a gateway that share a 128-bit AES key. Show all steps, state the cryptographic primitive used, and compute the probability that an impostor without knowledge of the key can successfully impersonate the sensor. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) HMAC-based Key Verification — Block Diagram Explanation**

The block diagram consists of three logical blocks: a **Sender / Key Distribution Center**, an **Insecure Channel**, and a **Receiver / IoT Node**.

- The **Sender** holds a symmetric key $K$ to be distributed and a master key $K_M$ that is pre-shared with the Receiver. The Sender computes:

$$ \text{Tag} = \text{HMAC}_{K_M}(K) $$

and transmits both $K$ and Tag over the channel.

- The **Receiver** has $K_M$ in its secure storage. Upon receiving $K'$ and Tag$'$, it independently computes:

$$ \text{Tag}'_{\text{computed}} = \text{HMAC}_{K_M}(K') $$

- A **Comparator** block checks:

$$ \text{Result} = (\text{Tag}'_{\text{computed}} == \text{Tag}') $$

- If the result is **1**, the key $K'$ is identical to the originally transmitted key $K$ (i.e., it has not been altered) and was produced by a Sender who possessed $K_M$ (i.e., it is authentic). The key is then inserted into the device's keystore.
- If the result is **0**, the key is rejected and an error is logged.

The diagram in **Section 4.2** of these notes should be reproduced. **(7 marks — 2 for the three-block diagram, 2 for the HMAC equations, 1 for the master key role, 2 for the comparator and decision logic)**

**(b) Challenge-Response Protocol Design**

**Setup:** Sensor node $S$ and gateway $G$ share a 128-bit AES key $K$ pre-provisioned at manufacture. The cryptographic primitive used is **AES-CMAC**, a NIST-standard MAC based on AES-128.

**Step 1:** Gateway $G$ generates a fresh 128-bit nonce.

$$ N \xleftarrow{\$} \{0,1\}^{128} $$

**Step 2:** $G$ transmits $N$ to the sensor node $S$.

**Step 3:** Sensor node $S$ computes the response:

$$ R = \text{AES-CMAC}_K(N) $$

**Step 4:** $S$ transmits $R$ to $G$.

**Step 5:** $G$ independently computes the expected response:

$$ R' = \text{AES-CMAC}_K(N) $$

**Step 6:** $G$ compares $R$ and $R'$.

- If equal, $G$ authenticates the sensor.
- If not equal, the authentication fails.

**Probability of Impostor Success:**

An impostor who does not know $K$ must guess the 128-bit output of AES-CMAC. The probability of a correct guess by random chance is:

$$ P_{\text{success}} = 2^{-128} \approx 2.94 \times 10^{-39} $$

This is cryptographically negligible. Even if the impostor makes $10^9$ guesses per second, the expected time to succeed exceeds the age of the universe by many orders of magnitude. **(7 marks — 1 for setup, 2 for steps, 2 for primitive, 2 for probability computation)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in Key Verification Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating integrity with authenticity** — A checksum detects *corruption* but not *forgery*. Students who say "checksum ensures the key is authentic" will lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Forgetting the nonce freshness requirement** in challenge-response — If the nonce $N$ is predictable or reused, the protocol is vulnerable to replay. KTU examiners specifically deduct for this.
> 3. **Confusing the master key with the session key** in HMAC verification — The HMAC tag is computed under the *master key* $K_M$, *not* the key $K$ being verified. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Omitting the KDF step** in DH key confirmation — Many students jump from $Z = g^{ab}$ directly to the MAC tag without showing $K_{ab} = \text{KDF}(Z, \cdot)$. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Wrong probability formula** in challenge-response — Impostor success is $2^{-t}$ where $t$ is the MAC tag length (128 for AES-CMAC), **not** $2^{-128}$ for the key length. Either is acceptable if stated explicitly, but mixing them is penalized.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Key verification** is the act of validating a cryptographic key's *integrity*, *authenticity*, and *correctness* before use.
- The **six methods** are: **C**hecksum, **H**MAC, **D**igital signature, **F**ingerprint, **C**hallenge-Response, **C**ertificate (mnemonic: **"CHF-CCC"**).
- A **plain checksum** = integrity only. An **HMAC** = integrity + authenticity (requires shared master key). A **digital signature** = integrity + authenticity + non-repudiation (requires PKI).
- **Key fingerprints** are short hashes of public keys, used for out-of-band identity verification (e.g., 160-bit truncated SHA-1 or full SHA-256 in Base64).
- In **Diffie-Hellman key confirmation**, both parties compute $K_{ab} = \text{KDF}(Z, \text{Transcript})$ and exchange HMAC tags with fresh nonces to *mutually prove* possession of the same shared secret.
- **Challenge-Response** uses $\text{MAC}_K(N)$ where $N$ is a fresh random nonce. Impostor success probability is $2^{-t}$.
- **X.509 certificates** chain trust from a Root CA through intermediates to the device's public key — used in TLS, MQTT, and most modern IoT protocols.
- IoT-specific considerations: prefer **HMAC and fingerprint methods** over heavy digital signatures for constrained devices (Class 0, Class 1) due to energy and bandwidth budgets.
- The **comparator** block in HMAC verification is the only place where the verification decision Boolean is generated — it is the heart of the verification logic.
- **Replay resistance** is *not* provided by checksums, HMACs alone, or static fingerprints — it requires a **fresh nonce** or **timestamp** mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Updating Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Updating Keys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Cryptography for IoT Applications**, **Updating Keys** (also called *Key Refreshing*, *Rekeying*, or *Key Evolution*) refers to the systematic cryptographic process of deriving new session keys, encryption keys, or authentication credentials from existing key material using one-way functions, Key Derivation Functions (KDFs), or contributory key agreement protocols. This process ensures **key freshness**, **forward secrecy**, and **backward secrecy** in resource-constrained IoT networks where static long-term keys present unacceptable security risks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Key updating is the cryptographic mechanism by which a previously established secret key is transformed or replaced with a new key, such that:
> 1. The new key cannot be feasibly derived from observed ciphertexts of the old key.
> 2. Compromise of the current key does not reveal past or future keys.
> 3. The computational and memory cost remains feasible for **Class 0, Class 1, and Class 2 IoT devices** (per RFC 7228).

## 1.2 Why Key Updating is Critical in IoT

IoT devices (smart sensors, RFID tags, wearable health monitors, industrial controllers) operate under **persistent connectivity**, are often deployed in **physically accessible environments**, and transmit **sensitive telemetry** for years without maintenance. A static key used over long durations becomes vulnerable to:
- **Brute-force cryptanalysis** over extended observation windows.
- **Side-channel attacks** (power analysis, electromagnetic emanation).
- **Insider threats** and physical capture attacks.
- **Replay attacks** exploiting old ciphertexts.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Rotating Bank Vault Code

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box — "The Hotel Key Card"**
>
> Imagine a hotel where every guest gets a key card that automatically expires after 24 hours. The card is generated from a master secret (the hotel's system) and the previous card, but **even if a thief clones today's card, tomorrow's card will be different and useless**. Furthermore, even with tomorrow's card, the thief cannot work backward to derive yesterday's card because the generation process is one-way (like crushing an egg to make an omelet — you cannot uncook it). This is exactly how **one-way key chains** work in IoT protocols like **μTESLA** and **SPINS**.

## 1.4 Three Pillars of Key Updating

| Pillar | Property | Threat Mitigated |
|---|---|---|
| **Key Freshness** | Each session uses a new key | Replay attacks |
| **Forward Secrecy** | Old keys remain secret if current key leaks | Long-term compromise |
| **Backward Secrecy** | Future keys remain secret if current key leaks | Pre-compromise attacks |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** In the **2024 Scheme**, Module 3 specifically emphasizes key updating for **lightweight IoT ciphers** (PRESENT, CLEFIA, SIMON/SPECK) and **low-power authentication protocols** (PUF-based, HMAC-based). You are expected to know the trade-off between **rekeying frequency** and **device energy budget**.

## 1.5 Categories of Key Update Mechanisms

1. **Unilateral Update (Sender-driven):** Base station generates new keys and pushes them to the IoT node.
2. **Bilateral/Contributory Update:** Both parties contribute entropy (e.g., Diffie-Hellman).
3. **Time-based Update:** Keys rotate on a fixed schedule using one-way key chains.
4. **Event-triggered Update:** Keys rotate after a threshold number of messages or error events.
5. **Hybrid Update:** Combines contributory agreement with KDF-based derivation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 One-Way Key Chain (Lamport Hash Chain) — The Foundation

A **one-way key chain** is a sequence of cryptographic keys $\{K_0, K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_n\}$ generated by repeatedly applying a **cryptographic one-way hash function** $F$ to a randomly chosen seed $K_n$.

### 2.1.1 Generation Phase (Offline, by the base station)

$$K_{i} = F(K_{i+1}) \quad \text{for } i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 0$$

Equivalently:

$$K_i = F^{(n-i)}(K_n)$$

where $F^{(k)}$ denotes $k$ successive applications of $F$, and $K_n$ is the randomly chosen initial seed.

### 2.1.2 Usage Phase (Online, by the IoT node)

The keys are released in **reverse order of generation**: $K_0$ first, then $K_1$, then $K_2, \ldots, K_n$.

- At time slot $j$, the node uses key $K_j$ to authenticate.
- Any verifier can check authenticity by applying $F$ **$j$ times** to $K_j$ and comparing with the previously disclosed $K_0$.

**Verification Equation:**

$$F^{(j)}(K_j) \stackrel{?}{=} K_0$$

## 2.2 Key Derivation Functions (KDFs)

A KDF deterministically derives one or more secret keys from a secret value (master key, password) using a Pseudo-Random Function (PRF).

### 2.2.1 Generic KDF Construction

$$\text{DerivedKey} = \text{KDF}(K_{master}, \text{Salt}, \text{ContextInfo}, L)$$

where:
- $K_{master}$: input keying material
- **Salt**: non-secret random value (defeats rainbow tables)
- **ContextInfo**: protocol/topic identifier
- $L$: required bit-length of output

### 2.2.2 HKDF (HMAC-based KDF) — RFC 5869

**Extract Phase:**

$$\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC-Hash}(\text{Salt}, \text{IKM})$$

where PRK = Pseudo-Random Key, IKM = Input Keying Material.

**Expand Phase:**

$$T(i) = \text{HMAC-Hash}(\text{PRK}, T(i-1) \Vert \text{Info} \Vert i)$$

$$\text{OKM} = T(1) \Vert T(2) \Vert \ldots \Vert T(k)$$

where OKM = Output Keying Material, truncated to length $L$.

## 2.3 Forward Secrecy & Backward Secrecy — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Forward Secrecy (PFS — Perfect Forward Secrecy):** A key agreement protocol provides forward secrecy if the compromise of long-term private keys does not compromise previously established session keys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Backward Secrecy (Future Secrecy / Post-Compromise Security):** A protocol provides backward secrecy if the compromise of a current session key does not allow an attacker to derive future session keys.

**Mathematical Statement (Forward Secrecy):**

$$\forall i < j : K_i \not\leftarrow_{\text{poly-time}} K_j \text{ (without additional info)}$$

The "poly-time" means the derivation cannot be performed in polynomial time given standard hardness assumptions.

## 2.4 Contributory Key Agreement — Diffie-Hellman Foundation

In **Diffie-Hellman key exchange**, two parties $A$ and $B$ with secret values $a$ and $b$ compute a shared session key $K_{AB}$.

### 2.4.1 Classical DH (Discrete Logarithm Setting)

**Public parameters:** Prime $p$, generator $g$ of cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$.

**Protocol:**

$$A \rightarrow B : g^a \bmod p$$
$$B \rightarrow A : g^b \bmod p$$

**Shared Secret (computed by both):**

$$K_{AB} = (g^a)^b \bmod p = (g^b)^a \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$$

### 2.4.2 ECDH (Elliptic Curve DH) — IoT Preferred

For IoT, **ECDH over NIST P-256** or **Curve25519** is preferred because it provides equivalent security to RSA with **$10\times$ smaller key sizes**, saving transmission energy.

$$K_{AB} = a \cdot B = b \cdot A \quad \text{(on elliptic curve } E \text{)}$$

where $A = aG$, $B = bG$, $G$ is the base point.

## 2.5 Rekeying in Symmetric Cryptosystems

In block ciphers (AES-128), a **session key** $K_s$ is often used with a **key-update function** that mixes in a counter or time-stamp:

$$K_s^{(i+1)} = F(K_s^{(i)} \oplus \text{Counter}_i)$$

Common constructions:
- **AES Key Wrap** (RFC 3394)
- **HKDF-based ratcheting** (used in Signal Protocol)
- **Treyfer / PRESENT-based rekeying** for ultra-lightweight IoT.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Equation | Notation / Variables | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | One-Way Key Chain Generation | $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ | $F$: hash function, $i \in \{0,\ldots,n\}$ | μTESLA, SPINS protocols |
| 2 | Key Chain Verification | $F^{(j)}(K_j) = K_0$ | $F^{(j)}$: $j$-fold iteration of $F$ | Authentication check |
| 3 | HKDF Extract | $\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC}(Salt, IKM)$ | PRK: Pseudo-Random Key | Lightweight IoT key derivation |
| 4 | HKDF Expand Block | $T(i) = \text{HMAC}(PRK, T(i-1) \Vert Info \Vert i)$ | $T(0) = \varepsilon$ (empty) | Multi-key extraction |
| 5 | Diffie-Hellman Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ | $a, b$: private exponents | Key agreement |
| 6 | ECDH Shared Secret | $K_{AB} = a \cdot b \cdot G$ | $G$: curve base point | IoT energy-efficient key exchange |
| 7 | Forward Secrecy Condition | $\Pr[K_i \mid K_j, i < j] \leq \epsilon$ | $\epsilon$ negligible | KTU 2024 Module 3 CO3 |
| 8 | Symmetric Rekeying | $K_s^{(i+1)} = H(K_s^{(i)} \Vert C_i)$ | $H$: hash, $C_i$: counter | Session key rotation |
| 9 | KDF Output Length Bound | $L \leq 255 \cdot HashLen$ | HashLen: hash output in bytes | HKDF limit |
| 10 | Lightweight Cipher Security | $S_{bits} \geq 2^{80}$ for 10-yr IoT | $S_{bits}$: effective security | KTU 2024 design constraint |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility in Real IoT Systems

- **LoRaWAN 1.1** uses two session keys: **AppSKey** (application) and **NwkSKey** (network), both derived via AES-128 CMAC-based KDF from the root key.
- **Zigbee 3.0** employs **APS Key Update** procedure with a Trust Center distributing updated network keys to all devices.
- **Bluetooth LE Secure Connections** uses **ECDH-P256** for initial pairing, then rekeying via **LE SC Reconnection**.
- **Thread / Matter** protocols use **ECJ-PAKE** (Password-Authenticated Key Exchange) for password-based key updates.
- **Industrial IoT (OPC-UA)** uses **RSA-OAEP + ECDH hybrid** for session key establishment with perfect forward secrecy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: One-Way Key Chain Construction

**Setup:** Let $n = 5$, $F = \text{SHA-256}$ (truncated to 128 bits for IoT), and choose a random seed $K_5$.

### Step 1 — Seed Selection (Base Station, offline)

$$K_5 = \text{Random}(128 \text{ bits}) = \texttt{0xA3F1...C7B2} \quad \text{(example)}$$

The base station stores $K_5$ in secure memory. The IoT node is pre-loaded with $K_0$.

### Step 2 — Chain Generation (Iterative Hashing)

Compute the chain backwards from $K_5$ to $K_0$:

$$K_4 = F(K_5) = \text{SHA-256}(K_5) \text{ truncated to 128 bits}$$

$$K_3 = F(K_4) = \text{SHA-256}(K_4) \text{ truncated to 128 bits}$$

$$K_2 = F(K_3) = \text{SHA-256}(K_3) \text{ truncated to 128 bits}$$

$$K_1 = F(K_2) = \text{SHA-256}(K_2) \text{ truncated to 128 bits}$$

$$K_0 = F(K_1) = \text{SHA-256}(K_1) \text{ truncated to 128 bits}$$

**Storage at IoT node:** Only $K_0$ is pre-loaded. The other keys are derived when needed.

### Step 3 — Key Release (Online, at time slot $j$)

At time slot $j$, the base station broadcasts key $K_j$ in a **delayed-disclosure** MAC packet.

**At time slot $j=2$:**

$$\text{Node authenticates as: } \text{MAC}_{K_2}(M \Vert \text{timestamp})$$

**Verification at receiver:**

$$F^{(2)}(K_2) = F(F(K_2)) = F(K_1) = K_0 \quad \checkmark$$

The receiver checks whether applying $F$ twice to the received $K_2$ yields the pre-shared $K_0$.

### Step 4 — Key Update After Compromise (Forward Secrecy Proof)

Suppose $K_3$ is leaked at time $t > 3$. The attacker computes:

$$F(K_3) = K_2, \quad F(K_2) = K_1, \quad F(K_1) = K_0$$

**Therefore**, all **past** keys $\{K_0, K_1, K_2\}$ are now exposed. This is a **limitation** of pure hash chains — they provide only **backward secrecy in one direction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Resolution:** To achieve **bidirectional secrecy** (both forward and backward), modern IoT protocols use **Merkle Trees with multiple branches** (TESLA++) or **ratcheting mechanisms** (Double Ratchet Algorithm used in Signal/Messaging IoT apps).

## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: HKDF for IoT Sensor Networks

**Scenario:** A temperature sensor (Class 1 IoT device) needs to derive three sub-keys from a master key $MK$ for different functions: encryption ($K_{enc}$), authentication ($K_{mac}$), and integrity ($K_{int}$).

### Step 1 — Extract Phase

**Inputs:**
- IKM = $MK$ (32 bytes, the master pre-shared key)
- Salt = $\text{DeviceID} \Vert \text{NetworkID}$ (16 bytes)

**Computation:**

$$\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(Salt, IKM)$$

This produces a 32-byte Pseudo-Random Key.

### Step 2 — Expand Phase for Three Sub-Keys

We need three 16-byte keys: $K_{enc}, K_{mac}, K_{int}$.

**Block $T(1)$ — generates $K_{enc}$:**

$$T(1) = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(\text{PRK}, \text{Info}_1 \Vert \texttt{0x01})$$

where $\text{Info}_1 = \texttt{"enc-key-v1"}$.

Take the first 16 bytes: $K_{enc} = T(1)[0:16]$.

**Block $T(2)$ — generates $K_{mac}$:**

$$T(2) = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(\text{PRK}, T(1) \Vert \text{Info}_2 \Vert \texttt{0x02})$$

where $\text{Info}_2 = \texttt{"mac-key-v1"}$.

Take the first 16 bytes: $K_{mac} = T(2)[0:16]$.

**Block $T(3)$ — generates $K_{int}$:**

$$T(3) = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(\text{PRK}, T(2) \Vert \text{Info}_3 \Vert \texttt{0x03})$$

where $\text{Info}_3 = \texttt{"int-key-v1"}$.

Take the first 16 bytes: $K_{int} = T(3)[0:16]$.

### Step 3 — Security Argument

Each $T(i)$ is a fresh HMAC output, so $K_{enc}, K_{mac}, K_{int}$ are **computationally independent** under the standard HMAC-PRF assumption. Compromise of $K_{enc}$ does not reveal $K_{mac}$ or $K_{int}$.

## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: ECDH Key Agreement (Lightweight IoT Scenario)

**Setup:** Two IoT devices $A$ (sensor) and $B$ (actuator) using **Curve25519** (an IoT-friendly curve with 128-bit security).

**Public parameters:** Curve $E: y^2 = x^3 + 486662 x^2 + x$ over $\mathbb{F}_{2^{255}-19}$, base point $G$ of order $\ell = 2^{252} + 27742317777372353535851937790883648493$.

### Step 1 — Private Key Generation

$$a \leftarrow_R \{1, 2, \ldots, \ell - 1\}$$

$$b \leftarrow_R \{1, 2, \ldots, \ell - 1\}$$

(Each device picks a random 32-byte private scalar.)

### Step 2 — Public Key Exchange

$$A \text{ computes } A_{pub} = a \cdot G \quad \text{(scalar multiplication)}$$

$$B \text{ computes } B_{pub} = b \cdot G \quad \text{(scalar multiplication)}$$

Both $A_{pub}$ and $B_{pub}$ are 32-byte curve points, transmitted in clear over the IoT link.

### Step 3 — Shared Secret Computation

$$K_{AB}^A = a \cdot B_{pub} = a \cdot b \cdot G$$

$$K_{AB}^B = b \cdot A_{pub} = b \cdot a \cdot G$$

By the commutativity of scalar multiplication:

$$K_{AB}^A = K_{AB}^B = K_{AB} = abG$$

### Step 4 — Key Derivation from Shared Point

The x-coordinate of the shared point is fed into a KDF:

$$K_{session} = \text{HKDF-Extract}(\text{Salt} = \text{NULL}, \text{IKM} = K_{AB}.x)$$

$$K_{enc}, K_{mac} = \text{HKDF-Expand}(K_{session}, \text{Info}, 64 \text{ bytes})$$

### Step 5 — Rekeying for New Session

For session $i+1$, both devices use new private scalars $a', b'$:

$$a' \leftarrow_R \{1, \ldots, \ell-1\}, \quad b' \leftarrow_R \{1, \ldots, \ell-1\}$$

**Forward Secrecy Guarantee:** If the attacker later compromises $a$ (long-term key), they cannot compute $a' b' G$ without also knowing $b'$, which was never transmitted. Hence, the **past session key $K_{AB} = abG$ remains secure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Cost Note for KTU:** ECDH on Curve25519 requires only **~1 million cycles** on an 8-bit AVR (ATmega256RFR2) — equivalent to **~0.5 mJ** at 3V, making it feasible for battery-powered IoT nodes (lifetime > 5 years on 2× AA batteries).

## 3.4 Security Analysis Matrix: Comparison of Key Update Schemes

| Scheme | Forward Secrecy | Backward Secrecy | IoT Energy Cost | Memory Cost | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Pre-Shared Key (PSK)** | ❌ None | ❌ None | **0** (none) | 16–32 B | Test bench only |
| **One-Way Hash Chain (Lamport)** | ❌ None (past revealed) | ✅ Yes (future safe) | **Low** (one hash) | $O(\log n)$ with checkpoint | Time-sync'd broadcast auth (μTESLA) |
| **HKDF-based Rekeying** | ⚠️ Conditional on master | ✅ Yes (with new salt) | **Low** (one HMAC) | 32 B | Multi-purpose sub-key derivation |
| **ECDH (one-shot)** | ✅ Yes (per session) | ✅ Yes (per session) | **Medium** (EC scalar mult) | 32–64 B | Pairwise device auth |
| **ECDH + Double Ratchet** | ✅ Yes (per message) | ✅ Yes (per message) | **High** | 64+ B | Messaging-grade IoT apps |
| **PUF-based Rekeying** | ✅ Yes (no stored secret) | ✅ Yes (regenerated) | **Medium** (PUF eval) | 0 B (no key storage!) | Tamper-resistant sensors |
| **Group Key (GKA)** | ✅ Yes (for new members) | ✅ Yes (for removed) | **High** | $O(\log n)$ | Industrial mesh networks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 One-Way Key Chain Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Seed K_n Random 128 bits"] --> B["Apply Hash F to get K_n-1"]
    B --> C["Apply Hash F to get K_n-2"]
    C --> D["Apply Hash F to get K_n-3"]
    D --> E["Apply Hash F to get K_n-4"]
    E --> F["Apply Hash F to get K_n-5 equals K_0 pre-shared"]
    
    F --> G["Pre-load K_0 into IoT Sensor at factory"]
    A --> H["Base station stores all K_i securely"]
    
    H --> I["Time slot 1: release K_1"]
    I --> J["Sensor uses K_1 for MAC"]
    J --> K["Receiver verifies F K_1 equals K_0"]
    
    H --> L["Time slot 2: release K_2"]
    L --> M["Sensor uses K_2 for MAC"]
    M --> N["Receiver verifies F applied twice to K_2 equals K_0"]
    
    H --> O["Time slot n: release K_n"]
    O --> P["Sensor uses K_n for final MAC"]
    P --> Q["Receiver verifies F applied n times to K_n equals K_0"]
    
    Q --> R["Chain exhausted: trigger rekeying protocol via ECDH or KDF"]
```

## 4.2 HKDF Extract-and-Expand Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EXTRACT["HKDF EXTRACT PHASE"]
        IKMIN["IKM Input Keying Material 32 bytes"] --> HMAC1["HMAC-SHA-256"]
        SALTIN["Salt non-secret 16 bytes"] --> HMAC1
        HMAC1 --> PRKOUT["PRK Pseudo Random Key 32 bytes"]
    end
    
    subgraph EXPAND["HKDF EXPAND PHASE"]
        PRKOUT --> HMAC2A["HMAC-SHA-256 Block T1"]
        INFO1["Info1 enc-key-v1"] --> HMAC2A
        HEX1["0x01 counter byte"] --> HMAC2A
        HMAC2A --> KENC["K_enc 16 bytes"]
        
        PRKOUT --> HMAC2B["HMAC-SHA-256 Block T2"]
        HMAC2A --> HMAC2B
        INFO2["Info2 mac-key-v1"] --> HMAC2B
        HEX2["0x02 counter byte"] --> HMAC2B
        HMAC2B --> KMAC["K_mac 16 bytes"]
        
        PRKOUT --> HMAC2C["HMAC-SHA-256 Block T3"]
        HMAC2B --> HMAC2C
        INFO3["Info3 int-key-v1"] --> HMAC2C
        HEX3["0x03 counter byte"] --> HMAC2C
        HMAC2C --> KINT["K_int 16 bytes"]
    end
```

## 4.3 ECDH Rekeying Interaction Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sensor as IoT Sensor A
    participant Actuator as IoT Actuator B
    participant Cloud as Cloud Server

    Note over Sensor,Actuator: Session N begins
    
    Sensor->>Sensor: Generate private a
    Actuator->>Actuator: Generate private b
    
    Sensor->>Actuator: Send A_pub = a * G
    Actuator->>Sensor: Send B_pub = b * G
    
    Sensor->>Sensor: Compute K_AB = a * B_pub
    Actuator->>Actuator: Compute K_AB = b * A_pub
    
    Note over Sensor,Actuator: Both derive K_session via HKDF
    
    Sensor->>Actuator: Encrypted telemetry with K_session
    
    Note over Sensor,Actuator: Session N ends
    
    Note over Sensor,Actuator: Session N+1 begins new ECDH exchange
    
    Sensor->>Sensor: Generate new private a_prime
    Actuator->>Actuator: Generate new private b_prime
    
    Sensor->>Actuator: Send A_pub_prime = a_prime * G
    Actuator->>Sensor: Send B_pub_prime = b_prime * G
    
    Note over Sensor,Actuator: Forward secrecy preserved even if a is later leaked
```

## 4.4 Key Update Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PreLoaded: Factory provisioning
    PreLoaded --> ActiveSession: First HKDF or ECDH derivation
    ActiveSession --> ActiveSession: Continue using K_session
    ActiveSession --> KeyRotationTrigger: Counter expires or message threshold reached
    ActiveSession --> Compromised: Intrusion detected
    
    KeyRotationTrigger --> Rekeying: Initiate ECDH or HKDF refresh
    Compromised --> EmergencyRekey: Force new contributory agreement
    
    Rekeying --> ActiveSession: New K_session established
    EmergencyRekey --> ActiveSession: New K_session with new long-term key
    
    ActiveSession --> [*]: Device decommissioned
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define "one-way key chain" as used in IoT authentication protocols. Mention the direction of key release and the verification equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A one-way key chain is a sequence of cryptographic keys $\{K_0, K_1, \ldots, K_n\}$ generated by repeatedly applying a one-way hash function $F$ to a random seed $K_n$, such that $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$ for $i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 0$.

**Key Release Direction:** Keys are released in the **reverse order** of generation, i.e., $K_0$ is used first, followed by $K_1, K_2, \ldots, K_n$. This is because the receiver is pre-loaded with $K_0$ (the tail of the chain) and verifies each subsequent key by applying $F$ iteratively.

**Verification Equation:** At time slot $j$, the receiver verifies the disclosed key $K_j$ by checking:

$$F^{(j)}(K_j) = K_0$$

where $F^{(j)}$ denotes $j$ successive applications of $F$. This is used in **μTESLA** and **SPINS** protocols for broadcast authentication in sensor networks.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between forward secrecy and backward secrecy in the context of key updating.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Forward Secrecy | Backward Secrecy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Compromise of the *current/long-term* key does not reveal **past** session keys. | Compromise of the *current* session key does not reveal **future** session keys. |
| **Also Called** | Perfect Forward Secrecy (PFS) | Post-Compromise Security (PCS) / Future Secrecy |
| **Achieved By** | Ephemeral key exchange (ECDH, DHE) with new randomness per session. | One-way functions (hash chains, KDFs) that prevent reversing to future keys. |
| **IoT Example** | LoRaWAN 1.1 session key derivation with new device nonce. | μTESLA one-way hash chain (attacker with $K_j$ cannot compute $K_{j+1}$). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Forward and backward secrecy are **complementary** — a robust IoT protocol must provide **both** to withstand compromise at any point in the key lifecycle.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: With suitable mathematical formulation, explain the construction and verification of a one-way key chain. Discuss its application in the μTESLA protocol for IoT broadcast authentication.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A one-way key chain is generated by applying a cryptographic hash function $F$ repeatedly to a randomly chosen seed $K_n$.

$$K_i = F(K_{i+1}) \quad \text{for } i = n-1, n-2, \ldots, 0$$

The total number of keys is $n+1$.

**Step 2 — Generation Phase (2 marks):**
The base station (sender) generates the chain offline. It picks $K_n$ uniformly at random and computes the chain in the backward direction. Only $K_0$ is disclosed to the receiver initially (pre-loaded into each IoT node at deployment time).

**Step 3 — Usage and Verification Phase (2 marks):**
At time interval $j$, the base station uses key $K_j$ to compute a Message Authentication Code (MAC) over the broadcast packet. The receiver buffers the packet, and at time $j + \delta$ (where $\delta$ is the disclosure delay), the base station reveals $K_j$. The receiver verifies by checking:

$$F^{(j)}(K_j) = K_0$$

If the equation holds, the MAC computed with $K_j$ is authentic; otherwise, the packet is rejected.

**Step 4 — Application in μTESLA (2 marks):**
μTESLA (micro Timed Efficient Stream Loss-tolerant Authentication) is a lightweight broadcast authentication protocol for sensor networks. It uses one-way key chains to achieve **asymmetric authentication using only symmetric primitives** (HMAC), making it feasible for resource-constrained IoT devices. The trade-off is the **delayed disclosure** — packets are not authenticated in real time but only after the key is revealed. This delay is the security parameter $\delta$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Describe the HKDF (HMAC-based Key Derivation Function) construction. Show how it is used to derive multiple sub-keys for an IoT device that needs separate keys for encryption, MAC, and integrity.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Why HKDF? (1 mark):**
IoT devices often need multiple distinct cryptographic keys (for AES-CTR encryption, HMAC authentication, and integrity checks) but should not store them all. HKDF allows **on-demand derivation** from a single master secret, reducing memory footprint.

**Step 2 — Extract Phase (2 marks):**

$$\text{PRK} = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(\text{Salt}, \text{IKM})$$

- IKM (Input Keying Material) = master pre-shared key (e.g., 32 bytes from provisioning).
- Salt = non-secret value (e.g., $\text{DeviceID} \Vert \text{NetworkID}$), 16 bytes.
- Output PRK = 32-byte strong pseudo-random key.

**Step 3 — Expand Phase (2 marks):**
For each sub-key, compute an HMAC block:

$$T(i) = \text{HMAC-SHA-256}(\text{PRK}, T(i-1) \Vert \text{Info}_i \Vert i)$$

with $T(0) = \text{empty string}$.

For three sub-keys:
- $K_{enc} = T(1)[0:16]$, where $\text{Info}_1 = \texttt{"enc-key-v1"}$.
- $K_{mac} = T(2)[0:16]$, where $\text{Info}_2 = \texttt{"mac-key-v1"}$.
- $K_{int} = T(3)[0:16]$, where $\text{Info}_3 = \texttt{"int-key-v1"}$.

**Step 4 — Security Property (2 marks):**
Under the HMAC-PRF assumption, each $T(i)$ is a fresh independent pseudo-random block. Compromise of $K_{enc}$ does not reveal $K_{mac}$ or $K_{int}$. The output length is bounded by $L \leq 255 \cdot 32 = 8160$ bytes (per RFC 5869).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Q: Explain contributory key agreement using the Diffie-Hellman protocol. How is ECDH preferred for IoT applications? Derive the shared secret computation.**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Protocol Setup (1 mark):**
Let $p$ be a large prime, $g$ a generator of the multiplicative group $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. Two parties $A$ and $B$ choose private secrets $a$ and $b$ uniformly from $\{1, 2, \ldots, p-2\}$.

**Step 2 — Public Value Exchange (2 marks):**

$$A \rightarrow B : g^a \bmod p \quad (\text{denoted as } A_{pub})$$

$$B \rightarrow A : g^b \bmod p \quad (\text{denoted as } B_{pub})$$

These are transmitted over the (insecure) IoT link.

**Step 3 — Shared Secret Computation (2 marks):**

$$A \text{ computes: } K_{AB} = (B_{pub})^a \bmod p = g^{ba} \bmod p$$

$$B \text{ computes: } K_{AB} = (A_{pub})^b \bmod p = g^{ab} \bmod p$$

Both obtain the same shared secret $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$ due to the commutativity of modular exponentiation. An eavesdropper sees only $g^a$ and $g^b$; computing $g^{ab}$ requires solving the **Computational Diffie-Hellman (CDH)** problem, which is believed to be hard.

**Step 4 — Why ECDH for IoT? (2 marks):**
ECDH (Elliptic Curve Diffie-Hellman) operates on points of an elliptic curve $E(\mathbb{F}_q)$ rather than in $\mathbb{Z}_p^*$. With **256-bit ECDH**, security equivalent to **3072-bit DH** is achieved, drastically reducing:
- Transmission size (32 bytes vs 384 bytes).
- Computation time on 8-bit/32-bit microcontrollers.
- Energy consumption (critical for battery-powered IoT).

Curves like **Curve25519** and **NIST P-256** are recommended for IoT deployments.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Q: Discuss the security properties of rekeying mechanisms. What are the trade-offs between rekeying frequency and energy budget in IoT nodes?**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Security Properties (3 marks):**
A rekeying mechanism should provide:
- **Key Freshness:** Each new key must be unpredictable from previous keys.
- **Forward Secrecy:** Past keys remain secure if the current key is compromised.
- **Backward Secrecy:** Future keys remain secure if the current key is compromised.
- **Key Independence:** Different applications (encryption vs authentication) should use cryptographically independent sub-keys.
- **Authentication of New Keys:** A new key must be bound to its source to prevent MITM injection.

**Step 2 — Rekeying Strategies (2 marks):**
- **Time-based:** Rotate every $T$ seconds (e.g., Zigbee default = 24 hours).
- **Counter-based:** Rotate every $N$ messages (e.g., TLS 1.3 limit = $2^{24.5}$ records).
- **Volume-based:** Rotate after $V$ MB of ciphertext (e.g., IPsec ESP).
- **Event-based:** Rotate on suspicious activity (failed auth, replay detection).
- **Hybrid:** Combination of the above.

**Step 3 — Energy Trade-off Analysis (2 marks):**
For a battery-powered IoT sensor (e.g., 2× AA = 2.5 Ah at 3V, ~27000 J total energy budget):

- One ECDH key exchange: $\approx 0.5$ mJ.
- One HKDF derivation: $\approx 0.05$ mJ.
- One AES rekeying: $\approx 0.01$ mJ.

If the device performs ECDH rekeying every session (say 1000 sessions/day), it consumes $1000 \times 0.5 = 500$ mJ/day, giving a battery life of $27000 / 500 = 54$ days. **Trade-off:** Frequent rekeying improves security but reduces battery life. **KTU 2024 design heuristic:** Rekeying should be event-triggered or volume-based rather than time-based for ultra-low-power IoT.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Direction of Key Chain (2-mark loss):** Many students write the chain in the wrong direction, e.g., $K_{i+1} = F(K_i)$ instead of $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$. The chain must be generated **backward** from a random seed, and released **forward** to the receiver. Mark this carefully — the equation is worth at least 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Confusing Forward and Backward Secrecy (2-mark loss):** Forward secrecy is about **PAST** keys being safe if the **CURRENT** key leaks. Backward secrecy is about **FUTURE** keys being safe if the **CURRENT** key leaks. Do not reverse these definitions.
>
> 3. **HKDF Counter Byte (1-mark loss):** The byte counter in $T(i)$ starts at $0x01$ (not $0x00$) for the first block. Writing $0x00$ will lose a mark.
>
> 4. **ECDH vs DH Mix-up (3-mark loss):** In ECDH, the operation is **scalar multiplication** ($a \cdot G$) on the curve, not modular exponentiation. Writing $g^{ab} \bmod p$ in an ECDH question is a conceptual error.
>
> 5. **Skipping Verification Step (1-mark loss):** When explaining one-way key chains, always include the verification equation $F^{(j)}(K_j) = K_0$. The KTU examiner allocates explicit marks for the verification step.
>
> 6. **No Diagram Penalty:** In 14-mark questions, a labeled diagram (even a hand-drawn one) of the key chain is worth **1–2 marks**. Always include one.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Updating Keys**
>
> - **One-Way Key Chain:** Generated as $K_i = F(K_{i+1})$; released in **reverse order**; verified by $F^{(j)}(K_j) = K_0$.
> - **HKDF (RFC 5869):** Two-phase (Extract + Expand); uses HMAC-SHA-256; counter starts at $0x01$; max output $255 \times 32$ bytes.
> - **Forward Secrecy (PFS):** Compromise of long-term key ⇒ past session keys still secure. Achieved by **ECDH/DHE** with new randomness per session.
> - **Backward Secrecy (PCS):** Compromise of current session key ⇒ future keys still secure. Achieved by **one-way functions** (hash chains, KDF).
> - **DH Shared Secret:** $K_{AB} = g^{ab} \bmod p$; relies on **CDH hardness** assumption.
> - **ECDH Shared Secret:** $K_{AB} = a \cdot B = b \cdot A = abG$ on elliptic curve; 256-bit ECDH ≈ 3072-bit DH security.
> - **μTESLA:** Uses one-way key chains for **asymmetric broadcast authentication** with only symmetric primitives; has $\delta$-delayed verification.
> - **LoRaWAN 1.1:** Derives AppSKey and NwkSKey via AES-128 CMAC from root key.
> - **Zigbee 3.0:** Trust Center pushes new network key during APS key update.
> - **Trade-off:** Rekeying frequency ↔ Energy budget; for ultra-low-power IoT, prefer **event-triggered** or **volume-based** rekeying over time-based.
> - **PUF-based Rekeying:** Eliminates key storage; ideal for tamper-resistant IoT sensors.
> - **Key Independence:** Different functions (enc, mac, int) MUST use independent keys — derive via HKDF or separate master keys.
> - **Memory Tip:** Remember **"E before X"** in HKDF = **Extract** before **Expand**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storing Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Storing Keys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Key Storage** refers to the set of mechanisms, hardware components, software protocols, and cryptographic procedures used to securely retain cryptographic key material (symmetric keys, private keys, certificates, and secrets) throughout its operational lifecycle — generation, active use, dormancy, archival, and destruction. In the context of IoT (Internet of Things), key storage is a sub-discipline of **key management** that addresses the unique constraints of resource-constrained embedded devices, including limited non-volatile memory, low computational power, and exposure to physical tampering.

According to NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5, cryptographic keys must be protected against unauthorized disclosure, modification, substitution, and use. For IoT endpoints, this protection is realized through a layered architecture combining **hardware roots of trust (RoT)**, **tamper-resistant memory**, **key wrapping**, and **access control mechanisms**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
