> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCIT602 / Module 3):**
> Key storage is the *bridge* between key generation (Module 2) and key usage/application. The KTU board frequently tests the difference between *volatile* and *non-volatile* storage, and the role of *Hardware Security Modules (HSM)* in IoT contexts.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vault Inside the Lock"

Imagine you live in a small studio apartment (the IoT device). You have a very valuable diamond (the cryptographic key). You cannot afford a giant bank vault (an enterprise HSM cluster), but you also cannot leave the diamond on your kitchen table (plain RAM).

So you do three things:
1. You buy a small, **tamper-resistant wall safe bolted into the concrete** (a Secure Element or TPM chip).
2. You **wrap the diamond in multiple layers of protective foam** before placing it inside (key wrapping — encrypting the key with another key, the KEK).
3. You keep the **combination to the safe in a separate hidden drawer** that only opens when you authenticate yourself with a PIN or biometric (access control / split knowledge).

This is, in essence, what key storage does in IoT: protect a small, precious secret inside a small, hardened, access-controlled enclosure.

### 1.3 Why Key Storage is Harder in IoT

| IoT Constraint | Impact on Key Storage |
|---|---|
| **8-bit / 32-bit MCUs** | Cannot run heavy OS-level key vaults (e.g., Keychain, KWallet) |
| **< 1 MB Flash memory** | No room to store long-term key archives |
| **Battery-powered nodes** | Volatile key loss on power-down if not persisted correctly |
| **Physically exposed devices** | Adversary can perform side-channel or fault injection attacks |
| **Long field deployment (10+ years)** | Keys must remain secure against future quantum & classical advances |

### 1.4 Categories of Key Storage

Keys may be stored in **four principal locations**, ordered from strongest to weakest in terms of resistance to physical and logical attacks:

1. **Hardware Security Modules (HSM)** — dedicated cryptographic processors.
2. **Secure Elements (SE) / Trusted Platform Modules (TPM)** — tamper-resistant chips.
3. **Encrypted Software Storage** — keys encrypted with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) and stored in Flash/EEPROM.
4. **Plaintext Volatile Memory (RAM)** — acceptable only for ephemeral session keys.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of IoT Key Storage:** *A key should never exist in plaintext outside of a tamper-resistant boundary for longer than the duration of its immediate cryptographic operation.*

### 1.5 Key Material Classification (for storage policy)

| Key Type | Typical Storage Location | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|
| Root Key / Master Key | Hardware RoT (eFuse, HSM) | Device lifetime |
| Key Encryption Key (KEK) | Secure Element / TPM NVRAM | Years |
| Device Identity Private Key | SE / TPM | Device lifetime |
| Session Keys (TLS, DTLS) | Volatile RAM (wiped after use) | Seconds to hours |
| Application Data Keys | Wrapped, in Flash | Variable |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Key Lifecycle and Storage Touchpoints

A cryptographic key passes through several lifecycle phases. Storage is required at every **persistent** transition:

1. **Generation** → key briefly exists in a register/buffer.
2. **Wrapping** → key is encrypted with KEK and stored to non-volatile memory (NVM).
3. **Active Use** → key is unwrapped into volatile memory (RAM/registers).
4. **Dormancy** → wrapped key remains in NVM.
5. **Rotation** → old key archived (wrapped) or destroyed.
6. **Destruction** → memory zeroized (overwritten with zeros/random).

### 2.2 Hardware-Based Key Storage

#### 2.2.1 Hardware Security Module (HSM)

A **Hardware Security Module** is a physical computing device that safeguards and manages digital keys, performs encryption/decryption operations, and provides strong authentication. In IoT, **microHSMs** (e.g., Microchip ATECC608B, NXP SE050) are integrated as companion chips.

**Key properties:**
- **FIPS 140-2 / FIPS 140-3** certified (Level 1 to Level 4).
- **Tamper-evident** (Level 2) or **tamper-resistant** (Level 3+).
- Hardware-accelerated crypto (AES, ECC, SHA, RSA).
- Secure True Random Number Generator (TRNG).

#### 2.2.2 Trusted Platform Module (TPM)

A **TPM** is a microcontroller standardized by ISO/IEC 11889 (TPM 1.2) and ISO/IEC 62443 (TPM 2.0). It provides:
- **Platform Integrity** via PCRs (Platform Configuration Registers).
- **Endorsement Key (EK)** — unique per device, burnt in at manufacture.
- **Storage Root Key (SRK)** — root of an encrypted key hierarchy.
- **Sealing** — binding a key to specific PCR values (only unwrappable if platform state matches).

#### 2.2.3 Secure Element (SE)

Common in **smart cards, embedded SIMs (eUICCs), and payment systems**. Form factors include:
- **Discrete SE chip** (e.g., NXP SE050, Infineon SLE 97).
- **Integrated SE** — built into the SoC (Apple Secure Enclave, Google Titan M2).

#### 2.2.4 eFuses and One-Time Programmable (OTP) Memory

**eFuses** are microscopic electrical fuses that can be blown once to permanently store a bit. Used for:
- Storing the device's **root key** or **identity hash**.
- Anti-rollback protection (preventing firmware downgrade).
- **Disadvantage**: cannot be updated; subject to reverse-engineering via electron microscopy.

### 2.3 Software-Based Key Storage

When no hardware RoT is available, software mechanisms are used:

#### 2.3.1 Key Wrapping

**Key Wrapping** is the encryption of one key (the *target* or *data key*) with another key (the *Key Encryption Key* or KEK). The result is a *wrapped key* that can be safely stored in untrusted memory.

$$
\text{WrappedKey} = E_{KEK}(\text{TargetKey} \parallel \text{IntegrityTag})
$$

NIST-standardized wrapping algorithm: **AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394)** and **AES Key Wrap with Padding (RFC 5649)**.

#### 2.3.2 Trusted Execution Environment (TEE)

A **TEE** is an isolated processing environment (e.g., ARM TrustZone, Intel SGX) running alongside a Rich OS. Secrets stored inside the TEE are protected even if the OS is compromised.

#### 2.3.3 Encrypted Filesystems and Key Vaults

- **Linux keyring** (`keyctl`), **Windows DPAPI**, **Android Keystore**, **Apple Keychain**.
- In IoT, lightweight equivalents like **Mbed TLS PSA Crypto API**, **WolfCrypt**, or **TinyCrypt** provide abstracted key storage.

### 2.4 Attack Vectors Against Stored Keys

| Attack | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Cold Boot Attack** | DRAM retains bits after power removal; freeze and read | Use SRAM with rapid decay; encrypt RAM contents |
| **Memory Dump / JTAG** | Read Flash or RAM via debug interface | Disable JTAG in production; lock debug ports |
| **Side-Channel (SPA/DPA)** | Power/EM analysis to extract keys | Constant-time algorithms; hardware masking |
| **Fault Injection** | Voltage/clock glitch to bypass security checks | Voltage glitch detectors; redundant computation |
| **Software Exploit** | Buffer overflow in key-handling code | Memory-safe APIs; formal verification |
| **Physical Probing** | Depackage chip and probe bus lines | Tamper meshes; mesh sensors |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the *most important* quantitative relationships that KTU examiners expect students to be able to write down and apply in an exam.

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Symmetric key entropy | $H(K) = \log_2(\vert S \vert)$ where $\vert S \vert$ = key space size | bits |
| 2 | AES-128 security level | $2^{128}$ brute-force operations | operations |
| 3 | AES key length vs. brute-force cost | $C = 2^{n}$ for $n$-bit key | operations |
| 4 | RSA modulus equivalent security | RSA-3072 $\approx$ AES-128 | NIST SP 800-57 |
| 5 | ECC key size equivalence | secp256r1 $\approx$ AES-128 $\approx$ RSA-3072 | NIST guidance |
| 6 | Memory size for $n$-bit key | $M = \lceil n / 8 \rceil$ bytes | bytes |
| 7 | Quantum (Grover) reduction | $2^{n/2}$ for symmetric; $2^{n/2}$ for hash pre-image | operations |
| 8 | Storage write endurance (Flash) | $10^{4}$ to $10^{6}$ cycles per cell | P/E cycles |
| 9 | SRAM data retention (no power) | $\approx 100$ ms to several seconds at room temperature | seconds |
| 10 | TPM PCR extension | $\text{PCR}_i^{\text{new}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{PCR}_i^{\text{old}} \parallel \text{new\_value})$ | hash state |

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Smart Meters (Electricity/Gas):** Each meter stores its identity private key in a SE to prevent cloning and billing fraud.
- **Connected Vehicles:** The car's **Secure Hardware Extension (SHE)** or **Hardware Security Module (HSM)** stores the master key for ECU-to-ECU communication.
- **Medical IoT (Pacemakers, Insulin Pumps):** FDA mandates tamper-resistant key storage to prevent malicious reconfiguration.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** OPC UA deployments use TPM 2.0 for endpoint authentication in factory floors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Entropy and Storage Size of a Symmetric Key

**Problem:** A symmetric cipher uses keys drawn uniformly from a set $S$ of size $\vert S \vert = 2^{128}$. Compute (a) the entropy of one key, and (b) the minimum number of bytes of non-volatile memory required to store the key.

**Step 1 — Identify the probability distribution.**

A uniformly random 128-bit key has probability $p(k) = \dfrac{1}{2^{128}}$ for every $k \in S$.

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's entropy formula.**

Shannon entropy is given by:

$$
H(X) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(x_i) \log_2 p(x_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the uniform distribution.**

$$
H(K) = -\sum_{i=1}^{2^{128}} \frac{1}{2^{128}} \log_2 \left( \frac{1}{2^{128}} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the logarithm.**

$$
\log_2 \left( \frac{1}{2^{128}} \right) = \log_2(1) - \log_2(2^{128}) = 0 - 128 = -128
$$

**Step 5 — Resolve the sum.**

$$
H(K) = -2^{128} \cdot \frac{1}{2^{128}} \cdot (-128) = 128 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to bytes for storage.**

$$
\text{Bytes} = \frac{H(K)}{8} = \frac{128}{8} = 16 \;\text{bytes}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 128-bit AES key has 128 bits of entropy and requires exactly **16 bytes** of NVM.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Storage Overhead of AES Key Wrapping (RFC 3394)

**Problem:** You wrap a 128-bit data key $KD$ using a 128-bit KEK. What is the total storage size of the wrapped key?

**Step 1 — Recall the AES Key Wrap algorithm.**

The AES Key Wrap of an $n$-bit plaintext key produces a ciphertext of $n + 64$ bits.

For our case, the input plaintext key length is $n = 128$ bits.

**Step 2 — Apply the length-expansion formula.**

$$
\text{WrappedSize} = n + 64
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{WrappedSize} = 128 + 64 = 192 \;\text{bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
\text{WrappedSize} = \frac{192}{8} = 24 \;\text{bytes}
$$

**Conclusion:** Wrapping a 16-byte AES-128 key consumes **24 bytes** of storage — a 50% overhead — which provides the **Integrity Check Value (ICV)** that detects tampering.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Storage Technologies for IoT

| Storage Technology | Volatile? | Tamper Resistance | Cost (USD, indicative) | Typical Use in IoT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **SRAM** | Yes | None | < $0.01 | Session keys |
| **DRAM** | Yes (refreshed) | Low | < $0.50 | Edge gateways |
| **Flash (NOR/NAND)** | No | Low | $0.05 – $1.00 | Firmware, wrapped keys |
| **EEPROM** | No | Low | $0.10 – $0.50 | Calibration, small keys |
| **eFuse (OTP)** | No | Medium (irreversible) | $0.01 – $0.10 | Root keys, anti-rollback |
| **TPM 2.0** | No | High (Common Criteria EAL4+) | $1.00 – $5.00 | PCs, gateways |
| **Secure Element** | No | Very High (EAL5+/6+) | $0.50 – $3.00 | Smart cards, IoT endpoints |
| **Embedded HSM** | No | Very High (FIPS 140-2 L3) | $5.00 – $50.00 | Vehicles, industrial |
| **TEE (TrustZone)** | Mixed (RAM-backed) | Medium-High | Bundled in SoC | Smartphones, edge AI |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Key Hierarchy in TPM 2.0

A TPM 2.0 organizes keys in a hierarchy. We trace how a *storage key* (used to wrap other keys) is created:

**Step 1 — TPM has a fixed *Storage Root Key (SRK)* generated internally at first use.**

**Step 2 — The user generates a child key $K_{\text{child}}$ under the SRK.**

$$
K_{\text{child}} \;\text{is created via}\;\text{TPM2_CreatePrimary} \;\text{or}\; \text{TPM2_Create}
$$

**Step 3 — The child key is *wrapped* by its parent before being exported.**

$$
\text{WrappedChild} = E_{\text{Parent}}(\text{ChildPrivKey} \parallel \text{IntegrityTag})
$$

**Step 4 — The wrapped child is stored in NVM (Flash), and only the parent reference is kept inside the TPM.**

This means the **parent (SRK) never leaves the TPM** — only wrapped blobs do. Even if Flash is dumped, the attacker sees only ciphertext.

**Step 5 — To use the child key, the TPM imports the wrapped blob, unwraps it internally, and performs the crypto operation. The cleartext key never leaves the chip.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the *core insight* behind hardware-backed key storage: the boundary of trust is the chip itself, not the host CPU.

---

### 3.5 Security Level Equivalence Table (NIST SP 800-57)

For exam purposes, KTU expects students to know the following equivalences:

| Security Strength (bits) | Symmetric | RSA / DH | ECC |
|---|---|---|---|
| **80** | 2TDEA | RSA-1024 (deprecated) | — |
| **112** | 3TDEA | RSA-2048 | — |
| **128** | AES-128 | RSA-3072 | secp256r1 / Curve25519 |
| **192** | AES-192 | RSA-7680 | secp384r1 |
| **256** | AES-256 | RSA-15360 | secp521r1 |

This table is critical when answering questions on **key length selection** for IoT deployments.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchical Key Storage Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HSM["Hardware Root of Trust (Secure Element / TPM)"]
        RK["Root Key (eFuse / FROZEN)"]
        EK["Endorsement Key (EK)"]
        SRK["Storage Root Key (SRK)"]
    end

    RK --> KEK["Key Encryption Key (KEK)"]
    EK --> IDK["Device Identity Private Key"]
    SRK --> WK1["Wrapped Session Key 1"]
    SRK --> WK2["Wrapped Session Key 2"]
    SRK --> WK3["Wrapped Session Key 3"]

    KEK -.encrypts.-> WK1
    KEK -.encrypts.-> WK2
    KEK -.encrypts.-> WK3

    WRAM["Volatile RAM (Working Memory)"] --> SSK["Active Session Key (Plaintext, Short-Lived)"]
    WK1 -.unwrap on demand.-> SSK
    WK2 -.unwrap on demand.-> SSK
    WK3 -.unwrap on demand.-> SSK

    FLASH["Encrypted Flash Storage"] --> WK1
    FLASH --> WK2
    FLASH --> WK3
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Root Key, EK, and SRK** never leave the hardware boundary.
- **Wrapped keys** sit in plain Flash, but are useless without the KEK.
- **Session keys** are dynamically unwrapped into RAM and zeroized after use.

---

### 4.2 Key Lifecycle Flow (Mermaid State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Generated
    Generated --> Wrapped : AES Key Wrap with KEK
    Wrapped --> Stored : Written to NVM Flash
    Stored --> Unwrapped : Authenticated Access Request
    Unwrapped --> Active : Plaintext in RAM
    Active --> Wrapped : Session Ends / Power Down
    Active --> Zeroized : Emergency Wipe
    Stored --> Rotated : Periodic Key Rotation
    Rotated --> Wrapped : New Version Stored
    Stored --> Destroyed : End of Life
    Zeroized --> [*]
    Destroyed --> [*]
```

---

### 4.3 Layered Trust Model (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Layer 1: Hardware Root of Trust\neFuse, HSM, TPM, SE\n(FIPS 140-2 Level 3+)"]
    L2["Layer 2: TEE / Secure Boot\nARM TrustZone, Intel SGX"]
    L3["Layer 3: Wrapped Key Store\nEncrypted Flash / Keyring"]
    L4["Layer 4: Volatile RAM\nSession Keys (Short-Lived)"]
    L5["Layer 5: Application\nCrypto APIs (PSA, Mbed TLS)"]

    L1 --> L2 --> L3 --> L4 --> L5

    APP["Application Calls crypto API"] --> L5
    ATTACK["Attacker"] -.tries to attack.-> L1
    ATTACK -.tries to attack.-> L2
    ATTACK -.tries to attack.-> L3
    ATTACK -.tries to attack.-> L4
```

**Interpretation:**
- An attacker must defeat **all layers in sequence** to extract a key.
- The hardware RoT (Layer 1) is the most expensive to defeat — typically requiring decapping, FIB (Focused Ion Beam), or other lab-grade equipment.

---

### 4.4 Comparison: Volatile vs. Non-Volatile Storage Trade-offs

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Need to Store a Key?"] --> B{"Used for a single session?"}
    B -- Yes --> C["Volatile RAM\nFast, auto-erased on reboot\nNot suitable for long-term"]
    B -- No --> D{"Hardware Secure Element available?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["SE / TPM / HSM\nBest Practice for IoT"]
    D -- No --> F{"Is the MCU flash accessible?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Wrap with KEK\nStore in Flash"]
    F -- No --> H["Re-evaluate design\nUnprotected storage not recommended"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
*Define key wrapping. Why is it essential for secure key storage in IoT devices?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Key wrapping is the process of encrypting a cryptographic key (the *data key*) with another key (the *Key Encryption Key, KEK*) to produce a *wrapped key* that can be safely stored in untrusted non-volatile memory.

**Mechanism (1 mark):** NIST standard AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394) encrypts the target key and appends a 64-bit Integrity Check Value, producing output of length $n + 64$ bits.

**Importance in IoT (1 mark):** IoT devices lack trusted storage; keys in plaintext Flash can be extracted via JTAG or memory dumps. Wrapping ensures the key remains confidential and tamper-evident even if the storage medium is compromised. The KEK itself is held in a hardware root of trust (e.g., Secure Element).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Compare TPM 2.0 and a generic Secure Element in terms of their use for IoT key storage.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | TPM 2.0 | Secure Element (SE) |
|---|---|---|
| **Standardization** | ISO/IEC 11889, TCG specs | Often proprietary, though Common Criteria certified |
| **Primary Use** | Platform integrity + key storage | Identity, payment, IoT authentication |
| **Key Hierarchy** | Yes (SRK → child keys) | Application-specific |
| **Interface** | TCG command set (TPM2_*) | ISO 7816, I²C, SPI |
| **Form Factor** | Discrete chip or integrated | Discrete chip or integrated |
| **IoT Suitability** | Higher for PCs/gateways | Higher for constrained sensors/edge devices |

Both provide **tamper resistance** and **hardware-backed key storage** but differ in interface and primary use case.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choices (14 Marks each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the different mechanisms used to securely store cryptographic keys in resource-constrained IoT devices. Discuss the role of Hardware Security Modules (HSM), Secure Elements, and key wrapping in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the TPM 2.0 key hierarchy in detail. How does the Storage Root Key (SRK) protect child keys, and what happens when the platform state changes? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) The Problem of Key Storage in IoT (2 marks):**
IoT devices operate under severe constraints — limited non-volatile memory (often < 1 MB), low computational power (8/32-bit MCUs), battery operation, and physical exposure. Storing keys in plaintext Flash is unsafe because an attacker with a JTAG debugger or a chip-off forensic tool can read memory directly.

**(ii) Hardware Security Module (HSM) (2 marks):**
An HSM is a tamper-resistant hardware device dedicated to cryptographic operations and key management. In IoT, **microHSMs** (e.g., NXP SE050, Microchip ATECC608B) integrate as companion chips. They:
- Generate keys internally using a hardware TRNG.
- Never expose private keys in plaintext to the host CPU.
- Provide FIPS 140-2 / Common Criteria certified security.

**(iii) Secure Element (SE) (1.5 marks):**
An SE is a tamper-resistant chip (often with EAL5+ certification) used for identity and authentication. SEs are ubiquitous in SIM cards (eUICCs), payment cards, and passports. In IoT, they store the device's identity private key, ensuring clone-resistance.

**(iv) Key Wrapping (1.5 marks):**
When no SE is available, keys are *wrapped* — encrypted with a KEK — and stored in Flash. The KEK itself is stored more securely (e.g., in eFuse or derived from a PUF). AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394) is the standard. The wrapped blob has length $n + 64$ bits, providing both confidentiality and integrity.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks**

**(i) TPM 2.0 Key Hierarchy (3 marks):**
The TPM maintains a tree of keys, rooted at three primary seeds:
- **Endorsement Hierarchy** — rooted at the Endorsement Key (EK), used for identity.
- **Platform Hierarchy** — rooted at the Platform Primary Key, controlled by the platform firmware.
- **Storage Hierarchy** — rooted at the **Storage Root Key (SRK)**, used for wrapping and storing user keys.

Each child key is generated under a parent via `TPM2_Create()` and is automatically wrapped by the parent before being exported. The cleartext child key never leaves the TPM.

**(ii) Role of the SRK (2 marks):**
The SRK is the root of the storage hierarchy. It is a 2048-bit RSA or P-256 ECC asymmetric key generated and held inside the TPM. All wrapped child keys (file system keys, application keys, etc.) are encrypted to the SRK's public part. Only the TPM, holding the private SRK, can unwrap them. This means an attacker dumping the Flash gets only ciphertext, useless without the TPM-resident SRK.

**(iii) Sealing and Platform State (2 marks):**
TPM 2.0 supports **sealing** — binding a key unwrap operation to specific Platform Configuration Register (PCR) values. The PCRs record the system boot state (e.g., firmware hashes). If the boot state changes (e.g., attacker installs malicious firmware), the PCRs change, and the sealed key cannot be unwrapped. The PCR is updated via the formula:

$$
\text{PCR}_i^{\text{new}} = \text{SHA256}(\text{PCR}_i^{\text{old}} \parallel \text{new\_measurement})
$$

This **measured boot + sealed storage** prevents offline attacks where an adversary boots a tampered OS to extract keys.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT device's secure key storage subsystem. How does key wrapping complement hardware roots of trust? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major attack vectors against stored keys in IoT environments. For each attack, state a specific mitigation technique. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks**

**(i) Architecture Diagram (3 marks):**

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  IoT Device Hardware                              |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  |  Secure Element / TPM                      |  |
|  |  - Endorsement Key (EK) [Locked inside]    |  |
|  |  - Storage Root Key (SRK) [Locked inside]  |  |
|  |  - KEK (Derived from SRK) [Locked inside]  |  |
|  +------------------+-------------------------+  |
|                     | wrap/unwrap (I2C/SPI)     |
|  +------------------v-------------------------+  |
|  |  MCU + Flash                                |  |
|  |  - Wrapped Identity Key  [Flash, encrypted]|  |
|  |  - Wrapped Session Keys  [Flash, encrypted]|  |
|  |  - Wrapped Data Keys     [Flash, encrypted]|  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  |  Volatile RAM                               |  |
|  |  - Active Session Key (plaintext, ephemeral)|  |
|  |  - Zeroized on power-down / session end    |  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

**(ii) Hardware Root of Trust (2 marks):**
The SE/TPM acts as the trust anchor. The KEK never leaves the chip; all wrapped blobs in Flash are useless without it. This is the **hardware root of trust**.

**(iii) Role of Key Wrapping (2 marks):**
Key wrapping allows the *untrusted* bulk storage (Flash) to safely hold many keys. The MCU can request the TPM to unwrap a specific key for a specific operation; the plaintext exists only inside the TPM's internal memory and the session RAM for the duration of the operation, after which it is zeroized.

---

**Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks**

| Attack Vector (1 mark each) | Mitigation (≈ 0.4 mark each) |
|---|---|
| **Cold Boot Attack**: DRAM retains data after power removal. | Use SRAM with fast decay; encrypt RAM contents with a key that is itself lost on power-down. |
| **JTAG / Debug Port Access**: Physical access to read Flash. | Permanently disable JTAG fuses in production; lock debug ports via OTP bits. |
| **Side-Channel Analysis (SPA/DPA)**: Power/EM traces reveal key bits. | Use constant-time crypto; hardware masking; randomize computation order. |
| **Fault Injection (Voltage/Clock Glitching)**: Causes erroneous crypto operations. | Voltage/clock glitch detectors; dual-rail logic; redundant computation. |
| **Software Exploits**: Buffer overflows in key-handling code. | Use memory-safe APIs (PSA Crypto); formal verification; code audits. |
| **Physical Deprobing / FIB**: Decapsulate chip and probe bus lines. | Tamper meshes; mesh sensors; package-level destruction on intrusion. |
| **Rollback / Replay Attacks**: Reuse old wrapped keys. | Use monotonic counters in TPM; store generation numbers alongside keys. |

*(Total: 7 marks — 1 mark per attack + 0.4 mark × 5 mitigations for the first 5, with the rest given 0.5 mark each as per KTU marking.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing KEK with SRK** — The KEK is used to wrap/unwrap data keys; the SRK is the *root* of the TPM hierarchy. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this mix-up.
> 2. **Stating "keys are stored in encrypted form" without specifying *how* (algorithm, key length, integrity tag).** Always name the algorithm: *AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394)*, and the integrity mechanism: *64-bit ICV*.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention zeroization.** Examiners expect that students know keys in RAM must be actively overwritten after use, not merely forgotten.
> 4. **Ignoring IoT constraints** — suggesting "use a full enterprise HSM" for a 32-bit sensor MCU. KTU wants IoT-appropriate solutions (microHSM, SE, key wrapping).
> 5. **Missing the PCR/sealing mechanism** in TPM questions — the link between boot state and key release is a favorite 2-mark question.
> 6. **Not converting bits to bytes** correctly when calculating storage sizes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Key Storage** protects cryptographic key material throughout its lifecycle.
- **Four storage tiers** (strongest → weakest): HSM → SE/TPM → Wrapped Flash → Plaintext RAM.
- **Hardware Roots of Trust** (HSM, SE, TPM, eFuse) form the foundation; everything else is built on top.
- **Key Wrapping** (AES-KW, RFC 3394) is the standard way to store keys in untrusted memory — adds **64 bits of integrity tag**.
- **TPM 2.0** uses a hierarchy rooted at the SRK; child keys are wrapped to the parent.
- **Sealing** binds keys to platform state via PCRs — keys become unrecoverable if boot state changes.
- **TEEs** (TrustZone, SGX) provide a software-layer boundary between rich OS and secret material.
- **Attack vectors:** cold boot, JTAG, side-channel, fault injection, physical probing, software exploits.
- **Mitigations:** tamper meshes, constant-time code, eFuse-disabled debug, monotonic counters, secure boot.
- **NIST equivalence table** (AES-128 ≈ RSA-3072 ≈ secp256r1) is essential for key length selection.
- **IoT-specific** choices: microHSMs (ATECC608B), SEs (NXP SE050), and TPM 2.0 for gateways.
- **Golden rule:** *A key should never exist in plaintext outside a tamper-resistant boundary longer than the operation requires.*
- **Bit-to-byte conversion:** $\text{Bytes} = \lceil n / 8 \rceil$ — used constantly in storage calculations.
- **Wrapped key size formula:** $n + 64$ bits (where $n$ = plaintext key length).
- **Zeroization** is mandatory after use — examiners expect this phrase explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backup Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Backup Keys

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **IoT Cryptography and Key Management**, **Backup Keys** refer to the secure, redundant copies of cryptographic keys (symmetric, private, or master keys) that are preserved in protected secondary storage so that encrypted data, devices, or communications can be recovered when the original operational key is **lost, corrupted, physically destroyed, or unintentionally erased**.

According to **NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Revision 5**, key backup is a sanctioned cryptographic operation where a key is securely stored in a form that allows **legitimate recovery** but not active operational use. It is classified as a **Cryptographic Key Management** lifecycle phase, sitting between *Key Generation* and *Key Destruction*.

In IoT environments, backup keys are particularly critical because:
- **End-node devices** (sensors, actuators, wearables) are frequently deployed in unattended, hostile, or physically accessible locations.
- IoT nodes have **limited non-volatile memory** and often suffer from firmware updates, power loss, or hardware failure.
- **Scalability**: Millions of devices make per-device key recovery infeasible without systematic backup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 - Key Management):** Backup keys form a *recovery* strategy distinct from *escrow* and *archival*. A backup allows the *owner* to recover the key; escrow allows a *third party* (court, government, employer) to recover it.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **house with a digital smart lock** (the IoT device). The smart lock requires a secret **PIN** (the cryptographic key) to open. If you forget this PIN, you are permanently locked out — even though the house is yours.

So, you do two things:
1. You **store a copy of the PIN inside a sealed envelope** inside a fireproof safe in your own bedroom (this is **Key Backup**).
2. You **split the PIN into 5 paper slips** and give one slip each to 5 trusted friends, telling them *"any 3 of you together can reconstruct my PIN, but 1 or 2 cannot"* (this is **Secret Sharing**, a popular mathematical backup technique).

**Backup keys** = the sealed envelope in your safe.
**Secret Sharing** = the 5 paper slips among friends.

Both protect you from lockout, but in different mathematical and trust structures.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** A *backup* is for the key *owner*; an *escrow* is for a *trusted third party*. In KTU examinations, this distinction is a frequently tested 2-mark concept.

### 1.3 Why Backup Keys are Critical in IoT

| Risk Scenario | Impact without Backup Key |
|---|---|
| Sensor node loses NVM contents due to power surge | Permanent loss of sensor data encryption key |
| Wearable device's Secure Element (SE) is reset | All user health data becomes unreadable forever |
| Smart meter firmware update wipes TPM | Utility cannot decrypt billing telemetry |
| Industrial IoT gateway hardware failure | Factory floor loses control of all PLC commands |

**Key Lifecycle Position** (per NIST SP 800-57): Generation → Registration → Distribution → **Backup** → Storage → Use → Archival → Destruction.

### 1.4 Categories of Backup Mechanisms

1. **Plaintext Backup** (INSECURE — discouraged): Direct copy stored in a Hardware Security Module (HSM).
2. **Wrapped Backup** (STANDARD): Key encrypted under a Key Encryption Key (KEK) and stored.
3. **Split-Knowledge / M-of-N Backup** (ADVANCED): Key split into *n* shares; any *m* can reconstruct.
4. **Hardware-Backed Backup**: TPM 2.0 *Sealed Storage*, Secure Element (SE) with anti-tamper.
5. **Cloud-Assisted Backup**: Wrapped key uploaded to encrypted cloud (e.g., AWS KMS, Azure Key Vault).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metrics:**
> - **NIST SP 800-57 Pt. 1 Rev. 5** (2020) — Key Management Recommendations.
> - **NIST SP 800-152** — A Profile for U.S. Federal CKMS.
> - **RFC 5869** — HKDF (used for deriving backup keys from a master).
> - **Shamir, A. (1979)** — *How to Share a Secret*, Communications of the ACM.
> - **TPM Library Specification 2.0** (TCG).

### 1.5 Physical and Cryptographic Constants Referenced

- **Shamir's Threshold**: A polynomial of degree *t-1* is used, requiring exactly *t* of *n* shares for reconstruction.
- **NIST Key Strength Symmetry** (SP 800-57): A **128-bit** symmetric key provides **128 bits of security**; a **256-bit** key provides **256 bits**.
- **Common IoT KEK Sizes**: AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 keys used as Key Encryption Keys.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle of Key Backup

The core principle of any cryptographic backup is the **separation of *operational use* from *recovery capability***. A live operational key performs encryption/decryption in real-time. A backup key is **never used operationally** — it exists purely for *recovery*.

The backup procedure involves four sub-operations:

1. **Wrapping** (Encryption under KEK): The operational key $K_{op}$ is encrypted using a Key Encryption Key $K_{KEK}$:

$$ C_{backup} = E_{K_{KEK}}(K_{op}) $$

2. **Storage**: $C_{backup}$ is stored in a non-volatile, access-controlled medium (HSM, TPM NVRAM, encrypted cloud).

3. **Authentication of Recovery Agent**: Before unwrapping, the requester proves identity via multi-factor authentication.

4. **Unwrapping (Recovery)**: The authorized user retrieves $C_{backup}$ and decrypts:

$$ K_{op} = D_{K_{KEK}}(C_{backup}) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why wrap the key at all?** A plaintext backup in a file is a *single point of compromise*. Wrapping forces an attacker to break *two* independent cryptographic boundaries — the storage layer *and* the KEK layer.

### 2.2 The Four Major Backup Schemes (Comparative Analysis)

#### A. Key Wrapping (KW / KWP per RFC 3394 / RFC 5649)
- **Mechanism**: AES in a special mode (KW for 64-bit blocks, KWP for arbitrary length).
- **Output**: A wrapped key that is *integrity-protected* (authenticated) and *confidentiality-protected*.
- **Use Case**: Backing up an AES-128 device key under a master KEK stored in an HSM.

$$ C = KW_{K_{KEK}}(K_{op}) $$

The AES Key Wrap algorithm uses an **IV = A6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6** and is fully specified in **RFC 3394**.

#### B. Key Escrow vs. Key Backup (Distinction)
- **Escrow**: Third party (e.g., government, corporate IT) holds the key. *Used in criminal/court investigation contexts.*
- **Backup**: The *owner* (or organization) holds the wrapped key. *Used in business continuity and disaster recovery.*

| Property | Backup | Escrow |
|---|---|---|
| Holder | Key owner | Trusted Third Party (TTP) |
| Purpose | Disaster recovery | Legal/mandatory access |
| KTU Mark Weightage | High | Medium |
| Example | Backing up corporate signing key | FBI wiretapping (Clipper Chip) |

#### C. Secret Sharing (Shamir's Scheme)
The key $K$ is split into $n$ shares such that any $m$ of $n$ can reconstruct $K$, but $m-1$ or fewer reveal **zero** information.

#### D. Hardware-Backed Backup (TPM 2.0 Sealing)
TPM 2.0 allows **sealing** a key to specific PCR (Platform Configuration Register) values. The key can only be unsealed when the device is in a known software state, binding the backup to a *trusted platform configuration*.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Description | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| Key Wrapping | $C = E_{K_{KEK}}(K_{op})$ | Encrypts operational key under KEK | NIST SP 800-57 |
| Key Unwrapping | $K_{op} = D_{K_{KEK}}(C)$ | Recovers original operational key | NIST SP 800-57 |
| Shamir Polynomial | $f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1}$ | Secret $a_0$ hidden in a degree-$(t-1)$ polynomial | Shamir 1979 |
| Secret Recovery | $a_0 = f(0) = \sum_{i=1}^{t} y_i \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i}$ | Lagrange Interpolation | Shamir 1979 |
| Number of Shares | $n$ | Total shares distributed | — |
| Threshold | $t$ | Minimum shares required to reconstruct | — |
| Security Condition | $t \leq n$ and $t-1$ shares give no information | Information-theoretic security | — |
| KEK Hierarchy | $Master \rightarrow KEK \rightarrow DEK$ | Three-tier key hierarchy for backup | NIST SP 800-57 |
| Sealed Storage | $K_{sealed} = E_{K_{TPM\_SRK}}(K, PCR_0, PCR_1, \ldots)$ | Binds key to platform state | TCG TPM 2.0 |
| AES Key Wrap IV | $IV = 0xA6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6$ | Initialization Vector for RFC 3394 KW | RFC 3394 |
| HKDF Backup Derivation | $K_{backup} = HKDF(salt, IKM, info)$ | Derives backup key from master | RFC 5869 |
| Cryptoperiod (Symmetric) | $\leq 2^{32}$ blocks for AES-128 in CBC | Lifetime constraint for backup reuse | NIST SP 800-38A |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In Shamir's scheme, $f(0) = a_0$ where $a_0$ is the *secret* (the key being backed up). All other coefficients $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{t-1}$ are randomly chosen in $\mathbb{Z}_p$ (a prime field).

### 2.4 Why These Schemes Matter in IoT Engineering

- **Scalability**: Split-knowledge backup enables *distributed trust* across millions of IoT devices without central key exposure.
- **Resilience**: A factory with 1000 sensor nodes does not need 1000 separate recovery procedures — a single 3-of-5 scheme suffices per gateway cluster.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: GDPR, HIPAA, and India's DPDP Act 2023 mandate *recoverability* of encrypted personal data → backup keys are *not optional*.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: SCADA systems need 24/7 availability; loss of the HSM-stored key would halt production. Wrapped backups enable **key rotation without re-encrypting** the entire data lake.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Shamir's (t, n) Secret Sharing for Backup Keys

**Problem Setup:** Suppose an IoT gateway's master key is $K = a_0 = 42$ (in some prime field). We want to back it up using a **(3, 5) scheme** — i.e., split into 5 shares, any 3 of which can reconstruct the secret, but no 2 can.

**Step 1: Choose a prime field.** Pick a large prime $p > K$. For pedagogical clarity, let $p = 61$ (a prime greater than 42).

**Step 2: Choose polynomial degree.** A $(t, n) = (3, 5)$ scheme requires a polynomial of degree $t - 1 = 2$:

$$ f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 \pmod{p} $$

**Step 3: Set the secret as the constant term.** $a_0 = K = 42$.

**Step 4: Choose random coefficients** $a_1$ and $a_2$ in $\mathbb{Z}_{61}$. Let $a_1 = 17$ and $a_2 = 23$.

**Step 5: Form the polynomial:**

$$ f(x) = 42 + 17x + 23x^2 \pmod{61} $$

**Step 6: Compute 5 shares** by evaluating $f(x)$ at $x = 1, 2, 3, 4, 5$:

$$ f(1) = 42 + 17(1) + 23(1) = 42 + 17 + 23 = 82 \equiv 82 - 61 = 21 \pmod{61} $$

$$ f(2) = 42 + 17(2) + 23(4) = 42 + 34 + 92 = 168 $$

$$ 168 \pmod{61} : \quad 61 \times 2 = 122, \quad 168 - 122 = 46 \implies f(2) \equiv 46 \pmod{61} $$

$$ f(3) = 42 + 17(3) + 23(9) = 42 + 51 + 207 = 300 $$

$$ 300 \pmod{61} : \quad 61 \times 4 = 244, \quad 300 - 244 = 56 \implies f(3) \equiv 56 \pmod{61} $$

$$ f(4) = 42 + 17(4) + 23(16) = 42 + 68 + 368 = 478 $$

$$ 478 \pmod{61} : \quad 61 \times 7 = 427, \quad 478 - 427 = 51 \implies f(4) \equiv 51 \pmod{61} $$

$$ f(5) = 42 + 17(5) + 23(25) = 42 + 85 + 575 = 702 $$

$$ 702 \pmod{61} : \quad 61 \times 11 = 671, \quad 702 - 671 = 31 \implies f(5) \equiv 31 \pmod{61} $$

**The 5 shares are:**

$$ (x_1, y_1) = (1, 21), \quad (x_2, y_2) = (2, 46), \quad (x_3, y_3) = (3, 56), \quad (x_4, y_4) = (4, 51), \quad (x_5, y_5) = (5, 31) $$

Each share is given to a different administrator. **Any 2 shares alone** cannot reconstruct the secret (infinite possible polynomials pass through 2 points).

**Step 7: Recovery using Lagrange Interpolation.** Suppose the three administrators holding shares 1, 3, and 5 come together: $(1, 21), (3, 56), (5, 31)$. We reconstruct $f(0) = a_0 = K$.

The Lagrange basis polynomials are:

$$ L_1(x) = \frac{(x - 3)(x - 5)}{(1 - 3)(1 - 5)} = \frac{(x-3)(x-5)}{(-2)(-4)} = \frac{(x-3)(x-5)}{8} $$

$$ L_3(x) = \frac{(x - 1)(x - 5)}{(3 - 1)(3 - 5)} = \frac{(x-1)(x-5)}{(2)(-2)} = \frac{(x-1)(x-5)}{-4} $$

$$ L_5(x) = \frac{(x - 1)(x - 3)}{(5 - 1)(5 - 3)} = \frac{(x-1)(x-3)}{(4)(2)} = \frac{(x-1)(x-3)}{8} $$

**Computing each at $x = 0$:**

$$ L_1(0) = \frac{(-3)(-5)}{8} = \frac{15}{8} $$

In $\mathbb{Z}_{61}$, the modular inverse of 8 is required: $8^{-1} \pmod{61}$. Solve $8k \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$. Trying $k = 23$: $8 \times 23 = 184 = 3 \times 61 + 1 = 184$. So $8^{-1} \equiv 23 \pmod{61}$.

$$ L_1(0) = 15 \times 23 = 345 \pmod{61} = 345 - 5(61) = 345 - 305 = 40 \pmod{61} $$

$$ L_3(0) = \frac{(-1)(-5)}{-4} = \frac{5}{-4} $$

Modular inverse of $-4 \pmod{61}$: $-4 \equiv 57 \pmod{61}$. Solve $57 k \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$. Note $-4 \times (-46) = 184 \equiv 1 \pmod{61}$. So $-4^{-1} \equiv -46 \equiv 15 \pmod{61}$.

$$ L_3(0) = 5 \times 15 = 75 \pmod{61} = 75 - 61 = 14 \pmod{61} $$

$$ L_5(0) = \frac{(-1)(-3)}{8} = \frac{3}{8} = 3 \times 23 = 69 \pmod{61} = 69 - 61 = 8 \pmod{61} $$

**Reconstruct the secret:**

$$ f(0) = y_1 L_1(0) + y_3 L_3(0) + y_5 L_5(0) $$

$$ f(0) = (21)(40) + (56)(14) + (31)(8) \pmod{61} $$

$$ = 840 + 784 + 248 = 1872 \pmod{61} $$

$$ 61 \times 30 = 1830, \quad 1872 - 1830 = 42 $$

$$ \boxed{f(0) \equiv 42 \pmod{61}} $$

**The secret $K = 42$ is perfectly recovered.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verifiability Check:** If we had used shares $(1,21), (2,46), (4,51)$ instead, we would also recover $K = 42$. This is the **uniqueness property of polynomial interpolation** through $t$ points.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Choosing a Backup Scheme for IoT

| IoT Scenario | Recommended Backup Scheme | Threshold | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Wearable health device | Hardware SE with wrapped backup | 1-of-1 | Single user recovery |
| Smart home gateway | Secret Sharing | 2-of-3 | User + manufacturer + service tech |
| Industrial SCADA | Key Wrapping under HSM KEK | 1-of-1 | Centralized HSM is the trust anchor |
| Smart meter network | Secret Sharing | 3-of-5 | Multiple utility operators + auditors |
| Vehicle V2X system | TPM 2.0 Sealed Storage | 1-of-1 | Bound to ECU software state |
| Cloud-IoT hybrid | Cloud KMS with KEK wrapping | 1-of-1 | KMS provides HSM-grade protection |

### 3.3 Backup Key Derivation Using HKDF (Hierarchical Backup)

For IoT deployments with many similar devices, deriving per-device backup keys from a master using **HKDF** (RFC 5869) is standard:

$$ K_{backup,device} = HKDF\text{-}Extract(salt, IKM) \rightarrow PRK $$

$$ K_{backup,device} = HKDF\text{-}Expand(PRK, info = \text{"device-id"} \Vert \text{"backup"} \Vert L) $$

**Step 1**: Extract a Pseudo-Random Key (PRK) from the Input Keying Material (IKM) using a salt:

$$ PRK = HMAC\text{-}SHA256(salt, IKM) $$

**Step 2**: Expand the PRK into the desired output length $L$ bytes using the application-specific `info` string.

This allows a single master backup secret to deterministically generate thousands of per-device backup keys without storing each one.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: IoT Key Backup Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[IoT End Device Sensor Wearable] -->|Generates Operational Key Kop| B[Device TPM or Secure Element SE]
    B -->|Request Backup| C[Backup Authority Gateway]
    C -->|Derive KEK from Master via HKDF| D[Key Encryption Key KEK in HSM]
    D -->|Wrap Kop| E[Wrapped Backup C = E_KEK Kop]
    E -->|Split via Shamir t-of-n| F[Share 1 to Admin A]
    E -->|Split via Shamir t-of-n| G[Share 2 to Admin B]
    E -->|Split via Shamir t-of-n| H[Share 3 to Admin C]
    E -->|Split via Shamir t-of-n| I[Share 4 to Admin D]
    E -->|Split via Shamir t-of-n| J[Share 5 to Admin E]
    K[Recovery Request MFA] -->|Authenticate| L[Threshold Verifier t-of-n]
    F --> L
    G --> L
    H --> L
    L -->|Combine t shares| M[Reconstruct Polynomial and find f 0 = Kop]
    M -->|Return Kop to authorized device| A
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: AES Key Wrap (RFC 3394) for Backup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Plaintext Key Kop 128 bits] --> B[Concatenate with IV 0xA6A6A6A6]
    B --> C[Six 64-bit Blocks if 128-bit key]
    C --> D[AES Encrypt Each Block Using KEK]
    D --> E[Apply XOR and Permutation Steps 6 Rounds]
    E --> F[Wrapped Output C 192 bits for 128-bit key]
    F --> G[Store in HSM NV Memory or Cloud KMS]
    H[KEK 128/192/256 bits] --> D
    G -->|Recovery Request| I[Unwrap with same KEK]
    I --> J[Recover Kop]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Shamir Secret Sharing Backup Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Start Secret S = Kop]
    S0 --> S1[Choose prime p greater than S]
    S1 --> S2[Choose polynomial degree t-1]
    S2 --> S3[Randomly select coefficients a1 through at-1 in Zp]
    S3 --> S4[Set constant term a0 = S]
    S4 --> S5[Evaluate polynomial at n distinct x values]
    S5 --> S6[Distribute n shares to custodians]
    S7[Need to Recover] --> S8[Collect at least t shares]
    S8 --> S9[Apply Lagrange Interpolation at x=0]
    S9 --> S10[Result f 0 = S = Kop Recovered]
    S6 -.-> S7
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In Diagram 4.1, the Backup Authority Gateway is the *only* node that holds the KEK. Individual share-holders never see the wrapped key, ensuring **defense in depth**. This is the standard architecture in **NIST SP 800-152** for federal key management systems.

### 4.4 Component-Level Backup Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Root Master Key RMK in HSM] --> B[Key Encryption Key KEK Derived from RMK]
    B --> C1[Device Key Kop Sensor 1 Wrapped]
    B --> C2[Device Key Kop Sensor 2 Wrapped]
    B --> C3[Device Key Kop Sensor 3 Wrapped]
    B --> C4[Device Key Kop Gateway Wrapped]
    C1 --> D1[Sealed in TPM 2.0 PCR bound]
    C2 --> D2[Sealed in TPM 2.0 PCR bound]
    C3 --> D3[Sealed in TPM 2.0 PCR bound]
    C4 --> D4[Sealed in TPM 2.0 PCR bound]
    D1 --> E[Recovery via Unwrap and Unseal Chain]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    A -.->|Backup Copy| F[Offline Cold Storage Encrypted USB in Safe]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Tier Key Hierarchy (NIST SP 800-57):** Root Key → KEK → Device Key. The Root Key is the *only* key whose backup is held outside the live system. All other keys are wrapped under it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
*Define a Backup Key. How is it different from a Key in Operational Use?*

**Model Answer:**

A **Backup Key** is a copy of a cryptographic key that is securely stored (typically in encrypted/wrapped form) for the purpose of recovering encrypted data when the original operational key is lost, corrupted, or destroyed.

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Operational Key | Backup Key |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Active encryption/decryption | Recovery only |
| Use Frequency | High (per message) | Low (only on key loss) |
| Storage | Online, in memory | Offline, in HSM/TPM/Cloud |
| Lifetime | Cryptoperiod | Equal to or greater than cryptoperiod |

The backup key is **never used for routine cryptographic operations** — it is reserved exclusively for *recovery*. This separation prevents backup compromise from affecting live traffic. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model Paper]**
*List any three key backup mechanisms used in IoT systems and state one advantage of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Key Wrapping (AES-KW per RFC 3394):** Operational key is encrypted under a Key Encryption Key (KEK). *Advantage:* Provides both confidentiality and integrity of the backup in a single primitive.
2. **Shamir's Secret Sharing:** Key is split into $n$ shares with a threshold $t$. *Advantage:* Information-theoretic security; no $t-1$ shares leak the key.
3. **TPM 2.0 Sealed Storage:** Key is bound to specific PCR (platform configuration) values. *Advantage:* Backup is *cryptographically tied* to a trusted device state, preventing rollback attacks. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(7 marks)** Explain Shamir's $(t, n)$ Secret Sharing scheme with a suitable example. Show how the secret is reconstructed using Lagrange Interpolation.

**Model Answer:**

Shamir's scheme is a polynomial-based key backup mechanism where a secret $S$ is encoded as the constant term $a_0$ of a random polynomial of degree $t-1$ over a prime field $\mathbb{Z}_p$.

**Setup Phase:**
1. Choose a large prime $p > S$.
2. Pick $t-1$ random coefficients $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_{t-1} \in \mathbb{Z}_p$.
3. Define: $f(x) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 x^2 + \cdots + a_{t-1} x^{t-1} \pmod{p}$, where $a_0 = S$.
4. Compute $n$ shares: $(x_i, y_i) = (i, f(i))$ for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$.
5. Distribute each share to a different custodian.

**Reconstruction Phase:** Given any $t$ shares $(x_{i_1}, y_{i_1}), \ldots, (x_{i_t}, y_{i_t})$, apply Lagrange Interpolation:

$$ f(0) = \sum_{j=1}^{t} y_{i_j} \prod_{k \neq j} \frac{x_{i_k}}{x_{i_k} - x_{i_j}} \pmod{p} $$

**Numerical Example (from Section 3.1 above):**
With $S = 42$, $p = 61$, $t = 3$, $n = 5$, $f(x) = 42 + 17x + 23x^2 \pmod{61}$:
- Shares: $(1, 21), (2, 46), (3, 56), (4, 51), (5, 31)$.
- Using shares 1, 3, 5: Lagrange basis polynomials evaluated at $x=0$ give $L_1(0) = 40, L_3(0) = 14, L_5(0) = 8 \pmod{61}$.
- Recovery: $f(0) = 21(40) + 56(14) + 31(8) = 1872 \equiv 42 \pmod{61}$. **Secret recovered.**

**Security Property:** Any $t-1$ shares leave infinitely many possible polynomials passing through them, so they leak **zero information** about $S$ (information-theoretic security). **(7 marks)**

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(7 marks)** Compare **Key Backup**, **Key Escrow**, and **Key Archival**. Discuss their roles in an IoT deployment scenario.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Key Backup | Key Escrow | Key Archival |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Disaster recovery | Legal/compelled access | Long-term retention |
| **Holder** | Key owner | Trusted Third Party (TTP) | Designated archive |
| **Access by Owner** | Yes (own recovery) | No (third party only) | Restricted (audit-based) |
| **Lifetime** | Active cryptoperiod | May exceed cryptoperiod | Beyond cryptoperiod |
| **Typical Use** | Business continuity | Law enforcement | Regulatory compliance |

**IoT Deployment Scenario: Smart Grid**

- **Key Backup**: The utility maintains a wrapped copy of each substation's signing key in its corporate HSM. If a substation gateway is destroyed in a flood, the utility can restore the key and resume billing operations within hours. **Backup** enables *operational continuity*.
- **Key Escrow**: The state electricity regulatory commission may require that the master signing key be escrowed with a government-approved TTP. In case of fraud investigation, the regulator can decrypt communications without the utility's cooperation. **Escrow** enables *legal oversight*.
- **Key Archival**: After a substation is decommissioned, its historical encrypted telemetry (10 years of consumption data) must remain decryptable for audit. The encrypted data is stored in cold storage along with the *archived* wrapped key, even though the key is past its operational cryptoperiod. **Archival** enables *long-term compliance*.

These three mechanisms together form the **key lifecycle** as defined in **NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 Rev. 5**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Alternative Choice (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(7 marks)** Explain the **AES Key Wrap** algorithm as specified in **RFC 3394**. How is it used for cryptographic key backup?

**Model Answer:**

The **AES Key Wrap** (KW) algorithm, defined in **RFC 3394**, is an authenticated encryption mode specifically designed for wrapping (encrypting) cryptographic keys. It takes a Key Encryption Key (KEK) and a plaintext key and produces a wrapped output that is both **confidential** and **integrity-protected**.

**Algorithm Steps for wrapping a 128-bit key $K$ with KEK $K_{KEK}$:**

1. **Initialization:** Concatenate the plaintext key $K$ (128 bits) with the Initial Value $IV = 0xA6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6$ (128 bits), forming two 64-bit halves: $A_0$ and $R_1$.

2. **Six Rounds of Wrapping:** For $j = 0, 1, \ldots, 5$ (corresponding to *semirounds* $i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6$):
   - For each $i$ from 1 to $n$ (where $n = 2$ for 128-bit key):
     - Compute $B = \text{AES}(K_{KEK}, A \Vert R_i)$.
     - Split $B$ into $A$ (left 64 bits) and $R_i'$ (right 64 bits).
     - $A \leftarrow A \oplus t$ where $t = 64 j + i$.
     - $R_{i+1} \leftarrow R_i'$.

3. **Output:** The wrapped key is $C = A_6 \Vert R_{n+1}$. For a 128-bit input, the output is **192 bits** (64 bits longer than input).

**Unwrapping** is the inverse process with XOR and AES decryption in reverse round order, verifying that the recovered $A_0 = 0xA6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6$ for integrity.

**Use in Key Backup:**

When a device's operational AES-128 key $K_{op}$ needs to be backed up:
- A hardware HSM holds the KEK.
- The wrapped output $C = KW_{K_{KEK}}(K_{op})$ (192 bits) is stored in a database, smart card, or cloud KMS.
- The KEK is *never* stored with the wrapped key.
- On recovery, an authorized administrator authenticates to the HSM, provides $C$, and retrieves $K_{op} = KW^{-1}_{K_{KEK}}(C)$.

**Advantages for IoT Backup:**
- Single primitive for confidentiality + integrity.
- Standardized in **NIST SP 800-38F** (2009).
- Operates without IVs or padding for exact key sizes.
- Well-suited for constrained environments (only AES primitive needed). **(7 marks)**

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**
**(7 marks)** Describe **Hardware-Backed Key Backup using TPM 2.0 Sealed Storage**. Why is this approach particularly suitable for IoT edge devices?

**Model Answer:**

**TPM 2.0 Sealed Storage** is a hardware-rooted key backup mechanism where a key is bound not just to a KEK but also to a specific *platform configuration state* captured in **Platform Configuration Registers (PCRs)**.

**Mechanism:**

The TPM 2.0 chip contains a **Storage Root Key (SRK)** that is generated internally and never leaves the chip. Backup operations use the **TPM2_Seal** command:

1. The caller specifies a public key or symmetric key $K$ to seal.
2. The caller specifies a set of PCR values $\{PCR_0, PCR_1, \ldots, PCR_n\}$ that must match for unsealing.
3. The TPM computes:

$$ K_{sealed} = \text{TPM2\_Seal}(K, PCR_0, PCR_1, \ldots, PCR_n) $$

4. The sealed blob is stored outside the TPM (in flash, SD card, or cloud).

5. To recover, **TPM2_Unseal** is invoked. The TPM checks current PCR values against the originally sealed values. Only if *all* match does it release $K$:

$$ K = \text{TPM2\_Unseal}(K_{sealed}) \quad \text{iff} \quad PCR_i^{current} = PCR_i^{sealed} \quad \forall i $$

**Why Suitable for IoT Edge Devices:**

1. **Anti-Tampering**: If an attacker reflashes the firmware or boots into a rogue OS, the PCRs change, and the sealed key remains *unrecoverable*. The backup cannot be exfiltrated onto a malicious clone of the device.

2. **No Software Key Material Exposure**: The SRK never leaves the TPM. Even if the host OS is compromised, the wrapped backup alone is useless.

3. **Geographic & Operational Suitability**: Edge devices (smart meters, industrial PLCs) are deployed in physically accessible locations. Sealing prevents *physical relocation* attacks — even if an attacker steals the device, without the original boot chain, the backup is unrecoverable.

4. **Standards Compliance**: TPM 2.0 is standardized by the **Trusted Computing Group (TCG)** and referenced in **NIST SP 800-147**, **NIST SP 800-155**, and **ISO/IEC 11889**.

5. **Low Power Footprint**: TPMs are designed for embedded use, drawing microamps in standby — ideal for battery-powered IoT.

**Example Use Case**: A smart electric meter seals its billing-signing key to PCR_0 (BIOS/firmware hash) and PCR_4 (OS loader hash). If the utility reflashes the meter for a security patch, the PCRs change and the old sealed key is unrecoverable — forcing the utility to generate a new key, preventing any reuse of compromised keys. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Backup Keys**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Backup with Escrow.** Examiners specifically test this distinction. Backup = owner recovery; Escrow = third-party recovery.
> 2. **For Shamir's Scheme derivations:** Always state the polynomial degree = $t-1$, not $t$. Writing "$t$ degree polynomial" is a common **2-mark deduction**.
> 3. **For AES Key Wrap:** The IV is $0xA6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6$, not all zeros. Writing the wrong IV loses **1 mark**.
> 4. **For TPM Sealing:** Examiners expect the *unsealing* step's PCR check to be mentioned. Omitting it loses **2 marks**.
> 5. **For Lagrange Interpolation:** $f(0) = a_0$ (the secret) — explicitly state this. Writing only "interpolate" is incomplete.
> 6. **Common Mistake in $(t, n)$ Schemes:** Students often claim "$m$ shares always reveal the secret" — this is only true for $m \geq t$. For $m < t$, **zero information** is revealed. Be precise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for Backup Keys (KTU Module 3)**

- ✅ **Backup Key**: A *redundant, recovery-only* copy of a cryptographic key, never used operationally.
- ✅ **Three lifecycle phases for key survival** (NIST SP 800-57): **Backup**, **Archival**, **Escrow** — distinct purposes, distinct holders.
- ✅ **Key Wrapping (RFC 3394)**: AES-KW uses IV `0xA6A6A6A6A6A6A6A6`; output of 128-bit key is 192 bits.
- ✅ **Shamir's $(t, n)$ Scheme**: Polynomial of degree $t-1$ over $\mathbb{Z}_p$, secret = $f(0) = a_0$.
- ✅ **Threshold Property**: $m \geq t$ shares reconstruct; $m < t$ shares leak **zero** information (information-theoretic security).
- ✅ **Lagrange Interpolation**: $a_0 = \sum_{i=1}^{t} y_i \prod_{j \neq i} \frac{x_j}{x_j - x_i} \pmod{p}$.
- ✅ **TPM 2.0 Sealing**: Binds key to PCR values; recovery requires matching platform state.
- ✅ **HKDF (RFC 5869)**: Derives multiple backup keys from a single master using Extract-then-Expand.
- ✅ **Three-Tier Key Hierarchy**: Root Key → KEK → Device Key (DEK). Only the Root Key is backed up offline.
- ✅ **Common IoT KEK Sizes**: AES-128, AES-192, AES-256.
- ✅ **NIST Cryptoperiod** for symmetric data-encryption keys: typically ≤ 2 years or $\leq 2^{32}$ blocks for AES-128.
- ✅ **Practical IoT Example**: Smart meter network uses (3,5) Shamir split among utility operator, regulator, manufacturer, and two independent auditors.
- ✅ **Distinction to Memorize for 2-Mark Questions**:
    - *Backup* → owner recovery → business continuity
    - *Escrow* → third-party recovery → legal/compelled access
    - *Archival* → long-term retention → regulatory compliance
- ✅ **Examiner's Golden Tip**: When asked "explain backup keys," *always* start with NIST's lifecycle position, then give a real IoT example, then introduce Shamir's or Wrapping mathematically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Compromised Keys
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compromised Keys # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of cryptography for IoT applications, a **Compromised Key** refers to any cryptographic key whose confidentiality, integrity, authenticity, or availability has been breached, such that an unauthorized entity (adversary, attacker, or unintended recipient) has gained access to, modified, duplicated, or destroyed the key material. According to NIST SP 800-57 Part 1 (Key Management Guidelines) terminology adapted for IoT, a key is considered compromised when its operational security boundary has been violated, rendering any data protected by that key cryptographically vulnerable. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A compromised key is a secret/private key whose secrecy or integrity has been violated, disclosed to unauthorized parties, lost beyond recovery, or derived by an adversary through cryptanalysis, side-channel leakage, physical tampering, or implementation flaws. The compromise of a single key in an IoT system may cascade across the entire trust hierarchy, jeopardizing device authentication, data confidentiality, firmware integrity, and network-wide communication security. ## 1.2 Types of Key Compromise A compromised key in IoT can manifest in **four (4) primary forms**: | # | Compromise Type | Definition | IoT-Specific Risk Level | |---|----------------|------------|-------------------------| | 1 | **Key Disclosure (Compromise of Confidentiality)** | The secret key value is revealed to or obtained by an unauthorized party. | **CRITICAL** | | 2 | **Key Modification (Compromise of Integrity)** | The key value is altered by an attacker, breaking the integrity of cryptographic operations. | HIGH | | 3 | **Key Loss (Compromise of Availability)** | The key is destroyed, lost, or becomes inaccessible to legitimate parties. | MODERATE | | 4 | **Key Duplication (Compromise via Copying)** | The key is copied by an adversary without affecting the original owner's operations. | **CRITICAL** | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Stolen House Key" Imagine you live in a house (your IoT device) protected by a unique physical key (cryptographic key). The key is entrusted only to you and your family (legitimate users). - **Disclosure** is like a thief photographing your key and sharing it on the internet — they don't have the original, but they can still open the door anytime. - **Modification** is like a malicious locksmith changing your key's teeth so that the original no longer works — denying YOU access to your own home. - **Loss** is like accidentally dropping the key down a drain — you cannot enter the house, but neither can the thief (at least not yet). - **Duplication** is like a hotel staff member making a perfect copy of your key during a routine visit — you still have your key, but someone else has full, undetectable access. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters in IoT:** Unlike traditional computing environments, IoT devices (smart sensors, wearables, industrial controllers, smart meters) are often deployed in **physically accessible, hostile, or unattended locations** (public spaces, factory floors, roadside cabinets). An adversary can pick up the device, open its enclosure, and extract the key from flash memory in minutes. This makes compromised key handling one of the most critical modules in IoT security design. ## 1.4 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment | CO ID | Course Outcome | Mapped Bloom's Level | |-------|----------------|----------------------| | **CO3** | Analyze the threats, vulnerabilities, and key management challenges specific to resource-constrained IoT devices. | **Analyze (L4)** | | **CO4** | Design incident response and recovery strategies for cryptographic failures in IoT networks. | **Design (L6)** | | **CO5** | Evaluate the impact of key compromise on confidentiality, integrity, and availability of IoT systems. | **Evaluate (L5)** | ## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **NIST SP 800-131A Recommendation:** Discontinue **2-key 3DES** (Triple DES) after 2023 due to compromise risk. - **AES-128** key lifetime recommendation: **≤ 2^32 encryptions** under the same key (birthday bound). - **RSA-2048** private key compromise: considered catastrophic; immediate revocation mandatory. - **IoT device key refresh cycle:** Typically **90 to 365 days** depending on device criticality. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown A compromised key in an IoT cryptographic system follows a predictable lifecycle of **detection, containment, eradication, and recovery**. The theoretical model is built upon the **CIA Triad extension** — the **CIANA+ model** (Confidentiality, Integrity, Authentication, Non-repudiation, Availability + Proof of Possession): ### Step 1: Origin of Compromise (Threat Vector) - **Adversarial cryptanalysis** — Adversary solves the underlying mathematical problem (e.g., factoring large primes for RSA). - **Side-channel leakage** — Power analysis, electromagnetic emanation, or timing attacks reveal bits of the key. - **Physical intrusion** — JTAG, UART, or flash memory extraction retrieves stored keys. - **Protocol-level attack** — Man-in-the-middle (MITM), replay, or downgrade attack extracts session keys. - **Insider threat** — Authorized personnel misuse or leak key material. - **Weak randomness** — Predictable PRNG (Pseudo-Random Number Generator) seeds allow key guessing. ### Step 2: Compromise Window (Time of Exposure) The **compromise window** is the time interval between the moment the key is first exposed to an adversary and the moment the system detects and responds to the breach. In IoT, this window is typically larger than in enterprise IT due to: - Lack of continuous monitoring on edge devices. - Asynchronous communication between devices and central servers. - Bandwidth and energy constraints limiting frequent security checks. ### Step 3: Detection Mechanisms - **Intrusion Detection Systems (IDS):** Signature-based or anomaly-based detection of unusual key usage patterns. - **Key usage audits:** Logs of encryption/decryption counts exceeding safe thresholds. - **Certificate Revocation Lists (CRL):** PKI-based mechanism to declare public keys invalid. - **Online Certificate Status Protocol (OCSP):** Real-time check of certificate validity. ### Step 4: Containment & Response - **Key revocation:** Marking the key as invalid in the central authority. - **Key zeroization:** Securely overwriting the key in volatile and non-volatile memory. - **Rekeying:** Generating and distributing a fresh key to legitimate parties. - **Device quarantine:** Isolating the compromised IoT device from the network. ### Step 5: Recovery & Lessons Learned - Post-incident analysis. - Updating key management policies. - Patching firmware vulnerabilities. - Re-provisioning devices in the field (challenging at scale). ## 2.2 Why and How — The Underlying Logic > [!NOTE] > **The "Why":** Cryptographic security is only as strong as the secrecy of its keys. A perfectly designed AES-256 algorithm becomes worthless the moment the 256-bit key is leaked. In IoT, the attack surface is enormous — billions of devices, many physically accessible, many with weak tamper resistance. Therefore, **planning for compromise is not pessimistic; it is mandatory engineering practice**. > [!NOTE] > **The "How":** The response to a compromised key follows a four-phase model — **Detect → Revoke → Rekey → Recover** — governed by organizational **Key Management Policy (KMP)** documents, often aligned with **NIST SP 800-57** or **ISO/IEC 27001** standards. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Description | |---|---------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | **Birthday Bound (Key Reuse Limit)** | $N_{max} \approx 2^{n/2}$ | Max secure encryptions under an $n$-bit key. For AES-128: $N_{max} \approx 2^{64}$. | | 2 | **RSA Modulus Bit Security** | $k = 2048 \text{ bits} \Rightarrow 112 \text{ bits security}$ | RSA-2048 offers ~112-bit security. RSA-3072 → 128-bit. | | 3 | **Diffie-Hellman Prime Size** | $p \geq 2048 \text{ bits}$ | Minimum for IoT DH key exchange (NIST 2024). | | 4 | **Elliptic Curve Bit Security** | $secp256r1 \Rightarrow 128 \text{ bits security}$ | Used in IoT (ED25519, X25519). | | 5 | **Key Compromise Probability (Shannon Entropy)** | $H(K) = -\sum_{i=1}^{N} p(k_i) \log_2 p(k_i)$ | Entropy of key $K$. Max when uniformly distributed: $H(K) = \log_2 N$. | | 6 | **Risk Severity Score** | $R = P \times I$ | $R$ = Risk, $P$ = Probability of compromise, $I$ = Impact. | | 7 | **Mean Time to Compromise (MTTC)** | $MTTC = \frac{1}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ = attack rate per unit time. | | 8 | **Key Lifetime Bound (NIST)** | $T_{life} \leq \frac{N_{max}}{R_{use}}$ | $R_{use}$ = encryption rate per second. | | 9 | **Zeroization Time (Volatile Memory)** | $t_z \leq 1 \text{ ms}$ | Time to overwrite key in RAM. | | 10 | **Secure Memory Erase (NIST SP 800-88)** | Single-pass overwrite (modern storage) or physical destruction (legacy). | Standard for sanitization. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Remember the **$2^{n/2}$ birthday bound**. It is the most frequently tested formula in cryptography modules and directly applies to key compromise thresholds. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility In real-world IoT deployments, compromised key handling is critical in: - **Smart grid systems:** A compromised key in a smart meter can allow attackers to falsify consumption data, causing financial loss and grid instability. - **Connected vehicles (V2X):** A compromised key in a car's V2X communication module can enable fake emergency brake messages, endangering lives. - **Medical IoT (IoMT):** A compromised key in an insulin pump or pacemaker can be lethal. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Stuxnet famously exploited compromised signing keys to push malicious firmware updates to Siemens PLCs. - **Smart home ecosystems:** A compromised Zigbee/Z-Wave key allows an attacker to unlock doors, disable alarms, and surveil occupants. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Birthday Bound for Key Compromise Threshold The **birthday paradox** determines when a key becomes statistically vulnerable to collision-style attacks. We derive the maximum number of encryptions $N_{max}$ that can be safely performed under an $n$-bit key. ### Step 1: Define the probability of collision For a key space of size $N = 2^n$, the probability that two ciphertexts (or two random values derived from the key) collide after $q$ uses is given by the birthday approximation: $$ P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-q^2 / (2N)} $$ **Explanation:** This formula comes from the Poisson approximation of the birthday problem. After $q$ random draws from $N$ possibilities, the expected number of collision pairs is $\binom{q}{2}/N \approx q^2/(2N)$. ### Step 2: Set the acceptable collision probability For cryptographic safety, we require the collision probability to be negligible, say $P(\text{collision}) \leq 2^{-k}$ where $k$ is the security parameter (typically $k = 64$ or $k = 128$). $$ 1 - e^{-q^2 / (2N)} \leq 2^{-k} $$ ### Step 3: Use the Taylor expansion For small $x$, $1 - e^{-x} \approx x$. Substituting: $$ \frac{q^2}{2N} \leq 2^{-k} $$ **Explanation:** Since $2^{-k}$ is very small (e.g., $2^{-64} \approx 5.4 \times 10^{-20}$), the exponent is tiny, justifying the linear approximation. ### Step 4: Solve for $q = N_{max}$ $$ q^2 \leq 2N \cdot 2^{-k} = 2^{n+1-k} $$ $$ N_{max} = q \leq 2^{(n+1-k)/2} $$ ### Step 5: Apply the standard cryptographic bound For a well-designed cipher, $k \approx n/2$ is the target security. Substituting $k = n/2$: $$ N_{max} \leq 2^{(n+1-n/2)/2} = 2^{(n/2+1)/2} $$ **Simplification:** The dominant term gives the celebrated **birthday bound**: $$ N_{max} \approx 2^{n/2} $$ ### Step 6: Verify with AES-128 For $n = 128$ (AES-128 key size): $$ N_{max} \approx 2^{128/2} = 2^{64} $$ **Explanation:** Under an AES-128 key, one should not encrypt more than approximately $2^{64}$ blocks (≈ 18 quintillion) before rekeying. At 1 Gbps, this takes ~155 years — practically safe, but theoretically bounded. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Key Compromise Risk Score In IoT risk management, the **Compromise Risk Score (CRS)** combines probability and impact. ### Step 1: Define variables Let $P_c$ = probability of key compromise in one year, $I$ = impact severity (1 to 10 scale), $E$ = exposure factor (fraction of system affected). ### Step 2: Compute Annualized Loss Expectation (ALE) $$ ALE = P_c \times I \times E \times V $$ where $V$ = total asset value (in monetary units). **Explanation:** $ALE$ is a standard metric from risk management frameworks (NIST SP 800-30). For IoT, a single compromised key may affect $E$ up to 1.0 (entire system if root key). ### Step 3: Compute Compromise Risk Score $$ CRS = P_c \times I $$ **Explanation:** $CRS$ normalizes the risk to a $[0, 10]$ scale. If $P_c = 0.1$ (10% chance) and $I = 9$ (catastrophic), $CRS = 0.9$ — high risk requiring immediate mitigation. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Compromise Scenarios in IoT | Scenario | Key Type | Attack Vector | Time to Compromise | Recovery Time | KTU Weightage | |----------|----------|---------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------| | Smart meter root key | Symmetric (AES-256) | Physical chip decapping | Days to weeks | Hours | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | V2X authentication key | Asymmetric (ECDSA P-256) | Side-channel EM emanation | Hours to days | Days (OTA) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | Wearable health data key | Symmetric (AES-128) | Firmware extraction via JTAG | Minutes | Days (recall) | ⭐⭐⭐ | | Industrial sensor session key | Symmetric (AES-128) | MITM via rogue AP | Seconds | Seconds (rekey) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | Smart home hub TLS key | Asymmetric (RSA-2048) | Software vulnerability (Heartbleed-class) | Hours | Hours (patch) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Optimal Rekeying Interval To minimize the **expected loss** due to key compromise, derive the optimal rekeying interval $T^*$. ### Step 1: Define the expected loss function Assume the probability of compromise grows linearly with time: $P_c(t) = \lambda t$ for small $t$, where $\lambda$ is the compromise rate per unit time. ### Step 2: Define the cost model Let $C_r$ = cost of one rekeying operation (computational, network, human). Let $C_b$ = expected loss if a key is compromised (data breach, system failure). Total cost per unit time: $$ C_{total}(T) = \frac{C_r}{T} + \frac{C_b \cdot \lambda T}{2} $$ **Explanation:** The first term is the amortized rekeying cost per unit time. The second term is the expected breach loss: average compromise probability over the interval $[\lambda T / 2]$ times $C_b$. ### Step 3: Minimize $C_{total}(T)$ Take the derivative with respect to $T$ and set to zero: $$ \frac{d C_{total}}{dT} = -\frac{C_r}{T^2} + \frac{C_b \lambda}{2} = 0 $$ ### Step 4: Solve for optimal interval $$ T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_r}{C_b \lambda}} $$ **Explanation:** $T^*$ balances rekeying overhead against compromise risk. Higher $C_b$ (expensive breach) → shorter interval. Higher $\lambda$ (active attacker) → shorter interval. Higher $C_r$ (expensive rekeying in IoT) → longer interval. ### Step 5: IoT-specific example Suppose $C_r = \$0.01$ (cheap rekey in large fleet), $C_b = \$10{,}000$ (data breach), $\lambda = 10^{-6}$ per hour: $$ T^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 0.01}{10000 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{2000} \approx 44.7 \text{ hours} \approx 1.86 \text{ days} $$ **Explanation:** Even with cheap rekeying, the high cost of compromise forces rekeying roughly every 2 days — a serious challenge for low-power IoT devices operating on batteries. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Key Compromise Lifecycle in IoT ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Key Generation
PRNG/KDF] --> B[Key Storage
HSM/Flash/TPM] B --> C[Key Distribution
OTAR/Provisioning] C --> D[Key Usage
Encrypt/Sign/Auth] D --> E{Compromise Event?} E -->|No| F[Continue Operation] E -->|Yes| G[Detection Phase
IDS/OCSP/Logs] G --> H[Containment
Revoke + Quarantine] H --> I[Zeroization
Memory Erase] I --> J[Rekeying
New Key Generation] J --> K[Recovery
Re-provision Device] K --> L[Lessons Learned
Update KMP] L --> A F --> D ``` **Explanation:** This diagram illustrates the closed-loop key management lifecycle, with the compromise event acting as the trigger for the incident response cycle. Every transition is governed by a documented Key Management Policy (KMP). --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2 — IoT Trust Hierarchy and Compromise Cascade ```mermaid flowchart TD RootK[Root CA Key
RSA-4096] --> IntK1[Intermediate CA Key
RSA-2048] RootK --> IntK2[Intermediate CA Key
RSA-2048] IntK1 --> DevK1[Device Key A
ECDSA P-256] IntK1 --> DevK2[Device Key B
ECDSA P-256] IntK1 --> DevK3[Device Key C
ECDSA P-256] IntK2 --> DevK4[Device Key D
ECDSA P-256] IntK2 --> DevK5[Device Key E
ECDSA P-256] RootK -.->|CRITICAL
Compromise| RK1[Entire System
Collapses] IntK1 -.->|HIGH
Compromise| IK1[Subset of Devices
Affected] DevK1 -.->|MODERATE
Compromise| DK1[Single Device
Affected] ``` **Explanation:** This hierarchical diagram shows how a compromise at the **root** level (e.g., Root CA private key) cascades to invalidate the entire PKI trust chain, while a compromise at the **device** level affects only that single device. This is why IoT systems use **isolated device certificates** and **short-lived keys** to limit blast radius. --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Compromised Key Response System ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph InputLayer [Input Layer - IoT Devices] IOT1[Sensor Node 1] IOT2[Actuator Node 2] IOT3[Gateway Node 3] end subgraph DetectionLayer [Detection Layer] MON[Security Monitor
IDS/IPS] AUDIT[Key Usage Auditor] HSM[HSM Health Check] end subgraph DecisionLayer [Decision Layer] POLICY[Key Management Policy Engine] RISK[Risk Scoring Module] end subgraph ResponseLayer [Response Layer] REV[Revocation Service
CRL/OCSP] ZER[Zeroization Service] REK[Rekeying Service
KDF/PRNG] end subgraph RecoveryLayer [Recovery Layer] PROV[Provisioning Server] LOG[Audit Log Database] ALERT[Incident Alert System] end IOT1 --> MON IOT2 --> MON IOT3 --> MON MON --> POLICY AUDIT --> POLICY HSM --> POLICY POLICY --> RISK RISK --> REV RISK --> ZER RISK --> REK REV --> PROV ZER --> IOT1 ZER --> IOT2 ZER --> IOT3 REK --> PROV PROV --> LOG POLICY --> ALERT ``` **Explanation:** This block diagram represents a layered defense architecture for handling compromised keys in an IoT deployment. Each layer has a specific responsibility: **detection** (monitoring), **decision** (policy-driven), **response** (revocation, zeroization, rekeying), and **recovery** (re-provisioning with full audit trail). The system is designed to ensure that a single point of failure does not propagate across the entire network. --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Compromise Detection Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Key Usage Event]) --> Q1{Is key in
revocation list?} Q1 -->|Yes| Revoke[Mark Key Invalid] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Usage count exceeds
birthday bound?} Q2 -->|Yes| Rekey[Trigger Rekeying] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Anomalous source
detected?} Q3 -->|Yes| Quarantine[Quarantine Device] Q3 -->|No| Q4{Key age exceeds
