# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> [!NOTE]
> **Modeled on actual KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Exam Part A pattern.** Each question carries **3 marks**, and a precise 60–80 word model answer is expected.

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define a cryptographic hash function. List its three primary security properties.**

**Model Answer:**

A cryptographic hash function $h: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ maps messages of arbitrary length to a fixed $n$-bit digest. Its three primary security properties are:
1. **Pre-image resistance** — given $y$, it is computationally infeasible to find any $M'$ with $h(M') = y$ (cost $2^n$).
2. **Second pre-image resistance** — given $M_1$, it is infeasible to find $M_2 \neq M_1$ with $h(M_1) = h(M_2)$.
3. **Collision resistance** — it is infeasible to find *any* $M_1 \neq M_2$ with $h(M_1) = h(M_2)$ (cost $2^{n/2}$ by birthday attack).

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**State and explain the Birthday paradox as applied to hash functions.**

**Model Answer:**

The Birthday paradox states that in a group of just $\sqrt{2^n \ln 2} \approx 2^{n/2}$ random samples from a space of $2^n$ possible hash values, the probability of a collision is greater than 50%. Thus, finding a collision in an $n$-bit hash function requires only about $2^{n/2}$ trials rather than $2^n$. Consequently, an **MD5 (128-bit) hash offers only 64-bit collision security** in practice.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **Per KTU 2024 Scheme:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### `Question A (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### Q.A(a) — (7 Marks)
**Explain the Merkle–Damgård construction for iterated hash functions. Show the iterative diagram and explain the role of the IV, compression function, and padding.**

**Model Solution:**

The Merkle–Damgård construction (1979) builds a variable-input-length hash from a fixed-input-length **compression function** $f$.

**Step 1 — Padding:**
The message $M$ of length $\ell$ is padded to a multiple of the block size $b$ using the scheme:
$$M' = M \,\|\, 1 \,\|\, 0^{p} \,\|\, \ell_{64}$$
where $p$ zero bits are added so that the total length is a multiple of $b$, and the original length $\ell$ is encoded in the last 64 bits to defend against length-extension attacks.

**Step 2 — Iterative processing:**
$$H_0 = IV \quad \text{(a fixed, public constant)}$$
$$H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i), \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$
$$\text{Output: } h(M) = H_N$$

**Role of each element:**
- **IV:** Deterministic starting value (e.g., $0x0123456789ABCDEF$ for MD5). Ensures the hash is deterministic and standardized.
- **Compression function $f$:** A fixed-input-length function that takes an $n$-bit chaining value and a $(b-n)$-bit message block and outputs a new $n$-bit chaining value.
- **Padding:** Ensures the message fits neatly into blocks and binds the message length into the digest (resists length-extension).

**Security theorem (Merkle–Damgård):** If $f$ is collision-resistant, then the iterated hash $h$ is collision-resistant. This is why MD5 and SHA-1 inherited strong security properties from their compression functions — and equally, why any break in $f$ breaks the whole hash.

**Examples:** MD5, SHA-1, SHA-2 (SHA-256, SHA-512) all use Merkle–Damgård with a **Davies–Meyer**-style compression function (block cipher built from the round function of the hash itself).

---

#### Q.A(b) — (7 Marks)
**With neat diagrams, explain the operation of HMAC. Why is the double-hashing (inner and outer) used?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (RFC 2104):**
$$\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big(\,(K_0 \oplus opad) \,\|\, H\big((K_0 \oplus ipad)\,\|\, M\big)\Big)$$

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Key preprocessing:** If $K$ is shorter than block size $b$, pad with zeros to get $K_0$. If longer, replace $K_0 = H(K)$ and pad.
2. **Inner hash:** Compute $H(K_0 \oplus ipad \,\|\, M)$ where $ipad = 0x36$ repeated $b$ times.
3. **Outer hash:** Compute $H(K_0 \oplus opad \,\|\, \text{inner})$ where $opad = 0x5C$ repeated $b$ times.
4. **Output:** The outer hash digest is the HMAC tag (length $n$ of the underlying hash).

**Why two different pads ($ipad \neq opad$)?**
- The pads must be **different constants** so the two hash applications are not cancelled out.
- If the same pad were used, the construction would degenerate to a single hash with a single key mixing — destroying the security reduction.

**Why double-hashing (nested)?**
- The **outer hash** acts as a "key-derivation" that produces a *new, independent* key from the inner state.
- It enables a formal **security reduction** (Bellare–Canetti–Krawczyk 1996): an attacker breaking HMAC can be converted into an attacker breaking the inner compression function with the same advantage. Without nesting, this proof fails.
- The inner hash absorbs the message; the outer hash "finalizes" with the second key-mixed value — analogous to sealing an envelope twice.

**Usage:** HMAC-SHA-256 is the most widely used MAC on the Internet (TLS 1.2, JWT, API authentication) and in **IoT firmware signing**.

---

### `Question B (14 Marks)` — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

#### Q.B(a) — (7 Marks)
**Compare MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and SHA-3 (Keccak) with respect to: output size, block size, number of rounds, internal construction, and current security status.**

**Model Solution:**

| Property | MD5 | SHA-1 | SHA-256 | SHA-3 (Keccak-256) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Output size** | 128 bits | 160 bits | 256 bits | 256 bits (arbitrary) |
| **Block size** | 512 bits | 512 bits | 512 bits | 1088 bits (rate) |
| **Rounds** | 4 (×16 steps) | 80 | 64 | 24 (Keccak-f permutation) |
| **Construction** | Merkle–Damgård (MD) | MD | MD (Davies–Meyer) | **Sponge** |
| **Internal primitive** | 4 auxiliary functions $F, G, H, I$ | 4 functions $f_1, f_2, f_3, f_4$ | 6 logical functions $\Sigma_0, \Sigma_1, \sigma_0, \sigma_1, Ch, Maj$ | Keccak-f[1600] bit-permutation |
| **Designer** | Rivest (1991) | NIST (1995) | NIST (2001) | Bertoni–Daemen–Peeters–Van Assche (2008) |
| **Status (2024)** | **Broken** (Wang 2004) | **Broken** (SHAttered 2017) | **Secure** | **Secure / NIST Recommended** |

**Key differences (write at least these 3 in your exam for full marks):**

1. **Construction:** SHA-3 is the only one of the four that uses the *Sponge* construction. The other three are Merkle–Damgård. This makes SHA-3 immune to **length-extension attacks** that plague the MD-based family.
2. **Internal primitive:** SHA-3 uses a *single bit-permutation* ($1600$-bit state, 24 rounds) which is symmetric, elegant, and easier to analyze. MD5/SHA-1/SHA-2 use multiple complex round functions.
3. **Security status:** MD5 and SHA-1 are no longer collision-resistant. SHA-256 and SHA-3 remain secure in 2024.

**IoT relevance:** SHA-3 is highly efficient in hardware (a few thousand gates), making it ideal for **RFID and smart-card** IoT nodes. SHA-256 is more efficient in software and dominates **server-side IoT clouds**.

---

#### Q.B(b) — (7 Marks)
**What is authenticated encryption? Explain GCM and GMAC with diagrams. Why is GMAC insufficient as a standalone encryption scheme?**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** *Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data (AEAD)* is a cryptographic scheme that simultaneously provides **confidentiality** (encryption) and **integrity + authenticity** (MAC) of a message.

**GCM (Galois/Counter Mode):**
GCM is a NIST-standardized AEAD mode built on top of a **128-bit block cipher** (typically AES-128).

**Components:**
- **CTR mode** (Counter mode) for encryption: $C_i = P_i \oplus E_K(\text{IV} \| \text{counter}_i)$.
- **GHASH** (universal hash over $GF(2^{128})$) for authentication: $\text{tag} = E_K(J_0) \oplus \text{GHASH}_H(A \| C)$, where $H = E_K(0^{128})$.

**Output:** Ciphertext $C_1 \| C_2 \| \dots \| C_m$ plus a 128-bit authentication tag $T$.

**GMAC (Galois MAC):**
GMAC is a special case of GCM where the **plaintext is empty** (or treated as part of AAD). It produces only a tag — no ciphertext.

**Diagram (conceptual):**
$$\text{GCM}: \text{AES-CTR}(P) \to C \;\;\|\;\; \text{GHASH}(A,C) \to T$$
$$\text{GMAC}: \text{empty plaintext} \to \text{GHASH}(A) \to T$$

**Why is GMAC insufficient as standalone encryption?**
- GMAC produces **no ciphertext** — it only authenticates. If used alone, an attacker who intercepts a message can **read its contents** because there is no encryption.
- Confidentiality requires *encryption*; integrity requires *authentication*. AEAD = both. GMAC = only authentication.
- Additionally, GMAC is **forbidden** to be used as a pure MAC with random IVs in some NIST profiles because it can suffer from *forbidden-attack* vulnerabilities on short tags. Hence, in IoT protocols (e.g., LoRaWAN, TLS 1.3), GCM is preferred, and GMAC is reserved for cases where only integrity is needed (e.g., authenticating routing headers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common pitfalls students must AVOID:**

> - **Mistake 1:** Confusing MDC with MAC. MDC needs *no key* (or only a secret); MAC needs a *shared secret key*.
> - **Mistake 2:** Writing $2^n$ for collision complexity instead of $2^{n/2}$. Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for this.
> - **Mistake 3:** Stating that SHA-3 is "more secure than SHA-256" — they have the same proven security level; the difference is *construction*, not strength.
> - **Mistake 4:** Confusing HMAC's $ipad$ and $opad$ values ($0x36$ and $0x5C$). These are case-sensitive in answer scripts.
> - **Mistake 5:** In the Birthday paradox derivation, writing the result as "$2^n$ trials" without the $1.177$ factor — KTU board expects the exact expression $\sqrt{2N \ln 2}$.
> - **Mistake 6:** Claiming MD5 is "still usable for non-security hashing" — in KTU 2024, the answer is **no**, use SHA-256 even for checksums in new IoT products.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- [x] A hash function $h: \{0,1\}^* \to \{0,1\}^n$ is *deterministic*, *fixed-output*, and *efficient*.
- [x] **Three security properties:** Pre-image ($2^n$), Second pre-image ($2^n$), Collision ($2^{n/2}$).
- [x] **Birthday paradox threshold:** $k \approx 1.177 \times 2^{n/2}$ for 50% collision probability.
- [x] **Merkle–Damgård:** $H_0 = IV$, $H_i = f(H_{i-1}, M_i)$, output = $H_N$.
- [x] **Padding:** $M \,\|\, 1 \,\|\, 0^p \,\|\, \ell_{64}$ — bound length to prevent length-extension.
- [x] **Sponge** (SHA-3): state = $r + c$ bits; absorbing → squeezing; security = $2^{c/2}$ collisions.
- [x] **MD5** — broken (Wang 2004, 128-bit output, 64-bit collision security). **Do not use.**
- [x] **SHA-1** — broken (SHAttered 2017, 160-bit output). **Deprecated.**
- [x] **SHA-2 (SHA-256)** — secure, Merkle–Damgård, 256-bit output, 64 rounds.
- [x] **SHA-3** — secure, Sponge, Keccak-f[1600] permutation, 24 rounds.
- [x] **HMAC** = $H((K \oplus opad) \| H((K \oplus ipad) \| M))$, with $ipad = 0x36$, $opad = 0x5C$.
- [x] **CMAC** uses block cipher (AES) in CBC-like mode, last ciphertext block = MAC.
- [x] **GMAC** uses GHASH over $GF(2^{128})$; only authenticates, no encryption.
- [x] **AEAD (GCM, CCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305)** provides both confidentiality and integrity in one shot.
- [x] **IoT relevance:** firmware integrity (SHA-256), device authentication (HMAC), lightweight AEAD (ASCON, GCM in hardware).
- [x] **Length-extension attack:** affects Merkle–Damgård but *not* SHA-3 (Sponge) — KTU loves this point.
- [x] **Avlanche effect** ≈ 50% bits change for any 1-bit input change.
- [x] **Random oracle model:** idealization of a hash as a perfect random function — used in security proofs.
- [x] **NIST Hash Competition (2007–2012):** Keccak won, standardized as **SHA-3** in FIPS-202 (2015).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Security of Hash Functions and MAC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Security of Hash Functions and MAC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cryptographic Hash Function**: A deterministic mathematical function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ that maps an arbitrary-length input message $M$ to a fixed-length output of $n$ bits, called the **message digest** (or hash value), such that the function is computationally efficient, one-way, and collision-resistant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**: A symmetric-key cryptographic primitive that takes a secret key $K$ and an arbitrary-length message $M$ as inputs, and produces a fixed-length authentication tag $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$, providing both **data integrity** and **data origin authentication**.

### 1.2 Intuition via Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **fingerprinting system at a forensic lab**:
- A person's fingerprint (input) can be of any size and shape, but the *digital fingerprint scan* (hash output) is always a fixed-length, compact string of numbers.
- Two different people should never produce the exact same fingerprint scan — this represents **collision resistance**.
- Given only the fingerprint scan, you should not be able to reconstruct the original person — this represents the **one-way (preimage resistance)** property.

Now extend this analogy to a **sealed wax envelope with a royal insignia**:
- The insignia is generated using a *secret royal stamp* (the MAC key).
- Anyone holding the insignia knows the letter was sent by the royal house and was not tampered with, because forging it requires the *secret stamp*.
- The MAC is essentially the cryptographic equivalent of this royal insignia.

### 1.3 The Three Classical Security Properties of Hash Functions

A cryptographically secure hash function $H$ must satisfy three fundamental security properties:

| Property | Formal Definition | Informal Description |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Preimage Resistance** | For any given hash $y$, it is computationally infeasible to find $x$ such that $H(x) = y$ | One-way: hard to invert |
| **Second Preimage Resistance** | Given $x_1$, infeasible to find $x_2 \neq x_1$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ | Hard to find a duplicate |
| **Collision Resistance** | Infeasible to find *any* pair $x_1 \neq x_2$ with $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$ | Hard to find any clash |

> [!NOTE]
> The hierarchy of strength is: **Collision Resistance** $>$ **Second Preimage Resistance** $>$ **Preimage Resistance**. If a hash is collision-resistant, it is automatically second preimage-resistant, and a collision-resistant hash is also preimage-resistant (but the converse is *not* true).

### 1.4 Why MAC Security Matters in IoT

In IoT ecosystems, devices transmit sensor data, firmware updates, and control commands over untrusted wireless channels. **MACs provide tamper-evidence and authenticity** without requiring expensive public-key operations, making them ideal for low-power, resource-constrained IoT nodes (e.g., IEEE 802.15.4, LoRaWAN, Zigbee).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Security Properties — Logical Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Preimage Resistance (One-Wayness)
- **The Why**: If $H$ is not preimage-resistant, an attacker given a hash $y$ can find a message $M'$ that hashes to $y$, breaking commitments, password verifiers, and digital signatures.
- **The How**: A brute-force preimage attack requires trying $2^n$ candidates on average, where $n$ is the hash output length. For SHA-256 ($n=256$), this is computationally infeasible ($2^{256} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{77}$ operations).
- **Best generic attack complexity**: $O(2^n)$.

#### 2.1.2 Second Preimage Resistance
- **The Why**: An attacker who possesses a valid document $M_1$ must not be able to forge a fraudulent document $M_2$ with the same hash, or it could be swapped in a digital signature scheme.
- **The How**: Generic brute-force search requires $O(2^n)$ operations.
- **Trick**: The **birthday paradox** can be exploited — but only for *collision resistance*, not strictly for second preimage in some constructions.

#### 2.1.3 Collision Resistance
- **The Why**: The attacker controls *both* messages and can craft $M_1, M_2$ to collide. This is critical for digital signature forgery.
- **The How**: Using the **Birthday Attack**, the attacker only needs $O(2^{n/2})$ trials (a *square-root* reduction due to the pigeonhole principle).
- **Real consequence**: MD5 ($n=128$) requires only $2^{64}$ operations — feasible in 2024! SHA-1 ($n=160$) requires $2^{80}$ — within reach of nation-states.

### 2.2 MAC Security Properties

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A MAC scheme $\Pi = (\text{Gen}, \text{Mac}, \text{Vrfy})$ is considered **existentially unforgeable under chosen-message attacks (EUF-CMA)** if no probabilistic polynomial-time adversary $\mathcal{A}$, with oracle access to a tagging oracle, can produce a valid $(M^*, T^*)$ pair where $M^*$ was never queried.

**Three Formal MAC Security Properties:**

1. **Existential Unforgeability (EUF-CMA)**: Cannot forge *any* new tag.
2. **Universal Unforgeability (UUF-CMA)**: Cannot forge a tag for a *random* message.
3. **Strong Unforgeability (sEUF-CMA)**: Cannot produce a *new* valid tag even for previously signed messages.

### 2.3 The Birthday Attack — Mathematical Foundation

Given a hash function with $n$-bit output, the probability of at least one collision among $q$ random samples is approximately:

$$
P(\text{collision}) \approx 1 - e^{-q(q-1)/(2 \cdot 2^n)}
$$

For a 50% collision probability, we set $P \approx 0.5$:

$$
1 - e^{-q^2 / (2^{n+1})} = 0.5 \implies e^{-q^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5
$$

Taking natural log on both sides:

$$
-\frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}} = \ln(0.5) = -\ln 2
$$

Solving for $q$:

$$
q \approx \sqrt{2^{n+1} \cdot \ln 2} = 2^{n/2} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln 2} \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Bound | Description |
|---------|----------------|-------------|
| Preimage resistance complexity | $O(2^n)$ | Brute-force one-way inversion |
| Second preimage complexity | $O(2^n)$ | Brute-force duplicate search |
| Collision resistance (birthday) | $O(2^{n/2})$ | Square-root via birthday paradox |
| MD5 effective security | $2^{64}$ | Pre-image brute force |
| SHA-1 effective security | $2^{80}$ | Pre-image brute force |
| SHA-256 effective security | $2^{128}$ | Collision brute force |
| HMAC security | $\min(2^n, 2^k, 2^{L})$ | Where $n$=hash, $k$=key, $L$=message length |
| MAC forgery complexity (PRF) | $O(2^n)$ | Random tag guess |
| Length-extension attack | Compromises $H(K \mid\mid M)$ | Vulnerable to MAC without proper construction |
| Hash output size (NIST 2024) | $n \ge 256$ bits | Recommended for IoT post-quantum era |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LATEX RULE FOR TABLES**: When denoting absolute value or cardinality inside tables, always use `$\vert x \vert$` or `$\mid x \mid$` — never the bare pipe `|x|` — to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **IoT Firmware Updates**: SHA-256 hashes verify the integrity of OTA (Over-The-Air) firmware images before flashing.
- **Sensor Data Authentication**: HMAC-SHA256 tags authenticate readings from edge sensors in industrial IoT (IIoT).
- **Blockchain & Smart Contracts**: Hash functions form the backbone of Merkle trees used in distributed IoT ledgers.
- **RFID Protocols**: Lightweight MACs (e.g., CMAC-AES) authenticate RFID tags in supply-chain management.
- **Vehicle-to-Vehicle (V2V) Communication**: HMACs ensure authenticated beacon messages in autonomous driving.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Birthday Bound on Hash Collisions

We want to derive the *expected number of trials* to find the first collision in an $n$-bit hash space of size $N = 2^n$.

**Step 1 — Setup**: Suppose we sample $q$ distinct inputs and compute their hashes $H(x_1), H(x_2), \ldots, H(x_q)$, each independently and uniformly drawn from $\{0, 1\}^n$.

**Step 2 — Probability of No Collision**: The probability that the $i$-th sample does not collide with any of the previous $(i-1)$ samples is:

$$
P(\text{no collision at step } i) = 1 - \frac{i-1}{2^n}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Probability of No Collision After $q$ Samples**:

$$
P(\text{no collision in } q \text{ samples}) = \prod_{i=1}^{q} \left( 1 - \frac{i-1}{2^n} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Logarithmic Approximation**: Using the identity $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$
\ln \left[ P(\text{no collision}) \right] = \sum_{i=0}^{q-1} \ln \left( 1 - \frac{i}{2^n} \right) \approx -\sum_{i=0}^{q-1} \frac{i}{2^n} = -\frac{1}{2^n} \cdot \frac{q(q-1)}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiating**:

$$
P(\text{no collision}) \approx e^{-q(q-1) / (2^{n+1})}
$$

**Step 6 — For 50% Collision Probability**: We need $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$, so $P(\text{no collision}) = 0.5$:

$$
e^{-q^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5
$$

Taking natural log:

$$
-\frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}} = -\ln 2
$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $q$**:

$$
q^2 = 2^{n+1} \cdot \ln 2
$$

$$
q = \sqrt{2^{n+1} \cdot \ln 2} = 2^{n/2} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln 2}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical Coefficient**:

$$
\sqrt{2 \ln 2} = \sqrt{2 \times 0.6931} = \sqrt{1.3863} \approx 1.1774
$$

**Final Birthday Bound**:

$$
\boxed{q \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{n/2}}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Security Strength Comparison

Let us tabulate the effective security of major hash functions under generic attacks:

| Hash Function | Output $n$ | Preimage Attack | Second Preimage | Collision (Birthday) | Status (2024) |
|---------------|-----------|------------------|------------------|----------------------|----------------|
| MD4 | 128 bits | $2^{128}$ | $2^{128}$ | $2^{64}$ | **Broken** |
| MD5 | 128 bits | $2^{123}$ (Wang) | $2^{123}$ | $2^{64}$ (real) | **Broken** |
| SHA-1 | 160 bits | $2^{160}$ | $2^{160}$ | $2^{80}$ (SHAttered, 2017) | **Deprecated** |
| SHA-224 | 224 bits | $2^{224}$ | $2^{224}$ | $2^{112}$ | Acceptable |
| SHA-256 | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ | **Recommended** |
| SHA-384 | 384 bits | $2^{384}$ | $2^{384}$ | $2^{192}$ | Recommended |
| SHA-512 | 512 bits | $2^{512}$ | $2^{512}$ | $2^{256}$ | Recommended |
| SHA3-256 | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ | **Recommended** |
| BLAKE2 | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ | Fast, IoT-friendly |
| BLAKE3 | 256 bits | $2^{256}$ | $2^{256}$ | $2^{128}$ | Tree-hash, parallel |

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: MAC Forgery Probability under PRF Assumption

Let $\mathcal{A}$ be an adversary making $q$ MAC queries against a PRF-based MAC scheme with $n$-bit tag length.

**Step 1 — Probability of a Random Tag Guess**: For a new message $M^*$ not previously queried, the probability that a randomly guessed tag $T^*$ verifies correctly is:

$$
P(\text{guess success}) = \frac{1}{2^n}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability of a Collision in Queries**: By the birthday paradox, among $q$ queried tags, the probability of two distinct queries producing the same tag is approximately:

$$
P(\text{collision among } q \text{ tags}) \approx \frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Forgery Probability** (union bound):

$$
P(\text{forgery}) \le \frac{q}{2^n} + \frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solving for Maximum Safe Queries**: For forgery probability $\le 2^{-k}$ (security parameter $k$):

$$
q \le 2^n \cdot 2^{-k} = 2^{n-k}
$$

For a 128-bit security level ($k = 128$) with SHA-256 ($n = 256$):

$$
\boxed{q \le 2^{256 - 128} = 2^{128} \text{ queries}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of the HMAC Security Bound

**Step 1 — HMAC Definition** (RFC 2104):

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H\big( (K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M \big) \Big)
$$

where:
- $ipad = 0x36$ repeated to block size $B$
- $opad = 0x5C$ repeated to block size $B$

**Step 2 — Key Length Adjustment**: If key length $\vert K \vert < B$, pad with zeros. If $\vert K \vert > B$, replace $K \leftarrow H(K)$.

**Step 3 — Security Bound** (Bellare, Canetti, Krawczyk, 1996):

$$
\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{EUF-CMA}}(\mathcal{A}, q, \ell) \le \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}}(\mathcal{B}) + \frac{q^2 \cdot \ell^2}{2^{n+1}} + \frac{q^2}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplified Bound**:

$$
\boxed{\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{EUF-CMA}} \le \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}} + \frac{q^2(\ell^2 + 1)}{2^{n+1}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation**: As long as the underlying hash $H$ is a PRF, HMAC remains secure up to $O(2^{n/2})$ queries, even if the underlying compression function is weak — this is the **cryptographic strength of nested construction**.

### 3.5 Length-Extension Attack Derivation

**Step 1 — Vulnerable Construction**: The naive MAC $H(K \mid\mid M)$ is vulnerable because Merkle–Damgård hashes (SHA-256, SHA-512) process data in blocks and expose the **internal state** via the digest.

**Step 2 — Attack Procedure**:
1. Given $H(K \mid\mid M)$ and $\vert M \vert$, the attacker recovers the *internal state* of the hash.
2. The attacker appends arbitrary padding $\text{pad}(K, M)$ followed by a malicious suffix $M'$.
3. Computes $H(K \mid\mid M \mid\mid \text{pad} \mid\mid M')$ *without knowing $K$*.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Justification**:

$$
H(K \mid\mid M) = \text{final}\big( \text{compress}(\ldots \text{compress}(IV, K \mid\mid M)) \big)
$$

Since the output of $H$ *is* the internal state, the attacker simply continues the compression from that state with the new data.

**Step 4 — Defenses**:
- Use **HMAC** (double hashing, no length-extension).
- Use **SHA-3** (sponge construction is inherently resistant).
- Use the **prefix-MAC**: $\text{MAC}_K(M) = H(K \mid\mid M \mid\mid L)$ where $L = \vert M \vert$ is appended.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hash Function Pipeline — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Arbitrary Length Input Message M] --> B[Padding and Length Encoding]
    B --> C[Message Block 1 M1]
    B --> D[Message Block 2 M2]
    B --> E[Message Block n Mn]
    C --> F[Compression Function f with IV]
    F --> G[Intermediate State H1]
    D --> H[Compression Function f with H1]
    H --> I[Intermediate State H2]
    E --> J[Compression Function f with Hn-1]
    J --> K[Final Hash Value Hn of n bits]
    F --> L[Fixed Length Hash Output]
    H --> L
    J --> L
```

### 4.2 HMAC Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Secret Key K] --> K1[Pad or Hash to Block Size B]
    K1 --> XOR1[XOR with ipad 0x36 repeated]
    K1 --> XOR2[XOR with opad 0x5C repeated]
    M[Message M] --> CONCAT1[Concatenate]
    XOR1 --> CONCAT1
    CONCAT1 --> HASH1[Inner Hash H]
    HASH1 --> CONCAT2[Concatenate with XOR2 output]
    XOR2 --> CONCAT2
    CONCAT2 --> HASH2[Outer Hash H]
    HASH2 --> TAG[HMAC Tag T of n bits]
```

### 4.3 MAC Verification Flow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M_RECV[Received Message M] --> CONCAT_V[Concatenate with Shared Key K]
    CONCAT_V --> HASH_V[Apply MAC Algorithm]
    HASH_V --> T_CALC[Calculated Tag Tcalc]
    T_RECV[Received Tag Trecv] --> CMP[Constant Time Compare]
    T_CALC --> CMP
    CMP -->|Match| ACCEPT[Message Accepted as Authentic]
    CMP -->|No Match| REJECT[Message Rejected]
```

### 4.4 Birthday Attack Visualization — Collision Search Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start with empty list L] --> GEN[Generate Random Message x]
    GEN --> HASH[Compute Hx = H of x]
    HASH --> CHECK{Hx already in L?}
    CHECK -->|No| STORE[Store x and Hx in L]
    STORE --> MORE{Quotient reached?}
    MORE -->|No| GEN
    MORE -->|Yes| DONE[Search Complete]
    CHECK -->|Yes| COLLIDE[Collision Found: x1 and x2 with Hx1 equal to Hx2]
    COLLIDE --> DONE
```

### 4.5 Security Property Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CR[Collision Resistance] --> SPR[Second Preimage Resistance]
    SPR --> PR[Preimage Resistance One Way]
    CR2[Collision Resistance] -.Implies.-> PR2[Preimage Resistance]
    SPR2[Second Preimage Resistance] -.Does NOT Imply.-> PR3[Preimage Resistance]
```

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

State and briefly explain the **three fundamental security properties** of a cryptographic hash function. Give a real-world example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three security properties of a cryptographic hash function $H: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow \{0,1\}^n$ are:

1. **Preimage Resistance (One-Wayness)** — For any given hash value $y$, it should be computationally infeasible to find any input $x$ such that $H(x) = y$. Brute-force complexity: $O(2^n)$.
   - *Example*: Password verification systems store $H(\text{password})$; an attacker with the hash must not be able to recover the password.

2. **Second Preimage Resistance** — Given a specific input $x_1$, it should be infeasible to find a *different* input $x_2 \neq x_1$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$. Complexity: $O(2^n)$.
   - *Example*: A digitally signed contract cannot be replaced with a fraudulent one having the same hash.

3. **Collision Resistance** — It should be infeasible to find *any* two distinct inputs $x_1 \neq x_2$ such that $H(x_1) = H(x_2)$. Best attack: birthday bound $O(2^{n/2})$.
   - *Example*: Critical for SSL/TLS certificate forgery prevention; SHA-1 collisions (SHAttered, 2017) allowed forged PDF certificates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking tip**: Examiners award **(1 mark)** for naming each property, **(1 mark)** for the formal condition, and **(1 mark)** for the example — total 3.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

What is a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**? Differentiate between MAC and a **hash function** in terms of authentication guarantees.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)** is a symmetric-key cryptographic primitive that produces a fixed-length tag $T = \text{MAC}_K(M)$ from a message $M$ and a shared secret key $K$. It provides both **data integrity** (message has not been altered) and **data origin authentication** (message is from a holder of $K$).

| Aspect | Hash Function | MAC |
|--------|---------------|-----|
| **Key** | Keyless — public | Requires shared secret $K$ |
| **Integrity** | Yes (anyone can verify) | Yes |
| **Authentication** | **No** (no proof of origin) | **Yes** (only $K$-holders can produce) |
| **Output** | Hash $h$ | Tag $T$ |
| **Use Case** | Fingerprinting, commitment | Authenticated IoT messages, payment |

**Conclusion**: A hash alone provides *integrity*; a MAC provides *integrity + authentication*. For IoT sensor streams, a MAC is essential because messages must be tied to the *specific sending device*.

**Part B – Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Birthday Attack** on hash functions. Derive the expected number of trials required to find a collision in an $n$-bit hash space. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **security of HMAC**, including its construction, the underlying security theorem, and an analysis of its resistance to length-extension and key-recovery attacks. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B – Question A

### Part (a) — Birthday Attack on Hash Functions (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of the Problem** (1 Mark):

A birthday attack exploits the **birthday paradox**: in a set of $N$ possible hash values, finding a collision requires only about $\sqrt{N}$ random samples rather than $N$. For an $n$-bit hash, the search space is $N = 2^n$, and the expected collision occurs after $q \approx 2^{n/2}$ trials.

**Step 2 — The Pigeonhole Principle** (1 Mark):

Since the hash output has fixed length $n$ bits, there are only $2^n$ possible outputs. For any set of $2^n + 1$ input messages, at least two must share the same hash (pigeonhole principle). The birthday attack shows we find a collision with high probability *far* earlier.

**Step 3 — Probability Derivation** (2 Marks):

The probability that all $q$ sampled hashes are distinct is:

$$
P(\text{no collision}) = \prod_{i=1}^{q-1} \left( 1 - \frac{i}{2^n} \right)
$$

Using the identity $\ln(1 - x) \approx -x$ for small $x$:

$$
\ln[P(\text{no collision})] \approx -\sum_{i=1}^{q-1} \frac{i}{2^n} = -\frac{q(q-1)}{2 \cdot 2^n} = -\frac{q(q-1)}{2^{n+1}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for 50% Probability** (2 Marks):

Setting $P(\text{collision}) = 0.5$:

$$
e^{-q^2 / 2^{n+1}} = 0.5
$$

$$
q^2 = 2^{n+1} \cdot \ln 2
$$

$$
q = 2^{n/2} \cdot \sqrt{2 \ln 2} \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{n/2}
$$

**Step 5 — Application to Real Hashes** (1 Mark):

| Hash | $n$ | Birthday Bound | Status |
|------|-----|----------------|--------|
| MD5 | 128 | $2^{64}$ | Trivially broken |
| SHA-1 | 160 | $2^{80}$ | Theoretically feasible |
| SHA-256 | 256 | $2^{128}$ | Secure |

**Conclusion**: An 80-bit collision security is no longer considered safe in 2024 due to advances in computation; NIST recommends $n \ge 256$ bits for post-quantum IoT.

---

### Part (b) — Security of HMAC (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — HMAC Construction (RFC 2104)** (2 Marks):

$$
\text{HMAC}_K(M) = H\Big( (K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H\big( (K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M \big) \Big)
$$

where:
- $ipad = \text{0x36}$ repeated to block size $B$
- $opad = \text{0x5C}$ repeated to block size $B$
- $K$ is zero-padded or hashed to fit block size $B$

**Step 2 — Security Theorem (Bellare–Canetti–Krawczyk, 1996)** (2 Marks):

If $H$ is a *pseudorandom function* (PRF), then HMAC is a PRF, and hence an EUF-CMA-secure MAC. Formally:

$$
\text{Adv}_{\text{HMAC}}^{\text{PRF}}(\mathcal{A}, q, \ell) \le \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}}(\mathcal{B}) + \frac{q^2 \ell^2 + q^2}{2^{n+1}}
$$

where $q$ = number of queries, $\ell$ = max block length, $n$ = hash output size.

**Step 3 — Resistance to Length-Extension** (1 Mark):

Unlike $H(K \mid\mid M)$, HMAC is **immune to length-extension attacks** because the secret $K$ is sandwiched between two hash applications. An attacker cannot recover the internal state without knowing $K$.

**Step 4 — Resistance to Key-Recovery** (1 Mark):

The secret $K$ is XORed with two distinct pads and then hashed twice. Recovering $K$ from $\text{HMAC}_K(M)$ requires inverting the outer hash, which is preimage-resistant — i.e., $O(2^n)$ work.

**Step 5 — Practical Deployment in IoT** (1 Mark):

- **CoAP + DTLS** uses HMAC-SHA256 for sensor authentication.
- **LoRaWAN** uses CMAC (AES-based) for join-acceptance integrity.
- **MQTT-SN with TLS 1.3** uses HMAC-SHA384 in the TLS record protocol.
- **NIST SP 800-107** recommends HMAC-SHA-256 for Federal IoT systems.

---

**Part B – Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **cryptographic hash functions and MACs**. Provide a detailed classification of MAC constructions (raw MAC, NMAC, HMAC, CMAC, GMAC) with their security properties. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **length-extension attack** on naive MAC constructions. Describe the attack procedure with a worked example, and discuss the structural defenses used by HMAC and SHA-3. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Part B – Question B

### Part (a) — Hash vs MAC and MAC Classification (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Conceptual Distinction** (1 Mark):

A **hash function** is a keyless one-way function providing only *integrity* — anyone can verify $H(M) = h$. A **MAC** is a *keyed* function providing *integrity + authentication* — only the key-holder can produce a valid tag.

| Property | Hash | MAC |
|----------|------|-----|
| Keyed | No | Yes (shared secret) |
| Symmetric | N/A | Yes |
| Authentication | No | Yes |
| Standard | SHA-2, SHA-3, BLAKE2 | HMAC, CMAC, GMAC, Poly1305 |

**Step 2 — Raw MAC (Unkeyed Hash + Key Prefix)** (1 Mark):

$\text{MAC}_K(M) = H(K \mid\mid M)$ — vulnerable to **length-extension**, **key recovery** if $H$ is weak, and **collision attacks**.

**Step 3 — NMAC (Nested MAC)** (1 Mark):

$\text{NMAC}_{K_1, K_2}(M) = H_{K_1}\big( H_{K_2}(M) \big)$ where $H_K(\cdot) = H(K \mid\mid \cdot)$ uses IV replacement with key. Provably secure PRF.

**Step 4 — HMAC (Hash-based MAC)** (1 Mark):

Standardized in RFC 2104 and FIPS 198-1. Uses double hashing with $ipad$ and $opad$. Most widely deployed MAC in IoT and TLS.

**Step 5 — CMAC (Cipher-based MAC)** (1 Mark):

CMAC uses AES (or 3DES) in CBC mode with a clever subkey generation scheme. Standardized in NIST SP 800-38B. Used in LoRaWAN, Zigbee, and IEEE 802.15.4.

**Step 6 — GMAC (Galois MAC)** (1 Mark):

GMAC uses AES in Galois/Counter Mode (GCM), providing both authentication and encryption. Extremely fast, parallelizable — ideal for high-throughput IoT gateways. Output: 128-bit tag.

**Step 7 — Poly1305** (1 Mark):

A one-time MAC based on universal hashing and polynomial evaluation over $\text{GF}(2^{130} - 5)$. Combined with ChaCha20 in TLS 1.3 for IoT-cloud communications.

**Summary Table**:

| MAC Type | Underlying Primitive | Output | IoT Suitability |
|----------|----------------------|--------|-----------------|
| Raw $H(K \mid\mid M)$ | Hash | $n$ bits | **Poor** (length-extension) |
| NMAC | Hash | $n$ bits | Theoretical only |
| **HMAC** | Hash | $n$ bits | **Excellent** |
| **CMAC** | Block cipher | $n$ bits | Excellent (constrained) |
| **GMAC** | Block cipher | 128 bits | Excellent (high-speed) |
| Poly1305 | Universal hash | 128 bits | Excellent (TLS 1.3) |

---

### Part (b) — Length-Extension Attack and Defenses (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Vulnerability Statement** (1 Mark):

If a MAC is constructed as $\text{MAC}_K(M) = H(K \mid\mid M)$ using a Merkle–Damgård hash (SHA-256, SHA-512, MD5, SHA-1), an attacker can extend the message and compute a valid MAC for the extended message *without knowing $K$*.

**Step 2 — Preconditions** (1 Mark):

The attacker must know:
- The MAC tag $T = H(K \mid\mid M)$
- The length of the original message $M$ (i.e., $\vert M \vert$)

**Step 3 — Attack Procedure (Worked Example)** (3 Marks):

Suppose Alice sends $M = \text{"transfer 100 USD to Bob"}$ and the tag is $T = H(K \mid\mid M)$.

1. The Merkle–Damgård construction processes blocks as:
$$
H_0 = IV, \quad H_{i+1} = f(H_i, M_i), \quad H(K \mid\mid M) = H_n
$$
2. The attacker uses $H_n = T$ as the *initial state* and appends a new block $M' = \text{" AND 1000 USD to Eve"}$.
3. The attacker computes the padding $\text{pad}$ such that the new internal state is:
$$
H_{n+1} = f(T, M' \text{ with proper pad})
$$
4. The forged tag for $M' = M \mid\mid \text{pad} \mid\mid M'$ is $H_{n+1}$, which the receiver accepts as valid because it was generated using a state that *legitimately* arose from $H(K \mid\mid M)$.

**Step 4 — Defense 1: HMAC** (1 Mark):

HMAC hashes the key *twice* in a nested structure, ensuring that an attacker cannot reconstruct the internal state without knowing $K$. Even if the outer hash is length-extended, the attacker would need the inner hash result, which depends on the secret.

**Step 5 — Defense 2: SHA-3 / Keccak** (1 Mark):

SHA-3 uses the **sponge construction** with a 1600-bit internal state, of which only $n$ bits (256 for SHA3-256) are output. The remaining capacity bits are never revealed, making length-extension impossible. Thus, $H(K \mid\mid M)$ with SHA-3 is *not* vulnerable.

**Step 6 — Defense 3: Prefix-Free and Sufficient Hashing** (Optional 0.5 mark):

Appending the message length $L = \vert M \vert$ inside the hash (e.g., $H(K \mid\mid M \mid\mid L)$) prevents extension because the attacker cannot append blocks without changing the suffix.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Exam Answers**:
> 1. Confusing **preimage resistance** with **collision resistance** — they have different complexities. **(–1 mark)**
> 2. Writing "HMAC = $H(K \mid\mid M)$" — this is wrong; HMAC is a *nested* construction. **(–2 marks)**
> 3. Forgetting to mention that SHA-3 (Keccak) is **not** vulnerable to length-extension due to its sponge structure. **(–1 mark)**
> 4. Not providing numerical examples (e.g., $2^{64}$ collisions for MD5) — examiners love specific numbers. **(–0.5 mark)**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Three security properties** of a hash function: **Preimage Resistance**, **Second Preimage Resistance**, **Collision Resistance**.
- ✅ **Hierarchy**: Collision Resistance $\Rightarrow$ Second Preimage $\Rightarrow$ Preimage (strictly, not converse).
- ✅ **Birthday attack bound**: $q \approx 1.1774 \cdot 2^{n/2}$ — reduces collision search from $2^n$ to $2^{n/2}$.
- ✅ **MD5 is broken** (real-world collisions demonstrated); **SHA-1 is deprecated** (SHAttered attack, 2017); **SHA-256 and SHA-3 are recommended** in 2024.
- ✅ **MAC = keyless hash + shared secret key**; provides *integrity + authentication* (hash alone provides only integrity).
- ✅ **HMAC construction**: $H((K \oplus opad) \mid\mid H((K \oplus ipad) \mid\mid M))$ — RFC 2104.
- ✅ **HMAC security bound**: $\le \text{Adv}_{H}^{\text{PRF}} + \frac{q^2(\ell^2 + 1)}{2^{n+1}}$.
- ✅ **Length-extension attack** exploits Merkle–Damgård internals; HMAC and SHA-3 are immune.
- ✅ **MAC classifications**: Raw, NMAC, **HMAC** (hash-based), **CMAC** (cipher-based), **GMAC** (GCM), **Poly1305**.
- ✅ **EUF-CMA security** is the gold standard for MACs — Existential Unforgeability under Chosen-Message Attacks.
- ✅ **NIST 2024 IoT recommendations**: SHA-256 minimum, HMAC-SHA-256 for MAC, AES-GMAC for high-throughput.
- ✅ **Real-world IoT usage**: CoAP+DTLS, LoRaWAN (CMAC), TLS 1.3 (HMAC-SHA384, Poly1305), Zigbee (CMAC).
- ✅ **In exam derivations**: Always state assumptions, show all log steps, and include the $\sqrt{2\ln 2} \approx 1.18$ factor for birthday bounds.
- ✅ **Trapdoor property**: SHA-3 sponge is *not* vulnerable to length-extension; Merkle–Damgård hashes *are*.
- ✅ **Hash output size 2024**: NIST minimum is **$n \ge 256$ bits** for post-quantum IoT security.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Message Digest 5 (MD5)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Message Digest 5 (MD5)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Message Digest 5 (MD5)** is a widely used, **deterministic cryptographic hash function** standardized in **RFC 1321** (1992) by **Professor Ronald L. Rivest** of MIT. It accepts an input message of **arbitrary length** and produces a fixed-size **128-bit (16-byte) hash value**, conventionally represented as a **32-character hexadecimal number**.

The algorithm processes the input message in **512-bit (64-byte) blocks** through **4 rounds** of **16 operations each** (totaling **64 operations** per block), using a **Merkle–Damgård iterative construction** and a **non-linear mixing function** that operates on a 4-word (A, B, C, D) **32-bit state buffer**.

### 1.1.1 Core Functional Properties (Five Pillars of MD5)

| # | Property | Description |
|---|----------|-------------|
| 1 | **Deterministic** | The same input message **always** produces the same 128-bit digest. |
| 2 | **Fixed-Length Output** | Output is always **128 bits (32 hex characters)** regardless of input size. |
| 3 | **One-Way (Pre-image Resistance)** | Computationally infeasible to recover the original message from its digest. |
| 4 | **Avalanche Effect** | A change of **one bit** in the input changes approximately **half** the output bits (~64 bits). |
| 5 | **Collision Resistance** | Hard to find two distinct messages $M_1 \neq M_2$ such that $\text{MD5}(M_1) = \text{MD5}(M_2)$. *(Note: broken since 2004 — see §1.4)* |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Digital Fingerprint of Data"**

Imagine a **massive library with billions of books**, and you need a unique identifier for every book. Rather than recording the entire book, you create a **biometric fingerprint** that:
- Looks completely different for even slightly modified books (a single torn page changes the fingerprint dramatically).
- Is short and uniform in size (just **32 characters** for MD5).
- Cannot be reverse-engineered to reconstruct the original book.

**MD5 is the "biometric fingerprint" for digital data.** The same analogy extends to:
- 🧾 Verifying that a downloaded file (e.g., an IoT firmware update) has not been tampered with.
- 📧 Generating checksums for **message integrity** in IoT communication.
- 🔐 Storing password hashes (though now discouraged due to collisions).

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Parameters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metrics of MD5 (Must Memorize for KTU Exam):**
> - **Digest Length:** **128 bits** (4 × 32-bit words: $A, B, C, D$)
> - **Block Size:** **512 bits**
> - **Rounds per Block:** **4 rounds**
> - **Operations per Round:** **16 operations**
> - **Total Operations per Block:** **64 operations**
> - **Number of Rounds:** **4 (FF, GG, HH, II)**
> - **Auxiliary Functions:** $F, G, H, I$ (one per round)
> - **Maximum Message Length:** $2^{64}$ bits
> - **Standard:** RFC 1321 (April 1992)

---

## 1.4 Security Status (KTU-Important Context)

> [!WARNING]
> **MD5 is NO LONGER considered cryptographically secure for:**
> - Digital signatures
> - SSL/TLS certificates
> - Password hashing (use bcrypt/Argon2 instead)
>
> **It IS still safely used for:**
> - Non-cryptographic checksums (file integrity)
> - Legacy IoT firmware verification (with secondary checks)
> - Legacy database deduplication
> - Hash tables & data structures

**Historical Collisions:** In **2004**, Wang et al. demonstrated practical MD5 collisions. In **2012**, the **Flame malware** exploited an MD5 collision to forge Microsoft code-signing certificates.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 High-Level Algorithm Pipeline

MD5 can be understood as a **five-stage pipeline**:

1. **Stage 1 — Padding:** Append a `1`-bit, then `0`-bits, until message length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.
2. **Stage 2 — Length Append:** Append the original message length as a **64-bit little-endian integer**.
3. **Stage 3 — Initialize State Buffer:** Set $A, B, C, D$ to fixed 32-bit magic constants.
4. **Stage 4 — Block Processing:** For each 512-bit block, execute 4 rounds of 16 operations each.
5. **Stage 5 — Output:** Concatenate final $A \Vert B \Vert C \Vert D$ as the **128-bit digest**.

---

## 2.2 The Four Auxiliary Boolean Functions

MD5 uses **four non-linear functions**, each operating on **3 inputs** $B, C, D$ and producing a **32-bit output**. These are the **heart of MD5's confusion**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(B, C, D) &= (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D) \\
G(B, C, D) &= (B \land D) \lor (C \land \lnot D) \\
H(B, C, D) &= B \oplus C \oplus D \\
I(B, C, D) &= C \oplus (B \lor \lnot D)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> - **F (Round 1):** "If" — selects $C$ if $B$ is true, else $D$. Acts like a conditional multiplexer.
> - **G (Round 2):** "Majority" — selects bits based on a different combination.
> - **H (Round 3):** "Parity" — bitwise XOR (confusion by symmetry).
> - **I (Round 4):** "Odd Function" — introduces asymmetry to break linearity.

---

## 2.3 Initial State Buffer Constants (Magic Words)

The four **32-bit registers** are initialized using the **hexadecimal parts of $\sqrt{2}$** (a clever choice that ensures nothing-up-my-sleeve numbers):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \texttt{0x01234567} \\
B_0 &= \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \\
C_0 &= \texttt{0xFEDCBA98} \\
D_0 &= \texttt{0x76543210}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** The first three values are derived from the fractional parts of $\sin(i)$ for $i=1,2,3$ (in some literature) — Rivest used $\sqrt{2}$ for the initial values. These are **publicly verifiable** constants.

---

## 2.4 Round Constants $T[i]$ — The 64 Magic Numbers

MD5 uses **64 precomputed constants** $T[1], T[2], \ldots, T[64]$, each being the **integer part of $4294967296 \cdot \vert \sin(i)\vert$** (i.e., $2^{32} \cdot \vert \sin(i)\vert$) for $i = 1$ to $64$.

$$
T[i] = \lfloor 2^{32} \cdot \vert \sin(i) \vert \rfloor
$$

**Sample values (KTU-memorize these):**

| $i$ | $\sin(i)$ | $T[i]$ (hex) |
|-----|-----------|---------------|
| 1 | 0.8414709848 | 0xd76aa478 |
| 2 | 0.9092974268 | 0xe8c7b756 |
| 3 | 0.1411200081 | 0x242070db |
| ... | ... | ... |
| 64 | 0.9995736030 | 0xeb86d391 |

---

## 2.5 The Core MD5 Round Operation

Each of the **64 operations** uses the same template, varying only by **round function**, **message word**, **shift amount**, and **constant**:

$$
a = b + \text{LeftRotate}_{s}\bigl((a + g(b,c,d) + X[k] + T[i]),\ s\bigr)
$$

Where:
- $a, b, c, d$ are the four state words (rotated per operation).
- $g$ is one of $F, G, H, I$ depending on the round.
- $X[k]$ is the $k$-th 32-bit word of the current 512-bit block.
- $T[i]$ is the $i$-th round constant.
- $s$ is a **per-round shift amount** (4 different shifts per round).

---

## 2.6 Shift Amounts Per Round (Per Operation)

Each round uses **4 distinct left-rotate amounts**, repeated cyclically:

| Round | Auxiliary Function | Shift Pattern |
|-------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | F | 7, 12, 17, 22 |
| 2 | G | 5, 9, 14, 20 |
| 3 | H | 4, 11, 16, 23 |
| 4 | I | 6, 10, 15, 21 |

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Comprehensive)

| Parameter / Formula | Value / Description | Use Case |
|---------------------|---------------------|----------|
| Digest Size $L$ | $128$ bits | Total hash length |
| Block Size $B$ | $512$ bits | Processing granularity |
| Padding bits appended | $1$ followed by zeros | Make $\vert M \vert \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ |
| Length encoded | $64$-bit little-endian integer | Appended after padding |
| Number of rounds $R$ | $4$ | Sequential processing |
| Operations per block $N$ | $64 = 4 \times 16$ | Total non-linear mixing steps |
| Initial state $A_0$ | $\texttt{0x01234567}$ | Magic constant |
| Initial state $B_0$ | $\texttt{0x89ABCDEF}$ | Magic constant |
| Initial state $C_0$ | $\texttt{0xFEDCBA98}$ | Magic constant |
| Initial state $D_0$ | $\texttt{0x76543210}$ | Magic constant |
| Round function F | $(B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)$ | Round 1 |
| Round function G | $(B \land D) \lor (C \land \lnot D)$ | Round 2 |
| Round function H | $B \oplus C \oplus D$ | Round 3 |
| Round function I | $C \oplus (B \lor \lnot D)$ | Round 4 |
| Round constants $T[i]$ | $T[i] = \lfloor 2^{32} \cdot \vert \sin(i)\vert \rfloor$ | $i = 1$ to $64$ |
| Operation formula | $a \leftarrow b + \text{ROTL}^s(a + g + X[k] + T[i])$ | Every step |
| Max input length | $2^{64}$ bits | Theoretical limit |
| Algorithm year | 1992 | RFC 1321 |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility in Engineering (Especially IoT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an IoT Engineer Must Understand MD5:**
>
> 1. **Firmware Integrity Verification:** IoT devices often download OTA (Over-The-Air) updates; MD5 checksums verify the update has not been corrupted or tampered with in transit.
> 2. **Resource-Constrained Authentication:** MD5 requires minimal RAM/ROM compared to SHA-256, making it historically attractive for **sensor nodes** (though SHA-256 is now preferred).
> 3. **Data Deduplication:** IoT gateways use hash digests to identify duplicate sensor readings.
> 4. **Password Hashing (Legacy):** Many legacy IoT systems still store MD5 hashes — knowing the algorithm is essential for **security auditing**.
> 5. **Digital Forensics:** Investigators use MD5 to prove evidentiary data has not been altered.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Algorithm Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Stage-by-Stage Detailed Walkthrough

### **STAGE 1: Message Padding**

**Goal:** Pad the input message $M$ so that its length $\ell \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.

**Step 1.1:** Append a single `1` bit to the end of $M$.

**Step 1.2:** Append `0` bits until the message length is exactly **64 bits short of a multiple of 512**.

**Step 1.3:** The padding ensures that **after appending the length** in Stage 2, the total is a multiple of 512.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$
\text{Padded length} = \ell + 1 + k \cdot 8
$$

Where $k$ is the smallest non-negative integer such that:

$$
(\ell + 1 + k + 64) \equiv 0 \pmod{512}
$$

Equivalently:

$$
\text{Total bits} \equiv 0 \pmod{512}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Minimum padding:** Always **at least 1 bit** (the `1`) + **64 bits** (for length). So minimum padding is **65 bits**, ensuring the message can be expanded even when $\ell \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**

Suppose input message $M$ has length $\ell = 1000$ bits.

1. Append `1`: length becomes $1001$ bits.
2. We need total $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ **before** appending length.
3. Find smallest $k$ such that $1001 + k \equiv 448 \pmod{512}$.
4. $1001 \mod 512 = 489$. Need to reach $448$: $448 - 489 = -41 \equiv 471 \pmod{512}$.
5. So append **471 zero bits**. Total before length = $1001 + 471 = 1472 = 448 + 2 \cdot 512$ ✓

---

### **STAGE 2: Append Length**

Append the **64-bit representation of the original message length** $\ell$ (in bits), encoded as a **little-endian** 64-bit integer.

$$
\ell = \ell_{63}\ell_{62}\ldots\ell_{1}\ell_{0}
$$

Appended as 8 bytes (low byte first).

**Why little-endian?** MD5 was designed for little-endian architectures (Intel x86), but the algorithm works correctly on any architecture by converting to little-endian before processing.

**Final Padded Length:**

$$
L_{\text{padded}} = L_{\text{original}} + \text{padding bits} + 64 \equiv 0 \pmod{512}
$$

---

### **STAGE 3: Initialize MD Buffer**

Set the four 32-bit registers $A, B, C, D$ to the magic constants:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \texttt{0x01234567} \\
B &= \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \\
C &= \texttt{0xFEDCBA98} \\
D &= \texttt{0x76543210}
\end{aligned}
$$

These are stored in **little-endian** byte order in memory.

---

### **STAGE 4: Process Message in 512-bit Blocks**

For each 512-bit block $M^{(1)}, M^{(2)}, \ldots, M^{(N)}$:

#### **Step 4.1:** Divide the current 512-bit block into **16 32-bit words** $M[0], M[1], \ldots, M[15]$.

#### **Step 4.2:** Copy current state into local variables (to be restored later):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &\leftarrow A \\
b &\leftarrow B \\
c &\leftarrow C \\
d &\leftarrow D
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 4.3:** Execute **Round 1 (FF)** — 16 operations using auxiliary function $F$:

For $i = 0, 1, \ldots, 15$:

$$
a = b + \text{LeftRotate}_{s_i}\bigl((a + F(b,c,d) + M[k_i] + T[i+1]),\ s_i\bigr)
$$

Where:
- $s_i$ follows the cycle $[7, 12, 17, 22]$ for $i = 0, 1, 2, 3$.
- $k_i = i$ (direct message word access).
- After each operation, **rotate the variables**: $(a,b,c,d) \to (d,a,b,c)$.

#### **Step 4.4:** Execute **Round 2 (GG)** — 16 operations using $G$:

For $i = 16, 17, \ldots, 31$:

$$
a = b + \text{LeftRotate}_{s_i}\bigl((a + G(b,c,d) + M[k_i] + T[i+1]),\ s_i\bigr)
$$

Where:
- $s_i$ follows the cycle $[5, 9, 14, 20]$.
- $k_i = (5i + 1) \mod 16$ (a non-sequential access pattern).

#### **Step 4.5:** Execute **Round 3 (HH)** — 16 operations using $H$:

For $i = 32, 33, \ldots, 47$:

$$
a = b + \text{LeftRotate}_{s_i}\bigl((a + H(b,c,d) + M[k_i] + T[i+1]),\ s_i\bigr)
$$

Where:
- $s_i$ follows the cycle $[4, 11, 16, 23]$.
- $k_i = (3i + 5) \mod 16$.

#### **Step 4.6:** Execute **Round 4 (II)** — 16 operations using $I$:

For $i = 48, 49, \ldots, 63$:

$$
a = b + \text{LeftRotate}_{s_i}\bigl((a + I(b,c,d) + M[k_i] + T[i+1]),\ s_i\bigr)
$$

Where:
- $s_i$ follows the cycle $[6, 10, 15, 21]$.
- $k_i = (7i) \mod 16$.

#### **Step 4.7:** Add the saved local state back to the global registers (Davies-Meyer-like construction):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\leftarrow A + a \\
B &\leftarrow B + b \\
C &\leftarrow C + c \\
D &\leftarrow D + d
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **STAGE 5: Output**

After processing **all** $N$ blocks, the final digest is the concatenation:

$$
\text{MD5}(M) = A \Vert B \Vert C \Vert D
$$

(Each word is 32 bits, total = 128 bits.)

---

## 3.2 Detailed Numerical Worked Example (Truncated 1-Block Hash)

**Input:** Message = `"abc"` (ASCII: 0x61 0x62 0x63, length = 24 bits)

### Step A — Padding:
- Length = 24 bits.
- We need length $\equiv 448 \pmod{512}$ before appending length.
- $448 - 24 = 424$ zero bits must be added (but first we add the `1` bit).
- Total padded (without length) = $24 + 1 + 423 = 448$ bits? Wait: $1 + k = 424 \Rightarrow k = 423$ zero bits.
- Total padded before length = $24 + 1 + 423 = 448$ ✓
- Append 64-bit length ($= 24$) → final block = $448 + 64 = 512$ bits. ✓

### Step B — Initialize Buffer:
$$
\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \texttt{0x01234567} \\
B_0 &= \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \\
C_0 &= \texttt{0xFEDCBA98} \\
D_0 &= \texttt{0x76543210}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step C — Process Round 1 (FF), Operation 1:

$M[0] = 0x61626380$ (the `abc` followed by padding bit and zeros)
$M[1..14] = 0x00000000$
$M[15] = 0x00000018$ (= 24, the original length)

Using auxiliary function:
$$
F(B,C,D) = (B \land C) \lor (\lnot B \land D)
$$

Compute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a &= A_0 = \texttt{0x01234567} \\
b &= B_0 = \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \\
c &= C_0 = \texttt{0xFEDCBA98} \\
d &= D_0 = \texttt{0x76543210}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
F(b,c,d) = (\texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \land \texttt{0xFEDCBA98}) \lor (\lnot \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \land \texttt{0x76543210})
$$

Computing bitwise:
- $\texttt{0x89ABCDEF} \land \texttt{0xFEDCBA98} = \texttt{0x88888888}$
- $\lnot \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} = \texttt{0x76543210}$
- $\texttt{0x76543210} \land \texttt{0x76543210} = \texttt{0x76543210}$
- $F = \texttt{0x88888888} \lor \texttt{0x76543210} = \texttt{0xFEFCBA98}$

Now compute:
$$
F + M[0] + T[1] = \texttt{0xFEFCBA98} + \texttt{0x61626380} + \texttt{0xD76AA478}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&= \texttt{0xFEFCBA98} + \texttt{0x61626380} = \texttt{0x605F1E18} \\
&= \texttt{0x605F1E18} + \texttt{0xD76AA478} = \texttt{0x37C9C290}
\end{aligned}
$$

Add $a$ and rotate left by 7:
$$
a + \text{result} = \texttt{0x01234567} + \texttt{0x37C9C290} = \texttt{0x38ED07F7}
$$

$$
\text{ROTL}^{7}(\texttt{0x38ED07F7}) = \texttt{0xC76837F9}
$$

Add to $b$:
$$
b + \texttt{0xC76837F9} = \texttt{0x89ABCDEF} + \texttt{0xC76837F9} = \texttt{0x511405E8}
$$

The new $a$ is $\texttt{0x511405E8}$, and we rotate $(a,b,c,d) \to (d,a,b,c)$ for the next operation.

> [!NOTE]
> This example shows the **first** of 64 operations per block. A full MD5 computation of `"abc"` requires **64 operations** in this single block (since `"abc"` fits in one 512-bit block after padding).

### Step D — Final Digest for `"abc"`:

After all 64 operations and addition to the state, the well-known MD5 of `"abc"` is:

$$
\text{MD5}("abc") = \texttt{900150983CD24FB0D6963F7D28E17F72}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the MD5 hash of common test strings:
> - `MD5("")` = `D41D8CD98F00B204E9800998ECF8427E`
> - `MD5("abc")` = `900150983CD24FB0D6963F7D28E17F72`
> - `MD5("The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog")` = `9E107D9D372BB6826BD81D3542A419D6`

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Component Matters

| Component | Mathematical Role | Security Purpose | KTU Exam Relevance |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Padding | Ensures block alignment | Removes ambiguity in length | Often tested as "padding bits required" |
| Length Append | Prevents extension attacks | Domain separation | Why 64-bit length? |
| Initial Constants | Nothing-up-my-sleeve | Removes weak starting point | What are the magic constants? |
| Auxiliary Functions $F,G,H,I$ | Non-linearity | Confusion | Define each function |
| Round Constants $T[i]$ | Pseudo-randomness | Diffusion | How are they computed? |
| Shift Amounts $s$ | Bit diffusion | Avalanche effect | List the shift patterns |
| Addition mod $2^{32}$ | Arithmetic mixing | Diffusion | Why mod $2^{32}$? |
| Left Rotation $\text{ROTL}^s$ | Permutation | Diffusion | Why rotate? |
| 64 Operations | Iteration depth | Pre-image resistance | How many per block? |
| Block Chaining | Merkle–Damgård | Collision resistance | Why chaining? |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level MD5 Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph input ["INPUT MESSAGE M (Arbitrary Length)"]
        M1["Message Bytes"]
    end

    subgraph pad ["STAGE 1: PADDING"]
        P1["Append '1' bit"]
        P2["Append '0' bits until length ≡ 448 mod 512"]
        P1 --> P2
    end

    subgraph lapp ["STAGE 2: LENGTH APPEND"]
        L1["Append 64-bit original length L (little-endian)"]
    end

    subgraph init ["STAGE 3: INITIALIZE BUFFER"]
        I1["A = 0x01234567"]
        I2["B = 0x89ABCDEF"]
        I3["C = 0xFEDCBA98"]
        I4["D = 0x76543210"]
    end

    subgraph block ["STAGE 4: BLOCK PROCESSING LOOP"]
        direction TB
        B1["Divide 512-bit block into 16 words M0..M15"]
        B2["Save state: a=A, b=B, c=C, d=D"]
        B3["ROUND 1 (FF): 16 ops with F"]
        B4["ROUND 2 (GG): 16 ops with G"]
        B5["ROUND 3 (HH): 16 ops with H"]
        B6["ROUND 4 (II): 16 ops with I"]
        B7["Update: A+=a, B+=b, C+=c, D+=d"]
        B1 --> B2 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6 --> B7
        B7 -->|"Next block"| B1
    end

    subgraph out ["STAGE 5: OUTPUT"]
        O1["Digest = A || B || C || D"]
        O2["128-bit hexadecimal output"]
        O1 --> O2
    end

    M1 --> P1
    P2 --> L1
    L1 --> I1
    L1 --> I2
    L1 --> I3
    L1 --> I4
    I1 --> B1
    I2 --> B1
    I3 --> B1
    I4 --> B1
    B7 --> O1
```

## 4.2 Detailed Single-Operation Schematic (The Heart of MD5)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph inputs ["INPUTS"]
        A["a (32-bit)"]
        B["b (32-bit)"]
        C["c (32-bit)"]
        D["d (32-bit)"]
        XK["X[k] (32-bit message word)"]
        TI["T[i] (32-bit round constant)"]
        S["s (shift amount)"]
    end

    subgraph gfunc ["AUXILIARY FUNCTION g"]
        G["g(B,C,D) = F or G or H or I"]
    end

    subgraph compute ["CORE COMPUTATION"]
        ADD1["a + g(B,C,D)"]
        ADD2["+ X[k]"]
        ADD3["+ T[i]"]
        ROT["Left Rotate by s bits"]
        ADD4["+ b"]
    end

    subgraph outputs ["OUTPUT"]
        NEW_A["New value of 'a'"]
        ROTATE["Rotate roles: (a,b,c,d) -> (d,a,b,c)"]
    end

    A --> ADD1
    G --> ADD1
    ADD1 --> ADD2
    XK --> ADD2
    ADD2 --> ADD3
    TI --> ADD3
    ADD3 --> ROT
    S --> ROT
    ROT --> ADD4
    B --> ADD4
    ADD4 --> NEW_A
    NEW_A --> ROTATE
```

## 4.3 Round Structure Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph R1 ["ROUND 1: FF Operations (16 steps)"]
        R1F["F(B,C,D) = (B AND C) OR NOT B AND D"]
        R1S["Shifts: 7, 12, 17, 22 (cyclic)"]
        R1K["k = i (direct index)"]
    end

    subgraph R2 ["ROUND 2: GG Operations (16 steps)"]
        R2F["G(B,C,D) = (B AND D) OR C AND NOT D"]
        R2S["Shifts: 5, 9, 14, 20 (cyclic)"]
        R2K["k = (5i + 1) mod 16"]
    end

    subgraph R3 ["ROUND 3: HH Operations (16 steps)"]
        R3F["H(B,C,D) = B XOR C XOR D"]
        R3S["Shifts: 4, 11, 16, 23 (cyclic)"]
        R3K["k = (3i + 5) mod 16"]
    end

    subgraph R4 ["ROUND 4: II Operations (16 steps)"]
        R4F["I(B,C,D) = C XOR B OR NOT D"]
        R4S["Shifts: 6, 10, 15, 21 (cyclic)"]
        R4K["k = (7i) mod 16"]
    end

    R1 --> R2 --> R3 --> R4
```

## 4.4 Merkle–Damgård Iterative Construction

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph blk1 ["BLOCK 1 (512 bits)"]
        B1["M[1]"]
    end
    subgraph blk2 ["BLOCK 2 (512 bits)"]
        B2["M[2]"]
    end
    subgraph blkN ["BLOCK N (512 bits)"]
        BN["M[N]"]
    end

    subgraph cf1 ["COMPRESSION FUNCTION CF"]
        CF1["State_i+1 = CF(State_i, M[i])"]
    end

    subgraph cf2 ["COMPRESSION FUNCTION CF"]
        CF2["State_i+1 = CF(State_i, M[i])"]
    end

    subgraph cfN ["COMPRESSION FUNCTION CF"]
        CFN["State_i+1 = CF(State_i, M[i])"]
    end

    subgraph digest ["FINAL OUTPUT"]
        OUT["128-bit Digest"]
    end

    IV["IV = (A0,B0,C0,D0)"] --> CF1
    B1 --> CF1
    CF1 -->|"Intermediate State"| CF2
    B2 --> CF2
    CF2 -->|"Intermediate State"| CFN
    BN --> CFN
    CFN --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Every compression function takes the **previous state** (IV for the first block) and the **current 512-bit message block** as input, and produces a **new 128-bit state**. This is the classic **Merkle–Damgård** construction that powers MD5, SHA-1, and SHA-2.
