A *Lightweight Block Cipher (LBC)* is a symmetric-key block cipher optimized for resource-constrained environments (RFID, sensors, IoT). It minimizes Gate Equivalents (GE), power, and energy per bit while providing adequate (typically 80–128 bit) security.

**Two ISO/IEC 29192-2 ciphers:**
1. **PRESENT** — 64-bit block, 80/128-bit key, SPN, 31 rounds, ~1075 GE.
2. **CLEFIA** — 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit key, GFN-4, 18/22/26 rounds, ~3000 GE.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`**
**Q: Differentiate between an SPN-based and a Feistel-based lightweight block cipher with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | SPN (e.g., PRESENT) | Feistel (e.g., SIMON) |
|---|---|---|
| Block coverage per round | Full block | Half block |
| Round function | Must be invertible (S-box + Permutation) | Need not be invertible |
| Hardware complexity | Lower (4-bit S-box) | Higher (rotations + AND) |
| Diffusion speed | Faster | Slower |
| Example | PRESENT | SIMON, LBlock |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of the PRESENT lightweight block cipher**. Explain its round function in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **PRESENT key schedule** for the 80-bit key version. How is the round counter incorporated and why? **(7 marks)**

### Question A — Option 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model]`**

**(a)** Explain the design of the **SIMON lightweight block cipher** with its round function. List the various block and key sizes supported. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare SIMON with SPECK. Why is SIMON preferred for pure hardware implementations while SPECK is preferred for software? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solutions (Question A — Option 1)

### (a) Solution: PRESENT Architecture

The **PRESENT cipher** is an SPN-based lightweight block cipher designed by Bogdanov et al. in 2007 and standardized in **ISO/IEC 29192-2**.

**Parameters:**
- Block size: **64 bits**
- Key size: **80 bits** (or 128 bits)
- Number of rounds: **31**
- Structure: **SPN**

**Round Function (each of the 31 rounds):**
1. **addRoundKey:** XOR the 64-bit state with the 64-bit round subkey $K_i$.
2. **sBoxLayer:** Apply a 4-bit → 4-bit S-box to 16 parallel 4-bit nibbles.
   $$S = s(b_{63..60}) \parallel s(b_{59..56}) \parallel \ldots \parallel s(b_{3..0})$$
   The S-box (hex) is: `C 5 6 B 9 0 A D 3 E F 8 4 7 1 2`.
3. **pLayer:** Permute bits using $P(i) = 16i \bmod 63$ for $i = 0, \ldots, 62$, and $P(63) = 63$.

The final round omits the permutation layer (classical SPN trick to make encryption/decryption symmetric in hardware).

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[64-bit State] --> B[addRoundKey XOR Ki]
    B --> C[16 parallel 4-bit S-boxes]
    C --> D[Bit Permutation Pi = 16i mod 63]
    D --> E[Next Round]
```

**Key properties:**
- Each round consumes only **16 S-boxes** + 64 XOR gates + 64 wire crossings.
- Bit-permutation is essentially **free in hardware** (just routing).
- Total hardware: **~1075 GE** for 80-bit key — among the smallest published ciphers.

---

### (a) Solution: SIMON Architecture

**SIMON** is a family of lightweight Feistel ciphers designed by the **NSA (2013)**. It is purely hardware-optimized, using **no S-boxes**.

**Block / Key sizes (10 variants):**
| Block | Key | Rounds |
|---|---|---|
| 32 | 64 | 32 |
| 48 | 72 / 96 | 36 / 36 |
| 64 | 96 / 128 | 42 / 44 |
| 96 | 96 / 144 | 52 / 54 |
| 128 | 128 / 192 / 256 | 68 / 69 / 72 |

**Round function (for 64-bit block):**
State is split into two 32-bit halves $x$ (left) and $y$ (right):
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_{\text{new}} &= y \\
y_{\text{new}} &= x \oplus f(y) \oplus k_i
\end{aligned}
$$
where
$$
f(y) = ((y \ll 1) \wedge (y \ll 8)) \oplus (y \ll 2)
$$
and $k_i$ is the round key.

**Note:** $f$ is a simple combination of bitwise AND and XOR on rotated versions of $y$ — making the round function trivially small in hardware. There is no S-box.

---

### (b) Solution: PRESENT Key Schedule (80-bit version)

The 80-bit master key is loaded into a register $K = K_{79} K_{78} \ldots K_0$. After each round, the register is updated as follows:

**Step 1 — Rotate left by 61 bit positions:**
$$K \leftarrow K_{18} K_{17} \ldots K_0 \, K_{79} K_{78} \ldots K_{19}$$

**Step 2 — Apply S-box to the 4 MSBs:**
$$[K_{79} K_{78} K_{77} K_{76}] \leftarrow S[K_{79} K_{78} K_{77} K_{76}]$$

**Step 3 — XOR round counter $i$ into bits $K_{19}K_{18}K_{17}K_{16}K_{15}$:**
$$[K_{19} K_{18} K_{17} K_{16} K_{15}] \leftarrow [K_{19} K_{18} K_{17} K_{16} K_{15}] \oplus i$$

**Step 4 — Extract 64-bit round key:**
$$K_i = \text{leftmost 64 bits of } K$$

**Why XOR the round counter?**
- Prevents *slide attacks* (where the adversary reuses a previous round's state).
- Ensures every round key is **unique** even if the master key is constant.
- Uses 5 bits because PRESENT has only 31 rounds — counter never overflows.

---

### (b) Solution: SIMON vs SPECK

| Property | SIMON | SPECK |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | Balanced Feistel | ARX (Add–Rotate–XOR) |
| **Round function** | AND, XOR, rotations | Modular addition, XOR, rotations |
| **S-boxes** | None | None |
| **Hardware** | Very small (no adders) | Slightly larger (needs 32/64-bit adders) |
| **Software** | Slower | Much faster (additions map to native CPU instructions) |
| **Best fit** | ASIC / FPGA / RFID tags | 8/16/32-bit microcontrollers |

**Why SIMON is hardware-favored:**
- The Feistel round uses **no arithmetic** — only bitwise operations.
- Bitwise AND, XOR, and rotations are *constant-cost* in CMOS.
- The round function $f(y) = ((y\!\ll\!1) \wedge (y\!\ll\!8)) \oplus (y\!\ll\!2)$ maps to ~50 GE per round.

**Why SPECK is software-favored:**
- Modular addition maps to a *single-cycle* instruction on most CPUs.
- ARX ciphers have naturally strong resistance to linear and differential cryptanalysis.
- SPECK achieves excellent throughput on 8/16/32-bit embedded processors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Many students **omit the round counter step** in the PRESENT key schedule. This costs 2 marks.
> - Students often confuse the PRESENT S-box: the correct mapping is `C 5 6 B 9 0 A D 3 E F 8 4 7 1 2`, not the AES one. Mixing them up loses 3 marks.
> - When asked for the number of rounds, write **31**, not 32. PRESENT uses 31 rounds + 1 final key XOR.
> - For SIMON, you must specify the **block size first, then the key size** (e.g., SIMON 64/128 means 64-bit block, 128-bit key).
> - The PRESENT permutation $P(i) = 16i \bmod 63$ is **bit 63 stays fixed** — students often forget this and lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Last-Minute Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick-Fire Recall — Master These for KTU 2024**

- **LBC Definition:** A symmetric cipher optimized for low GE, low power, low energy per bit on constrained devices.
- **Standardizing Body:** **ISO/IEC 29192** — covers both lightweight block ciphers and stream ciphers.
- **PRESENT:** 64-bit block, 80/128-bit key, **31 rounds**, SPN, 4-bit S-box, $P(i) = 16i \bmod 63$, ~1075 GE.
- **PRESENT S-box hex:** `C 5 6 B 9 0 A D 3 E F 8 4 7 1 2`. $\text{DP}_{\max} = 2^{-2}$.
- **PRESENT Key Schedule:** Rotate 61 → S-box on MSB nibble → XOR 5-bit round counter → Extract 64-bit subkey.
- **CLEFIA:** 128-bit block, 128/192/256-bit key, **GFN-4**, 18/22/26 rounds, two F-functions $F_0, F_1$.
- **SIMON:** Feistel, no S-box, AND-XOR-rotation round function, multiple block/key variants (smallest: 32/64).
- **SPECK:** ARX, software-friendly, uses modular addition.
- **AES** is **not** a lightweight cipher — ~2400–3600 GE.
- **Why bit-permutation is "free":** No logic gates, only routing — does not consume active silicon area.
- **Matsui's bound on minimum rounds:** $r \geq 4 + \max(\text{DP}, \text{LP})^{-1}$.
- **Two metrics for cryptanalytic resistance:** Differential Probability (DP) and Linear Probability (LP). Lower = more secure.
- **Why round counter in key schedule?** Defeats **slide attacks**, ensures unique round keys.
- **Real-world picks:** PRESENT → passive RFID; CLEFIA → 128-bit IoT; SIMON → constrained hardware; SPECK → embedded software.
- **GFN vs Feistel:** GFN splits the block into $b > 2$ branches (CLEFIA uses $b=4$); standard Feistel uses 2.
- **Whitening key XOR** is the additional key XOR before the first and after the last round — PRESENT applies it once at the start.
- **Security vs Area trade-off:** 80-bit key = adequate for short-life RFID; 128-bit key = needed for long-life IoT.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonics:**
> - **PRESENT** = **P**lain **R**ound **E**ncryption **S**ubstitution **E**ven **N**ibble-**T**imes.
> - **CLEFIA** = **C**lever **L**ightweight **E**ncryption **F**or **I**oT **A**pplications.
> - **SIMON** = **S**imple **I**terative **M**anipulation **O**f **N**umbers (no S-box).
> - **SPECK** = **S**mall **P**ractical **E**ncryption **C**ipher **K**it (ARX family).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stream Cipher - Permutation Based
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Stream Cipher - Permutation Based

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stream Cipher (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):** A *symmetric key cipher* in which the plaintext digits (typically bits or bytes) are encrypted one at a time, and the encryption transformation varies with the position of the digit in the plaintext stream. The encryption algorithm is applied to each successive plaintext symbol to produce the corresponding ciphertext symbol, using a **pseudo-random keystream** generated from a secret key.

A **Permutation-Based Stream Cipher** is a subclass of stream ciphers in which the encryption is performed by applying a **bijective permutation function** to the plaintext symbol set, where the permutation is derived from (or controlled by) the secret key. Every key produces a unique permutation of the alphabet, and the inverse permutation is used for decryption.

For a binary stream cipher working on bits, the *permutation space* is the symmetric group $S_n$, and the *number of possible permutations* of $n$ symbols is $n!$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric Group $S_n$:** The set of all bijections (one-to-one, onto functions) from an $n$-element set to itself, under the operation of function composition. Its order is $\vert S_n \vert = n!$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you and your friend share a **shuffled deck of 52 playing cards** (the *secret key / permutation*). To send a hidden message:

1. The sender maps each letter of the alphabet (A–Z) to a card position (1–26, e.g., Hearts).
2. For every plaintext letter, the sender looks up its position in the deck and writes down the *card at that position* in the *key-permuted order*.
3. The receiver, holding the **identical shuffled deck**, reverses the lookup.

Because only a *permutation* of the original card order is used, every plaintext letter maps to a **unique ciphertext symbol** — no letter repeats, and the mapping is fully reversible. This is exactly how a permutation-based stream cipher operates: a secret permutation table replaces plaintext symbols with ciphertext symbols.

**Real-World IoT Analogy:** A smart home temperature sensor (IoT device) encrypts each 8-bit temperature reading by XOR-ing it with a key-bit generated from a permutation table. The hub uses the inverse permutation table (same key) to recover the reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Why Permutations?** Permutations are *bijective* by definition. This guarantees a **one-to-one mapping** between plaintext and ciphertext, which is a strict requirement for any lossless, reversible encryption scheme.

## 1.3 Key Distinguishing Properties

- **Symmetric Key**: The same secret key is used for both encryption and decryption.
- **Stream-oriented**: Operates on one symbol/bit at a time (low latency → ideal for IoT).
- **Permutation-based**: The substitution is governed by a *bijective permutation* of the symbol alphabet.
- **Memory-light**: Suits constrained IoT devices (no large block buffers needed).
- **Stateless / Stateful**: May be *synchronous* (keystream independent of plaintext/ciphertext) or *self-synchronizing* (keystream depends on previous ciphertext).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory — Step-by-Step

A permutation-based stream cipher can be decomposed into the following logical pipeline:

1. **Key Initialization**
   - The secret key $K$ (of length $k$ bits) is loaded into the cipher's state register.
   - A **Key Scheduling Algorithm (KSA)** expands $K$ into an initial permutation $P_0$ of the symbol alphabet.

2. **Permutation Generation (Keystream Update)**
   - A **Pseudo-Random Generation Algorithm (PRGA)** updates the permutation to $P_i$ at time $i$.
   - In simple ciphers, the same permutation $P$ is used for the entire stream (a *static* permutation).
   - In advanced ciphers (e.g., RC4), the permutation evolves per symbol (a *dynamic* permutation).

3. **Symbol Mapping (Encryption)**
   - For each plaintext symbol $p_i$ in the alphabet $\Sigma = \{0, 1, \ldots, n-1\}$:
   - The cipher outputs $c_i = P_i(p_i)$, i.e., the image of $p_i$ under the current permutation.
   - For binary stream ciphers operating on bits, $n = 2$ and the permutation space is just $S_2$ (essentially the XOR operation).

4. **Decryption (Inverse Permutation)**
   - The receiver, sharing the same secret key, regenerates the identical permutation sequence $\{P_i\}$.
   - Decryption applies the inverse permutation: $p_i = P_i^{-1}(c_i)$.

5. **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
   - **Why a permutation?** Bijectivity guarantees no two plaintext symbols map to the same ciphertext — a *necessary condition* for unambiguous decryption.
   - **Why evolving permutations?** A static permutation is vulnerable to known-plaintext attacks. A time-varying permutation produces a keystream that resists frequency analysis even on symbol alphabets (English, ASCII, etc.).
   - **Why XOR for binary ciphers?** XOR is its own inverse: $a \oplus b \oplus b = a$. This makes it the *only* bit-level permutation that is trivially reversible without an explicit inverse table.

## 2.2 Binary Additive Stream Cipher (The Core Model)

For an alphabet of size 2 (i.e., bits), the permutation is uniquely determined by a single keystream bit $k_i$:

$$c_i = p_i \oplus k_i$$

This is a **permutation of $\{0, 1\}$** — either the identity ($k_i = 0$) or the swap ($k_i = 1$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Vernam Cipher (One-Time Pad):** The theoretically unbreakable stream cipher where the keystream $K = (k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n)$ is (i) as long as the plaintext, (ii) truly random, and (iii) used only once. For $n$-bit plaintext, the key length must be exactly $n$ bits. Shannon proved that the one-time pad provides *perfect secrecy* under these conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Domain | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Binary Encryption | $c_i = p_i \oplus k_i$ | Bits ($0, 1$) | XOR is the bit-level permutation |
| 2 | Binary Decryption | $p_i = c_i \oplus k_i$ | Bits ($0, 1$) | Self-inverse property of XOR |
| 3 | Permutation Count | $\vert S_n \vert = n!$ | Count | Number of bijections on $n$ symbols |
| 4 | Keystream Symbol | $k_i = f(K, i)$ | Symbol | PRGA output at position $i$ |
| 5 | Modular Encryption | $c_i = (p_i + k_i) \bmod n$ | Mod $n$ | Additive cipher over alphabet $Z_n$ |
| 6 | Modular Decryption | $p_i = (c_i - k_i) \bmod n$ | Mod $n$ | Inverse of addition mod $n$ |
| 7 | Multiplicative Key | $k_i \in Z_n^*$ with $\gcd(k_i, n) = 1$ | Mod $n$ | Required for bijection in $Z_n$ |
| 8 | Shannon Entropy (Perfect Secrecy) | $H(P) = H(P \mid C)$ | Bits | One-time pad condition |
| 9 | Effective Key Length | $L_K \geq L_P$ | Bits | Mandatory for one-time pad |
| 10 | Permutation Cycle Length | $L_{\text{cycle}} \leq n$ | Steps | Each $k$-cycle repeats after $L_{\text{cycle}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Writing $c = p \cdot k \pmod n$ when $\gcd(k, n) > 1$. This produces a non-bijective mapping, and decryption becomes impossible for some ciphertext symbols. KTU examiners frequently test this with $n = 26$ (English alphabet): the only valid multiplicative keys are the 12 integers coprime with 26.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in IoT Engineering

- **Low-Latency Telemetry Encryption:** Sensors stream 8-bit ADC readings; permutation-based bitwise XOR adds negligible latency.
- **Resource-Constrained Devices:** ESP32, ARM Cortex-M0+ microcontrollers have < 32 KB RAM. Stream ciphers (no S-boxes, no large tables) fit comfortably.
- **Real-Time Audio/Video in Wearables:** Bluetooth LE encrypts voice packets with stream ciphers (e.g., E0 historically; modern LE uses AES-CCM with counter mode — also stream-like).
- **OTA Firmware Updates:** Lightweight keystreams authenticate and encrypt the firmware bitstream during wireless transfer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — From Permutation Notation to Bitwise XOR

We derive the equivalence between a binary permutation-based cipher and the XOR operation.

**Step 1: Define the alphabet.**
For binary stream ciphers:
$$\Sigma = \{0, 1\}$$

**Step 2: Enumerate all permutations of $\Sigma$.**

The symmetric group $S_2$ contains $2! = 2$ permutations:
$$\text{Id} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}, \quad \text{Swap} = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 \\ 1 & 0 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Map permutations to keystream bits.**
- If the keystream bit $k_i = 0$, we apply the identity permutation: $c_i = p_i$.
- If the keystream bit $k_i = 1$, we apply the swap permutation: $c_i = 1 - p_i$ (i.e., flip the bit).

**Step 4: Recognize the unifying operation.**
Both cases collapse into a single algebraic expression. For any bits $p_i, k_i \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
c_i = p_i \oplus k_i
$$

This is because:
- If $k_i = 0$: $p_i \oplus 0 = p_i$ (identity).
- If $k_i = 1$: $p_i \oplus 1 = 1 - p_i$ (swap / bit flip).

Hence, **the binary XOR operation is the algebraic realization of permutation-based encryption on bits**.

**Step 5: Verify self-inverse property (decryption).**
For any bits $a, b \in \{0, 1\}$:

$$
a \oplus b \oplus b = a \oplus (b \oplus b) = a \oplus 0 = a
$$

The XOR is involutory, so the same operation with the same key recovers the plaintext — no separate inverse table required.

> [!NOTE]
> **Implication for IoT:** The involutory nature of XOR means the encryption and decryption *hardware/software modules are identical*. This is why XOR-based stream ciphers are so popular on resource-constrained IoT chips — only one code path needs to be implemented and tested.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Worked Numerical Example (Modular Additive Cipher)

Encrypt the plaintext message $P = \text{HELLO}$ using a permutation-based additive stream cipher over the English alphabet ($n = 26$) with keystream $K = \text{XMKEY}$.

**Step 1: Map letters to integers (A = 0, B = 1, ..., Z = 25).**

$$
P = (7, 4, 11, 11, 14), \quad K = (23, 12, 10, 4, 24)
$$

**Step 2: Apply the encryption formula $c_i = (p_i + k_i) \bmod 26$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= (7 + 23) \bmod 26 = 30 \bmod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E \\
c_2 &= (4 + 12) \bmod 26 = 16 \bmod 26 = 16 \rightarrow Q \\
c_3 &= (11 + 10) \bmod 26 = 21 \bmod 26 = 21 \rightarrow V \\
c_4 &= (11 + 4) \bmod 26 = 15 \bmod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P \\
c_5 &= (14 + 24) \bmod 26 = 38 \bmod 26 = 12 \rightarrow M
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: State the ciphertext.**

$$
C = \text{EQVPM}
$$

**Step 4: Decrypt to verify the inverse permutation.**

Using $p_i = (c_i - k_i) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_1 &= (4 - 23) \bmod 26 = -19 \bmod 26 = 7 \rightarrow H \\
p_2 &= (16 - 12) \bmod 26 = 4 \rightarrow E \\
p_3 &= (21 - 10) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
p_4 &= (15 - 4) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
p_5 &= (12 - 24) \bmod 26 = -12 \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Confirm the recovered plaintext.**

$$
P_{\text{recovered}} = \text{HELLO} \;\checkmark
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Permutation Ciphers vs. Other Ciphers

| Property | Permutation Stream Cipher | Block Cipher (e.g., AES) | One-Time Pad |
|----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Operates on | 1 bit / symbol at a time | Fixed block (e.g., 128 bits) | 1 bit at a time |
| Memory Footprint | Very low | High (S-boxes, round keys) | Key = Plaintext length |
| Latency | Minimal (real-time) | Block-aligned delay | Minimal |
| Diffusion | Per-symbol | Full block | Perfect (if key is random) |
| IoT Suitability | Excellent | Moderate (needs hardware accel.) | Poor (key management) |
| Key Reuse Risk | High (keystream reuse leaks data) | Low | Catastrophic |
| Reversibility Mechanism | Inverse permutation $P^{-1}$ | Inverse round-key schedule | XOR self-inverse |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Permutation-Based Stream Cipher (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K[Secret Key K] --> KSA[Key Scheduling Algorithm KSA]
    KSA --> P0[Initial Permutation P0]
    P0 --> PRGA[Pseudo Random Generation Algorithm PRGA]
    PRGA --> KS[Keystream Symbol ki]
    PI[Plaintext Symbol pi] --> XOR[Permutation XOR Module]
    KS --> XOR
    XOR --> CI[Ciphertext Symbol ci]
    CI --> NET[IoT Network Channel]
    NET --> CI2[Received Ciphertext ci]
    CI2 --> XOR2[Inverse XOR with same ki]
    KS2[Regenerated Keystream ki] --> XOR2
    XOR2 --> PO[Recovered Plaintext pi]
```

## 4.2 Sequence Diagram — Encryption/Decryption Lifecycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Sender as IoT Sensor Node
    participant KSA as KSA Engine
    participant PRGA as PRGA Engine
    participant Channel as Wireless Channel
    participant Receiver as IoT Hub
    Sender->>KSA: Load Secret Key K
    KSA-->>Sender: Initial Permutation P0
    loop For each plaintext symbol pi
        Sender->>PRGA: Request keystream symbol ki
        PRGA-->>Sender: ki = PRGA(Pi-1, i)
        Sender->>Sender: ci = pi XOR ki
        Sender->>Channel: Transmit ci
        Channel->>Receiver: Deliver ci
        Receiver->>PRGA: Regenerate ki using same K
        PRGA-->>Receiver: ki (synchronized)
        Receiver->>Receiver: pi = ci XOR ki
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Modular Additive Cipher Permutation Table (n = 26)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Plaintext[Plaintext Alphabet]
        A0[A=0] --> B0[B=1]
        B0 --> C0[C=2]
        C0 --> D0[D=3]
        D0 --> E0[E=4]
    end
    subgraph Ciphertext[Ciphertext Alphabet after key K=3]
        A1[D=3] --> B1[E=4]
        B1 --> C1[F=5]
        C1 --> D1[G=6]
        D1 --> E1[H=7]
    end
    Plaintext -->|Permutation +3 mod 26| Ciphertext
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read the diagram:** Each plaintext letter is shifted forward by 3 positions (Caesar's cipher with $k=3$) to produce the ciphertext. The arrow from A→D means $P(A) = D$. This is the simplest instance of a *permutation-based stream cipher* on the English alphabet.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a stream cipher. How does a permutation-based stream cipher differ from a block cipher?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *stream cipher* is a symmetric key cipher that encrypts plaintext one bit or symbol at a time by combining each plaintext symbol with a corresponding keystream symbol produced by a pseudo-random generator (PRG). **(1 mark)**
In a *permutation-based* stream cipher, the encryption transformation is a bijection (permutation) on the symbol alphabet, derived from the secret key. **(1 mark)**
In contrast, a *block cipher* encrypts a fixed-size block of plaintext (e.g., 128 bits) as a single unit using a fixed, key-dependent substitution-permutation network. Block ciphers require padding and have higher latency; stream ciphers are real-time, low-memory, and ideal for IoT. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and prove the involutory property of XOR. Why is this property critical for stream ciphers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For any bits $a, b \in \{0, 1\}$, the XOR operation satisfies: $a \oplus b \oplus b = a$. **(1 mark)**
*Proof:* XOR is associative and $b \oplus b = 0$ for any bit, so $a \oplus (b \oplus b) = a \oplus 0 = a$. **(1 mark)**
This property means the same operation $c = p \oplus k$ and $p = c \oplus k$ can be used for both encryption and decryption. Hence, the hardware/software implementation is identical for both directions — a major advantage for resource-constrained IoT devices. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students are expected to attempt **one full question** (either Q. A or Q. B) and the choice is *internal* to the module.

---

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**A. (a) Explain the structure of a binary additive stream cipher with a neat block diagram. Derive the encryption and decryption equations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A *binary additive stream cipher* is a symmetric key stream cipher that operates on individual bits using the XOR operation. Its architecture is shown in the diagram below.

**Block diagram description (3 marks):**
The cipher consists of a key register holding the secret key $K$, a *keystream generator* (typically a Linear Feedback Shift Register, LFSR, or a keyed PRGA) that outputs a stream of bits $k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n$, and an XOR gate. Each plaintext bit $p_i$ enters the XOR gate together with the corresponding keystream bit $k_i$ to produce the ciphertext bit $c_i = p_i \oplus k_i$. On the receiver side, the same keystream is regenerated, and the identical XOR gate recovers $p_i = c_i \oplus k_i$.

**Encryption equation derivation (2 marks):**
The cipher applies a bit-level permutation selected by the keystream bit:
$$
c_i = p_i \oplus k_i, \quad k_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$
- If $k_i = 0$ → identity permutation (bit passes unchanged).
- If $k_i = 1$ → swap permutation (bit flipped).

**Decryption equation derivation (2 marks):**
Since XOR is its own inverse:
$$
p_i = c_i \oplus k_i = (p_i \oplus k_i) \oplus k_i = p_i
$$
The receiver, holding the same key $K$, regenerates the same keystream $\{k_i\}$ and applies XOR to recover the plaintext.

---

**A. (b) Encrypt the plaintext "ATTACK" using a permutation-based additive stream cipher with key "LEMON" over the English alphabet. Show all intermediate steps. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Map letters A=0, B=1, ..., Z=25.

$$
P = (0, 19, 19, 0, 2, 10), \quad K = (11, 4, 12, 14, 13)
$$

Note: Key has length 5, plaintext has length 6, so the key is repeated cyclically: $\text{LEMONLE}$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Concatenate the key to match plaintext length:
$$
K = (11, 4, 12, 14, 13, 11)
$$

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Apply encryption formula $c_i = (p_i + k_i) \bmod 26$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
c_1 &= (0 + 11) \bmod 26 = 11 \rightarrow L \\
c_2 &= (19 + 4) \bmod 26 = 23 \rightarrow X \\
c_3 &= (19 + 12) \bmod 26 = 31 \bmod 26 = 5 \rightarrow F \\
c_4 &= (0 + 14) \bmod 26 = 14 \rightarrow O \\
c_5 &= (2 + 13) \bmod 26 = 15 \rightarrow P \\
c_6 &= (10 + 11) \bmod 26 = 21 \rightarrow V
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** State the ciphertext:
$$
C = \text{LXFOPV}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Verify decryption using $p_i = (c_i - k_i) \bmod 26$ (students should demonstrate this and obtain "ATTACK").

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**B. (a) What is a permutation? Why must every cipher based on permutations be bijective? Show that the number of permutations on an alphabet of size $n$ is $n!$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A *permutation* of a finite set $S$ is a bijection (one-to-one and onto function) from $S$ onto itself. Formally, $\sigma: S \to S$ such that for all $x, y \in S$, $\sigma(x) = \sigma(y) \Rightarrow x = y$ (injectivity) and for every $z \in S$, there exists $x \in S$ with $\sigma(x) = z$ (surjectivity).

**Why bijective? (3 marks):**
- *Injectivity* ensures no two distinct plaintext symbols map to the same ciphertext symbol — without this, decryption is ambiguous and some plaintexts are unrecoverable.
- *Surjectivity* ensures every ciphertext symbol has a pre-image — without this, some ciphertext outputs never appear, leaking structural information about the plaintext.
- A *bijection* has a unique inverse $\sigma^{-1}$, which is the decryption function: $p = \sigma^{-1}(c)$.
- For binary ciphers, the only non-trivial bijections are the identity and the bit-flip, both expressible as $c = p \oplus k$.

**Proof that $\vert S_n \vert = n!$ (3 marks):**
- To define a permutation of $n$ elements, assign a unique image to each of the $n$ elements.
- For the first element, we have $n$ choices. For the second, $n - 1$ remaining choices. Continue inductively. By the multiplication principle:
$$
\vert S_n \vert = n \cdot (n-1) \cdot (n-2) \cdots 2 \cdot 1 = n!
$$

---

**B. (b) Discuss the One-Time Pad (Vernam Cipher) and explain Shannon's condition for perfect secrecy. Why is the OTP impractical for IoT, and what is the standard workaround? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**One-Time Pad definition (2 marks):**
The Vernam cipher (one-time pad) is a stream cipher in which the plaintext $P = (p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_n)$ is XOR-ed with a truly random keystream $K = (k_1, k_2, \ldots, k_n)$ of equal length:
$$
c_i = p_i \oplus k_i
$$
The key is used **exactly once** and then discarded. The receiver, holding an identical copy of $K$, decrypts via $p_i = c_i \oplus k_i$.

**Shannon's perfect secrecy condition (2 marks):**
A cipher achieves *perfect secrecy* if the ciphertext $C$ reveals **no information** about the plaintext $P$, i.e.,
$$
\Pr(P = p \mid C = c) = \Pr(P = p) \quad \text{for all } p, c
$$
Shannon proved that a cipher provides perfect secrecy **if and only if** the number of possible keys is at least as large as the number of possible plaintexts, *and* each key is used with equal probability. The OTP satisfies this: with $n$-bit messages, the key is also $n$ random bits, giving a uniform $2^n$-sized key space.

**Impossibility of OTP in IoT (2 marks):**
- **Key distribution**: Sharing an $n$-bit random key for every $n$-bit message is infeasible for a battery-powered IoT sensor that streams megabytes of data per day.
- **Key storage**: Storing a key as long as the message stream is impossible on memory-constrained MCUs.
- **Key synchronization**: Both endpoints must stay perfectly aligned; loss of one bit breaks the entire stream.
- **True randomness**: IoT devices rarely have hardware TRNGs (True Random Number Generators); software PRNGs are deterministic.

**Workaround used in IoT (1 mark):**
In practice, IoT systems use **pseudo-random keystream generators (PRG)** seeded with a short, pre-shared key. Although the keystream is not truly random, modern PRGs (e.g., ChaCha20, AES-CTR) produce keystreams that are computationally indistinguishable from random, providing *computational* (not perfect) secrecy. Examples include:
- **ChaCha20** (used in TLS 1.3 for IoT protocols like MQTT 5.0 over TLS).
- **AES-128 in CTR mode** (hardware-accelerated on most modern IoT SoCs).
- **Grain-128 / Trivium** (lightweight stream ciphers designed for RFID and sensor networks).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Permutation-Based Stream Ciphers:**
> 1. **Forgetting modular reduction** — Writing $c = p + k$ instead of $c = (p + k) \bmod 26$. Lose **1 mark** for each occurrence.
> 2. **Using the wrong alphabet size** — For ASCII, $n = 256$, not 26. For binary, $n = 2$. Examiners explicitly state which alphabet is used; do not assume.
> 3. **Not addressing bijectivity** — In (b) parts, examiners often ask *why* a mapping is bijective. Students who skip this lose **2 marks** easily.
> 4. **Confusing static and dynamic permutations** — A Caesar cipher uses a *static* permutation. RC4 uses a *dynamic, evolving* permutation. This distinction is frequently tested.
> 5. **Missing the "Why" in definitions** — KTU follows OBE (Outcome-Based Education). A definition without its purpose/significance scores partial credit only.
> 6. **Keystream reuse** — If two messages are encrypted with the *same* keystream, then $c_1 \oplus c_2 = p_1 \oplus p_2$, leaking the XOR of plaintexts. Examiners love to test this in 14-mark questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Permutation-Based Stream Ciphers**

- ☐ A **stream cipher** encrypts one symbol at a time using a keystream from a PRG.
- ☐ A **permutation-based** stream cipher applies a bijective permutation (keyed) to each plaintext symbol.
- ☐ The **symmetric group** $S_n$ has $\vert S_n \vert = n!$ permutations.
- ☐ **Binary stream cipher** reduces to XOR: $c_i = p_i \oplus k_i$.
- ☐ **XOR is its own inverse**: $a \oplus b \oplus b = a$. Hardware/software can use the same code path for encryption and decryption.
- ☐ The **one-time pad (Vernam cipher)** offers *perfect secrecy* but requires a key as long as the message, used only once.
- ☐ IoT ciphers (ChaCha20, AES-CTR, Grain, Trivium) use *pseudo-random* keystreams for *computational* secrecy.
- ☐ **Caesar cipher** = static permutation = additive cipher with $k = 3$ (mod 26).
- ☐ **Multiplicative ciphers** require $\gcd(k, n) = 1$ for bijectivity.
- ☐ **Keystream reuse is catastrophic** — it leaks the XOR of plaintexts.
- ☐ **Decryption formula (modular additive)**: $p_i = (c_i - k_i) \bmod n$.
- ☐ **Decryption formula (modular multiplicative)**: $p_i = (c_i \cdot k_i^{-1}) \bmod n$.
- ☐ For IoT, prefer **lightweight, hardware-accelerated** ciphers (AES-NI, ARMv8 CE).
- ☐ **Synchronicity**: Synchronous ciphers (keystream ∝ plaintext) are robust against bit-insertion; self-synchronizing ciphers (keystream ∝ previous ciphertext) auto-recover from bit-loss.

> **Mnemonic:** *"Permutations are Powerful because they are Punctiliously Pairwise-distinct and Perfectly reversible."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples and Comparisons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT602 - CRYPTOGRAPHY FOR IOT APPLICATIONS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples and Comparisons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Examples and Comparisons" in IoT Cryptography?

In the context of **Cryptography for IoT Applications**, the topic *"Examples and Comparisons"* refers to the systematic study of representative **lightweight cryptographic primitives** (block ciphers, stream ciphers, and hash functions) that are specifically engineered for resource-constrained IoT devices, along with their **head-to-head comparative analysis** across security strength, hardware footprint, energy consumption, and throughput.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examiner-Standard):**
> *Lightweight Cryptography (LWC)* is a sub-field of cryptography that designs and evaluates symmetric-key primitives (block ciphers, stream ciphers, hash functions, MACs, and authenticated encryption schemes) optimized for implementation in **constrained environments** — where metrics such as **gate equivalents (GE)**, **power (µW)**, **energy per bit (pJ/bit)**, and **latency (cycles/byte)** are as critical as cryptographic security.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lunchbox" Metaphor

Imagine you need to send a **secure locked lunchbox** across a city:

- **Standard AES (Advanced Encryption Standard)** is like a **bank vault on wheels** — extremely secure, but heavy, requires a large truck (lots of transistors), and gulps fuel (power). It is overkill for a child's school lunch.
- **Lightweight ciphers (PRESENT, SIMON, SPECK, CLEFIA)** are like a **specially engineered compact bike-lock** — small enough to fit on a keychain (low GE), uses little battery (low power), but still resists common thieves (adequate security for IoT threats).
- **Stream ciphers (Grain, Trivium)** are like a **continuously changing code** spoken over a walkie-talkie — extremely fast and low-power, but you must keep the code secret and synchronized.

The **"Comparison"** part is the engineering trade-off study: deciding which "lock" is right for which IoT device (smartcard, RFID tag, sensor node, wearable).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU 2024 Benchmark Constants (must memorize):**
> - **1 Gate Equivalent (GE)** ≈ *the area of a 2-input NAND gate in 0.13 µm CMOS* ≈ **~5 µm² of silicon area**.
> - **AES-128 baseline** ≈ **~3,400 GE** (compact hardware).
> - **PRESENT-80 baseline** ≈ **~1,570 GE** (one of the smallest block ciphers).
> - **Security strength** is measured in **bits** (e.g., 80-bit security ≈ 2⁸⁰ operations to break).

### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU Exams

- KTU 2024 examiners frequently test **comparison tables** (3-mark direct questions) and **algorithm selection rationale** (14-mark design questions).
- The topic bridges *theoretical cryptography* (security proofs) with *practical IoT engineering* (resource budgeting).
- It directly maps to Course Outcomes like **CO4: Design and evaluate lightweight cryptographic solutions for IoT systems**.

---

## 1.4 Canonical Examples Covered in KTU 2024 Module 4

| # | Primitive | Type | KTU Tag |
|---|-----------|------|---------|
| 1 | **AES-128** | Block cipher (standard) | Baseline reference |
| 2 | **PRESENT** | Ultra-lightweight block cipher | ISO/IEC 29192-2 standard |
| 3 | **CLEFIA** | Lightweight block cipher (Sony) | ISO/IEC 29192-2 standard |
| 4 | **SIMON / SPECK** | NSA lightweight family (block ciphers) | Optimized for HW / SW |
| 5 | **Grain-128** | Lightweight stream cipher | ISO/IEC 29192-3 |
| 6 | **Trivium** | Lightweight stream cipher | ISO/IEC 29192-3 |
| 7 | **PHOTON / SPONGENT** | Lightweight hash functions | ISO/IEC 29192-5 |

> [!TIP]
> Always cite the **ISO/IEC 29192** standard family when comparing lightweight primitives — it is the universally accepted reference framework and examiners reward this terminology.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of Each Example Cipher

### 2.1.1 PRESENT Cipher (the "Poster Child" of LWC)
- **Block size:** **64 bits**
- **Key size:** **80 bits** (also 128-bit variant exists)
- **Rounds:** **31 rounds**
- **Structure:** **Substitution–Permutation Network (SPN)**
- **Round operations:** `addRoundKey → sBoxLayer → pLayer → addRoundKey`
- **S-box:** A single **4-bit-to-4-bit S-box** (only 16 entries, hence ultra-cheap).
- **P-layer:** Bit permutation (pure wiring, zero gates).

### 2.1.2 CLEFIA Cipher
- **Block size:** **128 bits**
- **Key size:** **128, 192, or 256 bits**
- **Rounds:** **18, 22, or 26** (depending on key size)
- **Structure:** **Generalized Feistel Network (GFN)** with 4 data lines.
- **S-boxes:** Two 8-bit S-boxes `S0` and `S1` (cleverly reused to save area).
- **Designer:** **Sony Corporation** (2007).

### 2.1.3 SIMON and SPECK (NSA Family, 2013)
- **Block size:** **32, 48, 64, 96, 128 bits**
- **Key size:** Scales with block size (e.g., SIMON 64/128 → 64-bit block, 128-bit key)
- **Rounds:** Vary (32 to 68)
- **SIMON:** Hardware-optimized (Feistel structure).
- **SPECK:** Software-optimized (ARX — Add-Rotate-XOR).

### 2.1.4 Stream Ciphers — Grain-128 and Trivium
- **Grain-128:** 128-bit key, 128-bit IV, uses **LFSR + NFSR + output function**.
- **Trivium:** 80-bit key, 80-bit IV, 288-bit state, **3 coupled shift registers**, very small (~2,000 GE).

### 2.1.5 Lightweight Hash Functions — PHOTON and SPONGENT
- **PHOTON:** 64/80/128/160/256-bit digests, sponge construction.
- **SPONGENT:** 88/128/160/224/256-bit digests, optimized for **passive RFID** (~1,000 GE).

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Design Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Engineering Trade-off (must understand for KTU):**
> $$\text{Security} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Area (GE)} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Power (µW)} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Throughput (Mbps)} \;\leftrightarrow\; \text{Latency (cycles)}$$

- **PRESENT** sacrifices throughput (low parallelism) to win on **area** (single 4-bit S-box, bit-permutation).
- **CLEFIA** uses **Diffusion Switching Mechanism (DSM)** to achieve AES-like security at *~50% of AES area*.
- **SIMON** wins on **hardware speed**; **SPECK** wins on **software speed** (no S-boxes → no ROM).
- **Grain/Trivium** win on **continuous keystream generation** (ideal for RF/wireless streaming data).
- **PHOTON/SPONGENT** win on **fixed-size tag generation** (RFID authentication).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{\text{Block size} \times f_{clk}}{\text{Cycles per block}}$ | **Mbps** | How fast plaintext is encrypted |
| 2 | **Energy per bit** | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{P}{T}$ | **pJ/bit** | Battery-life indicator |
| 3 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{T}{\text{Area (GE)}}$ | **kbps/GE** | Hardware efficiency |
| 4 | **Security-per-area** | $\sigma = \dfrac{k}{\text{GE}}$ | **bits/GE** | Design "bang for buck" |
| 5 | **Security strength bound** | $k \ge 2^n \text{ (block+key) for } n$-bit security | bits | NIST minimum: **112-bit** post-2020 |
| 6 | **Gate Equivalent (GE)** | $\text{GE} = \dfrac{\text{Total NAND-2 area}}{\text{Area of one NAND-2}}$ | dimensionless | Hardware area measure |
| 7 | **Avalanche probability** | $P_{av} \approx 0.5$ per output bit | probability | Diffusion quality |
| 8 | **LFSR period (max)** | $T = 2^n - 1$ | cycles | Stream cipher cycle |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL KTU PITFALL:** Never use the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside the table above (e.g., for absolute value). I have intentionally used `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking Markdown rendering. *Do not* write `|k| ÷ GE|` — write `$\vert k \vert$ ÷ GE` instead.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

| Application Domain | Recommended Primitive | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **RFID / Smart Cards** | PRESENT, SPONGENT | Ultra-low area (~1,000–1,500 GE) |
| **Body Area Networks (BAN)** | SIMON, SPECK | Low power, balanced performance |
| **Smart Meters (Industrial IoT)** | AES-128 (HW), CLEFIA | Regulatory compliance + speed |
| **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** | Grain-128, Trivium | Continuous stream, low latency |
| **Vehicular IoT (V2X)** | AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305 | High throughput, authenticated |
| **Wearables (Health)** | PRESENT, ASCON (NIST LWC winner) | Battery-critical |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Throughput for a Cipher

**Given:**
- Cipher: **PRESENT-80**
- Block size: $n = 64$ bits
- Clock frequency: $f_{clk} = 100$ kHz (typical RFID)
- Cycles per block: $C = 32$ cycles (one cycle per round + final key-update)

**Find:** Throughput $T$ in kbps.

**Step-by-step (KTU board-work style):**

Step 1 — Write the throughput formula:
$$T = \frac{\text{Block size} \times f_{clk}}{C}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the known values (in consistent units):
$$T = \frac{64 \; \text{bits} \times 100 \times 10^3 \; \text{Hz}}{32 \; \text{cycles}}$$

Step 3 — Simplify numerator and denominator:
$$T = \frac{64 \times 10^5}{32} \; \text{bits/second}$$

Step 4 — Perform the division (64 ÷ 32 = 2):
$$T = 2 \times 10^5 \; \text{bits/second}$$

Step 5 — Convert to kilobits per second (1 kbps = 10³ bps):
$$T = 200 \; \text{kbps}$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_{\text{PRESENT-80}} = 200 \text{ kbps}$ at 100 kHz clock.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy per Bit Calculation

**Given:**
- PRESENT-80 power consumption: $P = 5$ µW (from datasheet)
- Throughput from §3.1: $T = 200$ kbps

**Find:** Energy per encrypted bit $E_{bit}$.

Step 1 — Write the energy-per-bit formula:
$$E_{bit} = \frac{P}{T}$$

Step 2 — Convert units consistently (P in watts, T in bits/sec):
$$E_{bit} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{W}}{200 \times 10^3 \; \text{bits/s}}$$

Step 3 — Simplify the fraction:
$$E_{bit} = \frac{5}{200} \times 10^{-9} \; \text{J/bit}$$

Step 4 — Perform the arithmetic (5 ÷ 200 = 0.025):
$$E_{bit} = 0.025 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{J/bit}$$

Step 5 — Convert to picojoules (1 pJ = 10⁻¹² J):
$$E_{bit} = 25 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{J/bit} = 25 \; \text{pJ/bit}$$

> **Final Answer:** PRESENT-80 consumes **25 pJ/bit** — far below AES (~100 pJ/bit) — explaining its RFID dominance.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Security-per-Area (Design Quality Metric)

**Given:**
- AES-128: Security $k = 128$ bits, Area = 3,400 GE
- PRESENT-80: Security $k = 80$ bits, Area = 1,570 GE

**Find:** Which has better security-per-area?

Step 1 — Compute AES metric:
$$\sigma_{AES} = \frac{k_{AES}}{GE_{AES}} = \frac{128}{3400} = 0.0376 \; \text{bits/GE}$$

Step 2 — Compute PRESENT metric:
$$\sigma_{PRESENT} = \frac{k_{PRESENT}}{GE_{PRESENT}} = \frac{80}{1570} = 0.0509 \; \text{bits/GE}$$

Step 3 — Compare:
$$\sigma_{PRESENT} > \sigma_{AES}$$

Step 4 — Ratio of efficiency:
$$\frac{\sigma_{PRESENT}}{\sigma_{AES}} = \frac{0.0509}{0.0376} \approx 1.354$$

> **Final Answer:** PRESENT-80 delivers **~35.4% more security per gate** than AES-128 — the quantitative proof of its IoT suitability.

## 3.4 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix (KTU-Exam-Ready Table)

| Parameter | **AES-128** | **PRESENT-80** | **CLEFIA-128** | **SIMON-64/128** | **Grain-128** | **Trivium** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Block | Block | Block | Block | Stream | Stream |
| **Block size (bits)** | 128 | 64 | 128 | 64 | N/A (keystream) | N/A |
| **Key size (bits)** | 128 | 80 | 128 | 128 | 128 | 80 |
| **Rounds** | 10 | 31 | 18 | 42 | 256 (init) | 1,152 (init) |
| **Structure** | SPN | SPN | Feistel | Feistel | LFSR+NFSR | 3 Registers |
| **Area (GE)** | 3,400 | 1,570 | 2,600 | 1,237 | ~1,300 | ~2,000 |
| **Throughput @100kHz** | ~12 Mbps* | 200 kbps | ~800 kbps | ~150 kbps | ~12.8 Mbps | ~10 Mbps |
| **Energy/bit (pJ)** | ~100 | ~25 | ~60 | ~20 | ~18 | ~22 |
| **Best for** | High-speed secure | RFID / Tags | Consumer IoT | Sensor nodes | Streaming | Streaming |
| **Standard** | FIPS-197 | ISO/IEC 29192-2 | ISO/IEC 29192-2 | — | ISO/IEC 29192-3 | ISO/IEC 29192-3 |

*\* AES throughput assumes 1 cycle/round pipelined implementation.*

## 3.5 KTU High-Yield Derivation: Maximal LFSR Period (Stream Cipher)

**Given:** Grain-128 uses an LFSR of $n = 128$ bits.

**Find:** Maximum period before keystream repeats.

Step 1 — For a *maximal-length LFSR* with primitive feedback polynomial:
$$T_{max} = 2^n - 1$$

Step 2 — Substitute $n = 128$:
$$T_{max} = 2^{128} - 1$$

Step 3 — Express in decimal (for exam brevity, leave as $2^{128}-1$):
$$T_{max} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{38} \; \text{cycles}$$

Step 4 — At 100 kHz, time to repeat:
$$t_{repeat} = \frac{2^{128}-1}{10^5} \approx 3.4 \times 10^{33} \; \text{seconds} \approx 1.08 \times 10^{26} \; \text{years}$$

> **Interpretation:** The keystream essentially **never repeats** in any realistic deployment — cryptographic soundness confirmed.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: PRESENT Cipher Round Structure (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[64-bit Plaintext Block] --> node2[addRoundKey XOR with Round Key]
    node2 --> node3[sBoxLayer: 16 Parallel 4-bit S-boxes]
    node3 --> node4[pLayer: Bit Permutation]
    node4 --> node5[Key Schedule: Update 80-bit Key]
    node5 --> node6{Is this Round 31?}
    node6 -->|No| node2
    node6 -->|Yes| node7[Final addRoundKey]
    node7 --> node8[64-bit Ciphertext Output]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The `addRoundKey → sBoxLayer → pLayer` cycle repeats **31 times**, then a final key XOR is applied. There is no MixColumns (unlike AES), which is the primary area-saving decision.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: IoT Cipher Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart[New IoT Cryptographic Design] --> nodeQ1{Is device area < 2000 GE?}
    nodeQ1 -->|Yes| nodeQ2{Is data streaming continuous?}
    nodeQ1 -->|No| nodeQ3{Is software-only implementation?}
    nodeQ2 -->|Yes| nodeA1[Choose Grain-128 or Trivium]
    nodeQ2 -->|No| nodeA2[Choose PRESENT-80]
    nodeQ3 -->|Yes| nodeA3[Choose SPECK family]
    nodeQ3 -->|No| nodeA4[Choose SIMON or CLEFIA]
    nodeA1 --> nodeEnd[Validated Design]
    nodeA2 --> nodeEnd
    nodeA3 --> nodeEnd
    nodeA4 --> nodeEnd
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** Drawing such a *decision tree* in a 14-mark design question immediately earns **2–3 bonus marks** for systematic engineering thinking.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any three lightweight block ciphers standardized under ISO/IEC 29192-2. State their block size and key size.**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Standard):**
The three ISO/IEC 29192-2 standardized lightweight block ciphers are:

1. **PRESENT** — Block size: **64 bits**, Key size: **80 bits** (or 128-bit variant).
2. **CLEFIA** — Block size: **128 bits**, Key size: **128/192/256 bits**.
3. **AES** (in the 29192 family context for 128-bit data path comparison) — Block size: **128 bits**, Key size: **128/192/256 bits**.

> All three are optimized for low gate-equivalent hardware, making them suitable for **RFID tags, smart cards, and sensor nodes** where silicon area is the primary constraint.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between PRESENT and AES on the basis of (i) structure, (ii) S-box size, and (iii) number of rounds.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **PRESENT-80** | **AES-128** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Structure | Substitution–Permutation Network (SPN), no MixColumns | SPN with SubBytes, ShiftRows, MixColumns, AddRoundKey |
| (ii) S-box size | **4-bit** (16-entry lookup) | **8-bit** (256-entry lookup, GF(2⁸) inversion) |
| (iii) Number of rounds | **31** | **10** |

> PRESENT's smaller S-box and bit-permutation yield **~50% area reduction** at the cost of needing more rounds to achieve full diffusion.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Compare PRESENT, CLEFIA, and SIMON lightweight block ciphers with respect to structure, key size, block size, rounds, and target application.**

**Model Answer (Systematic Table + Explanation):**

| Feature | **PRESENT-80** | **CLEFIA-128** | **SIMON-64/128** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block size | 64 bits | 128 bits | 64 bits |
| Key size | 80 bits | 128 bits | 128 bits |
| Rounds | 31 | 18 | 42 |
| Structure | SPN | Generalized Feistel (4-branch) | Feistel (balanced) |
| S-box | 4-bit (one type) | 8-bit (S0, S1 — reusable) | None (AND-based) |
| Area (approx) | ~1,570 GE | ~2,600 GE | ~1,237 GE |
| Best for | RFID, smart cards | Consumer IoT, multimedia | Sensor nodes, low-power HW |

**Explanation (board-work paragraphs):**

- **PRESENT** uses a single 4-bit S-box applied 16 times in parallel and a fixed bit-permutation layer. It is the smallest standardized block cipher and dominates ultra-constrained RFID deployments.
- **CLEFIA** employs a 4-branch Generalized Feistel Network with two carefully designed 8-bit S-boxes and a Diffusion Switching Mechanism (DSM). It provides AES-128-level security at roughly **60–75% of the hardware area**, making it ideal for high-performance consumer IoT.
- **SIMON** uses only **AND, XOR, and bitwise rotations** — completely S-box-free — which makes it the **fastest in hardware** and immune to cache-timing attacks. It targets low-power sensor network ASICs.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] An IoT sensor node encrypts 1 KB of telemetry data using PRESENT-80. The clock is 1 MHz and the cipher requires 32 cycles per 64-bit block. Compute the (i) Throughput and (ii) Time to encrypt 1 KB.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Throughput using the standard formula:**

$$T = \frac{\text{Block size} \times f_{clk}}{\text{Cycles per block}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 64$ bits, $f_{clk} = 1 \text{ MHz} = 10^6$ Hz, $C = 32$:**

$$T = \frac{64 \times 10^6}{32} = 2 \times 10^6 \; \text{bits/second} = 2 \; \text{Mbps}$$

**Step 3 — For 1 KB = $8 \times 1024 = 8192$ bits, compute encryption time:**

$$t_{enc} = \frac{\text{Total bits}}{T} = \frac{8192}{2 \times 10^6}$$

**Step 4 — Final arithmetic:**

$$t_{enc} = 4096 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{seconds} = 4.096 \; \text{ms}$$

> **Final Answer:** **Throughput = 2 Mbps**, **Encryption time = 4.096 ms**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) [7 Marks] Explain the architecture of Grain-128 stream cipher. Why is it preferred for streaming IoT applications?**

**Model Answer:**

**Architecture (3 components):**

1. **LFSR (Linear Feedback Shift Register):** 128-bit linear recurrence providing keystream diffusion.
2. **NFSR (Non-Linear Feedback Shift Register):** 128-bit non-linear recurrence providing confusion.
3. **Output function $h(x)$:** Combines one bit from each register + a pre-output bit to produce the keystream bit $z_t$.

**Initialization:** Load 128-bit key $K$ and 128-bit IV into the LFSR and NFSR, then run the cipher for 256 clock cycles without producing output to mix the state.

**Keystream generation:** After initialization, each clock cycle produces 1 keystream bit, XORed with the plaintext bit.

**Why preferred for IoT streaming:**

- **Tiny area** (~1,300 GE) — fits on passive RFID.
- **Continuous keystream** — no block boundaries; ideal for packetized sensor data.
- **High throughput** — can run at hundreds of MHz.
- **Low latency** — first keystream bit available immediately after init.
- **Standardized** under ISO/IEC 29192-3.

---

#### **(b) [7 Marks] A smartcard uses PRESENT-80 at 100 kHz with 32 cycles/block and consumes 5 µW. Compute (i) Throughput in kbps, (ii) Energy per encrypted bit in pJ, and (iii) Total energy to encrypt 1 KB of data.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Throughput calculation:**

$$T = \frac{64 \times 100 \times 10^3}{32} = 200 \times 10^3 \; \text{bps} = 200 \; \text{kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per bit:**

$$E_{bit} = \frac{P}{T} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{W}}{200 \times 10^3 \; \text{bps}} = 25 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{J/bit} = 25 \; \text{pJ/bit}$$

**Step 3 — Total energy for 1 KB = 8192 bits:**

$$E_{total} = E_{bit} \times N = 25 \; \text{pJ/bit} \times 8192 \; \text{bits}$$

**Step 4 — Final arithmetic:**

$$E_{total} = 204{,}800 \; \text{pJ} = 204.8 \; \text{nJ}$$

> **Final Answers:** (i) **200 kbps**, (ii) **25 pJ/bit**, (iii) **204.8 nJ per 1 KB block**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Avoid These!):**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing kHz with MHz without conversion → lose 1 mark. *Always* write units explicitly.
> 2. **Wrong block size for PRESENT:** Writing 128 bits (that's AES) → lose 1–2 marks. PRESENT = **64-bit block**.
> 3. **Omitting the 1 KB → 8192 bits conversion** in Part B derivations → lose 1 mark.
> 4. **No formula, only final number:** Board examiners *require* the formula with substitution → lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing SIMON and SPECK:** SIMON is hardware-optimized (Feistel), SPECK is software-optimized (ARX) — students often swap them.
> 6. **Forgetting ISO/IEC 29192 reference:** When the question asks "standardized," you *must* cite the standard number.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **PRESENT** = 64-bit block, 80-bit key, 31 rounds, 4-bit S-box, ~1,570 GE → **RFID standard**.
- ⭐ **CLEFIA** = 128-bit block, 128-bit key, 18 rounds, Feistel, ~2,600 GE → **Consumer IoT**.
- ⭐ **SIMON/SPECK** = NSA family, multi-block-size, AND-XOR-rotation, ~1,237 GE → **Sensor nodes**.
- ⭐ **Grain-128** = 128-bit stream cipher, LFSR+NFSR, ISO/IEC 29192-3 → **Streaming data**.
- ⭐ **Trivium** = 80-bit stream cipher, 288-bit state, ~2,000 GE → **Lightweight streaming**.
- ⭐ **PHOTON/SPONGENT** = Lightweight hash functions for **RFID authentication**.
- ⭐ **Throughput formula:** $T = \dfrac{n \times f_{clk}}{C}$.
- ⭐ **Energy per bit formula:** $E_{bit} = \dfrac{P}{T}$.
- ⭐ **Security-per-area formula:** $\sigma = \dfrac{k}{GE}$ — higher is better.
- ⭐ **LFSR maximal period:** $T = 2^n - 1$ (stream cipher soundness).
- ⭐ **ISO/IEC 29192** is the umbrella standard for all of the above.
- ⭐ **NIST security minimum (post-2020):** ≥ **112-bit security** for cryptographic primitives.
- ⭐ **Always convert:** 1 KB = 8 × 1024 = **8192 bits**; 1 kbps = 10³ bps; 1 pJ = 10⁻¹² J.
- ⭐ **Trade-off mantra:** "Smaller area ⇒ fewer rounds of complex operations ⇒ more rounds of simple operations" — this is the *essence* of LWC design philosophy.
